CVE-2026-54420.name: LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin UNIX Symbolic Link (Symlink) Following Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A low-privileged tenant who already holds FTP or web-shell access to one account on a shared CloudLinux/CageFS host plants a malicious symlink in a path the LiteSpeed cPanel plugin subsequently operates on with root privilege (during the generateEcCert / packageUserSize JSON-API operations). Because the plugin follows the attacker-controlled symlink without validating its target (CWE-61/CWE-59), the privileged process reads or writes files outside the tenant's CageFS jail. This escapes the per-account sandbox to reach other customers' database credentials, config, and source — or system files — escalating a single jailed account to root-level, server-wide cross-tenant compromise. The CVSS:3.1 8.5 (AV:N/AC:H/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H) reflects changed scope and full CIA impact, gated behind pre-existing low-privilege access and high attack complexity (a timing/race window characteristic of symlink TOCTOU). CISA KEV-listed 2026-06-15 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

CVE-2026-20262.name: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager Directory or Path Traversal Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: The web UI/API of Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager (formerly SD-WAN vManage) is network-reachable and exposes a file-upload handler that fails to validate user-supplied file paths during upload processing. An authenticated attacker holding only a low-privileged, single-task user account sends a crafted HTTP request with path-traversal (../) sequences, obtaining an arbitrary file create/overwrite primitive anywhere on the underlying operating system. That write primitive is then chained — by overwriting a privileged configuration, cron, or startup file, or planting executable content — to elevate to root, yielding full compromise of the SD-WAN control plane that pushes policy and routing to every managed edge device. CVE-2025-34028.name: Commvault Command Center Path Traversal Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: The Command Center exposes the deployWebpackage.do endpoint without authentication (CWE-306), making it remotely reachable by any unauthenticated network actor. CVE-2024-58136.name: Yiiframework Yii Improper Protection of Alternate Path Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A Yii 2 application that lets attacker-controlled array input reach Yii's behavior/configuration attachment logic is reachable over the network with no authentication. When the supplied array contains a `__class` key, Yii instantiates the named class with attacker-chosen parameters — an unsafe-reflection / object-injection primitive reintroduced as a regression of CVE-2024-4990 (incompletely fixed in 2.0.50). By selecting a class whose constructor or destructor performs file writes or command execution, the attacker converts that instantiation into remote code execution. In the observed campaign the input was delivered by chaining Craft CMS pre-auth RCE CVE-2025-32432, so a single unauthenticated request escalated straight to web-shell deployment and data theft on the host. CVE-2024-38475.name: Apache HTTP Server Improper Escaping of Output Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: mod_rewrite is reachable on any internet-facing Apache HTTP Server 2.4.59 or earlier whose configuration uses a server-context RewriteRule where the substitution's first segment is a backreference or variable. Because the substituted output is improperly escaped, attacker-controlled request input can steer the post-rewrite target to a filesystem location the server is permitted to serve but that no URL was ever meant to reach. The primitive yields source-code disclosure of on-disk files, or code execution when the mapped path lands on an executable handler. In the SonicWall SMA100 deployment that bundles Apache, UNC6148 used the unauthenticated traversal to read SQLite databases holding admin credentials and OTP seeds, then replayed those secrets to regain privileged access and install the OVERSTEP rootkit for persistence and data theft. CISA KEV-listed 2025-05-01 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

CVE-2025-1976.name: Broadcom Brocade Fabric OS Code Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Exploitation requires a pre-authenticated local user already holding a pre-defined admin role or a user-defined role with admin-level privileges on the Fabric OS switch (CVSS 3.1 AV:L / PR:H; CVSS 4.0 rescored AV:A adjacent). A flaw in IP-address input validation lets that admin user smuggle arbitrary code into a command path that executes as root, breaking out of the restricted admin shell. The primitive yields full root code execution — letting the attacker run any Fabric OS command or modify the OS itself (e.g. inserting custom subroutines), which defeats the root-access removal Broadcom introduced starting in Fabric OS 9.1.0. Net effect is an admin-to-root privilege escalation on the SAN fabric switch. CISA KEV-listed 2025-04-28 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

CVE-2025-42599.name: Qualitia Active! Mail Stack-Based Buffer Overflow Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Active! Mail's webmail front-end is internet-facing and parses incoming HTTP requests prior to authentication. A remote, unauthenticated attacker sends a specially crafted request whose over-length field overflows a fixed-size stack buffer (CWE-121), overwriting the saved return address and redirecting execution into attacker-controlled data. On successful control this yields arbitrary code execution in the context of the web service on the mail server; on failed control it crashes the service for a denial of service. In observed incidents the foothold was used to reach hosted mailboxes and harvest stored credentials (including cloud-admin credentials at affected providers). CISA KEV-listed 2025-04-28 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

CVE-2025-24054.name: Microsoft Windows NTLM Hash Disclosure Spoofing Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Reachable with minimal user interaction: an attacker delivers a malicious .library-ms (XML library-description) file, typically inside a ZIP archive sent via malspam or a Dropbox link. The file embeds a UNC path to an attacker-controlled SMB server, so when Windows Explorer parses it — triggered merely by navigating to, viewing, or single-clicking the containing folder, with no file actually opened — Windows initiates an outbound SMB/NTLM authentication and leaks the victim's NTLMv2-SSP hash (external-control-of-path / forced authentication, CWE-73). The captured hash is then cracked offline for the cleartext password or relayed (NTLM relay) to authenticate to other services, enabling lateral movement and, against privileged accounts, potential domain compromise. CISA KEV-listed 2025-04-17 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

CVE-2025-31201.name: Apple Multiple Products Arbitrary Read and Write Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Reachability is network-based and zero-click: a maliciously crafted media file delivered over iMessage/SMS is processed by CoreAudio's AudioConverterService (CVE-2025-31200), yielding initial code execution without user interaction. CVE-2025-31201 then supplies an arbitrary read/write primitive that defeats ARM Pointer Authentication (RPAC), neutralizing Apple's primary ROP/JOP control-flow mitigation. The two CVEs are exploited together as one chain against specifically targeted individuals. CISA KEV-listed 2025-04-17 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

CVE-2025-31200.name: Apple Multiple Products Memory Corruption Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Apple's CoreAudio framework parses audio streams embedded in media files without adequate bounds checking (CWE-119). A remotely delivered, maliciously crafted media file — reachable with no user interaction via zero-click iMessage delivery — triggers an out-of-bounds memory write during audio-stream decoding, yielding the primitive for arbitrary code execution in the media-processing context (network attack vector, no privileges, no UI: CVSS 9.8). CVE-2021-20035.name: SonicWall SMA100 Appliances OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: The SMA 100 web-based management interface fails to neutralize shell metacharacters in attacker-supplied input (CWE-78), so a remote attacker authenticated to the management plane can inject arbitrary OS commands that execute as the low-privilege 'nobody' account. SonicWall initially scored it DoS-only (CVSS 3.1 6.5, A:H) but later confirmed it can be driven to arbitrary code execution. In the UNC6148 campaign the actor first obtains valid admin credentials/OTP seeds (likely harvested in earlier intrusions), then uses this injection as the on-box code-execution primitive to repack the INITRD boot image and install the OVERSTEP rootkit via /etc/ld.so.preload — converting a limited 'nobody' command run into persistent, reboot-surviving, log-tampering control of the appliance and a credential-exfiltration foothold for lateral movement. attack_vector.description: The Linux kernel's ALSA USB-audio driver (snd-usb-audio) traverses clock-source, clock-multiplier, and clock-selector descriptors of an attached USB audio device without validating each descriptor's bLength field. A malicious or bogus USB device can present a descriptor whose declared length is shorter than the structure the driver dereferences, so descriptor parsing reads past the end of the allocated descriptor buffer (CWE-125 out-of-bounds read). Reachability requires physical USB access and the (low) privilege of the kernel auto-binding the audio driver on enumeration; the primitive is an information leak of adjacent kernel memory (C:H), which in a forensic-extraction chain helps defeat at-rest protections / leak sensitive data from a locked device, and the malformed-parse path can also crash the driver (A:H). It is not remotely reachable (AV:L) and yields disclosure/DoS rather than direct code execution on its own. CISA KEV-listed 2025-04-09 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (physical USB access to a locked device)
attack_vector.complexity: moderate — KEV-listed and actively exploited; treat as weaponized.

attack_vector.description: An attacker with brief physical access plugs a malicious USB gadget into the target's locked Linux/Android device, reaching the kernel's ALSA USB-audio driver through normal USB enumeration. The crafted device matches the Extigy/Mbox quirk path and advertises a bNumConfigurations value larger than the configuration array actually allocated by usb_get_configuration(), so subsequent indexed access (e.g. in usb_destroy_configuration / snd_usb_create_quirk) reads and writes past the dev->config allocation — an out-of-bounds primitive (CWE-787). Combined with the other USB-driver bugs in the Cellebrite chain, this memory corruption is leveraged to bypass the lockscreen and escalate from the unprivileged USB-handling context to kernel/root code execution, giving full device compromise. CVE-2025-29824.name: Microsoft Windows Common Log File System (CLFS) Driver Use-After-Free Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local, low-privileged authenticated user reaches the Common Log File System (clfs.sys) kernel driver through standard CLFS APIs operating on a crafted base-log (.blf) file, triggering a use-after-free (CWE-416) on a freed CLFS structure. The actor leaks kernel addresses to user mode via NtQuerySystemInformation, then weaponizes the dangling pointer together with the RtlSetAllBits primitive to overwrite the exploiting process's token privileges field with 0xFFFFFFFF, granting all privileges and effectively SYSTEM. With SYSTEM, the chain injects into winlogon.exe, dumps LSASS for credentials, and launches RansomEXX from dllhost.exe — turning a post-compromise foothold into domain-wide ransomware impact. CISA KEV-listed 2025-04-08 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local user; PipeMagic/RansomEXX pre-ransomware escalation)

CVE-2025-30406.name: Gladinet CentreStack and Triofox Use of Hard-coded Cryptographic Key Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: The CentreStack/Triofox web portal ships a hard-coded ASP.NET machineKey in its IIS web.config, so any unauthenticated remote attacker who knows that static key (it is the same across installs) can forge a valid, MAC-signed __VIEWSTATE payload. Because IIS trusts the signed ViewState and deserializes it server-side, the forged payload carries a malicious .NET object graph (a standard ViewState/ysoserial gadget chain) that executes arbitrary code at deserialization time. Code runs as the IIS application-pool identity (IISAPPPOOL\portaluser / portal app pool), which is then trivially escalated toward NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM for full server compromise. Reachability is the internet-exposed portal endpoint; the primitive is signed-ViewState deserialization RCE; escalation is app-pool-to-SYSTEM plus lateral movement. CISA KEV-listed 2025-04-08 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker reaches Tomcat's default servlet when write support is enabled (off by default) and partial PUT is supported (on by default). The original partial-PUT implementation wrote the request body to a temporary file whose name was derived from the user-supplied path with the path separator replaced by a dot ("internal dot" path equivalence, CWE-706/CWE-44), letting an attacker place attacker-controlled bytes at a predictable filesystem location outside the intended upload target. If the application uses Tomcat's file-based session persistence at the default storage location and ships a deserialization-exploitable library on the classpath, the attacker stages a malicious serialized session object via partial PUT, then forces Tomcat to deserialize it with a crafted JSESSIONID request (CWE-502), achieving remote code execution; absent the deserialization preconditions the same primitive yields read/inject of security-sensitive uploaded files (information disclosure / content tampering). CISA KEV-listed 2025-04-01 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

CVE-2024-20439.name: Cisco Smart Licensing Utility Static Credential Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Cisco Smart Licensing Utility ships an undocumented static administrative account (CWE-798 / CWE-912) whose credentials (cslu-windows-client:Library4C$LU) are hardcoded in the binary. When the CSLU service is running, its REST API listens on TCP/8182, so an unauthenticated, remote attacker who reaches the host over the network can present the static Basic-auth credentials to authenticate as administrator with no prior access. That yields full administrative control over the CSLU application API, which an attacker chains with CVE-2024-20440 (an excessive-verbosity log endpoint) to harvest further credentials and tokens. Because CSLU only listens while actively invoked, exposure is intermittent, but any reachable, running 2.0.0–2.2.0 instance is trivially compromisable. CISA KEV-listed 2025-03-31 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

CVE-2025-2783.name: Google Chromium Mojo Sandbox Escape Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A victim using Chrome/Chromium on Windows visits an attacker-controlled page (delivered via spear-phishing), giving the compromised renderer a reachable path to the Mojo IPC layer. A logic error causes an incorrect/sentinel OS handle (the -2 pseudo-handle) to be supplied and used without validation when the broker process duplicates handles across the renderer-to-broker boundary, yielding a sandbox escape primitive that lets renderer-controlled data operate on a privileged broker-side handle. Combined with a renderer-side code-execution bug, this escalates the constrained, sandboxed renderer to arbitrary code execution in the medium-integrity browser/broker context, from which the ForumTroll operators staged the Dante spyware loader — all with no user interaction beyond opening the link. CISA KEV-listed 2025-03-27 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

CVE-2019-9875.name: Sitecore CMS and Experience Platform (XP) Deserialization Vulnerability (post-authentication)

attack_vector.description: The Sitecore.Security.AntiCsrf module deserializes the attacker-supplied __CSRFTOKEN HTTP POST parameter via ASP.NET's ObjectStateFormatter BEFORE comparing it to the __CSRFCOOKIE value, and because the formatter is instantiated with a null _page it performs no MAC/signature validation on the LOSFormatter stream. An attacker who can reach a guarded Sitecore endpoint (e.g. CreateNewUser.aspx) supplies a ysoserial.net TypeConfuseDelegate gadget encoded for the ObjectStateFormatter, which executes during deserialization regardless of the subsequent cookie-mismatch error. For the 9.x branch this CVE requires a valid authenticated session (PR:L); code runs in-process as the IIS app-pool identity, after which the gadget's PowerShell stager pulls a second stage and opens a reverse shell, giving arbitrary command execution on the web server and access to the underlying filesystem. CISA KEV-listed 2025-03-26 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

CVE-2019-9874.name: Sitecore CMS and Experience Platform (XP) Deserialization Vulnerability (unauthenticated)

attack_vector.description: The Sitecore.Security.AntiCSRF module accepts the value of the HTTP POST parameter __CSRFTOKEN and deserializes it with .NET BinaryFormatter without type restriction, reachable over the network on any AntiCSRF-protected page. On affected 8.x and earlier builds the deserialization occurs before authentication is enforced, so an unauthenticated remote attacker (CVSS 9.8, AV:N/PR:N) can submit a crafted serialized object. Supplying a known deserialization gadget chain turns this primitive into arbitrary command execution in the context of the IIS application pool identity hosting Sitecore, yielding full server compromise without needing a separate privilege-escalation step. attack_vector.description: The vulnerable scheduler/ui/js/ffffffffbca41eb4/UIUtilJavaScriptJS handler in SAP NetWeaver AS Java 7.5 is reachable pre-authentication over HTTP and fails to normalize `..` sequences in its query string. CVE-2024-48248.name: NAKIVO Backup and Replication Absolute Path Traversal Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: The NAKIVO Director web service exposes an unauthenticated /c/router dispatcher; the STPreLoadFacade.getImageByPath handler takes an attacker-controlled path, naively rewrites the leading "/c" to "userdata" and passes the result straight to a File read, so an absolute path such as C:/windows/win.ini or /etc/passwd escapes the intended directory and yields arbitrary file read (CWE-36, no auth, network-reachable). The primitive is escalated by reading NAKIVO's own configuration: the encryption key in config.properties plus the bundled H2 database let an attacker decrypt the PhysicalDiscovery feature's stored cleartext credentials for the systems being backed up. Because a backup product holds privileged credentials across the estate, this file-read pivots into broad credential theft and remote code execution on integrated hypervisors/hosts, which is why the CVSS scope is Changed (8.6). Patched releases replace the path handling with FilenameUtils.getName(), stripping any directory component before the read. CISA KEV-listed 2025-03-19 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

CVE-2025-1316.name: Edimax IC-7100 IP Camera OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: The Edimax IC-7100's web management interface exposes the /camera-cgi/admin/param.cgi endpoint, which fails to neutralize OS shell metacharacters in the NTP_serverName field of the ipcamSource option. An attacker who reaches the endpoint (commonly via unchanged default credentials admin:1234, often on internet-exposed cameras) injects a shell command string that the device executes as the CGI's privileged context, yielding remote code execution. In observed campaigns the injected command downloads a curl.sh/wget.sh stager that pulls architecture-matched Mirai ELF payloads into a world-writable directory and runs them, enrolling the camera into a DDoS botnet. Because the device is end-of-life with no patch, the only durable remediation is network isolation or hardware replacement. CISA KEV-listed 2025-03-19 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

CVE-2025-24472.name: Fortinet FortiOS and FortiProxy Authentication Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A remote, unauthenticated attacker who can reach the FortiGate/FortiProxy management plane and who knows the serial numbers of the upstream and downstream Security Fabric (CSF) devices sends crafted CSF proxy requests over an alternate path/channel (the Node.js websocket and CSF request handler). Because authentication is enforced on the primary path but not consistently on this alternate channel (CWE-288, Authentication Bypass Using an Alternate Path or Channel), the request is processed with downstream-device super-admin authority. The bypass yields full super-admin on the downstream device, from which the operator creates persistent admin and SSL-VPN accounts, alters firewall policy, and pivots into the internal network. Reachability requires the Security Fabric to be enabled and the HTTP/HTTPS admin interface (or CSF proxy) to be exposed. CISA KEV-listed 2025-03-18 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (authentication bypass to super-admin)

CVE-2025-21590.name: Juniper Junos OS Improper Isolation or Compartmentalization Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Reachability requires a local attacker who already holds high (root/shell) privileges on the device and can drop from the Junos CLI into the underlying FreeBSD shell — it is explicitly not exploitable from the Junos CLI itself. The primitive is improper process isolation (CWE-653): the attacker writes attacker-controlled shellcode into the memory of a legitimate hung process via /proc/<pid>/mem and overwrites the GOT entry for fclose, so triggering EOF on the process executes the loader instead. This circumvents Junos OS Veriexec verified-execution integrity protection, allowing arbitrary unsigned code (PIC TINYSHELL backdoors) to run despite file-integrity controls — converting existing root shell access into persistent, signature-evading implant execution on the router. CISA KEV-listed 2025-03-13 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: high (local shell with limited privilege; UNC3886 espionage)

CVE-2025-24993.name: Microsoft Windows NTFS Heap-Based Buffer Overflow Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Reachability is via the NTFS driver (ntfs.sys) parsing attacker-controlled on-disk metadata: an attacker crafts a malicious VHD/VHDX (or other disk image) and induces a local user to mount it — Windows auto-mounts a double-clicked VHD through Explorer, so this needs only file delivery plus a single user action (UI:R). The malformed NTFS structure causes improper bounds checking in the driver to overflow a heap buffer (CWE-122). Controlling the overwrite of adjacent kernel/heap structures yields local arbitrary code execution; because the parsing happens in the file-system driver running with high privilege, a successful exploit escalates from an ordinary user to SYSTEM, giving full host compromise that can stage ransomware or further implants. CISA KEV-listed 2025-03-11 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (victim mounts an attacker-supplied VHD)
attack_vector.complexity: low — KEV-listed and actively exploited; treat as weaponized.

CVE-2025-24991.name: Microsoft Windows NTFS Out-Of-Bounds Read Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Reachability requires local presence plus user interaction (CVSS UI:R): an attacker must get a victim to mount a specially crafted VHD/VHDX, typically delivered through phishing or a bundled archive. When the NTFS driver parses the malicious file-system structures on the mounted volume, missing bounds validation lets a read operation run past the allocated buffer (CWE-125), returning adjacent kernel/heap memory to the attacker-controlled context. The primitive is an out-of-bounds read that discloses small portions of heap memory, which can leak secrets such as cached credentials, cryptographic key material, or addresses useful for defeating ASLR. There is no direct privilege escalation from this bug alone, but the leaked data is commonly chained with the co-patched NTFS/FAT memory-corruption flaws (CVE-2025-24993 RCE, CVE-2025-24985) to harden an exploit chain into code execution. CISA KEV-listed 2025-03-11 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

CVE-2025-24985.name: Microsoft Windows Fast FAT File System Driver Integer Overflow Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: CVE-2025-24985 is an integer overflow/wraparound (CWE-190) in the Windows Fast FAT file-system driver (fastfat.sys) leading to a heap-based buffer overflow (CWE-122). Reachability requires a local user to be tricked into mounting an attacker-crafted virtual hard disk (VHD/VHDX); when the kernel parses the malformed FAT metadata, the size miscalculation overflows a heap allocation. The corrupted-heap primitive yields kernel-mode memory corruption that an attacker shapes into arbitrary code execution in the context of the file-system driver, escalating from a normal user to SYSTEM. Net effect is local code execution gated only by user interaction (the mount), with no prior privileges required. CISA KEV-listed 2025-03-11 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.

CVE-2025-24984.name: Microsoft Windows NTFS Information Disclosure Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: CVE-2025-24984 is a CWE-532 flaw in the Windows NTFS driver that inserts sensitive in-memory data (portions of heap, potentially containing cached credentials, tokens, or keys) into NTFS log/metadata structures. Reachability requires physical/local access (CVSS AV:P) — the attacker mounts a crafted NTFS volume, typically via a malicious USB device or VHD image, prompting the driver to process attacker-influenced filesystem structures. The primitive is an out-of-bounds/uninitialized read disclosed into log data that the attacker can then read back, yielding heap-memory disclosure (C:H, I:N, A:N). It does not by itself grant code execution or privilege escalation, but the leaked heap contents can seed a follow-on attack and it was shipped/exploited in the same window as the NTFS heap-overflow RCE CVE-2025-24993, making it a useful information-leak primitive in a physical-access chain. CISA KEV-listed 2025-03-11 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (physical access; attacker plugs in a malicious USB)
attack_vector.complexity: moderate — KEV-listed and actively exploited; treat as weaponized.

attack_vector.description: An attacker who already has low-privilege local code execution on an affected, pre-1809 Windows build triggers a use-after-free in the Win32k kernel subsystem: by winning a race condition, the W32PROCESS structure is dereferenced one more time than its reference count allows, leaving a dangling pointer to freed kernel memory. Grooming the kernel pool to reclaim that allocation with attacker-controlled data turns the stale dereference into a controlled kernel write/execute primitive, escalating the process token to SYSTEM. Because the bug is reached only after initial foothold (here, via the PipeMagic backdoor), it is a second-stage local privilege escalation that hands the operator full machine takeover for follow-on actions such as credential theft, persistence, and ransomware staging. The high attack complexity reflects the race that must be won, which is why Microsoft rated it Important rather than Critical. CISA KEV-listed 2025-03-11 with confirmed in-the-wild exploitation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local user; delivered via PipeMagic per ESET)
attack_vector.complexity: low — KEV-listed and actively exploited; treat as weaponized.

CVE-2023-20118.name: Cisco Small Business RV Series Routers Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description:
A vulnerability in the web-based management interface of Cisco Small Business Routers RV016, RV042, RV042G, RV082, RV320, and RV325 Routers could allow an authenticated, remote attacker to execute arbitrary commands on an affected device.
 This vulnerability is due to improper validation of user input within incoming HTTP packets. An attacker could exploit this vulnerability by sending a crafted HTTP request to the web-based management interface. A successful exploit could allow the attacker to gain root-level privileges and access unauthorized data. To exploit this vulnerability, an attacker would need to have valid administrative credentials on the affected device.
 Cisco has not and will not release software updates that address this vulnerability.   However, administrators may disable the affected feature as described in the Workarounds ["#workarounds"] section.

CVE-2026-56291.name: Balbooa Forms Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker submits a Balbooa Forms attachment field with a .php file; the extension performs no file-type or authentication check and stores the file in a public uploads folder, which the attacker then requests directly to execute the code.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, single unauthenticated HTTP request)
attack_vector.complexity: low — no CSRF token, no auth, no file-type check to bypass
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by independent researcher Phil Taylor via private disclosure to Balbooa. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update to Balbooa Forms 2.4.1+; compensating control of disabling anonymous file attachments or placing an upload allow-list / WAF rule blocking executable extensions at the reverse proxy.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone is reactive; exploitation began before the patch existed, so a compensating upload-type restriction was the only control that would have worked during the exposure window.

detection.what_would_have_worked: File-integrity monitoring on the uploads directory for new executable files, and web-server log alerting on POSTs to the attachment endpoint followed by a GET to the same path.
detection.adequacy: Effective only if FIM/log monitoring is already deployed on the Joomla webroot, which most small/mid sites running plugin marketplaces do not have.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the site, remove planted webshells, rotate Joomla super-user credentials and any secrets reachable from the web root, then update to 2.4.1.
response.adequacy: Standard IR steps apply but require the operator to already know the extension was compromised, which an unmonitored small site is unlikely to detect quickly.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Balbooa shipped a fix within a day of the private report, but exploitation was already underway before any patch existed — flaw-remediation SLAs measured in weeks cannot outrun a pre-disclosure zero-day.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch-applications control assumes a known-vulnerable window between disclosure and exploitation; here the window was negative, so patch cadence alone can't be the only control.

CVE-2026-48939.name: iCagenda Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker submits a crafted file through iCagenda's public event-attachment feature; the extension does not validate file type or require authentication, letting a PHP payload land in a web-accessible directory and execute.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, single HTTP request)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoC automates extension-disguise and upload
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to iCagenda maintainers via coordinated disclosure. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update to iCagenda 4.0.8 (or 3.9.15 for the legacy branch); compensating control of a WAF rule blocking executable extensions on iCagenda's attachment endpoint.
prevention.adequacy: Two parallel supported branches (3.x and 4.x) mean patch coverage is incomplete unless both are tracked, which most inventories miss.

detection.what_would_have_worked: File-integrity monitoring on the iCagenda attachment directory and alerting on executable-extension uploads.
detection.adequacy: Requires FIM tooling most small Joomla deployments don't run.

response.what_would_have_worked: Remove planted webshells, audit for newly created admin accounts, rotate secrets, then patch.
response.adequacy: Standard but contingent on the operator noticing the compromise before secondary persistence is established.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: iCagenda shipped fixes across both the 3.x and 4.x branches, but sites running either legacy or current branches remained exploitable until each was individually patched.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Secure-by-default configuration for the CMS platform doesn't extend to an unauthenticated file-attachment feature bundled inside a calendaring extension.

CVE-2026-56290.name: Joomlack Page Builder Improper Access Control Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker scrapes a CSRF token from a public page, then uses it to call Page Builder CK's front-end upload endpoint with an arbitrary destination folder and a PHP payload, achieving RCE without any real authentication check.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; CSRF token is publicly readable, not a real credential)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoC and Exploit-DB module automate the token-scrape-then-upload flow
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported via coordinated disclosure to Joomlack. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update to Page Builder CK 3.6.0 (or the 3.1.1 / 3.4.10 back-ports for legacy Joomla 3/4); compensating WAF rule blocking uploads of executable extensions to non-standard paths.
prevention.adequacy: Three separate patched releases were needed to cover the supported branch matrix, so a single patch check is insufficient to confirm remediation.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Webshell/malware scanning of the site's file tree, and log monitoring for uploads landing outside the expected media directory.
detection.adequacy: Effective only where file-integrity or malware scanning is already running, which is inconsistent across small Joomla hosts.

response.what_would_have_worked: Remove planted webshells across all writable directories (not just the default upload folder, since the flaw allows arbitrary destination), rotate credentials, then patch.
response.adequacy: The arbitrary-destination nature of the flaw complicates cleanup — a shell could be anywhere under the webroot, not just the usual uploads path.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality controls should prevent an unauthenticated caller from choosing an arbitrary upload destination, but the extension exposed that as normal front-end behavior.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch cadence alone doesn't help sites still on end-of-support Joomla 3/4 branches, which needed separate back-ported fixes (3.1.1 / 3.4.10).

CVE-2026-55255.name: Langflow Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An authenticated low-privilege attacker enumerates flow UUIDs via /api/v1/flows/, then submits a victim's flow ID to /api/v1/responses along with an injected instruction (e.g. "leak api keys"), causing the platform to execute the victim's flow — including any embedded credentials — under the attacker's control.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (any authenticated account on the same Langflow deployment)
attack_vector.complexity: high — AC:H because the attacker needs a valid low-privileged account and to enumerate a target flow ID, though observed operators did this without automation
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Langflow AI via coordinated GitHub Security Advisory disclosure. No AI involvement in discovery documented; the observed in-the-wild abuse uses a prompt-injection payload as its exploitation technique, not an AI-discovered flaw.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update to Langflow 1.9.1+; in multi-tenant deployments, additionally isolate flow execution per-tenant via network segmentation until patched.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the object-ownership check, but the underlying pattern — API keys embedded directly inside executable AI-agent flows — remains a residual exposure to any future authorization bug in the platform.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Anomaly detection on API calls where the flow owner's user ID differs from the requesting account's ID, and alerting on prompt-injection-style strings in flow execution inputs.
detection.adequacy: Requires application-level authorization logging that most AI-agent platforms don't yet emit by default.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate all API keys/secrets embedded in Langflow flows on the affected deployment, audit /api/v1/responses access logs for cross-tenant flow_id usage, and patch.
response.adequacy: Effective but requires the operator to already treat flow-embedded credentials as sensitive enough to rotate broadly, which many self-hosted deployments don't.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement failed at the object level — /api/v1/responses executed any flow ID supplied in the request without verifying the caller owns it.

GDPR-Art32.gap: Security-of-processing obligations are undermined when embedded API keys/credentials inside a 'flow' are exfiltratable by any authenticated user of the same multi-tenant deployment.

CVE-2026-48908.name: JoomShaper SP Page Builder Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker POSTs directly to SP Page Builder's asset.uploadCustomIcon task, which accepts any file type with no session, permission, or CSRF check, then requests the uploaded PHP file to execute code and create a rogue Super User account.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, single HTTP request)
attack_vector.complexity: low — 13+ public PoCs automate the upload-and-execute chain
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — first publicly surfaced by a community member on the JoomShaper forum. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update to SP Page Builder 6.6.2+ and independently verify the IconsTrait.php code path is closed, since community reports flag the official patch as potentially incomplete.
prevention.adequacy: Patch-and-forget is insufficient here — the vendor's own forum contains a post-patch report that the flaw may persist, so remediation needs independent verification.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Log monitoring for unauthenticated requests to task=asset.uploadCustomIcon and alerting on new Super User account creation.
detection.adequacy: Straightforward to implement but requires the operator to already log and review Joomla admin-account changes, which is inconsistent in practice.

response.what_would_have_worked: Remove planted webshells, revoke any rogue Super User accounts created during the exposure window, rotate all credentials, and re-verify the patch closes IconsTrait.php.
response.adequacy: The rogue-admin-account chaining means standard webshell cleanup is insufficient without an account audit.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: JoomShaper's 6.6.2 patch was reported by a follow-up forum thread as still incomplete (IconsTrait.php not fully patched), meaning flaw-remediation verification needs to go beyond 'version >= 6.6.2'.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical vulnerability management requires confirming remediation efficacy, not just patch application — relevant here given the reported incomplete fix.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted request to ColdFusion's RDS FILEIO endpoint (/CFIDE/main/ide.cfm?ACTION=FILEIO) with a path-traversal payload in the FILE parameter, writing an arbitrary file (e.g. a .cfm webshell) into the webroot for RCE, or reading sensitive files as reconnaissance.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, single crafted HTTP request)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the public technical write-up documents the exact payload; scope-changed CVSS vector confirms no auth barrier
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Adobe via coordinated disclosure and fixed in APSB26-68. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the APSB26-68 update; disable/block RDS endpoints (/CFIDE/main/ide.cfm) at the network boundary in production, since RDS is a development feature that should never be internet-reachable.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone is necessary but not sufficient — RDS being reachable from the internet at all is a boundary-protection failure independent of this specific CVE, and the pattern will likely recur in the RDS component.

detection.what_would_have_worked: WAF/IDS signatures for ACTION=FILEIO with traversal sequences, and file-integrity monitoring on the ColdFusion webroot for unexpected new files.
detection.adequacy: Effective and reportedly how KEVIntel honeypots caught the earliest exploitation attempts, but requires monitoring infrastructure many ColdFusion operators lack.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate affected servers, hunt for planted .cfm/.jsp webshells anywhere under the webroot, rotate all credentials/keys accessible to the ColdFusion service account, and apply APSB26-68.
response.adequacy: Arbitrary file write means the search for planted files must cover the full filesystem the ColdFusion process can reach, not just the default webroot.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: RDS (Remote Development Services) endpoints like /CFIDE/main/ide.cfm are development tooling that should be blocked at the network boundary in production, not internet-reachable.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Adobe's patch existed before the exploitation wave, but the ~24-hour gap between WatchTowr's public technical analysis and CISA's KEV addition shows flaw-remediation timelines can't outrun a public write-up that doubles as an exploitation guide.

attack_vector.description: An attacker who has already obtained an unpatched Craft installation's application security key (often via a chained separate flaw) uses it to trigger code injection through Craft's database-backup path handling, achieving remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (requires prior possession of the site's compromised security key, not a normal user account)
attack_vector.complexity: high — AC:H reflects the precondition that the security key must already be leaked through some other vector before this flaw is reachable
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Craft CMS (Pixel & Tonic) via coordinated disclosure. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update to Craft 4.13.8 / 5.5.8+ AND rotate the security key regardless of patch status, since a previously leaked key remains dangerous even after patching unless rotated.
prevention.adequacy: Patching alone is insufficient — Craft's own advisory explicitly calls out key rotation as a required companion step, which many operators skip if they only track CVE/patch status.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitoring for anomalous database-backup/restore operations and unexpected outbound connections from the Craft application process.
detection.adequacy: Requires application-level behavioral monitoring that most Craft CMS deployments (often small-to-mid marketing sites) don't run.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate the security key immediately, audit for webshells/backdoors installed via the injection path, and patch to 4.13.8/5.5.8.
response.adequacy: Effective, but requires the operator to already suspect key compromise, which the lack of public in-the-wild reporting makes hard to detect proactively.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Patch alone (4.13.8 / 5.5.8) doesn't remediate an already-compromised security key from before the update — rotation is a separate required action the patch cannot force.

GDPR-Art32.gap: Security-of-processing is directly undermined when a leaked application security key enables remote code execution against a system that may process personal data.

CVE-2025-0108.name: Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS Authentication Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker with network access to the PAN-OS management web interface exploits an nginx/Apache path-confusion bug to invoke restricted PHP scripts without authenticating, then commonly chains the bypass with a separate command-injection flaw (CVE-2024-9474) to reach full remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to the management interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a public PoC automates the path-confusion request; the only precondition is network reachability of the management plane
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported through Palo Alto Networks PSIRT and independently detailed by SL Cyber researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Patch to the fixed PAN-OS maintenance release AND restrict the management interface to trusted internal IPs per vendor best practice, which Palo Alto explicitly recommends as a pre-patch mitigation
prevention.adequacy: Patching alone is insufficient if the management interface stays internet-exposed against vendor guidance; network isolation is the primary control and patching is the backstop

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unauthenticated requests to management-interface PHP endpoints and on mass internet-wide scanning signatures
detection.adequacy: Requires management-plane traffic visibility that many organizations don't instrument separately from data-plane traffic

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate admin credentials and audit for follow-on CVE-2024-9474 command-injection indicators if any management-interface access is suspected
response.adequacy: Response is only as fast as detection; without management-plane logging, compromise can go unnoticed until the chained RCE stage

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection guidance recommends restricting the management interface to trusted internal IPs, but this is advisory in PAN-OS deployments — it doesn't enforce network isolation by default, leaving internet-exposed interfaces reachable pre-patch.

CVE-2024-53704.name: SonicWall SonicOS SSLVPN Improper Authentication Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A remote, unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted session cookie to the SonicOS SSLVPN authentication endpoint, exploiting improper session validation to hijack an already-authenticated user's active VPN session and reach internal network resources.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to the SSLVPN portal)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a public PoC with a documented crafted-cookie technique is available
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reverse-engineered and reported by Bishop Fox after SonicWall's own PSIRT disclosure. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Patch to a fixed SonicOS release and disable SSL VPN internet exposure where not operationally required
prevention.adequacy: Patch-only remediation trailed the exploitation window; roughly 4,500 internet-facing instances stayed unpatched even after a public PoC existed

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for anomalous SSLVPN session-cookie values and unexpected session takeovers (forced logout followed by a new login from a different IP)
detection.adequacy: Most SMB firewall deployments lack SSLVPN session-anomaly monitoring

response.what_would_have_worked: Force-terminate all active SSL VPN sessions and rotate credentials on any account with anomalous session activity
response.adequacy: Effective only if session hijacking is detected quickly; ransomware affiliates moved from hijack to lateral movement within the same operational window

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: Identification/authentication control assumes session-validation logic is sound; here the SSLVPN mechanism itself failed to validate sessions correctly, bypassing IA-2's intent regardless of any MFA configuration.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker sends crafted HTTP requests with directory-traversal sequences to the SimpleHelp toolbox resource handler, downloading server configuration files (including hashed credentials and secrets) that are then used for follow-on ransomware intrusion.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to the SimpleHelp web server)
attack_vector.complexity: low — multiple public PoCs and a Metasploit module automate the traversal request
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by Horizon3.ai's Attack Research team and confirmed by the vendor. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to SimpleHelp 5.5.8+ and isolate the management server from direct internet exposure
prevention.adequacy: Patching closes the traversal path, but any secrets already harvested pre-patch (server config, hashed passwords) still need rotation

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unauthenticated requests containing ../ traversal sequences to the toolbox resource endpoint and on anomalous config-file downloads
detection.adequacy: RMM/remote-support tooling is rarely instrumented for web-request anomaly detection at the level applied to customer-facing apps

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate all credentials referenced in serverconfig.xml and hunt for ransomware-precursor activity (credential reuse, lateral movement) across every downstream customer network the SimpleHelp instance manages
response.adequacy: Blast radius extends to every customer network the compromised RMM server touches, so response scope is much larger than the vulnerable host itself

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement assumed authentication was required before file retrieval; the traversal bug let the toolbox handler serve files outside any access-control check.

CVE-2025-24200.name: Apple iOS and iPadOS Incorrect Authorization Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker with physical possession of a locked iOS/iPadOS device uses specialized forensic-extraction hardware to exploit a state-management flaw and disable USB Restricted Mode, restoring full USB data access to a device that should only permit charging when locked.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none in the software sense, but requires physical possession of the target device plus specialized hardware/tooling
attack_vector.complexity: high — requires physical access, purpose-built forensic hardware, and precise timing/state manipulation; Apple describes the observed use as 'extremely sophisticated'
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Bill Marczak of The Citizen Lab. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Patch to iOS 18.3.1/iPadOS 18.3.1 (or the corresponding fix for older-branch devices); enable auto-update and minimize the window a device sits unpatched
prevention.adequacy: Patching is necessary but not sufficient for high-risk individuals whose devices may be physically seized before an update cycle completes

detection.what_would_have_worked: MDM/EMM monitoring for unexpected USB Restricted Mode state changes or device-lock-state anomalies on managed devices
detection.adequacy: Personal devices belonging to high-risk individuals are frequently unmanaged, so this detection surface doesn't exist for the population most targeted

response.what_would_have_worked: Forensic re-imaging and credential/session revocation for any device suspected of physical tampering, plus threat-intel engagement (e.g., Citizen Lab, Amnesty Security Lab) for suspected targeted-surveillance cases
response.adequacy: Effective response requires specialist digital-forensics capability most individuals and even most enterprises lack in-house

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement for a locked device assumed the USB Restricted Mode state machine was sound; the authorization flaw let a physical attacker force the device back into an accessible state without credentials.

CVE-2024-41710.name: Mitel SIP Phones Argument Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker who already holds administrative access to a Mitel SIP phone's management interface injects shell metacharacters into a boot-process parameter, escaping the intended parameter context to execute arbitrary commands in the phone's underlying OS.
attack_vector.privileges_required: high (authenticated administrator on the phone's management interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a public PoC documents the exact injection point in the boot process
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported through Mitel's own coordinated disclosure program. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Patch to the fixed firmware release and restrict/rotate SIP phone admin credentials so a compromised admin account can't reach the injection point
prevention.adequacy: Patching removes the injection flaw, but doesn't address weak/shared VoIP admin credential hygiene that made the precondition reachable in the first place

detection.what_would_have_worked: Log and alert on SIP phone provisioning/boot-parameter changes containing shell metacharacters
detection.adequacy: VoIP endpoint management traffic is rarely captured by enterprise SIEM pipelines, so this detection is largely absent in practice

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate and re-image affected phones, rotate the compromised admin credential, and audit for lateral movement from the phone's network segment
response.adequacy: VoIP endpoints are often on flat network segments, so containment requires broader network review, not just the phone itself

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege control is undermined because the flaw is reachable by any admin-privileged account — a compromised or over-provisioned VoIP admin credential is sufficient to pivot to full command execution.

CVE-2025-21418.name: Microsoft Windows Ancillary Function Driver for WinSock Heap-Based Buffer Overflow Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local attacker who already has code execution on a Windows host (typically via a prior phishing-delivered payload) sends crafted IOCTL requests to the kernel-mode afd.sys driver, triggering a heap-based buffer overflow to corrupt kernel memory and escalate to SYSTEM privileges.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (a standard authenticated local session is sufficient to reach the vulnerable IOCTL handler)
attack_vector.complexity: high — reliable kernel heap-overflow exploitation requires precise memory-layout control; no public exploit exists, suggesting a sophisticated actor developed it
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified through Microsoft's internal security response process. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the February 2025 cumulative update promptly; this was a zero-day so no pre-patch mitigation existed beyond limiting local code-execution opportunities (phishing/malware defenses)
prevention.adequacy: Patching only helps after release; the real gap during the zero-day window was preventing the initial-access step that made local exploitation possible

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR rules for anomalous kernel-driver IOCTL activity and unexpected SYSTEM-privileged process creation from a standard user session
detection.adequacy: Kernel-level exploitation is specifically designed to operate below most EDR visibility, making this detection unreliable in practice

response.what_would_have_worked: Full re-image of any host where SYSTEM-level compromise is suspected, since kernel-level access can persist below OS-level remediation
response.adequacy: Kernel compromise materially raises the bar for confident remediation short of a full rebuild

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Monthly Patch Tuesday cadence is the fastest routine remediation path, but this flaw was exploited in the wild as a zero-day before the February 2025 patch existed.

CVE-2025-21391.name: Microsoft Windows Storage Link Following Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local attacker with standard user access creates a symbolic link that redirects a privileged Windows Storage file-management operation to an unintended target, allowing them to delete arbitrary files — including security event logs — and escalate privileges.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (a standard authenticated local session is sufficient to stage the link-following condition)
attack_vector.complexity: low — link-following/race-style exploitation of storage operations is a well-understood technique once the vulnerable operation is identified
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified through Microsoft's internal security response process. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the February 2025 cumulative update promptly; this was exploited as a zero-day, so no pre-patch software mitigation existed
prevention.adequacy: Patch-only remediation addresses the flaw but not the log-deletion damage already caused during the zero-day exploitation window

detection.what_would_have_worked: Forward security/event logs to a separate, tamper-resistant log-collection pipeline (e.g. SIEM with write-once storage) so local log deletion doesn't erase the only copy
detection.adequacy: Only effective if remote log forwarding was already configured before the intrusion; many endpoints rely solely on the local event log

response.what_would_have_worked: Treat any host with unexplained gaps in local security logs as a likely privilege-escalation indicator and investigate via centrally-forwarded logs or endpoint telemetry instead
response.adequacy: Effective only where centralized logging exists independent of the compromised host

NIST-800-53-AU-9.gap: Protection-of-audit-information control is directly undermined — the observed exploitation pattern deletes security logs via link-following, defeating log-integrity assumptions.

CVE-2024-40891.name: Zyxel DSL CPE OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker with a Telnet session to a legacy Zyxel DSL CPE device (using default or otherwise-obtained service-account credentials) sends management commands containing embedded shell metacharacters, which are passed unsanitized to the underlying OS shell and executed.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (authenticated Telnet session using a service account such as supervisor/zyuser, often default or trivially obtained on legacy CPE)
attack_vector.complexity: low — no special conditions required beyond reaching the Telnet management port and authenticating
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by VulnCheck security researchers; exploitation activity documented by GreyNoise. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: No vendor patch exists; the only durable prevention is decommissioning the EOL device or firewalling the Telnet management interface off the WAN entirely.
prevention.adequacy: Patch-based remediation is not available at all for this entry — BOD 22-01's remediation deadline can only be met via mitigation/decommissioning, not a vendor patch.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Egress monitoring for anomalous outbound scanning/C2 traffic from consumer/SMB router IP ranges, and Telnet exposure scanning of the managed fleet.
detection.adequacy: Detection is a compensating control only; most affected devices are unmanaged consumer/SMB equipment with no monitoring agent.

response.what_would_have_worked: ISP/operator-driven notification and forced decommissioning or firmware replacement of affected legacy CPE.
response.adequacy: Response is slow and diffuse because affected devices are distributed across many individual subscribers rather than a centrally managed fleet.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation is structurally impossible: Zyxel has declared these DSL CPE devices end-of-life/end-of-service with no patch planned, so the standard remediate-within-SLA control has no path to closure short of hardware replacement.

CVE-2024-40890.name: Zyxel DSL CPE OS Command Injection Vulnerability (CGI)

attack_vector.description: An attacker with an authenticated session sends a crafted HTTP POST request to the CGI management program on a legacy Zyxel DSL CPE device; the CGI program passes request parameters unsanitized to the underlying OS shell, achieving command execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (authenticated HTTP session using a service account such as supervisor/zyuser, often default or trivially obtained on legacy CPE)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted HTTP POST is sufficient once authenticated
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by VulnCheck security researchers; exploitation activity documented by GreyNoise. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: No vendor patch exists; the only durable prevention is decommissioning the EOL device or restricting the CGI management interface from the WAN entirely.
prevention.adequacy: Patch-based remediation is not available at all for this entry — BOD 22-01's remediation deadline can only be met via mitigation/decommissioning, not a vendor patch.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Web-application-firewall or perimeter logging on the CGI endpoint for shell-metacharacter payloads in POST bodies, plus fleet-wide exposure scanning.
detection.adequacy: Detection is a compensating control only; most affected devices are unmanaged consumer/SMB equipment with no monitoring agent.

response.what_would_have_worked: ISP/operator-driven notification and forced decommissioning or firmware replacement of affected legacy CPE.
response.adequacy: Response is slow and diffuse because affected devices are distributed across many individual subscribers rather than a centrally managed fleet.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection is absent by design: the CGI management program is reachable via HTTP POST from the untrusted WAN interface with no network-layer restriction.

attack_vector.description: An attacker with a low-privileged authenticated session to a Cityworks-hosted IIS server submits a crafted serialized object that, upon deserialization, achieves remote code execution on the underlying Windows/IIS host — used in the wild to stage Rust-based loaders that deploy Cobalt Strike/VShell.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (authenticated Cityworks user session)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted request against the deserialization endpoint is sufficient once authenticated
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified via incident response and reported by Trimble/CISA. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply Cityworks 15.8.9 / 23.10 promptly and restrict IIS/Cityworks management access to trusted networks (VPN/allow-list) rather than exposing it directly to the internet.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone is insufficient against a zero-day: the exploitation window opened before the patch existed, so network-layer restriction of the management interface is a necessary compensating control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on anomalous child processes from w3wp.exe and on Rust-compiled loader binaries staging Cobalt Strike/VShell.
detection.adequacy: Effective if deployed, but several victims reportedly reached the post-exploitation staging phase before detection, indicating monitoring maturity gaps.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the affected IIS host, rotate credentials used by the Cityworks service account, and hunt for the Rust-loader/Cobalt Strike/VShell IOCs shared by Trimble/CISA across the fleet.
response.adequacy: Timely response depends on having the Trimble/CISA IOC set pre-loaded into detection tooling, which is not universal across critical-infrastructure operators.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The emergency patches (15.8.9 / 23.10, released Jan 28-29 2025) landed only after in-the-wild zero-day exploitation was already underway, showing standard patch-SLA timelines don't cover a pre-disclosure exploitation window.

CVE-2025-0411.name: 7-Zip Mark of the Web Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A victim opens a spear-phished, homoglyph-spoofed double-encapsulated 7-Zip archive; because 7-Zip fails to propagate the outer archive's Mark-of-the-Web to the inner archive's extracted files, script files (.js/.wsf/.url) run without triggering Windows SmartScreen or Office Protected View, executing attacker code (observed delivering SmokeLoader).
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated remote attacker; requires user interaction to open the crafted archive)
attack_vector.complexity: high — requires social-engineering (homoglyph filename spoofing) plus a specifically-crafted double-encapsulated archive structure
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified in the wild by Trend Micro's Zero Day Initiative Threat Hunting team. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update to 7-Zip 24.09+ and enforce Essential-8-style application hardening blocking direct execution of .js/.wsf/.url files extracted from archives.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone is insufficient without also blocking risky script-file execution, since 7-Zip lacks auto-update and the campaign already ran as a zero-day for months before the fix existed.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Email-gateway detection of nested/double archives and homoglyph filename spoofing; EDR alerting on script interpreters (wscript/cscript) launched from freshly-extracted archive contents.
detection.adequacy: Effective when deployed, but homoglyph spoofing is specifically designed to defeat naive extension-based filtering.

response.what_would_have_worked: Hunt for SmokeLoader C2 indicators and script-interpreter execution chains following archive extraction across the fleet; block identified C2 infrastructure.
response.adequacy: Timely only if IOCs from Trend Micro's published analysis are ingested promptly.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protections that gate on Mark-of-the-Web (SmartScreen prompts, Protected View) are bypassed entirely by the double-archive technique, so endpoint controls relying solely on MotW propagation are insufficient.

CVE-2024-21413.name: Microsoft Outlook Improper Input Validation Vulnerability (MonikerLink)

attack_vector.description: An attacker sends an email containing a crafted 'file://' moniker hyperlink with a '!' new-window parameter; when the recipient clicks it, Outlook bypasses Office Protected View and opens the linked remote document in edit mode, enabling NTLM hash leakage via an attacker SMB share or remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated remote attacker; requires the recipient to click the crafted link)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted email link is sufficient, no additional conditions
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — discovered and reported by Check Point Research. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the February 2024 Outlook security update fleet-wide and block outbound SMB (445) to the internet at the perimeter.
prevention.adequacy: Patch availability preceded KEV addition by a year, showing patch-SLA policy alone did not drive timely remediation across the installed base — boundary controls on outbound SMB provide a necessary independent layer.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Email-gateway detection of moniker-scheme ('file://...!') links in inbound mail, and SIEM alerting on outbound NTLM authentication to non-corporate hosts.
detection.adequacy: Effective when deployed but requires email-gateway rules specifically tuned for the moniker-link pattern, which many default configurations lack.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force NTLM credential rotation for any user who triggered an outbound SMB authentication to an untrusted host, and hunt for follow-on RCE indicators.
response.adequacy: Timely only if outbound SMB/NTLM telemetry is already being collected and alerted on.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection blocking outbound SMB (445) to arbitrary external hosts would have prevented NTLM hash relay/capture even where the client-side Protected View bypass succeeded.

attack_vector.description: An attacker with local access places a malicious 'dal_keepalives.dll' in the application directory of the legitimate, signed mDNSResponder.exe (part of Audinate's Dante Application Library for Windows); when the trusted binary runs, it loads the attacker's DLL and executes code in the context of the trusted process.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none beyond local access to the filesystem (PR:N); requires the user/attacker to trigger execution of mDNSResponder.exe (UI:R)
attack_vector.complexity: low — placing a same-named DLL in the application's search path is sufficient, no additional conditions
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — discovered and reported by Check Point Research (CPR-Zero). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update to Dante Application Library for Windows v1.2.1+ (mDNSResponder.exe v1.3.2) and enforce application-hardening controls restricting DLL loading to trusted system paths.
prevention.adequacy: Patch adoption depends on every downstream OEM that bundles Dante Application Library repackaging and redistributing the fix, so patch alone is not a fast or complete remediation path.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR rules alerting on unsigned/unexpected DLLs loaded by signed binaries (image-load monitoring) for mDNSResponder.exe specifically.
detection.adequacy: Effective when deployed, but broadcast/pro-audio production workstations are frequently outside standard EDR coverage.

response.what_would_have_worked: Remove the malicious DLL, verify integrity of the Dante Application Library installation, and audit for other side-loadable directories on affected hosts.
response.adequacy: Requires awareness that Dante software is present, which is often overlooked in general IT asset inventories.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality/secure-configuration baselines should prevent a signed executable from loading DLLs out of a user-writable application directory; safe DLL search-order hardening wasn't applied.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker submits crafted SQL injection payloads to the CyberoamOS WebAdmin login/authentication endpoint, executing arbitrary SQL statements against the backend database — in observed cases used to create unauthorized administrative accounts on the appliance.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated remote attacker)
attack_vector.complexity: low — direct SQL injection against a network-exposed login endpoint, no special conditions
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to and fixed by Sophos. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the December 2020 hotfix; for appliances that are now EOL/EOS, isolate or decommission the WebAdmin interface from any untrusted network.
prevention.adequacy: The hotfix closed the flaw in 2020, but the product's subsequent EOL/EOS status means operators who never applied it now have no vendor remediation path at all — only isolation or replacement remains.

detection.what_would_have_worked: WAF/IPS signatures for SQL injection patterns on the WebAdmin login endpoint, and monitoring for unexpected new administrative accounts on the appliance.
detection.adequacy: Effective when deployed, but EOL appliances are frequently excluded from modern security-monitoring tooling and asset inventories.

response.what_would_have_worked: Audit all local admin accounts on the appliance for unauthorized additions, rotate credentials, and review WebAdmin access logs for injection payload patterns.
response.adequacy: Requires the appliance to be within scope of routine account/log auditing, which EOL devices often are not.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Unauthenticated SQL injection against the WebAdmin login endpoint bypasses access enforcement entirely at the authentication layer itself, before any access-control policy is evaluated.

CVE-2020-15069.name: Sophos XG Firewall Buffer Overflow Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted HTTP/S request to the XG Firewall's clientless-access User Portal Bookmarks feature, triggering a buffer overflow that yields remote code execution on the firewall appliance itself.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network-reachable via the WAN-exposed User Portal)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted request against a default-enabled portal feature, no user interaction
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified by Sophos's incident-response investigation into active exploitation. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply Hotfix HF062020.1 immediately, or disable/firewall-off the WAN-facing User Portal and clientless VPN bookmark feature when not required.
prevention.adequacy: Patching alone is insufficient for units past end-of-support; Sophos's own required action is to discontinue use if mitigations cannot be applied.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor firewall-originated outbound connections and User Portal access logs for anomalous bookmark-feature requests preceding data exfiltration or ransomware staging.
detection.adequacy: Detection on the boundary device itself is inherently weaker once the device is compromised; external network monitoring is a necessary compensating control.

response.what_would_have_worked: Treat any XG Firewall showing Asnarök/Cring IOCs as fully compromised: rotate all credentials that transited the device, rebuild the appliance from a patched firmware image, and hunt for lateral movement.
response.adequacy: Because the compromised device is the network boundary itself, incident response must assume broader network exposure, not just appliance-local compromise.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: A 2020 hotfix still left units exploitable by ransomware actors in 2025 — patch-management processes did not reach every internet-facing unit within any reasonable SLA.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential Eight patch-applications timelines (days, not years) were not met for a large population of still-exploited XG units.

attack_vector.description: An attacker with physical (or emulated/virtual) access presents a malicious or specially crafted UVC device to a target host; the uvcvideo driver mis-sizes its frame buffer for UVC_VS_UNDEFINED frame types, causing an out-of-bounds kernel write that can be leveraged for privilege escalation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none from software, but physical/proximate access to attach a malicious USB device is required
attack_vector.complexity: low once a malicious device is built — no remote network vector, but requires physical presence or device-seizure access
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — fixed via routine upstream Linux kernel driver hardening. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the kernel patch across all affected branches and Android OEM builds; restrict USB device-class allowlists (e.g. via USBGuard or MDM policy) on devices handling sensitive data.
prevention.adequacy: Patch rollout to the fragmented Android OEM ecosystem is slow; USB device-class restriction is a necessary compensating control for devices that cannot be patched quickly.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor kernel logs for uvcvideo parsing errors or unexpected driver crashes correlated with new USB device attach events, especially on devices in forensic custody.
detection.adequacy: Kernel-level crash telemetry is not commonly collected on consumer/mobile endpoints, limiting detection coverage.

response.what_would_have_worked: Treat any device that was connected to an unknown or untrusted UVC peripheral as potentially compromised; re-image and rotate credentials.
response.adequacy: Physical-access compromise of a kernel driver is difficult to fully remediate short of a full re-flash, since it can affect the trust root of the device.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: The uvcvideo driver auto-loads for any connected UVC-class device; least-functionality controls should restrict which USB device classes are permitted on managed endpoints, especially locked/seized devices handled by third parties.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential Eight patch-OS timelines are typically prioritized by remote exploitability; physical-access LPE bugs used in real device-seizure scenarios need equivalent urgency.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker directly requests a view-controller endpoint (forced browsing) that bypasses OFBiz's view-authorization checks, then abuses the reachable file-import functionality to plant and execute a malicious server-side file for full remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network-reachable)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a crafted HTTP POST against a documented endpoint, no user interaction
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found through manual security research by Rapid7. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to Apache OFBiz 18.12.16 or later; additionally restrict /webtools/control/* to authenticated/internal network access only as defense-in-depth.
prevention.adequacy: Patch-only reliance is weakened by the CVE's own history of three prior bypassed patches; network-level restriction of admin/webtools paths provides a more durable compensating control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unauthenticated requests to /webtools/control/* endpoints and on unexpected file writes under the OFBiz webapp directories.
detection.adequacy: Effective if webtools traffic is otherwise rare/internal-only; less effective on internet-facing instances with legitimate anonymous webtools traffic.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the OFBiz host, review for planted Groovy/FTL files and unauthorized user accounts, and rotate any credentials stored in OFBiz.
response.adequacy: RCE-level compromise requires full forensic review, not just patching, given the near-1.0 EPSS and confirmed mass exploitation.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: The prior three OFBiz authorization patches were each bypassed at the controller/view layer; access-enforcement logic based on view name rather than a default-deny posture repeatedly failed to hold.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Given a near-1.0 EPSS score, Essential Eight's most urgent patch-applications timeline (extreme-risk, days) was necessary but adoption still lagged for many self-hosted OFBiz instances.

attack_vector.description: An attacker sends a crafted HTTP request to a vulnerable .NET Remoting-over-HTTP endpoint to leak a serialized ObjRef URI, then uses that reference to open a raw remoting channel and trigger unsafe deserialization for remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network-reachable)
