prevention.adequacy: Patching addresses the command-injection flaw itself, but the vendor advisory is explicit that the network-exposure configuration is the dominant risk factor, so patching without also restricting management-interface access leaves the door open to related chained flaws.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor management-interface authentication and administrative API logs for anomalous sequences (auth-bypass-style access followed immediately by command execution), and alert on new local admin accounts or outbound connections from the management plane.
detection.adequacy: Firewall management-plane logging is often under-monitored relative to traffic/policy logging, delaying detection of this class of abuse.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the affected firewall's management plane, rebuild/reimage from a patched known-good configuration, rotate all credentials and certificates managed through the device, and hunt for persistence (webshells, new admin accounts) across the fleet.
response.adequacy: Given the KEV ransomware flag, response must assume follow-on lateral movement and ransomware deployment beyond the firewall itself.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection guidance directly addresses this threat: the vendor's own advisory states risk is greatest when the management interface is reachable from the internet or an untrusted network, which is a configuration control independent of patching.

CVE-2024-1212.name: Progress Kemp LoadMaster OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted request to the LoadMaster management web interface, injecting OS commands that execute in the context of the web service; a subsequent sudo-abuse step escalates from the default admin account to root.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to the management interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted HTTP request achieves command execution, no authentication or user interaction needed
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found and disclosed by Rhino Security Labs. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Patch to LMOS 7.2.59.2 / 7.2.54.8 / 7.2.48.10 or later, combined with restricting the management interface to trusted internal networks only.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone is sufficient once applied, but the roughly seven-month window of silent in-the-wild exploitation before KEV listing shows network isolation of the management plane is a necessary compensating control while unpatched.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitoring the LoadMaster management interface for command-injection-pattern requests and unexpected child-process spawning on the appliance.
detection.adequacy: Most LoadMaster deployments lack host-based telemetry on the appliance itself, making this detection layer uncommon in practice.

response.what_would_have_worked: Immediate isolation of the appliance from the network, credential rotation for any downstream systems the LoadMaster had access to, and forensic review for sudo-abuse root escalation artifacts.
response.adequacy: Effective if executed promptly, but detection latency for appliance compromises is typically high, extending attacker dwell time.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection didn't stop exploitation because the LoadMaster management interface was reachable from untrusted networks; restricting management-plane access was vendor guidance, not an enforced default.

CVE-2024-0012.name: Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS Management Interface Authentication Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker with network access to the PAN-OS management web interface gains administrator privileges, then chains CVE-2024-9474 for full root-equivalent compromise of the firewall.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to the management interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the bypass requires only network reachability to the management interface, no credentials or user interaction
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified via Palo Alto Networks PSIRT / Unit42 investigation of active exploitation. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Patch to a fixed PAN-OS release and restrict the management interface to trusted internal IP addresses per Palo Alto's best-practice deployment guide.
prevention.adequacy: Restricting management-interface exposure is a well-documented mitigation, but adoption was inconsistent enough that mass exploitation still occurred against internet-exposed devices.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitoring for unauthenticated configuration changes, new admin accounts, and webshell deployment on PAN-OS management interfaces.
detection.adequacy: Effective when firewall management-plane audit logging is enabled and reviewed, but many operators don't centrally monitor appliance management-plane activity.

response.what_would_have_worked: Immediate firewall isolation, credential rotation, admin-account audit, and forensic review for webshells or configuration tampering following the CVE-2024-9474 chain.
response.adequacy: Effective if executed promptly; ransomware operators known to use this chain move quickly from initial access to network-wide impact.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement on the management plane failed because the interface itself lacked authentication for privileged actions, not because an access policy was misconfigured.

CVE-2024-9465.name: Palo Alto Networks Expedition SQL Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends an SQL-injection payload to the Expedition web application, dumping the database contents including PAN-OS firewall password hashes, usernames, device configurations, and API keys.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to Expedition)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single unauthenticated crafted request extracts sensitive database contents
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found and disclosed by Horizon3.ai. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Patch to Expedition 1.2.96 or later, and avoid exposing Expedition to untrusted networks since it is a migration/configuration tool holding firewall credentials.
prevention.adequacy: Patching closes the injection flaw, but the underlying design issue — a migration tool storing plaintext-recoverable firewall credentials — remains a high-value target requiring its own hardened deployment posture.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Web application firewall rules or logging tuned to detect SQL-injection payload patterns against Expedition endpoints.
detection.adequacy: Effective when WAF coverage extends to internal migration/admin tooling, which is inconsistently applied in practice.

response.what_would_have_worked: Immediate rotation of all firewall credentials and API keys ever stored in the affected Expedition instance, plus review of downstream PAN-OS devices for unauthorized configuration changes.
response.adequacy: Necessary but resource-intensive at scale, since one compromised Expedition instance can expose credentials for many downstream firewalls.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege wasn't enforced at the Expedition database layer, which stored recoverable firewall credentials and API keys reachable via a single unauthenticated SQLi.

CVE-2024-9463.name: Palo Alto Networks Expedition OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted request to the Expedition web application, injecting OS commands that execute as root, exposing every firewall credential and API key the tool ever handled.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to Expedition)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single unauthenticated crafted request achieves root command execution
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found and disclosed by Horizon3.ai. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Patch to Expedition 1.2.96 or later and avoid running Expedition's web service as root, alongside restricting network exposure of the tool.
prevention.adequacy: Patching fixes the injection point, but running the service as root magnifies the blast radius of any future injection-class bug in the same tool.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Logging and alerting on shell-metacharacter patterns and unexpected child-process spawning from the Expedition web service.
detection.adequacy: Effective if host-based monitoring is deployed on the Expedition host, which is inconsistent for internal admin tooling.

response.what_would_have_worked: Immediate rotation of every firewall credential and API key stored in the affected Expedition instance, and audit of downstream PAN-OS devices for unauthorized changes.
response.adequacy: Necessary but resource-intensive since a single compromised Expedition instance can expose credentials for many downstream firewalls.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege wasn't enforced on the Expedition process, which ran the vulnerable code path as root, maximizing the impact of the injection.

CVE-2024-49039.name: Microsoft Windows Task Scheduler Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: After gaining code execution inside a sandboxed AppContainer (e.g. via a browser renderer exploit), an attacker invokes privileged Task Scheduler RPC functions to escape the sandbox and escalate to Medium integrity, with no user interaction required beyond the initial browser compromise.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (code execution inside a sandboxed AppContainer process)
attack_vector.complexity: low once initial sandboxed execution is achieved — the RPC abuse itself requires no further user interaction
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified via ESET's tracking of the RomCom threat-actor campaign. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the November 2024 Patch Tuesday update; keep browsers (especially Firefox, given the CVE-2024-9680 chain) fully patched to prevent the initial sandboxed foothold.
prevention.adequacy: Patching both ends of the chain (browser + OS) is required since either alone leaves the attack path partially open until both vendors ship fixes.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR rules for anomalous RPC calls from AppContainer-sandboxed processes to Task Scheduler service endpoints.
detection.adequacy: Sandbox-escape-specific telemetry is not part of most default EDR rule sets, making this detection layer uncommon.

response.what_would_have_worked: Endpoint isolation, memory/process forensics for RomCom backdoor artifacts, and browser/OS patch verification across the fleet.
response.adequacy: Effective but requires fast triage given the zero-click nature of the chained exploit once a user simply browses to a malicious page.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: Endpoint anomaly detection wasn't tuned to catch AppContainer-to-service RPC escalation, which requires sandbox-escape-specific telemetry most EDR baselines lack.

CVE-2024-43451.name: Microsoft Windows NTLMv2 Hash Disclosure Spoofing Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A phishing email delivers a malicious .url shortcut file; minimal interaction (even right-clicking, deleting, or moving it) forces the victim's machine to initiate an SMB authentication handshake to an attacker server, leaking the user's NTLMv2 hash for pass-the-hash reuse or offline cracking.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated relative to the target; requires only that the victim receive and minimally interact with the file)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the required interaction is far below opening/executing the file, which is what made this an effective zero-day phishing lure
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified via CERT-UA and ClearSky Cyber Security tracking of the UAC-0194 campaign. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the November 2024 Patch Tuesday update; block outbound SMB (445) to the internet at the network boundary; filter .url attachments at the email gateway.
prevention.adequacy: Patching closes the specific hash-leak path, but SMB egress filtering is the more durable compensating control against the broader class of forced-authentication techniques.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alerting on outbound SMB connections to external IPs and on delivery of .url files via email.
detection.adequacy: Effective when egress monitoring and email-gateway file-type filtering are both deployed, which is inconsistent across organizations.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force password/hash resets for potentially exposed accounts and hunt for SparkRAT indicators on endpoints that interacted with the phishing lure.
response.adequacy: Effective if the exposed account set can be scoped quickly; delay increases risk of hash reuse via pass-the-hash before rotation completes.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary/egress filtering didn't block outbound SMB (445) to external IPs, which is what let the forced-authentication hash-leak succeed.

CVE-2021-41277.name: Metabase GeoJSON API Local File Inclusion Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker submits a `file://` URL to Metabase's custom-map GeoJSON API, which loads the target path without scheme or path validation, disclosing arbitrary local files and process environment variables (including cloud credentials).
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to the Metabase API)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single unauthenticated GET request with a `file://` URL achieves file disclosure
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Metabase via coordinated disclosure. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to Metabase 0.40.5/1.40.5 or later; restrict Metabase instances from being directly internet-exposed and place them behind authentication at the network layer.
prevention.adequacy: The patch fully closes the flaw, but the three-year exploitation tail shows patch availability alone doesn't guarantee remediation without active internet-exposure scanning of deployed instances.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitoring Metabase access logs for `file://` scheme usage in the `/api/geojson` endpoint.
detection.adequacy: Effective when centralized logging of self-hosted analytics tools is in place, which is inconsistent for internally-run BI platforms.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate any cloud/database credentials that were ever exposed as environment variables to the Metabase process, and audit for follow-on cloud API abuse.
response.adequacy: Necessary but often overlooked, since operators may not realize environment-variable credentials were reachable via a BI tool's file-read bug.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The roughly three-year gap between the 2021 patch and 2024 KEV listing shows unpatched internet-exposed instances persisted well past any reasonable flaw-remediation SLA.

CVE-2021-26086.name: Atlassian Jira Server and Data Center Path Traversal Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted HTTP GET request with encoded path-traversal sequences at a Jira static-resource endpoint, escaping the intended webapp root to read /WEB-INF/web.xml and potentially other sensitive files.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated remote request)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted HTTP GET request with an encoded traversal sequence, no race conditions or multi-step chaining required.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Atlassian via standard PSIRT disclosure. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to Jira Server/DC 8.5.14, 8.13.6, or 8.16.1+; a WAF/reverse-proxy rule blocking traversal sequences and direct /WEB-INF/ access is an effective compensating control pending patch.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone doesn't retroactively invalidate any configuration secrets already disclosed prior to remediation.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Web-server / WAF logging alerting on requests containing traversal sequences or targeting /WEB-INF/web.xml directly.
detection.adequacy: Effective only if traversal-sequence detection rules are already tuned; encoded variants can evade naive signature matching.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate any credentials or secrets referenced in disclosed configuration files; review access logs for the exploitation window.
response.adequacy: Effective if logs retain sufficient history to identify the exploitation window; short retention periods limit this.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Fixes existed years before the KEV listing; SI-2 SLAs tracking vendor severity rather than active-exploitation status left a MEDIUM-scored bug unpatched until mass exploitation forced attention.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential Eight patch-applications guidance targets prompt remediation for exploited internet-facing services; a years-old MEDIUM CVE quietly returning to active-exploitation status falls outside routine patch-cadence triage.

CVE-2014-2120.name: Cisco Adaptive Security Appliance (ASA) Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker crafts a URL targeting the ASA WebVPN login page with a script-injection payload in an unspecified parameter; a decade after disclosure, actors including AndroxGh0st began chaining it with other Cisco flaws to attempt file upload and backdooring.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, but requires a victim to load the crafted link, or chaining into a broader automated exploitation toolkit)
attack_vector.complexity: low — standard reflected-XSS payload crafting; the nontrivial part is that it was rediscovered as useful within a decade-old, still-deployed appliance.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — originally reported to Cisco PSIRT in 2014; 2024 renewed exploitation surfaced via PSIRT monitoring and third-party (CloudSEK) reporting. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to a fixed ASA software release; decommission EOL ASA hardware still exposing WebVPN to the internet.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone doesn't address appliances that are EOL and no longer receive fixes; those require replacement, not remediation.

detection.what_would_have_worked: WAF/IPS signatures for script-injection payloads on the WebVPN login page, and monitoring for anomalous file-write attempts following login-page requests.
detection.adequacy: Requires signature coverage extended to a decade-old CVE, which most monitoring baselines exclude by default.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the appliance, review logs for chained exploitation (unexpected PHP file writes), and rotate any credentials exposed via the WebVPN portal.
response.adequacy: Effective only if log retention covers the exploitation window on an appliance that may be lightly monitored.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: A 2014 flaw resurfacing a decade later shows patch-verification programs rarely re-check advisories on EOL-adjacent devices; SI-2 needs a KEV-relisting trigger, not just first-disclosure tracking.

UK-CAF-C1.gap: Renewed exploitation of a decade-old, MEDIUM-severity XSS wasn't detected by most operators until Cisco publicly revised its bulletin; CAF C1 security monitoring needs signature coverage extended to old CVEs.

CVE-2024-5910.name: Palo Alto Networks Expedition Missing Authentication Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker with network access to Expedition sends a request to its admin password-reset function, which performs no authentication check, resetting the admin password and gaining access to imported firewall configuration secrets and credentials.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to Expedition)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single request to a missing-authentication function resets the admin password.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed via Palo Alto Networks PSIRT; public exploitation analysis by Horizon3.ai. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade Expedition to 1.2.92+; restrict network access to the Expedition management interface to trusted administrative hosts only.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone doesn't rotate credentials already imported and potentially exposed prior to remediation; a credential-rotation step is also required.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alerting on admin password-reset events not initiated by a known administrator, and on Expedition logins from unfamiliar source IPs.
detection.adequacy: Effective only if Expedition's own audit logging is enabled and monitored, which is not default for a tool often treated as a one-off migration utility.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate all credentials imported into Expedition (firewall admin accounts, API keys) and review firewall configuration-change history for unauthorized modifications.
response.adequacy: Full remediation requires coordinated rotation across every downstream firewall Expedition had access to, not just the Expedition instance itself.

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: Expedition's admin password-reset function lacked identification/authentication entirely; IA-2 controls assumed at the app layer were absent by design.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-handling.gap: Expedition is itself security infrastructure whose compromise cascades to every firewall it manages, raising the bar beyond routine vulnerability-handling timelines.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted request to CyberPanel's /dataBases/upgrademysqlstatus endpoint, bypassing the method-only secMiddleware check and injecting shell metacharacters into the statusfile property to execute arbitrary commands as root; the PSAUX ransomware operation automated this against 22,000+ exposed instances.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated)
attack_vector.complexity: low — one crafted request bypasses the method-only secMiddleware check and injects a shell-metacharacter payload; PSAUX operators fully automated it via ak47.py.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — documented via independent researcher analysis and fixed by the CyberPanel maintainer. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade past the 5b08cd6 fix commit immediately; restrict CyberPanel's admin interface from public internet exposure.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone doesn't recover instances already ransomed before remediation; a decryptor or backup-restore path is separately required.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alerting on requests to /dataBases/upgrademysqlstatus containing shell metacharacters, and on unexpected process execution (ak47.py/actually.sh) from the CyberPanel web process.
detection.adequacy: Effective only where host-based EDR or web-server logging is deployed on the control-panel host, which many low-cost hosting deployments lack.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate compromised hosts, use the LeakIX/Chocapikk decryption tool if the .psaux encryption weakness applies, and rebuild from clean backups otherwise.
response.adequacy: The decryption tool only works against the specific PSAUX encryption weakness; a robust backup strategy remains the reliable recovery path.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: secMiddleware only validated the HTTP method (POST), not caller identity — an AC-3 access-enforcement gap at the exact function performing privileged database operations.

UK-CAF-D1.gap: CAF D1 response-and-recovery planning is what actually determined outcomes for the 22,000 compromised instances — patching alone doesn't undo an existing PSAUX ransomware infection.

attack_vector.description: A malicious or compromised app on the device supplies a Unicode-normalized path to ExternalStorageProvider's shouldHideDocument() filter, bypassing the restricted-directory check to reach files it should not be able to access, escalating local privileges with user interaction required.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (a co-located app with minimal permissions)
attack_vector.complexity: low technically, but requires getting a malicious or compromised app installed and user interaction — the real-world bar is social engineering or a spyware app, not exploit-development difficulty.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — fixed via Google/AOSP internal security review; in-the-wild use surfaced by mobile-security researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the March 2025 Android Security Bulletin patch; restrict install sources to vetted app stores and use mobile threat-defense tooling to block known spyware implants.
prevention.adequacy: Patch delivery depends on OEM/carrier update cadence, which frequently lags the AOSP fix by months for non-Pixel devices.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile EDR/threat-defense monitoring for anomalous content-provider access patterns and known spyware indicators.
detection.adequacy: Most consumer and even enterprise mobile fleets lack this level of on-device monitoring, which is why targeted campaigns went undetected for extended periods.

response.what_would_have_worked: Forensic imaging of suspected-compromised devices, especially for high-risk individuals (journalists, activists), and coordination with mobile-security researchers for spyware identification.
response.adequacy: Requires specialized forensic capability rarely available to the individuals most targeted by this class of campaign.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Bypassing shouldHideDocument() grants an app filesystem reach beyond its declared, least-functionality scope inside the OS content-provider layer.

AU-ISM-1546.gap: The ISM's patch-timeframe control for extreme-risk vulnerabilities is difficult to meet given fragmented Android OEM patch-distribution timelines.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted HTTP request whose traversal sequence escapes the served directory inside the http_verify() authentication code path on a non-chrooted nhttpd instance, spawning a shell and achieving remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated remote request)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted HTTP request combining directory traversal with the server's authentication-check code path yields a shell.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — publicly disclosed by independent researchers in 2019. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the developer-confirmed patch or, given the project's largely unmaintained status, decommission and replace nhttpd with an actively supported web server.
prevention.adequacy: Because the project is effectively abandoned, 'patch' in practice means migration off the software rather than an in-place update.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Network IDS/IPS signatures for traversal sequences in HTTP requests to nhttpd, and outbound-connection monitoring for unexpected reverse shells from the web-server host.
detection.adequacy: Effective only where the host is inventoried and monitored at all; EOL software is frequently forgotten and excluded from monitoring scope.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the compromised host, rebuild from a known-clean image, and migrate off the unmaintained nhttpd software entirely.
response.adequacy: Rebuilding without replacing the vulnerable software leaves the host re-exploitable.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality controls should have flagged an EOL, non-chrooted web server as prohibited software years before the 2024 KEV listing.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: CAF B4 secure configuration explicitly expects removal of unsupported internet-facing software; the real fix here is decommissioning, not patching.

CVE-2024-8957.name: PTZOptics PT30X-SDI/NDI Cameras OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker who can reach the camera's CGI API (directly if authenticated, or unauthenticated by first chaining CVE-2024-8956's weak-authentication bypass) sets the ntp_addr configuration parameter to a value containing shell metacharacters, which execute as OS commands when the ntp_client service starts.
attack_vector.privileges_required: high standalone (CVSS assumes authenticated access to the CGI API) — but effectively none when chained with CVE-2024-8956's authentication bypass, which is how it was exploited in the wild.
attack_vector.complexity: low once chained — a single crafted request to /cgi-bin/param.cgi with a malicious ntp_addr value.
attack_vector.ai_factor: AI-assisted discovery — GreyNoise's Sift LLM flagged anomalous honeypot traffic that led researchers to identify this OS command-injection chain paired with CVE-2024-8956. Defender-side AI discovery of in-the-wild exploitation; no evidence the attacker used AI to develop the exploit.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update camera firmware to 6.3.40+; place NDI/PTZ camera management interfaces on an isolated management VLAN not reachable from the general network or internet.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone doesn't compensate for the broader class of exposed CGI configuration endpoints on IoT/NDI devices; network segmentation remains necessary.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Anomaly-based network monitoring (of the kind GreyNoise's Sift provides) for unusual CGI request patterns against camera management interfaces, since no prior signature existed for this novel chain.
detection.adequacy: Most organizations lack anomaly-based detection at this depth for IoT device traffic; this discovery only happened because GreyNoise runs a large honeypot network with AI-assisted triage.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate and factory-reset affected cameras, rotate any credentials exposed via CVE-2024-8956, and confirm firmware version post-recovery.
response.adequacy: Effective assuming the organization has an inventory of deployed camera devices and firmware versions, which many conferencing/AV deployments lack.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Traditional signature-based malicious-traffic detection missed this novel 0-day chain entirely; only anomaly-based AI detection (GreyNoise Sift) caught it.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Essential Eight application-hardening guidance targets exactly this class of exposed CGI configuration endpoint on network video devices.

CVE-2024-8956.name: PTZOptics PT30X-SDI/NDI Cameras Authentication Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker sends requests to the camera's /cgi-bin/param.cgi endpoint without an HTTP Authorization header; the CGI script fails to enforce authentication and returns or accepts configuration values including credentials.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to the camera's CGI web interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted HTTP request with no Authorization header is sufficient
attack_vector.ai_factor: AI-assisted discovery — GreyNoise's internal LLM tool 'Sift' flagged the anomalous honeypot traffic that led researchers to the flaw. No AI involvement in in-the-wild exploitation is documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Firmware update to 6.3.40+ plus network segmentation keeping camera management interfaces off the open internet
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone is insufficient if cameras remain internet-reachable during the update rollout window across distributed industrial/gov/healthcare fleets.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alerting on unauthenticated requests to /cgi-bin/param.cgi and on config-file write activity outside maintenance windows
detection.adequacy: Most camera deployments lack any logging/SIEM integration, so this detection path is rarely operationalized.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate all camera credentials, isolate affected devices, and audit for unauthorized configuration changes
response.adequacy: Credential rotation is necessary because leaked hashes remain crackable after patching.

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: The device relies on the presence of an HTTP Authorization header rather than server-side session validation; simply omitting the header bypasses identification and authentication entirely.

attack_vector.description: An attacker emails a victim an SVG element containing a crafted, whitespace-padded <animate> attribute; Roundcube's HTML sanitizer fails to strip the payload, so when the victim opens or previews the message, injected JavaScript executes in their authenticated session and can inject a fake login form to harvest credentials.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated attacker; only requires the target to view/preview the email)
attack_vector.complexity: low — one crafted email, no additional interaction beyond normal message preview
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported via standard responsible disclosure to the Roundcube project. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to 1.5.7/1.6.7+; email-gateway stripping of SVG animate/script-bearing content as a compensating control
prevention.adequacy: Patch is fully effective once deployed, but webmail upgrade cycles lag behind the observed phishing exploitation window.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Email-gateway detection of SVG animate payloads and anomalous outbound POSTs following message preview
detection.adequacy: Few mail gateways inspect SVG-embedded script vectors, so detection coverage was inconsistent during active exploitation.

response.what_would_have_worked: Forced session/credential reset for users who previewed suspect messages; phishing-awareness notification
response.adequacy: Credential reset is necessary because the fake-login-form technique captures live credentials, not just session tokens.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code/content filtering at the email gateway rarely sanitized SVG animate attributes, so the payload reached the webmail client unfiltered.

CVE-2024-20481.name: Cisco ASA and FTD Denial-of-Service Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker sends a large number of VPN authentication requests to the RAVPN service, exhausting device resources and causing a denial of service that may require a device reload to fully restore.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to the RAVPN listener)
attack_vector.complexity: low — bulk authentication traffic is sufficient, no crafted exploit payload required
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified by Cisco PSIRT following Talos threat-intel observation. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply Cisco's fixed software release; rate-limit or geofence RAVPN authentication attempts
prevention.adequacy: No workaround existed pre-patch, so exposed devices remained vulnerable to resource-exhaustion DoS until upgraded.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitoring RAVPN authentication-request rate and source-IP reputation (TOR/anonymizer feeds) for spikes
detection.adequacy: Detection alone doesn't prevent the resource exhaustion once the request volume is underway.

response.what_would_have_worked: Block anonymizer-sourced traffic at the perimeter and reload the affected device to restore RAVPN service
response.adequacy: Reload restores availability but doesn't address the underlying resource-exhaustion flaw without the patch.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection assumed the RAVPN authentication path had rate-limiting; resource exhaustion from bulk auth requests bypassed standard perimeter controls.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker registers a rogue device against the fgfmd daemon using a crafted certificate/serial number, bypassing FGFM protocol authentication, then sends crafted management-protocol requests to execute arbitrary code or commands on the FortiManager server.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to the fgfmd/FGFM listener)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the missing authentication check accepts any crafted device-registration request
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by Fortinet PSIRT following observed zero-day exploitation. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the fixed FortiManager release or, pre-patch, enable fgfm-deny-unknown to block unrecognized device registration
prevention.adequacy: Because this was exploited as a zero-day, patching alone couldn't have prevented the earliest attacks — only the interim workaround or network isolation of the FGFM listener could.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alerting on new/unrecognized device registrations against fgfmd and unexpected FGFM connections from unmanaged source IPs
detection.adequacy: Requires FortiManager-specific logging review most organizations don't baseline routinely.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate FortiManager, audit all managed-device registrations, rotate credentials for every FortiGate under its management
response.adequacy: Full response scope is large because FortiManager compromise can extend to every managed FortiGate device.

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: fgfmd accepted device-registration requests presenting a self-signed certificate/serial number without verifying prior trust establishment, defeating device-level authentication entirely.

attack_vector.description: An attacker holding Site Owner-level SharePoint permissions (often obtained via a previously compromised privileged account) submits a crafted request that triggers unsafe .NET deserialization in a SharePoint API endpoint, achieving remote code execution and, in observed incidents, planting a webshell for persistence.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low-to-high (requires Site Owner-level SharePoint permissions, typically obtained through a prior account compromise)
attack_vector.complexity: low once the required SharePoint privilege level is held — a publicly disclosed PoC exists
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — fixed via Microsoft's standard Patch Tuesday process. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the July 2024 SharePoint security update; enforce least-privilege so service accounts don't hold unnecessary Site Owner rights
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone is insufficient without also revoking excess Site Owner privileges from the account used in the observed chain.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on w3wp.exe spawning shells and file-integrity monitoring on SharePoint web-application directories for new .aspx files
detection.adequacy: The documented intrusion evaded detection for two weeks, indicating existing monitoring wasn't tuned for this webshell pattern.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the compromised server, remove the webshell, rotate the compromised privileged account's credentials, and hunt for lateral movement (e.g., Impacket usage)
response.adequacy: Full remediation requires addressing both the SharePoint RCE and the upstream domain-admin account compromise that enabled it.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege wasn't enforced in the documented intrusion — an over-privileged (domain admin) service account reached the vulnerable Site-Owner-level endpoint.

attack_vector.description: An attacker exploits an unspecified remote code execution flaw in a third-party utility bundled with ScienceLogic SL1 to gain unauthorized access to the monitoring platform, in the observed case disrupting monitoring-graph availability and reading customer account/device data.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none documented publicly (CVSS vector indicates unauthenticated network exploitation)
attack_vector.complexity: unknown / low per CVSS AC:L, though the vulnerable component's identity remains undisclosed by the vendor
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified jointly by Rackspace and ScienceLogic during incident investigation. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the fixed SL1 releases (12.1.3+/12.2.3+/12.3+ or the backported 10.x-11.x remediations)
prevention.adequacy: Patching couldn't have prevented the original zero-day exploitation since the flaw was unknown to the vendor at the time of attack.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitoring SL1 portal availability and data-access patterns for anomalies inconsistent with normal monitoring-graph usage
detection.adequacy: The compromise was only noticed via a customer-visible service disruption, indicating detection coverage was reactive rather than proactive.

response.what_would_have_worked: Joint vendor/customer incident response, credential rotation for exposed accounts, and customer notification
response.adequacy: Because encrypted internal credentials were exposed, rotation is necessary even though the encryption may mitigate immediate reuse risk.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Component-inventory and least-functionality practices didn't flag an unreviewed bundled third-party utility as an independent attack surface within SL1.

CVE-2024-40711.name: Veeam Backup and Replication Deserialization Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker who has gained network access (commonly via compromised VPN credentials) sends a crafted request to the Veeam.Backup.MountService on TCP/8000 that triggers deserialization of untrusted .NET objects, achieving remote code execution on the backup server; ransomware affiliates chained this with local-account creation and rclone-based exfiltration before deploying ransomware.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none-to-low against the Veeam service itself, though observed campaigns first used compromised VPN credentials for network access
attack_vector.complexity: low — a public exploit chain exists and was rapidly weaponized by multiple ransomware affiliates
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported via responsible disclosure by watchTowr Labs. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply Veeam 12.2+; restrict network reachability of the Veeam backend service to trusted management hosts only
prevention.adequacy: Patch is fully effective, but backup infrastructure is frequently deprioritized in patch cadence relative to internet-facing systems, despite being a prime ransomware target.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alerting on Veeam.Backup.MountService.exe spawning shell processes, new local-admin account creation, and rclone execution
detection.adequacy: Detection is effective if EDR is deployed on the backup server itself, which is sometimes excluded from standard endpoint tooling.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the Veeam server, remove the rogue local account, rotate all backup-service credentials, and verify backup integrity before any restore
response.adequacy: Full response must also address the initial VPN-credential compromise that gave the attacker network access in the first place.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Backup servers are frequently granted broad domain-trust/administrative reach; least-privilege segmentation wasn't enforced, letting a single RCE cascade into domain and backup-destruction access.

attack_vector.description: A crafted web page triggers a use-after-free in Firefox's Animation timeline handling inside the content process; RomCom operators chained this with a separate Windows Task Scheduler privilege-escalation zero-day (CVE-2024-49039) so a mere page visit resulted in sandbox-escaped code execution and silent backdoor install.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated remote attacker; victim only needs to browse to the malicious page)
attack_vector.complexity: low for the victim (zero-click after landing on the page) but high for the attacker to develop — a genuine content-process UAF plus a kernel/OS sandbox-escape zero-day chained together
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found and reported by ESET researchers tracking the RomCom threat actor. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Emergency patch to Firefox 131.0.2/ESR within the vendor's ~24-hour turnaround, plus site-isolation/sandbox hardening as defense-in-depth against the paired OS-level escape
prevention.adequacy: Patch-only prevention is insufficient against a zero-day actively exploited before public disclosure; sandbox integrity depends on a second, independently-patched OS flaw

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on content-process crash immediately followed by privileged process creation with no user interaction, and outbound connections to newly-registered exploit-hosting domains
detection.adequacy: No prior signature existed for a first-seen exploit chain; behavioral detection of the crash-then-escalate pattern is the only viable signal

response.what_would_have_worked: Endpoint isolation and backdoor-artifact hunting (RomCom implant indicators) for any host that browsed flagged domains in the exposure window
response.adequacy: Incident response for a zero-click chain requires retroactive browsing-history correlation since the victim has no visible compromise indicator

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Standard flaw-remediation SLAs assume disclosed vulnerabilities, not zero-days actively weaponized in a drive-by chain before a patch exists.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Security monitoring tuned to known-bad indicators misses a first-seen exploit chain with no prior signature.

CVE-2024-30088.name: Microsoft Windows Kernel TOCTOU Race Condition Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local attacker who already has code execution wins a timing race in the Windows kernel's NtQueryInformationToken handling — the token is validated (check) and then used after it may have changed (use) — to escalate from a standard user to SYSTEM; APT34 leveraged this for post-compromise privilege escalation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (requires existing local code execution as a standard user)
attack_vector.complexity: high — reliably winning a kernel-level timing race requires exploit engineering, though public PoCs have since automated it
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported through Microsoft's standard channel; exploitation attributed to APT34 (OilRig). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: June 2024 cumulative update deployed within CISA's KEV remediation window, combined with reboot-enforced patch compliance for kernel updates
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone doesn't address the four-month gap between fix availability (June) and confirmed active nation-state exploitation reported later — organizations that deferred the reboot remained exposed

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR rules for anomalous SYSTEM-level token/privilege changes on hosts with no corresponding legitimate elevation event
detection.adequacy: Requires kernel-level telemetry most EDR agents don't expose by default, so most environments have no native detection for this class of race condition

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate and forensically image any host showing unexplained SYSTEM-privilege processes; hunt for OilRig TTPs (webshells, credential dumping tools) post-escalation
response.adequacy: Local privilege escalation is typically a late-stage indicator, meaning initial access already succeeded by the time this is detected

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Patch-SLA windows (typically 30 days for high severity) exceed the observed exploitation timeline; this flaw was already being used by a nation-state actor before the CISA KEV due date.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch Operating Systems (48-hour extreme-risk target) is frequently missed for kernel updates that require coordinated reboot scheduling.

CVE-2024-28987.name: SolarWinds Web Help Desk Hardcoded Credential Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: SolarWinds Web Help Desk ships with a developer login credential hardcoded directly into application code rather than pulled from configuration; any remote attacker who knows the credential (recovered via decompilation, as Horizon3.ai did) can authenticate and read or modify all help-desk ticket data, including tickets carrying reset passwords.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated remote attacker armed with the recovered hardcoded credential)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the credential is static and reusable across every unpatched deployment; a public PoC automates the whole flow
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by Horizon3.ai human researchers during manual code analysis. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the 12.8.3 Hotfix 2 patch that removes the hardcoded credential path, plus network-level restriction of WHD admin interfaces to trusted internal ranges
prevention.adequacy: Patching removes the specific credential, but any product architecture that embeds authentication material in shipped code needs a supply-chain-level control, not just a point patch

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on WHD authentication events tied to the internal developer account class, and on bulk ticket API access with no prior session context
detection.adequacy: Most WHD deployments don't log at the granularity needed to distinguish the hardcoded developer login from legitimate admin activity

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate every credential that ever appeared in a WHD ticket (password resets, shared service accounts) and audit ticket access logs for the exposure window
response.adequacy: Because ticket data itself often contains secrets, response requires a full credential-rotation sweep, not just patching the application

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: Identification and authentication requirements are bypassed entirely by a credential the vendor embedded in the product itself, outside any operator-managed identity control.

NIST-800-63B-rev4.gap: Authenticator-management guidance assumes operator-issued credentials that can be rotated; a compiled-in credential cannot be rotated by the operator at all.

CVE-2024-9380.name: Ivanti Cloud Services Appliance (CSA) OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker with admin access to the Ivanti CSA administrative web console — obtained directly or by chaining a separate CSA authentication-bypass flaw — injects OS commands through the console that are passed unsanitized to the underlying operating system, yielding full remote code execution on the appliance.
attack_vector.privileges_required: high on paper (authenticated admin), but low in practice once chained with the paired CSA auth-bypass vulnerability used in the same campaign
attack_vector.complexity: low — CISA/FBI documented threat actors chaining this reliably as part of a repeatable multi-CVE intrusion set
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — surfaced by Ivanti during incident response on compromised CSA 4.6 customers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to CSA 5.0.2+ and decommission the end-of-life CSA 4.6.x branch entirely, since no patch exists for it
prevention.adequacy: Patching alone is insufficient for the EOL 4.6.x branch — the compliant action is full decommission, which many affected organizations had not completed

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor CSA admin-console access and outbound traffic for webshell-drop and chained-exploitation patterns described in CISA/FBI AA25-022a
detection.adequacy: Appliance-level logging on CSA is limited, and the chained multi-CVE attack path wasn't publicly documented until the joint advisory

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the appliance, hunt for implanted webshells and harvested credentials, and rotate all credentials the CSA appliance had access to
response.adequacy: Because the chain grants full RCE, response must assume complete appliance compromise and treat any credentials it held as burned

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: CSA 4.6.x is past end-of-life with no vendor patch available for this branch, so flaw-remediation controls have no compliant remediation path short of migration.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch Applications guidance assumes a supported product; CSA 4.6.x's EOL status means the compliant action is decommission, not patch.

CVE-2024-9379.name: Ivanti Cloud Services Appliance (CSA) SQL Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker with admin access to the Ivanti CSA administrative web console — obtained directly or by chaining a separate CSA authentication-bypass flaw — injects arbitrary SQL statements through the console, allowing extraction or modification of appliance database contents including stored credentials.
attack_vector.privileges_required: high on paper (authenticated admin), but low in practice once chained with the paired CSA auth-bypass vulnerability used in the same campaign
attack_vector.complexity: low — CISA/FBI documented threat actors chaining this reliably alongside CVE-2024-9380 in a repeatable multi-CVE intrusion set
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — surfaced by Ivanti during incident response on compromised CSA 4.6 customers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to CSA 5.0.2+ and decommission the end-of-life CSA 4.6.x branch entirely, since no patch exists for it
prevention.adequacy: Patching alone is insufficient for the EOL 4.6.x branch — the compliant action is full decommission

detection.what_would_have_worked: Database-layer query monitoring on the CSA appliance for injection patterns correlated with admin-console access from unexpected sources
detection.adequacy: Appliance-level database logging is limited by design, and the chained attack path wasn't publicly documented until the joint CISA/FBI advisory

response.what_would_have_worked: Assume database contents (including stored credentials) are compromised, rotate them, and hunt for the same threat-actor TTPs described in AA25-022a
response.adequacy: SQL injection against a credential-storing appliance requires a full credential-rotation response, not just patching

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: CSA 4.6.x is past end-of-life with no vendor patch available, leaving no compliant remediation path other than decommission.

PCI-DSS-4.0-6.3.3.gap: Public-facing application patching requirements don't cover an appliance whose vulnerable branch has no vendor-supplied fix.

CVE-2024-23113.name: Fortinet Multiple Products Format String Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends crafted packets to the FGFM (FortiGate-to-FortiManager) management service on TCP/541 that contain externally-controlled format-string specifiers, corrupting memory in the fgfmd daemon to achieve remote code execution or crash the service.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated remote attacker on the FGFM management port)
attack_vector.complexity: low — multiple public PoCs and nuclei detection templates exist, and FortiGuard confirmed real-world exploitation
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported and disclosed through Fortinet PSIRT, with independent research from watchtowr Labs. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to FortiOS 7.0.14/7.2.7/7.4.3 or later, or restrict/disable FGFM access from untrusted interfaces as an interim compensating control
prevention.adequacy: Patch-SLA timelines for appliance firmware are slower than the observed exploitation window; the interim FGFM-disable workaround is necessary but often skipped since it's not the default guidance

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for FGFM (TCP/541) connections from unrecognized peers and for fgfmd crash/restart events indicating exploitation attempts
detection.adequacy: Appliance management-plane logging is often excluded from SIEM ingestion, leaving this traffic under-monitored relative to data-plane firewall logs

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the affected FortiGate/FortiProxy device, assume full compromise of stored credentials and configuration, and rebuild from a known-good firmware image
response.adequacy: Because this is unauthenticated preauth RCE on the management plane, response must assume complete device takeover rather than partial compromise

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary-protection assumptions fail when the vulnerable service (FGFM) is itself part of the management/boundary plane and reachable without authentication.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch Applications extreme-risk timelines (48 hours) are routinely missed for appliance firmware requiring scheduled maintenance windows.

CVE-2024-43573.name: Microsoft Windows MSHTML Platform Spoofing Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker convinces a victim to open a crafted file that abuses the Windows MSHTML platform to spoof its true content/rendering context, following the same technique family Void Banshee used with CVE-2024-38112/CVE-2024-43461 to disguise malware droppers as benign documents and deliver the Atlantida infostealer.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated remote attacker; requires the victim to open a malicious file)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the technique is a refinement of a previously-abused MSHTML spoofing pattern with an established delivery playbook
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed through Microsoft's standard security-update process. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the October 2024 cumulative update, plus disabling/restricting the legacy MSHTML/Internet Explorer rendering engine where not business-required
prevention.adequacy: Patching this single CVE doesn't retire the broader legacy-MSHTML attack surface that has now produced three related spoofing CVEs abused by the same threat actor

detection.what_would_have_worked: Detect execution of the deprecated MSHTML/IE rendering engine triggered by non-browser file types, and flag infostealer-consistent network activity post-execution
detection.adequacy: Most environments don't specifically monitor for legacy-engine invocation, since it's expected to be effectively unused

response.what_would_have_worked: Assume infostealer compromise (credential theft) on any host that opened a flagged file; rotate credentials and hunt for Atlantida-stealer indicators
response.adequacy: Because the payload is an infostealer, response must include full credential rotation, not just malware removal

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection tuned to known file signatures misses a spoofing technique whose payload is only revealed after the deprecated MSHTML engine renders it deceptively.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application hardening that doesn't disable/restrict the legacy MSHTML engine leaves this repeatedly-abused spoofing surface exposed.

CVE-2024-43572.name: Microsoft Windows Management Console Remote Code Execution Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker crafts a malicious .msc console file referencing a vulnerable ActiveX control (apds.dll) via the file's StringTable section, smuggling in an old XSS flaw that executes arbitrary JScript inside mmc.exe when the victim opens the file — the 'GrimResource' technique.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated delivery); requires the victim to open the malicious .msc file
attack_vector.complexity: low — the technique was fully documented publicly and a crafted file requires no further victim interaction beyond opening it
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found and disclosed by Elastic Security Labs threat researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: October 2024 Windows security update plus blocking/quarantining .msc file attachments at the email/web gateway
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone doesn't help against the roughly four-month zero-day window before the fix shipped; file-type blocking at the perimeter was the only viable interim control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on mmc.exe spawning script hosts or unexpected child processes, and detonation-sandbox inspection of unsolicited .msc attachments
detection.adequacy: Behavioral detection of anomalous mmc.exe activity was the primary way defenders caught this before Microsoft's fix.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, hunt for GrimResource-pattern .msc samples across the fleet, and rotate any credentials cached on the affected endpoint
response.adequacy: Because initial access frequently led to stealer payloads, credential rotation is a necessary post-incident step, not merely host cleanup.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality/least-privilege baselines rarely block execution of attacker-supplied .msc files by default prior to this patch — MMC did not treat untrusted console files as high-risk content.

attack_vector.description: A use-after-free in Qualcomm DSP Services mismanaging HLOS memory maps is exploited locally on an Android device for privilege escalation, chained after physical/forensic-tool access (e.g. Cellebrite UFED) to install spyware.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local code execution with limited privileges required as a starting point)
attack_vector.complexity: high — requires a working local exploit chain and, in observed cases, prior physical device access via forensic-extraction tooling
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by Seth Jenkins (Google Project Zero) and Conghui Wang (Amnesty International Security Lab). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the October 2024 Qualcomm-based OEM security update as soon as available; restrict physical custody/forensic access to devices issued to high-risk personnel
prevention.adequacy: OEM patch propagation lag for Android chipset firmware routinely runs months behind the Qualcomm bulletin, leaving high-risk users exposed regardless of policy.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile threat-detection tooling (e.g. Mobile Verification Toolkit) scanning for known spyware artifacts and anomalous DSP-service crash logs
detection.adequacy: Detection tools for this class of spyware exist but are not standard endpoint-protection coverage for most organizations.

response.what_would_have_worked: Forensic imaging and analysis (Amnesty/Citizen Lab-style) of any device with unexplained custody gaps, followed by full factory reset and credential rotation
response.adequacy: Only specialized digital-forensics response, not routine IR, reliably confirms and remediates this class of compromise.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: OEM/carrier patch propagation for Android chipset firmware routinely lags the Qualcomm bulletin by months, leaving a long exploitation window for exactly this class of targeted attack.

CVE-2024-45519.name: Synacor Zimbra Collaboration Suite (ZCS) Command Execution Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Zimbra's postjournal service parses SMTP CC-header content and executes base64-decoded shell commands via `sh` without authentication, letting attackers send a crafted email to drop a webshell on the mail server.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, remote via crafted SMTP traffic)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoC exists and mass-exploitation followed within days of disclosure
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — vulnerability and PoC published by the ProjectDiscovery research team; mass-exploitation tracked by Proofpoint. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the 2024-09-04 Zimbra patches (8.8.15p46 / 9.0.0p41 / 10.0.9 / 10.1.1) before the 2024-09-28 mass-exploitation wave began
prevention.adequacy: A roughly 3.5-week gap between patch and mass exploitation was enough time to patch, but most self-hosted deployments missed it; perimeter mail filtering was the only compensating control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mail-gateway inspection for base64-blob CC headers and file-integrity monitoring on the Zimbra webapp directories for unexpected .jsp files
detection.adequacy: Most Zimbra deployments lack file-integrity monitoring on webapp directories, so webshell drops went undetected until active abuse.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the mail server, hunt for unauthorized .jsp files under the Zimbra webapp tree, and rotate all mailbox and admin credentials
response.adequacy: Because postjournal runs with mailbox-server privileges, a full credential rotation and mailbox-access audit is required, not just webshell removal.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Zimbra's official fix shipped 2024-09-04 but mass exploitation began 2024-09-28 — a roughly 3.5-week patch window closed by attackers well before most self-hosted deployments applied it.

CVE-2024-29824.name: Ivanti Endpoint Manager (EPM) SQL Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker on the same network sends a crafted goodApp.md5 value to the EPM Core server's /WSStatusEvents/EventHandler endpoint, exploiting SQL injection in RecordGoodApp (PatchBiz.dll) to reach xp_cmdshell and execute arbitrary commands.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, but requires network adjacency to the EPM Core server)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a public working PoC blindly automates the injection-to-RCE chain
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported via Trend Micro's Zero Day Initiative by an anonymous researcher; public deep-dive and PoC by Horizon3.ai. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply Ivanti's May 2024 EPM patch and segment EPM Core servers away from general user/endpoint network segments
prevention.adequacy: Patch availability alone did not stop exploitation; network segmentation limiting who can reach the EPM Core server on the adjacent network was the necessary compensating control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Database-activity monitoring for xp_cmdshell invocations and WAF/IDS signatures on the /WSStatusEvents/EventHandler endpoint
detection.adequacy: Few EPM deployments monitor xp_cmdshell usage, so exploitation typically surfaced only after follow-on lateral movement was noticed.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the EPM Core server, audit for newly created accounts/scheduled tasks pushed via EPM's endpoint-management channel, and rotate the SQL service account credentials
response.adequacy: Because EPM has privileged reach into every managed endpoint, response must include auditing all managed clients for pushed payloads, not just the Core server itself.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Ivanti's fix shipped in May 2024 but active exploitation was still being observed and added to KEV in October 2024 — a five-month unpatched-fleet exposure window well beyond a reasonable SLA.

CVE-2023-25280.name: D-Link DIR-820 Router OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A remote, unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted request to the router's ping.ccp CGI handler with shell metacharacters embedded in the ping_addr parameter, achieving root command execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, remote)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a public GitHub PoC directly demonstrates the injection
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — publicly disclosed by an independent researcher via a GitHub proof-of-concept. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: No vendor patch exists; the only real prevention is decommissioning/replacing the end-of-life router
prevention.adequacy: CISA's own required action for this entry is 'discontinue use' rather than patch — the device cannot be secured in place.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Network-edge IDS signatures for shell-metacharacter payloads in ping_addr/CGI requests to consumer router management interfaces
detection.adequacy: Home/small-office networks running EoL routers typically have no IDS coverage at all, so this class of attack usually goes undetected until the device joins a botnet.

response.what_would_have_worked: Factory-reset and replace the device; if replacement isn't immediate, isolate it from the internet-facing interface
response.adequacy: Because there is no patch, 'response' here is really decommissioning — any factory reset without replacement leaves the same exploitable firmware in place.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The product is end-of-life with no vendor patch — flaw remediation as a control is structurally unavailable, and CISA's required action is decommissioning rather than patching.

CVE-2020-15415.name: DrayTek Multiple Vigor Routers OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker uploads a crafted configuration file with shell metacharacters in the filename to cgi-bin/mainfunction.cgi/cvmcfgupload using a text/x-python-script content type, achieving command execution as root.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, remote)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a public GitHub PoC directly demonstrates the injection
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — publicly disclosed by the CLP-team researcher group. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply DrayTek firmware 1.5.1 or later and disable/restrict WAN-facing access to the router's administrative CGI endpoints
prevention.adequacy: The fix has existed since 2020; four years later devices are still being exploited, showing patch availability without enforcement is insufficient.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Edge IDS/IPS signatures for text/x-python-script uploads with shell metacharacters to cvmcfgupload, and outbound-connection monitoring for botnet C2 beaconing from router management IPs
detection.adequacy: SMB deployments running these routers rarely monitor router-originated outbound traffic, so compromise is usually detected only via downstream abuse (e.g. spam, DDoS participation).

response.what_would_have_worked: Factory-reset and re-flash to the patched firmware, then restrict the administrative interface to a trusted management VLAN
response.adequacy: Simply patching without restricting WAN exposure to the CGI endpoint leaves the device reachable for the next command-injection variant.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The vendor fix has existed since 2020, yet KEV addition in 2024 shows a four-year unpatched-device population still being actively targeted — patch-management enforcement, not availability, is the gap.

CVE-2019-0344.name: SAP Commerce Cloud Deserialization of Untrusted Data Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: SAP Commerce Cloud's virtualjdbc extension exposes Java serialization over HTTP/RMI/JMXMP; an attacker sends a crafted gadget-chain serialized payload directly to the exposed listener to achieve remote code execution as the 'Hybris' OS user.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, remote, network access to the exposed listener)
attack_vector.complexity: low — generic ysoserial-style gadget chains work without protocol-specific knowledge
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed and patched by SAP's Product Security Response Team in 2019. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply SAP Security Note 2786035 (2019) and firewall the virtualjdbc/JMXMP listener from any untrusted network
prevention.adequacy: A patch has existed since 2019; five years later CISA still found active exploitation, showing patch availability alone does not close long-tail exposure in legacy e-commerce deployments.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Network monitoring for Java-serialized payloads on the virtualjdbc/JMXMP port and application-layer WAF rules blocking raw Java object streams
detection.adequacy: Legacy SAP Commerce deployments rarely have deserialization-aware WAF rules, so exploitation of this five-year-old flaw can still go unnoticed.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the Commerce Cloud instance, audit for webshells/scheduled tasks planted via the deserialization RCE, and rotate all Hybris service-account and database credentials
response.adequacy: Because the 'Hybris' OS account typically has broad application-server privileges, response must assume full application compromise, not just the initial RCE foothold.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: SAP shipped a fix in 2019, but KEV addition in 2024 shows five years of unpatched exposure among internet-facing instances — patch-SLA enforcement failed long-term, not just at disclosure.

CVE-2024-7593.name: Ivanti Virtual Traffic Manager Authentication Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker exploits a flaw in vTM's authentication algorithm to bypass the admin-panel login entirely and POST a crafted user-creation request, planting a rogue administrator account they then use to log in with full admin privileges.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated attacker reaching the vTM admin management interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a public PoC script automates the entire bypass-and-account-creation flow
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported through Ivanti's own security disclosure process. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Patching to 22.2R1/22.3R3/22.5R2/22.6R2/22.7R2 or later, plus removing the vTM admin panel from direct public-internet exposure (VPN-only/management-network access)
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone is insufficient without also restricting network reachability of the admin panel — the underlying design let any reachable unauthenticated client attempt the bypass

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alerting on new local-admin account creation events on the vTM appliance and on unauthenticated POST traffic to the LocalUsers management endpoint
detection.adequacy: Most organizations don't forward vTM management-plane audit logs to a SIEM, so the rogue-account creation event is invisible until the attacker logs in and acts

response.what_would_have_worked: Auditing and removing unauthorized local admin accounts, rotating all vTM credentials, and reviewing TrafficScript rules for attacker-planted changes after any suspected compromise
response.adequacy: Because the attacker ends up with legitimate-looking admin credentials, standard login-anomaly detection may miss the intrusion unless account-creation events are specifically audited

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: IA-2 assumes the identification/authentication mechanism itself is sound; CVE-2024-7593 is a flaw IN the authentication algorithm, so identity verification is bypassed regardless of IA-2 policy configuration.

CVE-2024-8963.name: Ivanti Cloud Services Appliance (CSA) Path Traversal Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a specially-encoded URL (a %3F-encoded path segment) against the CSA broker web server, exploiting inconsistent URL-decoding order between the broker and PHP CGI to reach restricted administrative scripts; chained with CVE-2024-8190 this yields authenticated command execution and webshell deployment.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated attacker reaching the CSA broker web interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the exact bypass URL pattern is publicly documented and has been independently reproduced by multiple security vendors
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified and disclosed by Ivanti through its own security research/PSIRT process. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrading to CSA 4.6 Patch 519 or later, or migrating off the End-of-Life CSA 4.6.x line entirely to the supported 5.0.x line
prevention.adequacy: Ivanti itself states CSA 4.6.x is EOL and unlikely to receive further security updates, so 'patch' is a dead end for organizations still on that branch — the only durable fix is migration

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitoring CSA broker web-server access logs for %3F-encoded or otherwise anomalous URL paths, and file-integrity monitoring on /gsb/ and /client/ for unexpected PHP files
detection.adequacy: The URL-encoding trick evades naive traversal detection (no literal '../' sequence), so signature-based WAF rules tuned only for classic traversal strings will miss it

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolating the appliance, hunting for the documented webshell filenames and C2 infrastructure, and treating any CSA 4.6.x instance that was internet-facing during the exploitation window as compromised
response.adequacy: Because attribution points to sophisticated (suspected nation-state) actors using custom implants, standard antivirus/EDR signatures on the appliance itself are unlikely to catch the intrusion

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: SC-7 boundary protection assumes the appliance correctly parses and enforces path restrictions at its network edge; the URL-decoding-order bug let attackers smuggle traversal past the boundary parser entirely.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-handling.gap: NIS2 vulnerability-handling expects prompt remediation, but Ivanti CSA 4.6 was already flagged End-of-Life when the flaw was disclosed, leaving organizations with no vendor-supported patch path other than full migration.

CVE-2026-58644.name: Microsoft SharePoint Deserialization of Untrusted Data Vulnerability (CVE-2026-58644)

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted serialized .NET payload to an internet-facing SharePoint endpoint; unsafe deserialization triggers a gadget chain that executes attacker code as the SharePoint service account, typically followed by a web-shell drop.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network attacker)
attack_vector.complexity: low — network-reachable, no authentication and no user interaction required per the CVSS vector.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — coordinated disclosure via Microsoft MSRC. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the MSRC security update immediately, enable AMSI integration for SharePoint, and restrict inbound access to the SharePoint web front-end.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is authoritative, but the 3-day due date means unpatched internet-facing instances are likely already compromised; assume-breach triage is needed alongside patching.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on w3wp.exe spawning shells, new .aspx files under the web root, and anonymous POSTs carrying serialized objects.
detection.adequacy: Detection catches post-exploitation web shells but not the initial deserialization itself.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate SharePoint machine keys and service credentials, hunt for planted web shells, and rebuild if compromise is confirmed.
response.adequacy: Machine-key rotation is essential because a leaked ValidationKey enables forged payloads even after patching.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The KEV due date is 3 days; a standard 30-day flaw-remediation SLA leaves internet-facing SharePoint exploitable for weeks after weaponization.

CVE-2026-25089.name: Fortinet FortiSandbox OS Command Injection Vulnerability (CVE-2026-25089)

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends crafted HTTP requests with malicious JSON to the FortiSandbox web management UI; input handled by the 'start VNC' feature is later executed as an OS command (a second-order injection), yielding command execution on the appliance.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network-reachable web UI)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted HTTP request reaches the vulnerable handler with no authentication
