attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported via Fortinet PSIRT (FG-IR-26-141). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to FortiSandbox 4.4.9+/5.0.6+ and remove the management interface from internet exposure (management-plane network segmentation, trusted-host restrictions).
prevention.adequacy: Patching closes the flaw, but the exploitation window opened before the KEV listing, so exposure reduction (segmenting the admin plane) is the load-bearing control until every instance is patched.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unauthenticated requests to the FortiSandbox web UI and on unexpected shell/child processes spawned by the web service; egress monitoring from the appliance.
detection.adequacy: Second-order injection means the trigger and execution are separated in time, so request-only signatures may miss it without process-level telemetry.

response.what_would_have_worked: Follow CISA Forensics Triage Requirements: isolate the appliance, capture volatile state, rotate any credentials/secrets it held, and rebuild from a known-good image if compromise is confirmed.
response.adequacy: A compromised security appliance can taint the very telemetry used to detect the intrusion, so out-of-band forensics is required.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: A security appliance whose management UI is internet-reachable defeats boundary protection; the FortiSandbox admin plane must not be exposed to untrusted networks.

CVE-2026-39808.name: Fortinet FortiSandbox OS Command Injection Vulnerability (CVE-2026-39808)

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends crafted HTTP requests to the FortiSandbox management interface; unsanitized arguments reach an OS command, yielding remote code execution on the appliance.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network-reachable management interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — CVSS AC:L, single crafted request, no user interaction
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed via Fortinet PSIRT (FG-IR-26-100). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the FortiSandbox fixed build per FG-IR-26-100 and restrict the management interface to a dedicated admin network/VPN.
prevention.adequacy: Patching alone is insufficient if the management interface remains internet-exposed during the patch window; network isolation is the load-bearing compensating control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unexpected child processes from the appliance web daemon and on anomalous outbound connections from the security appliance.
detection.adequacy: Appliance telemetry is often limited; host-level process visibility on a closed appliance may be unavailable to defenders.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the appliance, capture forensic triage per CISA guidance, rotate any credentials/secrets the appliance held, rebuild from known-good firmware.
response.adequacy: A compromised inspection appliance can taint the very telemetry used to scope the breach; assume full compromise.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The KEV due date (2026-07-19) is three days from listing; a standard 30-day flaw-remediation SLA leaves the appliance exploitable across the entire observed active-exploitation window.

CVE-2026-46817.name: Oracle E-Business Suite Improper Privilege Management Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends crafted HTTP requests to the Oracle Payments File Transmission (ibytransmit) endpoint, abusing missing authentication and improper privilege management to read arbitrary server files and ultimately take over the Payments component.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network attacker over HTTP)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the endpoint is reachable pre-auth and the exploit calls an internal Java function directly with no special conditions
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed through Oracle's May 2026 Critical Patch Update. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Oracle May 2026 CPU for EBS 12.2.x and remove the Payments servlet from internet exposure (reverse-proxy allowlist / WAF virtual patch on the ibytransmit path).
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone closes the flaw but the six-week gap between patch release and first exploitation means unpatched, internet-exposed instances were already at risk; network isolation is needed as a compensating control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unauthenticated requests to ibytransmit and on file-read responses containing /etc/passwd or other OS paths from the EBS host.
detection.adequacy: Detection catches probing but the takeover is fast; without blocking, detection only shortens dwell time.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate exposed EBS instances, rotate Payments and integration credentials, and perform CISA forensic-triage collection per BOD 26-04 guidance.
response.adequacy: Credential rotation is essential post-compromise because Payments takeover can expose downstream financial integration secrets.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The KEV due date (2026-07-18) was three days after listing, against an exploitation window that opened six weeks after the May patch — a standard flaw-remediation SLA is far too slow for an unauthenticated ERP takeover already probed in the wild.

CVE-2023-4346.name: KNX Association KNX Protocol Connection Authorization Option 1 Overly Restrictive Account Lockout Mechanism Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker who can reach the KNX bus over the network (or physically) interfaces with the installation, purges devices that lack additional security options, and sets a BCU key that cannot be reset without the current key, locking legitimate operators out.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network or physical access to the KNX installation)
attack_vector.complexity: low — abuses the documented Connection Authorization / BCU-key workflow, no exploit primitive required
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — coordinated ICS disclosure (CISA ICSA-23-236-01). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Enable KNX Data Secure / IP Secure (the additional security options) on all devices and isolate the KNX/KNXnet-IP bus from untrusted networks
prevention.adequacy: Enabling security options prevents the purge/lock, but devices already provisioned without it must be re-commissioned physically.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitoring for unexpected BCU-key changes and device purges on the KNX bus
detection.adequacy: Most building-automation deployments have no bus-level telemetry, so detection is unlikely before lockout.

response.what_would_have_worked: Physical re-provisioning of locked devices and segmenting the bus off untrusted networks
response.adequacy: Recovery is manual and per-device; no remote reset path exists once the BCU key is set.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security measures for essential entities seldom treat the building-automation/OT bus as in-scope, leaving the KNX segment flat and reachable.

CVE-2026-56155.name: Microsoft Active Directory Federation Services Insufficient Granularity of Access Control Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A low-privileged local principal abuses an over-permissive DKM container ACL in AD FS to access the federation master key or elevate to administrator, enabling token-signing forgery that can be chained with an RCE in ransomware-style intrusions.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (authenticated local access to the AD FS server)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the weakness is a static, insufficiently-granular ACL requiring no race or memory-corruption primitive
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported via Microsoft MSRC as an exploited zero-day. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install the July 2026 AD FS update and set the RemediateDkmAcl registry key to harden the DKM container ACL rather than waiting for the October 2026 auto-remediation.
prevention.adequacy: Patch installs an audit mode but does not change permissions automatically; operators must opt into remediation, so applying the update without the registry key leaves the ACL exposed.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor AD FS Admin event log for Event ID 1132 and alert on unexpected DKM container ACL reads / token-signing anomalies.
detection.adequacy: Event 1132 only fires after the update is installed in audit mode; pre-patch environments have no native signal.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate AD FS token-signing/DKM keys, hunt for forged SAML tokens, and review federated sign-ins to Microsoft 365 / Entra ID.
response.adequacy: Key rotation is essential post-exposure because a stolen DKM key remains valid until rotated.

ISO-27001-2022-A.5.16-Federated.gap: Federated-identity management guidance assumes the token-signing key material is protected; it does not detect the insufficiently-granular ACL that exposes the AD FS DKM key to lateral privilege elevation.

CVE-2026-56164.name: Microsoft SharePoint Server Missing Authentication for Critical Function Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted SOAP payload to /_vti_bin/client.svc targeting User Profiles administrative endpoints, omitting the X-RequestDigest header and adding routing headers to reach an unvalidated code path, elevating privileges over the network.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network attacker)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted unauthenticated HTTP/SOAP request with specific headers triggers the bypass.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by Mandiant/Google FLARE during incident response. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the July 14 2026 SharePoint security update; restrict internet exposure of on-prem SharePoint and enforce AMSI integration where available.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the missing-auth path, but zero-day exploitation preceded the fix, so patching alone did not protect early victims — pre-patch compensating controls and compromise assessment were also needed.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unauthenticated /_vti_bin/client.svc SOAP calls missing X-RequestDigest and on anomalous User Profiles administrative activity.
detection.adequacy: Detection is retrospective for a zero-day and depends on SharePoint request-level logging that many operators do not capture by default.

response.what_would_have_worked: Follow CISA Forensics Triage Requirements: hunt for unauthorized admin objects, rotate SharePoint machine keys/service credentials, and assess for persistence.
response.adequacy: Key rotation is essential because SharePoint auth-context material may be exfiltrated during exploitation.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The 3-day KEV due date reflects that this was exploited as a zero-day before any patch existed; a standard 30-day flaw-remediation SLA is far longer than the observed exploitation window.

CVE-2026-15409.name: SonicWall SMA1000 Appliances Server-Side Request Forgery Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A remote unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted request to the SMA1000 Work Place interface that coerces the appliance into making server-side requests to attacker-chosen destinations, enabling internal reconnaissance, cloud-metadata theft, or pivoting through the trusted appliance.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network-reachable)
attack_vector.complexity: low — single crafted request, no authentication and no user interaction
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by SonicWall PSIRT. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the SNWLID-2026-0008 fixed firmware and restrict the Work Place interface to trusted networks plus strict egress filtering from the appliance.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the request-forgery path, but without appliance egress filtering an unpatched or newly-found SSRF still reaches internal targets.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on appliance-originated outbound connections to internal ranges or metadata endpoints and on Work Place access without a valid session.
detection.adequacy: Egress anomaly detection catches exploitation attempts that perimeter inbound controls miss.

response.what_would_have_worked: Follow CISA Forensics Triage Requirements, rotate any credentials the appliance could reach, and review outbound logs for pivot activity.
response.adequacy: SSRF pivots may have already touched internal services before patching.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Perimeter boundary controls do not stop an SSRF that turns the trusted appliance itself into the request origin, letting outbound calls reach internal services the firewall would otherwise block.

CVE-2026-15410.name: SonicWall SMA1000 Appliances Code Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker holding administrator access to the SMA1000 AMC injects OS command syntax into an accepted management field, causing the appliance to execute attacker-chosen commands at the underlying OS level.
attack_vector.privileges_required: high (authenticated AMC administrator)
attack_vector.complexity: low — once admin access exists, the injection uses ordinary management input; the barrier is obtaining/abusing the admin session.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported via SonicWall PSIRT. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the SNWLID-2026-0008 firmware fix and restrict AMC exposure to a management-only network with tightly controlled admin accounts.
prevention.adequacy: Patching closes the injection, but because any admin credential yields OS RCE, admin-account hardening and console isolation are needed alongside the patch.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unexpected child processes spawned by AMC service accounts and on admin logins from anomalous source IPs.
detection.adequacy: Appliance logging often lacks process-level telemetry, so command execution may only be visible via downstream network artifacts.

response.what_would_have_worked: Follow CISA forensic-triage guidance, rotate admin credentials, and rebuild the appliance if OS execution is confirmed.
response.adequacy: A confirmed OS-level compromise of a VPN appliance generally warrants full reimaging rather than in-place cleanup.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The KEV due date is three days after listing; a standard flaw-remediation SLA measured in weeks leaves the internet-facing AMC exploitable well past the observed exploitation window.

CVE-2008-4128.name: Cisco IOS Cross-Site Request Forgery Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker lures an already-authenticated IOS administrator to a crafted page that forces requests to /level/15/exec/- and /level/15/exec/-/configure/http, executing privilege-15 and configuration commands through the router HTTP admin server.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated attacker; relies on an authenticated admin session and user interaction)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the forged request is a static crafted page, though it requires an admin with an active HTTP-admin session
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — third-party disclosed against Cisco IOS HTTP admin, re-listed by CISA KEV. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Disable the IOS HTTP server (no ip http server / restrict to a mgmt ACL) and retire end-of-support 12.4 devices
prevention.adequacy: No vendor patch exists for the retired train, so disabling the admin server / replacing the device is the only durable fix — patching alone is not an option.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for /level/15/exec/ URIs and unexpected privilege-15 command execution from the HTTP interface
detection.adequacy: Legacy IOS logging of HTTP-driven exec commands is sparse, limiting detection fidelity.

response.what_would_have_worked: Reset device credentials, audit running-config for injected aliases/HTTP config, and rebuild on a supported platform
response.adequacy: Config tampering can persist across sessions, so a full config review is required.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality is the real fix here: the IOS HTTP admin server should be disabled, but CM-7 baselining rarely reaches retired edge routers still exposing /level/15/exec, so the CSRF surface persists.

CVE-2020-14644.name: Oracle WebLogic Server Remote Code Execution Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted serialized Java object over the WebLogic T3 or IIOP protocol; WebLogic deserializes it through a vulnerable gadget chain and executes attacker code as the server process, resulting in full takeover.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to the T3/IIOP port)
attack_vector.complexity: low — mass-scannable, public gadget chains, no user interaction
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported via Oracle coordinated disclosure and fixed in the July 2020 CPU. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the July 2020 Oracle CPU; where patching lags, restrict or disable T3/IIOP and firewall the admin/managed-server ports to a trusted management segment.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the specific gadget, but new deserialization gadgets recur in WebLogic; protocol-level filtering of T3/IIOP is the durable compensating control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unauthenticated T3/IIOP sessions carrying serialized-object magic bytes and on the WebLogic JVM spawning shells or writing JSP files.
detection.adequacy: Network detection is feasible but T3 is opaque to most WAFs, so process-spawn and file-write telemetry on the host is the more reliable signal.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the server, hunt for dropped JSP webshells and persistence, rotate WebLogic and downstream datastore credentials.
response.adequacy: RCE-as-server-account means any secret the server could read must be treated as compromised.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection does not help when T3/IIOP management ports are exposed to the app tier or internet, which is common for WebLogic; the deserialization sink stays reachable.

CVE-2022-21445.name: Oracle ADF Faces Deserialization of Untrusted Data Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted HTTP request to an ADF Faces resource-loader endpoint carrying a serialized Java gadget (LambdaIdentity); the server deserializes it without validation, executing arbitrary commands and taking over the WebLogic-hosted application.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network via HTTP)
attack_vector.complexity: low — single crafted request against an internet-facing endpoint with public PoCs available
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Oracle PSIRT by a human researcher. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Oracle April 2022 CPU to ADF/WebLogic; front the app with a WAF stripping serialized-object payloads and block internet exposure of ADF resource endpoints.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone is sufficient only if applied promptly; the CPU cadence means most estates were exposed for a long window, so compensating WAF/egress controls were needed in the interim.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on serialized-object markers (rO0AB / 0xac 0xed) in ADF request bodies and on the WebLogic JVM spawning child OS processes.
detection.adequacy: Detection catches exploitation attempts but does not prevent the initial deserialization.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the managed server, rotate WebLogic and downstream data-store credentials, hunt for webshells and persistence dropped post-exploitation.
response.adequacy: Post-compromise response cannot undo data already exfiltrated via the RCE.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Oracle's quarterly CPU cadence plus enterprise change windows leaves ADF/WebLogic unpatched for months; the observed exploitation window is far shorter than the typical Fusion Middleware patch SLA.

CVE-2020-0618.name: Microsoft SQL Server Reporting Services Remote Code Execution Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A low-privileged authenticated user POSTs a crafted __VIEWSTATE payload to an SSRS report-rendering page; SSRS deserializes it insecurely (LosFormatter/ObjectStateFormatter), executing attacker code as the Report Server service account.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (any authenticated SSRS user)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public ViewState gadget chains and PoCs make weaponization trivial once the endpoint is reachable
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by MDSec's Soroush Dalili. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the February 2020 SSRS cumulative update and restrict report-server access to authenticated, network-segmented users only
prevention.adequacy: Patch removes the insecure deserialization, but exposed SSRS servers frequently miss BI-tier patch cycles, so network segmentation is needed alongside.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on child processes of ReportingServicesService.exe/w3wp and on oversized __VIEWSTATE parameters to report endpoints
detection.adequacy: Deserialization sinks execute in-process, so only process-lineage and payload-size heuristics catch it.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the SSRS host, rotate the service-account credentials, and hunt for planted webshells/scheduled tasks
response.adequacy: Service-account code execution can pivot to the SQL data tier, so credential rotation is essential.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: SSRS is often a long-lived internal BI server outside the standard OS patch cadence; a 21-day KEV due date lags the observed exploitation of exposed instances.

CVE-2024-27348.name: Apache HugeGraph-Server Improper Access Control Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker submits a crafted Gremlin script to /apis/gremlin that abuses Java reflection/OGNL to escape HugeGraphSecurityManager and run arbitrary OS commands on the server.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated when the Auth system is disabled, the default)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single HTTP POST with a public reflective payload against a default install
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to the Apache HugeGraph PMC by external researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to HugeGraph-Server 1.3.0 on Java11 and enable the Auth system; place the server behind authenticated network boundaries and never expose /apis/gremlin to the internet.
prevention.adequacy: Patching to 1.3.0 removes the sandbox escape, but without also enabling Auth the Gremlin endpoint stays reachable, so the patch alone is insufficient if operators keep the insecure default.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on /apis/gremlin requests containing reflective class names and on the HugeGraph JVM spawning shell children.
detection.adequacy: Post-exploitation shell spawns are detectable but occur after code execution has already succeeded.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, rotate any credentials reachable from the server, and rebuild given RCE-level access.
response.adequacy: RCE implies full server compromise; assume persistence until proven otherwise.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The 2024-10-09 KEV due date trailed real exploitation; SI-2 flaw-remediation SLAs measured in weeks are far longer than the hours-to-days scanning window for an internet-facing preauth RCE.

CVE-2014-0502.name: Adobe Flash Player Double Free Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A crafted SWF served from a compromised or attacker-controlled page triggers a double free in the Flash plugin, corrupting memory and allowing arbitrary code execution in the browser plugin process.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated drive-by; requires the victim to load a malicious page — UI:R)
attack_vector.complexity: low — reliable public exploit code and a stable heap-grooming primitive exist
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — surfaced via in-the-wild detection and Adobe PSIRT. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Remove Flash from endpoints (EoL) or, historically, apply the APSB14-07 update; enforce click-to-play / plugin blocking on browsers.
prevention.adequacy: Patching is impossible post-EoS; only full removal closes the surface, which patch-centric controls do not guarantee.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR on plugin-process anomalies (Flash spawning children, unexpected outbound C2) and web-proxy detection of SWF served from low-reputation domains.
detection.adequacy: Detection catches post-exploitation but not the initial memory corruption.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate affected hosts, uninstall Flash fleet-wide, hunt for RokRAT-style implants and C2 beacons.
response.adequacy: Response is reactive; the durable fix is eliminating the plugin.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: SI-2 flaw remediation has no patch path for an EoL plugin; only removal mitigates, which SI-2 alone does not compel.

CVE-2013-0648.name: Adobe Flash Player Code Execution Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: The attacker lures a user to a page serving a malicious SWF that abuses the ExternalInterface ActionScript functionality, corrupting memory and executing arbitrary code in the Flash Player process.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated), but user interaction is required to load the malicious SWF
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted SWF and a lured click trigger the flaw; no authentication needed.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — surfaced from February 2013 targeted attacks and disclosed by Adobe in APSB13-08. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Remove Flash Player entirely (the product is EoL) and block Flash content at the browser and gateway.
prevention.adequacy: Because the product is end-of-life, patching is not an option; only decommissioning removes the exposure.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Flag SWF loads from untrusted origins and Flash plugin process crashes or child-process spawns.
detection.adequacy: Signature-based SWF detection lags novel obfuscation; removal is more reliable than detection.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate hosts that rendered untrusted Flash, hunt for dropped payloads, and uninstall Flash fleet-wide.
response.adequacy: Response is moot once Flash is removed; the control objective is elimination.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality controls that still permit the Flash browser plugin leave this client-side RCE reachable; the plugin must be removed, not merely patched.

CVE-2013-0643.name: Adobe Flash Player Incorrect Default Permissions Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A victim's Firefox browser loads attacker-controlled SWF content; the Flash Player sandbox fails to properly restrict privileges, letting the SWF execute arbitrary code outside the sandbox. Delivered via drive-by / targeted web content in February 2013.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated remote attacker), but requires user interaction to load the SWF
attack_vector.complexity: low — a crafted SWF served to a vulnerable Flash/Firefox client is sufficient
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed to Adobe during the February 2013 Flash zero-day response. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Uninstall/disable the Flash Player plugin (now EoL) and block SWF content at the web gateway; where transitional use was required, apply the APSB13-08 update.
prevention.adequacy: Because the product is end-of-life, patching is no longer possible; only removal of Flash eliminates the risk.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Web-proxy alerting on SWF delivery and endpoint monitoring for anomalous Flash Player process behavior.
detection.adequacy: Targeted SWF payloads were tailored per campaign, limiting signature-based detection.

response.what_would_have_worked: Purge Flash from managed endpoints, hunt for post-exploitation second-stage payloads, and reimage confirmed-compromised hosts.
response.adequacy: Client compromise typically leads to credential theft, so credential rotation is warranted after any confirmed hit.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality/removal of the end-of-life Flash Player plugin is the only durable fix; a control that merely inventories software does not force removal of an unsupported client component.

CVE-2014-0497.name: Adobe Flash Player Integer Underflow Vulnerablity

attack_vector.description: An integer underflow during SWF parsing corrupts heap memory; a victim who loads an attacker-controlled Flash object (drive-by or malvertising) triggers arbitrary code execution in the browser context.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; requires the victim to view a malicious page)
attack_vector.complexity: low — CVSS AC:L; reliable exploitation was packaged into exploit-kit/drive-by delivery in the wild
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Kaspersky Lab. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Uninstall/disable Adobe Flash Player (now EoL) and migrate off Flash content; historically, applying the APSB14-04 update.
prevention.adequacy: Because the product is end-of-life, only removal is durable; any residual Flash install remains exploitable.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on Flash Player process spawning children or dropping executables; web-proxy detection of malicious SWF delivery.
detection.adequacy: Detection is retrospective; the exploit executes in-process before most signatures fire.

response.what_would_have_worked: Remove Flash from all endpoints, reimage confirmed-compromised hosts, hunt for exploit-kit follow-on payloads.
response.adequacy: Follow-on malware from drive-by chains is the real damage; scoping must extend beyond the browser.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality controls that still permit the Flash Player browser plugin leave a memory-corruption RCE surface exposed; the plugin should have been disabled/removed.

CVE-2024-6670.name: Progress WhatsUp Gold SQL Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a SQL injection payload to a WhatsUp Gold web endpoint to read the stored encrypted user password, then authenticates to the admin console and abuses monitoring tooling for code execution and lateral movement.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network attacker)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted request with a public PoC available
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Sina Kheirkhah of Summoning Team. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to WhatsUp Gold 2024.0.0 and remove the console from internet/management-network exposure; enforce parameterized queries at the app tier.
prevention.adequacy: Patch removes the injection but any credential exposed before patching must be rotated, since attackers retained the recovered admin password.

detection.what_would_have_worked: WAF/SQLi alerting on WhatsUp Gold endpoints and monitoring for anomalous admin logins immediately following unauthenticated requests.
detection.adequacy: Detection is useful but the credential leak is instantaneous, so blocking is preferable to alerting.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate all WhatsUp Gold and monitored-device credentials, hunt for ransomware staging, and review poller/task configurations for backdoors.
response.adequacy: Because the flaw is ransomware-linked, response must assume credential compromise and lateral movement have already occurred.

SOC2-CC6-logical-access.gap: Logical-access controls assume the admin password stays secret, but an unauthenticated SQLi extracts it — the control's trust boundary is bypassed rather than enforced.

CVE-2024-43461.name: Microsoft Windows MSHTML Platform Spoofing Vulnerability (CVE-2024-43461)

attack_vector.description: Void Banshee lured victims with a .URL that used CVE-2024-38112 to open attacker content in MSHTML/IE, then used CVE-2024-43461 to spoof the downloaded HTA's filename as a PDF so the victim ran it, dropping Atlantida stealer.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated); user interaction required (victim opens the spoofed file)
attack_vector.complexity: low — social-engineering delivery plus a reliable filename-padding spoof, no memory-corruption exploit
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Trend Micro ZDI; exploited by Void Banshee APT. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the September 2024 Windows update and disable/remove the MSHTML/IE-mode rendering path
prevention.adequacy: Patching fixes the spoof, but the chained CVE-2024-38112 MSHTML surface should be disabled to remove the class.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on mshta.exe executing files delivered as PDFs and on .url mhtml: handler use
detection.adequacy: Detection catches execution after the user is already deceived; extension-based filters do not.

response.what_would_have_worked: Credential rotation and stealer-focused IR after any Atlantida execution
response.adequacy: Infostealer impact (browser creds/wallets) may already have exfiltrated before response.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection keyed on file extension/type is defeated precisely because the vulnerability misrepresents the extension to both the user and naive filters.

CVE-2024-8190.name: Ivanti Cloud Services Appliance OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker with admin access to the CSA console injects OS commands through the DateTimeTab.php TIMEZONE parameter to run arbitrary code on the appliance; admin access is often obtained by chaining the CVE-2024-8963 authentication bypass.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low-to-high (application admin; in practice supplied by the chained CVE-2024-8963 bypass)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a public PoC automates authentication and command injection end to end
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — Ivanti PSIRT disclosure with Horizon3.ai analysis. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Migrate off the EoL CSA 4.6.x line to 5.0.x (which removed the vulnerable functionality) or remove CSA from service; restrict admin-console exposure.
prevention.adequacy: Patch 519 addresses 4.6.x but the line is EoL; only migration to 5.0.x is a durable fix.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on requests to DateTimeTab.php and unexpected shell processes spawned by the appliance web service; deploy the Emerging Threats CSA command-injection signature.
detection.adequacy: Appliance is closed; network IDS at the boundary is the primary detection surface.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rebuild the appliance, rotate credentials/secrets it held, and hunt for web shells and lateral movement.
response.adequacy: Given nation-state exploitation, assume full compromise and rebuild rather than clean in place.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation SLAs are outpaced by exploitation that began days after disclosure on an end-of-life 4.6.x appliance line that receives no further security fixes.

CVE-2024-38217.name: Microsoft Windows Mark of the Web (MOTW) Protection Mechanism Failure Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker delivers a crafted .lnk file whose target path forces Windows to rewrite/canonicalize the shortcut, which drops the Zone.Identifier ADS and removes the Mark-of-the-Web so subsequently opened files skip Protected View and SmartScreen prompts.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated), but requires the victim to open the delivered file (UI:R)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the LNK-stomping crafting is trivial once the technique is known.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Elastic Security Labs. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the September 2024 Windows update; enforce macro blocking and Attack Surface Reduction rules that do not depend solely on MOTW tagging.
prevention.adequacy: Patch restores MOTW integrity, but any defense that keyed purely on MOTW needs a defense-in-depth layer since MOTW can be stripped by design flaws.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Hunt for LNK files with non-canonical target paths and for internet-sourced files missing a Zone.Identifier ADS.
detection.adequacy: Detection requires ADS-aware tooling that many endpoint stacks do not inspect by default.

response.what_would_have_worked: Quarantine delivered LNK/archive lures and re-scan payloads independent of MOTW state.
response.adequacy: Response depends on catching the initial lure since post-execution the MOTW signal is already gone.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection that keys off MOTW/Protected View is directly undermined when the mark is stripped, so signature/behavioral gating never triggers on the downloaded payload.

CVE-2024-38014.name: Microsoft Windows Installer Improper Privilege Management Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local low-privileged user triggers a Windows Installer repair, which briefly runs an elevated process; by hijacking that elevated context (or racing the rollback temp files with symlinks/oplocks) the attacker obtains a SYSTEM shell.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local authenticated user)
attack_vector.complexity: low — reliable public technique using the built-in MSI repair flow
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by SEC Consult (Michael Baer). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the September 2024 cumulative update and set AlwaysInstallElevated to disabled.
prevention.adequacy: Patch removes the repair-flow race; least-privilege alone cannot stop it because the flaw escalates from an already-authorized standard user.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR rules for msiexec spawning SYSTEM command interpreters and standard-user writes to C:\Config.msi.
detection.adequacy: Behavioral detection catches exploitation even before patch deployment.

response.what_would_have_worked: Investigate the host for the preceding initial-access malware, since EoP is a post-compromise step.
response.adequacy: SYSTEM escalation implies an attacker already had code execution.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege alone does not help — the flaw lets an already-present low-privileged user escalate to SYSTEM, defeating the privilege boundary the control assumes.

CVE-2024-38226.name: Microsoft Publisher Protection Mechanism Failure Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A user is lured into opening a malicious Publisher file; the security-feature bypass causes Office to run embedded macros even though macro-blocking policy would normally stop untrusted content, giving the attacker code execution in the user's context.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local user opening a file)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a crafted document plus routine social engineering; user interaction is required.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — fixed by Microsoft PSIRT. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install the September 2024 Office update and reinforce with attachment sandboxing plus blocking Publisher files from untrusted sources at the mail gateway.
prevention.adequacy: Because the flaw defeats macro policy itself, patching plus gateway/EDR layering is needed rather than relying on the bypassed Office control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR rules flagging Publisher spawning script interpreters or unusual child processes after opening a downloaded file.
detection.adequacy: Detection depends on post-execution behavior since the blocking prompt the user expects never fires.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, hunt for macro-dropped payloads, and reset any credentials exposed to the executed macro.
response.adequacy: Follow-on payloads determine scope; the bypass itself is only the initial-access enabler.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Essential Eight application hardening relies on macro-execution restrictions in Office, exactly the enforcement this flaw defeats, so the mitigation is silently bypassed.

CVE-2024-40766.name: SonicWall SonicOS Improper Access Control Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker reaches SonicOS management access to obtain unauthorized resource access (or crash the firewall); ransomware affiliates chained this with reused local-user credentials on SSL VPN to gain rapid internal footholds.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network attacker against the management/VPN interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — network-reachable improper access control with no user interaction
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by SonicWall PSIRT. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to fixed SonicOS AND reset all local user passwords, restrict management/SSL-VPN to trusted sources, enforce MFA
prevention.adequacy: Patching alone was demonstrably insufficient: Akira/Fog kept compromising 'patched' firewalls where migrated local credentials were never reset.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on SSL VPN logins from unfamiliar ASNs and sudden internal scanning/encryption behavior
detection.adequacy: Sub-hour dwell time leaves a very narrow detection-to-response window.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the firewall, rotate all credentials, hunt for Akira/Fog persistence, restore from clean backups
response.adequacy: Given encryption within ~55 minutes, response is largely post-impact unless prevention held.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation closed the access-control bug, but the observed ransomware window (<1h) and the credential-reuse follow-on mean patch-only remediation without a mandatory local-user password reset leaves the device compromisable.

CVE-2017-1000253.name: Linux Kernel PIE Stack Buffer Corruption Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local user executes a specially-linked PIE binary so that its later PT_LOAD segments are mapped above mm->mmap_base into the stack gap; the resulting stack corruption is leveraged to escalate to root on unpatched long-term kernels.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local unprivileged user able to execute a binary)
attack_vector.complexity: low — deterministic on affected kernels with a crafted binary; public technical details from Qualys
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Qualys as part of the Stack Clash research. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Backport commit a87938b2e246 / apply the distro kernel update; where reboot is deferred, apply a kpatch/Ksplice/kGraft live patch.
prevention.adequacy: Patch fully closes the primitive, but the reboot requirement is why unpatched long-term kernels lingered for years; live-patching is the practical bridge.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for anomalous local root escalations, unexpected PIE binaries, and kernel oops/stack-corruption events during ELF loads.
detection.adequacy: Detection is post-escalation; exploitation is quiet, so host EDR + auditd on execve of untrusted binaries is the main signal.

response.what_would_have_worked: Treat any host that ran the exploit as root-compromised: reimage or thoroughly hunt for persistence and rotate host-stored credentials/keys.
response.adequacy: Local root undermines all host trust boundaries, so containment must assume full host compromise.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The fix was upstreamed without a security classification, so advisory-driven flaw remediation did not backport it to long-term kernels for over two years, leaving a durable local-root LPE.

attack_vector.description: An attacker uploads a crafted MVG/MSL/SVG image whose coder fields contain shell metacharacters; when a server passes the file to ImageMagick's delegate command, the metacharacters are executed as an OS command, yielding remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated where uploads are accepted anonymously)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoCs and a Metasploit module make weaponization trivial
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by Mail.Ru Security Team researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade ImageMagick past 6.9.3-10 / 7.0.1-1 and deploy a restrictive policy.xml disabling the vulnerable coders (MVG, MSL, EPHEMERAL, HTTPS, TEXT, SHOW, WIN, PLT); validate uploaded file content, not just extension.
prevention.adequacy: Even patched builds benefit from a hardened policy.xml and sandboxing, since new coder-based bypasses have recurred.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for image-processing workers spawning shells and for MVG/MSL delegate strings in uploaded content.
detection.adequacy: Detection is reactive; a single processed image already yields code execution.

response.what_would_have_worked: Contain the image-processing host, rotate secrets reachable from it, and audit for webshells or cron/persistence dropped via the RCE.
response.adequacy: Response cannot recover data exfiltrated during the exploitation window.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection that signature-scans uploads does not catch a crafted MVG/MSL image whose payload is a delegate command-injection string rather than known malware, so the file reaches the vulnerable ImageMagick coder.

CVE-2024-7262.name: Kingsoft WPS Office Path Traversal Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A victim opens (or single-clicks a link inside) a malicious WPS spreadsheet that invokes the custom ksoqing protocol; promecefpluginhost.exe fails to validate the supplied plugin path and loads an attacker-controlled DLL, executing the SpyGlace backdoor.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated victim opening a document / single click)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the weaponized document and public PoCs reduce exploitation to a single user interaction
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by ESET Research analyzing APT-C-60. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to WPS Office 12.2.0.16412+ and block/harden the ksoqing custom protocol handler
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the path-validation gap, but organizations with unmanaged WPS installs remain exposed until users update.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor promecefpluginhost.exe for DLL loads from user-writable/remote paths and flag ksoqing:// protocol invocations
detection.adequacy: The loader is a signed vendor binary, so only load-path and protocol-handler telemetry surfaces the abuse.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the endpoint, hunt for the SpyGlace DLL and its persistence, and reimage on confirmed backdoor
response.adequacy: Espionage backdoors persist quietly, so post-exploitation hunting is required beyond patching.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: WPS Office is user-installed productivity software often outside enterprise patch tooling, so the fix lags the one-click in-the-wild exploitation.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a directory-traversal request to the VigorConnect WebServlet download endpoint and retrieves arbitrary files from the host with root privileges.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted HTTP GET with ../ traversal against an internet-exposed endpoint
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found and disclosed by Tenable Research. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the DrayTek fix or remove/upgrade VigorConnect, and restrict the management portal to trusted networks only.
prevention.adequacy: Patching addresses the traversal, but because the flaw is only reachable when the portal is exposed, network isolation is the durable control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on traversal patterns in WebServlet request URIs and on file reads returning system files.
detection.adequacy: Detection catches enumeration but the read is a single benign-looking GET, so signatures on ../ sequences are essential.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate any credentials or keys stored on the appliance and audit for follow-on access using leaked material.
response.adequacy: File disclosure of credentials enables downstream authenticated attacks; assume harvested secrets are compromised.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection is the real mitigation here — the file-read is only exploitable when the WebServlet is internet-reachable, yet SC-7 controls frequently leave device-management portals exposed.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted request to the DownloadFileServlet endpoint using directory-traversal sequences, causing VigorConnect to return arbitrary files from the OS with root privileges.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, no user interaction)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted GET with ../ sequences suffices; public PoC exists
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by Tenable Research. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to VigorConnect 1.6.1; remove the management portal from the public Internet and restrict to a management VLAN/VPN.
prevention.adequacy: Patching alone is insufficient while the management interface remains Internet-exposed; network isolation is the decisive control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: WAF/IPS signatures for traversal patterns against DownloadFileServlet; alerting on access to system credential files.
detection.adequacy: Signature detection helps but is bypassable via encoding variants.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate any credentials readable from the host, review access logs for traversal reads, take the portal off the edge.
response.adequacy: Reactive; exposure removal is the durable fix.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: SC-7 boundary protection does not by itself force a device-management portal off the public Internet, which is the decisive exposure here.

CVE-2024-7965.name: Google Chromium V8 Inappropriate Implementation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A user visits a crafted HTML page that exploits an inappropriate implementation in V8 to corrupt the heap in the renderer, typically chained with a sandbox escape for full code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated), user interaction required to visit the page
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single malicious page reaches the flaw; exploitation is drive-by.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by an external researcher via Google's Chrome VRP. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Force-update Chrome to 128.0.6613.84+ and mandate browser relaunch fleet-wide.
prevention.adequacy: The updated binary does not protect users until the browser is relaunched; patch deployment must be paired with enforced restart.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for renderer-process crashes and anomalous browser child processes.
detection.adequacy: Crash telemetry is noisy and post-hoc; it rarely catches a clean exploit.

response.what_would_have_worked: Investigate hosts that visited attacker-controlled pages and confirm the browser version post-update.
response.adequacy: Response depends on knowing which sites were visited, which is often unavailable.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Even with an available patch, the interval between the emergency Chrome release and enterprise-wide browser update rollout leaves a window that observed in-the-wild exploitation can hit.

attack_vector.description: OFBiz checks authorization against the requestUri but renders the screen named by overrideViewUri. An unauthenticated attacker prefixes a public endpoint (e.g. forgotPassword) and overrides the view to ProgramExport, then posts a Base64-encoded Groovy program that executes as the OFBiz process user.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted HTTP POST using published PoC parameters
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by SonicWall Capture Labs. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to OFBiz 18.12.15; until patched, block or authenticate the /webtools ProgramExport endpoint and put OFBiz behind a WAF rule matching the override-view chain.
prevention.adequacy: Patching is definitive; the near-zero PoC-to-exploitation window means compensating WAF/endpoint controls were needed immediately, not just at the next maintenance window.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on requests where overrideViewUri targets ProgramExport, on groovyProgram parameters, and on child processes spawned by the Java/OFBiz service.
detection.adequacy: Base64-encoded Groovy hides command content, so process-level and decode-aware detection outperforms raw string matching.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, review OFBiz access logs for ProgramExport hits, rotate application/database credentials, and rebuild if RCE is confirmed.
response.adequacy: RCE runs as the OFBiz user with database access, so data-exposure assessment and credential rotation are required after any confirmed hit.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement is bypassed because authorization is checked on requestUri while rendering follows overrideViewUri — the control assumes a single consistent decision point, which this split defeats.

CVE-2024-7971.name: Google Chromium V8 Type Confusion Vulnerability (CVE-2024-7971)

attack_vector.description: A victim visits an attacker-controlled page that runs crafted JavaScript triggering a V8 type confusion; the resulting heap corruption yields renderer RCE, which Citrine Sleet chained with a Windows sandbox escape to deploy the FudModule rootkit.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; requires victim to load a malicious page — CVSS UI:R)
attack_vector.complexity: low — CVSS AC:L; a single crafted HTML page triggers the bug, though the full kernel-rootkit chain is sophisticated
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Microsoft Threat Intelligence and Google. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update Chrome/Chromium browsers to 128.0.6613.84+ and enforce auto-update; block known Citrine Sleet infrastructure.
prevention.adequacy: Patching closes the window after disclosure but cannot protect against the pre-patch zero-day period; defense-in-depth (sandbox integrity, EDR on rootkit behavior) is needed.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR detection of the sandbox escape and FudModule rootkit load; monitoring for the crypto-themed C2 domains named in the Microsoft report.
detection.adequacy: Renderer-stage exploitation is hard to see; detection realistically starts at the post-escape rootkit stage.

response.what_would_have_worked: Reimage compromised hosts, hunt for FudModule and BYOVD artifacts, rotate credentials for crypto/finance staff.
response.adequacy: A kernel rootkit undermines host trust; assume full compromise of affected endpoints.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection at the endpoint did not stop a memory-corruption chain that escaped the browser sandbox and loaded a kernel rootkit (FudModule).

CVE-2024-39717.name: Versa Director Dangerous File Type Upload Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker who has (or obtains) high-privilege Provider-Data-Center admin access uploads a Java/JSP payload disguised as a .png via the Change Favicon function; in observed intrusions attackers reached the exposed management ports and then loaded the memory-resident VersaMem web shell to steal credentials.
attack_vector.privileges_required: high (Provider-Data-Center-Admin / System-Admin) — but exposed management ports and credential capture let attackers chain to full compromise
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted upload once the admin UI is reachable
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — in-the-wild activity identified by Lumen Black Lotus Labs. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to Versa Director 22.1.4+ and firewall management ports 4566/4570 to trusted networks only, blocking the initial reachability the exploit depends on.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is necessary but the observed VersaMem web shell is memory-resident, so patched hosts must also be rebooted and hunted to evict any pre-existing implant.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for .png uploads containing non-image content, unexpected JARs in the Tomcat process, and outbound connections to SOHO egress IPs.
detection.adequacy: Because VersaMem lives in memory and hooks authentication, disk-based file-integrity monitoring alone misses it.

response.what_would_have_worked: Reboot to clear the in-memory web shell, rotate all credentials that transited the Director since June 2024, and review captured-credential exposure.
response.adequacy: Credential capture means downstream tenant and device secrets should be treated as compromised.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Versa's own guidance was to firewall management ports 4566/4570, but many operators left them internet-reachable; the boundary control was available yet not enforced, enabling the initial upload.

CVE-2021-31196.name: Microsoft Exchange Server Remote Code Execution Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker with valid Exchange authentication reaches an internet-facing OWA/ECP back-end endpoint and triggers server-side code execution, then persists via an ASPX web shell in an Exchange virtual directory.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (authenticated Exchange user — PR:H per CVSS, but obtainable via credential theft or chaining with an Exchange SSRF)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoCs exist and Exchange back-end endpoints are reliably reachable
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — coordinated researcher disclosure in the ProxyOracle/ProxyShell wave. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the July 2021 Exchange cumulative-update/security patch and restrict OWA/ECP exposure
prevention.adequacy: Patching closes the RCE, but unpatched, internet-facing Exchange remains a top ransomware entry point.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on w3wp spawning shells and on new ASPX files in Exchange virtual directories
detection.adequacy: Web-shell detection catches post-exploitation, not the initial code execution.

response.what_would_have_worked: Web-shell hunt, credential reset, and assume-breach IR on the Exchange host
response.adequacy: Exchange compromise often precedes lateral movement, so scoping must extend to the domain.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Perimeter controls do not block the attack because Exchange OWA/ECP must be internet-reachable for mail flow, so the vulnerable endpoints stay exposed.

CVE-2022-0185.name: Linux Kernel Heap-Based Buffer Overflow Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unprivileged local user opens a filesystem that falls back to legacy Filesystem Context handling and supplies an over-length parameter, overflowing the kernel heap in legacy_parse_param to corrupt adjacent objects and escalate to root, including breaking out of a container.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unprivileged local user when unprivileged user namespaces are enabled; otherwise namespaced CAP_SYS_ADMIN)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoCs reliably weaponize the overflow on affected kernels
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by the Crusaders of Rust CTF team. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Patch to a fixed kernel (or apply a livepatch) and, as an interim mitigation, disable unprivileged user namespaces (kernel.unprivileged_userns_clone=0 / user.max_user_namespaces=0).
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone requires a reboot; the namespace-hardening mitigation removes the exploit precondition immediately and should be applied in parallel.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for kernel oops in legacy_parse_param and for unprivileged processes creating user namespaces followed by fs-context syscalls.
detection.adequacy: Exploitation is local and fast; auditd/eBPF syscall telemetry is the practical detection surface.

response.what_would_have_worked: Treat any host showing the syscall pattern as potentially root-compromised; rebuild container hosts and rotate secrets.
response.adequacy: Container escape means blast radius extends to co-tenant workloads on the same node.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality does not disable unprivileged user namespaces by default on many distros, leaving the exploit precondition present unless kernel.unprivileged_userns_clone is explicitly hardened.

CVE-2021-33045.name: Dahua IP Camera Authentication Bypass Vulnerability (CVE-2021-33045)

attack_vector.description: An attacker sends a crafted login packet to an internet-exposed Dahua device specifying the loopback device as the client, which causes the firmware to bypass identity authentication and grant a session without valid credentials.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network attacker)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single malformed login handshake bypasses authentication.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Dahua PSIRT by external researcher. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply Dahua firmware updates and remove cameras from direct internet exposure behind VPN/segmentation.
prevention.adequacy: Patching helps only where firmware is still maintained; end-of-support and OEM-rebadged units require network isolation or replacement.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for loopback-device fields in remote login handshakes and for unauthenticated session establishment on camera ports.
detection.adequacy: Camera telemetry is rarely centralized, so detection depends on network-level inspection of the proprietary protocol.

response.what_would_have_worked: Factory-reset compromised devices, rotate credentials, and check for botnet persistence/outbound C2.
response.adequacy: Reset is only durable if the device is also removed from direct exposure and patched.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection is routinely absent for cameras placed directly on the internet or in flat networks; the auth bypass reaches the device's login service with no perimeter mediation.

CVE-2021-33044.name: Dahua IP Camera Authentication Bypass Vulnerability (CVE-2021-33044)

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker sends a crafted login packet specifying the NetKeyboard authentication type, which bypasses the credential check and grants an authenticated session to the camera or NVR.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network-reachable)
attack_vector.complexity: low — single malformed login packet, widely automated by botnets
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by Dahua PSIRT. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update to fixed firmware per Dahua advisory 957 and remove cameras/NVRs from direct internet exposure behind a segmented VLAN/VPN.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the NetKeyboard bypass, but many deployed devices never receive firmware updates, so network isolation is essential.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for NetKeyboard-type logins and unexpected outbound connections from camera subnets.
detection.adequacy: Segmentation plus egress monitoring catches compromised devices even when firmware stays vulnerable.

response.what_would_have_worked: Factory-reset affected devices, rotate credentials, and isolate the CCTV VLAN.
response.adequacy: Reset is only durable once patched firmware is applied.

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: The authentication mechanism itself is bypassable via the NetKeyboard packet type, so an identification/authentication control that assumes credentials are checked provides no protection.

CVE-2024-23897.name: Jenkins Command Line Interface (CLI) Path Traversal Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker sends a Jenkins CLI command whose argument contains '@' followed by a server file path; the args4j parser substitutes the file's contents, disclosing secrets such as the Jenkins master key that are then used to achieve remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated for partial reads; Overall/Read for full-file reads)
attack_vector.complexity: low — turnkey public PoCs automate the read-then-RCE chain against exposed controllers.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Yaniv Nizry of SonarSource. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to Jenkins 2.442 / LTS 2.426.3 (which disable expandAtFiles) or, as interim mitigation, disable CLI access and remove the controller from untrusted networks.
prevention.adequacy: Patching stops the read, but because secrets may already be exfiltrated, credential and key rotation is required alongside the upgrade.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Inspect Jenkins access logs for CLI requests containing '@'-prefixed paths and monitor for reads of secret.key/master.key.
detection.adequacy: Detection catches exploitation attempts but not silent secret theft that already occurred before logging was tuned.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate all Jenkins-stored credentials and signing keys, review pipeline integrity, and hunt for downstream use of leaked secrets.
response.adequacy: A CI compromise can taint every artifact built during the exposure window, so response must extend to the software supply chain.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Public PoCs landed within hours of disclosure; any remediation SLA slower than same-day left internet-facing controllers readable long before patch windows closed.

CVE-2024-28986.name: SolarWinds Web Help Desk Deserialization of Untrusted Data Vulnerability (CVE-2024-28986)

attack_vector.description: An attacker sends a crafted serialized Java object to Web Help Desk; unsafe deserialization instantiates a gadget chain that runs arbitrary OS commands as the WHD service account.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none reported in the wild (originally reported unauthenticated; SolarWinds could only reproduce authenticated)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted request drives the deserialization gadget when the host is reachable
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — external researcher disclosure to SolarWinds. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply WHD 12.8.3 Hotfix 1 and remove WHD from direct internet exposure
prevention.adequacy: Patch is necessary but the recurring WHD deserialization class means network isolation and deserialization allow-listing are needed alongside it.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on the WHD/Tomcat Java process spawning cmd/powershell/sh and on serialized-object payloads inbound
detection.adequacy: Requires process-lineage telemetry many WHD hosts do not ship by default.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate host, rotate WHD service and integrated-system credentials, hunt for web shells/tooling
response.adequacy: WHD often holds broad AD/service credentials, so blast radius extends beyond the host.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation via WHD 12.8.3 Hotfix 1 is correct, but the 3-week KEV due window is slow against a CVSS 9.8 deserialization RCE already being exploited on internet-facing help-desk servers.

CVE-2024-38107.name: Microsoft Windows Power Dependency Coordinator Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local, authenticated attacker triggers a use-after-free in the Windows Power Dependency Coordinator driver to corrupt kernel memory and elevate to SYSTEM, chaining it after an initial foothold to gain full host control.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local authenticated standard user)
attack_vector.complexity: low — reliable enough to be exploited in the wild; requires only local code execution
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Microsoft and fixed in August 2024. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the August 2024 Windows security updates; enforce application control (WDAC/AppLocker) to limit which local code can run and reach the driver.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the UAF, but between patch release and endpoint rollout the only mitigation is limiting local code execution.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR rules for privilege-transition to SYSTEM from a medium-integrity parent and anomalous pdc.sys interactions.
detection.adequacy: Kernel-driver UAFs leave little payload signature; behavioral token-elevation telemetry is the primary detection.

response.what_would_have_worked: Treat the endpoint as SYSTEM-compromised: isolate, hunt for follow-on persistence and credential theft, and reimage if confirmed.
response.adequacy: SYSTEM privilege enables credential dumping and defense evasion, so response must assume broader host compromise.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Staged monthly Windows patching leaves endpoints exposed for weeks after the KEV add, during which the SYSTEM-grade LPE is already being used post-foothold.

CVE-2024-38106.name: Microsoft Windows Kernel Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: After gaining initial low-privilege code execution (e.g. via a Chromium renderer sandbox escape), the attacker triggers a Windows Kernel race condition to corrupt kernel state and elevate to SYSTEM. Exploitation depends on repeatedly winning the race.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local, requires an existing foothold)
attack_vector.complexity: high — success requires winning a kernel race condition, per the CISA KEV note
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found in-the-wild by Microsoft threat intelligence. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the August 2024 cumulative update; reduce initial-access surface (browser hardening, application allowlisting) so an attacker cannot reach the kernel-race trigger.
prevention.adequacy: Because it was a zero-day, patching offered no protection during the pre-disclosure window; layered exploit-mitigation and least-privilege were the only pre-patch defenses.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR behavioral detection of SYSTEM token theft / handle-duplication from a sandboxed child process and browser-to-kernel chaining.
detection.adequacy: Detection can surface the escalation attempt but not stop a successful race win.

response.what_would_have_worked: Reimage compromised hosts, rotate credentials cached on the machine, and hunt for post-SYSTEM persistence and lateral movement.
response.adequacy: SYSTEM-level compromise implies full host takeover; response must assume total credential exposure.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: Standard endpoint monitoring rarely flags a race-condition kernel EoP; without behavioral SYSTEM-token-theft detection the escalation step is invisible, and no patch existed to remediate during the zero-day window.

CVE-2024-38193.name: Microsoft Windows Ancillary Function Driver for WinSock Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local, low-privileged process abuses a use-after-free in afd.sys (the WinSock Ancillary Function Driver) to corrupt kernel memory and elevate to SYSTEM, after which Lazarus installs the FudModule rootkit to disable security monitoring.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local authenticated user / initial foothold)
attack_vector.complexity: low — reliable exploitation demonstrated in the wild and in public PoCs
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found in-the-wild by Gen Digital (Avast) researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the August 2024 Windows cumulative update and reboot; enforce least-privilege to limit the initial foothold
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the UAF, but as a zero-day it was exploited before fixes existed, so behavioral kernel-integrity controls matter.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Kernel-integrity/attack-surface-reduction telemetry flagging afd.sys IOCTL abuse and rootkit-driven callback tampering
detection.adequacy: FudModule disables EDR callbacks, so detection must occur at or before the escalation, not after.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate and reimage affected hosts, rotate credentials, and hunt for FudModule persistence across the environment
response.adequacy: A rootkit foothold undermines trust in the host, so reimaging is the reliable response.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Signature-based malicious-code protection is precisely what FudModule disables post-escalation, so it cannot be relied on to catch this chain.

CVE-2024-38213.name: Microsoft Windows SmartScreen Security Feature Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker stages a payload on a WebDAV share and lures the user to copy-paste it locally; the copied file lacks Mark-of-the-Web, so opening it skips SmartScreen and Protected View, executing without reputation checks.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated remote attacker, but requires user interaction to copy and open the file)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the bypass is reliable, though it depends on social-engineering the user into a copy-paste and open
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by Trend Micro ZDI Threat Hunting. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the August 2024 Windows cumulative update, block outbound WebDAV/WebClient service, and restrict search-ms/UNC lure handling.
prevention.adequacy: The patch restores MotW tagging, but the WebClient/WebDAV exposure and user copy-paste behaviour remain viable delivery paths for other loaders.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Hunt for locally-executed files that lack Zone.Identifier despite a remote origin, and for WebDAV connections preceding execution.
detection.adequacy: Because the bypass strips the very artifact detections key on, detection must shift to WebDAV network telemetry and process lineage.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate infected hosts, remove DarkGate persistence, and rotate credentials harvested by the loader.
response.adequacy: DarkGate is a full loader; treat initial access as leading to broader compromise.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection leans on SmartScreen/Protected View reputation checks that this bypass specifically defeats, so SI-3 controls that assume MotW is enforced are silently undermined.

CVE-2024-38178.name: Microsoft Windows Scripting Engine Memory Corruption Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A victim using Edge in Internet Explorer mode loads attacker-controlled JavaScript (delivered via a compromised ad service); the JScript9 JIT performs an incorrect type optimization, producing a type confusion that leads to remote code execution and RokRAT deployment.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; requires the victim to load attacker content in IE mode — UI:R)
attack_vector.complexity: high — high attack complexity (AC:H); a reliable exploit requires the JIT type-confusion primitive and IE-mode reachability
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — attributed to APT37 activity reported by AhnLab and NCSC. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the August 2024 cumulative update and disable Edge Internet Explorer mode / legacy WebView where not required.
prevention.adequacy: Patching cannot pre-empt a zero-day; disabling the legacy engine removes the reachable attack surface entirely.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on iexplore/WebView spawning script hosts, in-memory injection into system32 binaries, and outbound connections to Yandex/pCloud C2.
detection.adequacy: Detects post-exploitation behavior of RokRAT, not the initial type confusion.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate hosts, hunt for RokRAT in-memory implants and cloud-API C2, force patching and IE-mode removal fleet-wide.
response.adequacy: Reactive; surface removal is the durable control.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: App-hardening maturity leaves Edge IE mode / legacy WebView enabled by default, keeping the JScript9 engine reachable even on patched systems.

CVE-2024-38189.name: Microsoft Project Remote Code Execution Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker delivers a malicious Microsoft Project file; when the victim opens it (with internet-macro blocking disabled) the flaw yields code execution in the user's context.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated attacker), user interaction required to open the file
attack_vector.complexity: low — crafting a malicious .mpp and social-engineering the open is straightforward, though it depends on weakened macro policy.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — coordinated disclosure via Microsoft MSRC. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the August 2024 Microsoft update and enforce 'Block macros from running in Office files from the Internet'.
prevention.adequacy: Patch plus enforced macro-blocking is needed; the exploit specifically targets environments where the macro safeguard is off.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on Microsoft Project spawning shell or script interpreters.
detection.adequacy: Behavioral detection catches post-exploitation but not the initial file parse.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, collect the malicious .mpp, and review Office macro-policy state across the fleet.
response.adequacy: Response is reactive; enforced macro policy prevents recurrence.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Configuring Microsoft Office to block internet-origin macros is the compensating control that neutralizes this file-borne RCE, but it is only effective where the baseline is actually enforced.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker embeds path-traversal sequences in the OFBiz controller URI to escape the intended request-to-view mapping and reach restricted screen/controller endpoints, which is then chained to code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a crafted HTTP request with traversal segments, per public PoC
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to the Apache OFBiz project (OFBIZ-13006). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to OFBiz 18.12.13; until patched, apply WAF rules blocking traversal sequences to /webtools/control and restrict endpoint exposure.
prevention.adequacy: Patching corrects the controller path handling; WAF traversal filters are a stopgap that can be bypassed with alternate encodings.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on traversal encodings ('..;/', '%2e%2e') in /webtools/control requests and on anonymous access to normally-authenticated endpoints.
detection.adequacy: Multiple traversal encodings exist, so decode-normalized detection is more reliable than literal string matching.
