response.what_would_have_worked: Review OFBiz access logs for traversal patterns reaching restricted endpoints, rotate credentials, and rebuild confirmed-compromised hosts.
response.adequacy: Because the traversal chains to RCE, treat any confirmed hit as full host compromise.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Traversal in the controller lets requests reach endpoints the access model intended to protect, bypassing the authorization mapping the control relies on.

CVE-2024-36971.name: Android Kernel Remote Code Execution Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local attacker manipulates UDP-socket destination-cache state so that __dst_negative_advice() releases a dst entry out of RCU order, causing a use-after-free that is groomed into kernel memory corruption and privilege escalation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local code execution on the device; CVSS AV:L/PR:L)
attack_vector.complexity: high — reliable kernel-heap grooming of a race-condition UAF requires sophistication, consistent with TAG-observed targeted use
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — root cause tracked by Clement Lecigne (Google TAG). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the kernel fix / September 2024 Android security patch level; on servers, update to fixed stable kernels and reboot.
prevention.adequacy: Patch delivery on fragmented Android OEM fleets lags disclosure, so the exposure window is long even when a fix exists.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Kernel crash/exploit telemetry and mobile EDR flagging privilege-escalation behavior on managed devices.
detection.adequacy: Kernel-level LPE is largely invisible to userspace controls; detection is limited.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force OS updates via MDM, retire devices that cannot receive the patch, investigate targeted users for follow-on spyware.
response.adequacy: TAG-observed targeting suggests spyware chains; scope beyond the single device to the targeted individual.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Kernel flaw remediation on mobile/embedded fleets depends on OEM backport cadence; the observed targeted exploitation preceded broad OTA availability, outrunning any patch SLA.

CVE-2018-0824.name: Microsoft COM for Windows Deserialization of Untrusted Data Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker delivers a crafted serialized COM object via a file or script; the Windows COM runtime unsafely deserializes it (UnmarshalPwn), yielding privilege escalation and code execution on unpatched hosts.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low — typically a local/medium-integrity foothold escalated to SYSTEM (UI:R indicates a delivered file/script)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a reliable public PoC exists and the technique is a well-known post-exploitation primitive
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Code White researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the May 2018 Microsoft security update and retire or isolate unsupported Windows builds that can no longer receive it.
prevention.adequacy: Patch fully fixes supported builds, but legacy/unsupported Windows versions remain exploitable, so isolation or replacement is required there.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR rules for anomalous COM object instantiation and known UnmarshalPwn tooling, plus alerting on unexpected SYSTEM-level child processes.
detection.adequacy: Because the primitive rides legitimate COM marshalling, behavioral EDR is more reliable than signatures.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rebuild compromised hosts, rotate local and cached-domain credentials, and confirm the OS patch level after re-imaging.
response.adequacy: As an escalation primitive it usually appears mid-intrusion, so response must assume prior initial access.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The flaw was patched in 2018 but reached KEV in 2024, meaning long-lived unpatched Windows hosts remained exploitable for years — flaw-remediation programs that leave legacy Windows 7/Server 2008/2012 systems unpatched miss this LPE-to-RCE primitive.

attack_vector.description: After gaining a foothold with sufficient AD permissions, ransomware operators delete and re-create the 'ESXi Admins' group in Active Directory; the AD-joined ESXi host re-grants full administrative access by group name, giving the attacker hypervisor control to encrypt all hosted VMs.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low-to-high (post-compromise AD account able to create/modify the ESXi-admin group)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a few AD group operations, no exploit primitive; publicly documented by Microsoft
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by Microsoft threat researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply ESXi 8.0 U3 / VMSA-2024-0013 mitigations, bind hypervisor admin to a specific verified group and remove name-based AD trust
prevention.adequacy: Patch/mitigation stops the re-grant, but AD-integrated ESXi should also be isolated from tier-0 AD compromise paths.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on creation/modification of any group named 'ESXi Admins' and on new ESXi admin logins
detection.adequacy: Detection depends on AD auditing that many environments do not forward for hypervisor-admin groups.

response.what_would_have_worked: Immediate isolation of ESXi management, AD privileged-account reset, and VM-backup integrity checks
response.adequacy: By the time it fires, mass VM encryption may already be underway.

NIST-800-53-AC-2.gap: Account-management controls do not detect that ESXi re-authorizes an AD group purely by name; deleting and re-creating 'ESXi Admins' re-grants full admin outside normal account provisioning review.

CVE-2023-45249.name: Acronis Cyber Infrastructure (ACI) Insecure Default Password Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A remote unauthenticated attacker uses the shipped default password to access the ACI web portal / PostgreSQL database, then uploads SSH keys to gain root on the appliance and execute arbitrary commands.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated — default credentials)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a Metasploit module fully automates the default-credential-to-root chain
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — Acronis vendor investigation of a customer intrusion. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to a fixed ACI build and rotate all default credentials; restrict management-plane exposure to the internet.
prevention.adequacy: The fixed build removes the insecure default, but any appliance that already accepted the default credential must also rotate secrets and check for planted SSH keys.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on new SSH authorized_keys entries, portal logins from unexpected sources, and crypto-mining process/network signatures.
detection.adequacy: Default-credential logins look legitimate; SSH-key and mining indicators are the reliable tells.

response.what_would_have_worked: Remove planted keys, rebuild affected appliances, and rotate all stored credentials and API tokens.
response.adequacy: Root access via SSH key implies durable persistence; assume full compromise.

PCI-DSS-4.0-2.2.3.gap: The vendor-default-account requirement is exactly the gap: the appliance shipped with a usable default password that operators did not change, yielding unauthenticated RCE.

CVE-2024-5217.name: ServiceNow Incomplete List of Disallowed Inputs Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker submits a crafted jvar_page_title value to /login.do containing Glide/Jelly expression syntax; the GlideExpression sanitizer's incomplete disallowed-input list lets the payload reach server-side template evaluation, yielding remote code execution and data exfiltration.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network attacker)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted login request triggers evaluation; chaining with CVE-2024-4879 broadens impact.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Assetnote. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the June 2024 ServiceNow patches/hot fixes for all three chained CVEs; restrict instance exposure and enforce IP allowlisting where feasible.
prevention.adequacy: Patching closes the sanitizer gap, but exposed instances were scanned within days, so patch alone left a live exploitation window that also required WAF/monitoring.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on /login.do requests with expression-language content in jvar_page_title and on anomalous sys_user bulk reads.
detection.adequacy: Detection needs application-layer request logging that captures parameter contents, not just access logs.

response.what_would_have_worked: Assume data exposure: rotate ServiceNow credentials/secrets, review OAuth tokens, and hunt for exfiltration of user tables.
response.adequacy: Credential rotation is essential because the exploit's proven goal was dumping user/credential data.

PCI-DSS-4.0-6.2.4.gap: Input-validation control expectations were not met by the GlideExpression sanitizer's incomplete disallowed-input list, allowing template-injection payloads through.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker submits a Jelly/Glide template expression to a public UI macro; the Now Platform evaluates it server-side, giving arbitrary expression/code execution and access to instance data.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, internet-reachable)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single ${...} payload against a public page, widely automated after public analysis
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to ServiceNow with public analysis by Assetnote. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the KB1645154 patches/hot fixes and deploy WAF rules blocking Jelly template syntax on public endpoints as an interim virtual patch.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is definitive; WAF virtual-patching covers the window before self-hosted instances are updated.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on ${...} Jelly syntax in requests to unauthenticated UI pages and on anomalous GlideRecord/bulk queries.
detection.adequacy: Payload signatures are distinctive and detectable at the WAF/log layer.

response.what_would_have_worked: Assume data exposure, rotate instance credentials/integration tokens, and hunt for chained exploitation of related ServiceNow CVEs.
response.adequacy: Preauth RCE implies full instance data was reachable.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Internet-facing Now Platform instances present the vulnerable UI macros pre-authentication, so boundary controls that assume login-gated access do not apply.

attack_vector.description: An attacker submitted large batches of phone numbers to an unauthenticated Authy API endpoint, which replied indicating whether each number was registered, enabling mass enumeration of Authy users for downstream phishing and SIM-swap targeting.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated public API)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a trivial script iterating phone numbers against an open endpoint.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified by Twilio following in-the-wild abuse. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Require authentication and enforce abuse-rate limiting on the endpoint (as Twilio ultimately did) so anonymous bulk lookups are impossible.
prevention.adequacy: Securing the endpoint stops future enumeration, but data already leaked to ShinyHunters cannot be recalled, so user-facing anti-phishing guidance is also needed.

detection.what_would_have_worked: API abuse monitoring alerting on high-volume phone-number lookups from single sources.
detection.adequacy: Without pre-existing rate/abuse telemetry the enumeration ran undetected until the leak surfaced.

response.what_would_have_worked: Notify affected users, push app updates, and warn of imminent smishing/social-engineering attempts.
response.adequacy: Response mitigates downstream harm but cannot undo the disclosure of the phone-number dataset.

GDPR-Art32.gap: Security-of-processing obligations were unmet because personal data (phone-number registration status) was disclosable without authentication or abuse-rate controls.

attack_vector.description: A victim using IE 6-8 visits a crafted (often watering-hole) page whose script grooms the heap and accesses a freed CDwnBindInfo object, giving the attacker arbitrary code execution in the browser context.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; requires the victim to browse to attacker-controlled/compromised content)
attack_vector.complexity: low — reliable public exploit code (Metasploit) exists, though it depends on the victim visiting a malicious page
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — surfaced through December 2012 in-the-wild watering-hole attacks and Microsoft/vendor analysis. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply MS13-008 and, decisively, retire end-of-life Internet Explorer 6-8
prevention.adequacy: The product is EOL; only disconnecting/replacing IE fully removes the exposure, so the patch is a stopgap.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Detect iexplore.exe spawning child processes and heap-spray patterns in served HTML
detection.adequacy: Memory-only UAF exploitation evades signature AV, limiting endpoint detection.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate affected hosts, hunt for dropped implants from the watering-hole, migrate off IE
response.adequacy: Drive-by delivery from a trusted site means many users may be hit before response begins.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation shipped as MS13-008, but the impacted IE is end-of-life; SI-2 does not force retirement of the unsupported browser that still renders attacker HTML.

CVE-2022-22948.name: VMware vCenter Server Incorrect Default File Permissions Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A user with non-administrative access to vCenter Server reads sensitive files exposed by incorrect default file permissions, harvesting configuration data and secrets that enable privilege escalation and lateral movement in the virtualization estate.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (non-administrative authenticated vCenter access)
attack_vector.complexity: low — exploitation is reading over-permissioned files; no memory corruption or special tooling
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to VMware and fixed in VMSA-2022-0009. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the VMSA-2022-0009 updates that correct the file permissions; audit appliance file ACLs and restrict non-admin access to the management plane.
prevention.adequacy: Patch corrects the ACLs, but any secrets read before patching should be considered exposed and rotated.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Audit-log review of non-administrative accounts accessing sensitive appliance files and subsequent anomalous authentications.
detection.adequacy: File reads by an authorized-but-low-privileged account are quiet; detection relies on downstream credential-misuse signals.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate any credentials/secrets stored on the vCenter appliance and review privileged authentications for misuse.
response.adequacy: Because the flaw yields secrets, response must assume harvested credentials and rotate them.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege role design cannot remediate a vendor-shipped incorrect-default-permission that exposes secrets to non-administrative accounts; only the vendor patch or an explicit ACL fix closes it.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted HTTP request to the Serv-U web client with traversal sequences (mixed Windows/Unix separators and encodings) in path parameters, causing the server to read and return files outside the served directory, including config and credential files.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network via HTTP)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted GET with public PoCs; trivially scriptable for mass scanning
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by researcher Hussein Daher. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply Serv-U 15.4.2 Hotfix 2; restrict inbound access to the Serv-U web interface to trusted networks and place it behind a VPN/WAF that normalizes and blocks traversal sequences.
prevention.adequacy: Patching closes the flaw, but exposed appliances were scanned within days, so network restriction was needed immediately as a compensating control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on Serv-U requests containing traversal tokens and on reads of sensitive host paths returned by the service.
detection.adequacy: Detection reveals reconnaissance/exfiltration attempts but does not stop file disclosure.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate any credentials or keys stored in files readable from the Serv-U host; review access logs for successful traversal reads.
response.adequacy: Once config/credential files are read, response must assume those secrets are compromised.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access-enforcement controls are bypassed because the traversal reads files entirely outside the application's authorization model, requiring no valid account.

CVE-2024-34102.name: Adobe Commerce and Magento Open Source Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference (XXE) Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker POSTs a crafted XML document referencing an external entity to Magento's guest-cart REST/GraphQL endpoint; the parser resolves the entity (CosmicSting), leaking the encryption key and files, and chained with object injection achieves remote code execution and skimmer/webshell deployment.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, no user interaction)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoCs and mass-scanning make weaponization trivial
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by researcher 'spacewasp'; exploitation analyzed by Sansec/Assetnote. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply Adobe's June 2024 patch (APSB24-40) and the isolated CosmicSting hotfix, and rotate the Magento crypt key after patching
prevention.adequacy: Patching alone is insufficient if the encryption key was already leaked pre-patch, so key rotation is mandatory.

detection.what_would_have_worked: WAF/log detection of XML DOCTYPE/external-entity payloads to cart APIs and outbound OAST callbacks during parsing
detection.adequacy: Blind XXE exfiltrates via out-of-band channels, so egress and callback monitoring is needed.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate crypt keys and admin credentials, scan for injected skimmers/webshells, and audit for unauthorized admin accounts
response.adequacy: Leaked keys enable persistent admin-session forgery, so credential and key rotation is essential.

PCI-DSS-4.0-6.3.3.gap: Payment-card patch requirements lag the near-immediate skimmer deployment on compromised commerce sites.

CVE-2024-36401.name: OSGeo GeoServer GeoTools Eval Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends an OGC request (WFS/WMS/WPS) whose property name is a JXPath expression; GeoServer evaluates it via the GeoTools/commons-jxpath path and executes arbitrary code.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated against a default GeoServer install)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted OGC request with a public expression payload
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported via the GeoServer/GeoTools GHSA advisory process. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to GeoServer 2.22.6/2.23.6/2.24.4/2.25.2, or as a stopgap remove the gt-complex-x.y.jar, and place OGC endpoints behind authentication/network controls.
prevention.adequacy: The jar-removal workaround can break functionality, so patching is the durable fix; unauthenticated OGC exposure should still be reduced at the boundary.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on OGC request parameters containing exec()/Runtime references and on the GeoServer JVM spawning children.
detection.adequacy: Signatures on expression payloads help but obfuscated JXPath can evade naive rules; process-lineage monitoring is more robust.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, remove dropped miners/webshells, and rebuild given confirmed RCE.
response.adequacy: Widespread commodity exploitation means assume secondary payloads (miners, backdoors) landed.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The 2024-08-05 KEV due date lagged the near-immediate mass exploitation of a preauth RCE affecting every default GeoServer install; SI-2 remediation windows are too slow for this exposure.

CVE-2024-23692.name: Rejetto HTTP File Server Template Engine Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends an HTTP request whose 'search' parameter contains HFS template macros with an empty Host header to bypass filtering; the server evaluates the injected {.exec.}/{.chr.} macros and runs arbitrary OS commands.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, no user interaction)
attack_vector.complexity: low — single crafted HTTP request; weaponized Metasploit module and exploit-db PoCs are public
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by a Positive Technologies researcher. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Migrate off the unsupported 2.3m branch (e.g. to HFS 3.x) or decommission it; restrict the service with authentication and take it off the public Internet.
prevention.adequacy: No patch exists for the EoL branch, so migration/removal — not patching — is the only durable fix.

detection.what_would_have_worked: IPS/WAF signatures for HFS macro tokens and empty Host headers; alerting on hfs.exe spawning cmd.exe/powershell.exe.
detection.adequacy: Signatures help but macro obfuscation via {.chr.} can evade naive rules.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, hunt for dropped miners/loaders and new persistence, rebuild and migrate to a supported file server.
response.adequacy: Reactive; the surface persists until the EoL software is removed.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: SI-2 flaw remediation has no vendor patch to apply for EoL HFS 2.3m; only decommission/migration mitigates.

CVE-2024-38080.name: Microsoft Windows Hyper-V Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local attacker who already has code execution as a standard user triggers an integer overflow in Windows Hyper-V to elevate to SYSTEM, enabling full host compromise.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (authenticated local user)
attack_vector.complexity: low — once the attacker has a foothold, the overflow is triggered locally with no user interaction.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — coordinated disclosure via Microsoft MSRC. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the July 2024 cumulative update and reboot Hyper-V hosts.
prevention.adequacy: Patching is authoritative, but as a local-EoP it is most valuable when paired with strong initial-access prevention so an attacker never reaches the escalation primitive.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Detect standard-user processes acquiring SYSTEM tokens and anomalous Hyper-V driver interaction.
detection.adequacy: Token-elevation detection is high-value but requires deep endpoint telemetry.

response.what_would_have_worked: Contain the affected host, reset credentials exposed to a SYSTEM-level attacker, and verify patch/reboot state.
response.adequacy: SYSTEM-level access implies full host compromise; rebuild may be warranted.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege limits initial access but cannot stop a local user from escalating to SYSTEM once this Hyper-V integer overflow is reachable.

CVE-2024-38112.name: Microsoft Windows MSHTML Platform Spoofing Vulnerability (CVE-2024-38112)

attack_vector.description: A victim opens a crafted .url internet-shortcut whose URL uses the 'mhtml:' scheme and an '!x-usc:' directive; Windows invokes the retired Internet Explorer/MSHTML engine to render attacker-hosted HTML/HTA, spoofing the perceived file type and executing code (Void Banshee delivered the Atlantida stealer this way).
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated remote), requires user to open the .url file
attack_vector.complexity: high — the CVSS vector marks AC:H, reflecting the crafted mhtml/!x-usc handler chain plus required user interaction
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Check Point Research (Haifei Li); campaign analysis by Check Point/Trend Micro. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the July 2024 Windows update and disable/remove the legacy Internet Explorer/MSHTML rendering path and mshta.exe execution via application control.
prevention.adequacy: Patching removes the mhtml handler abuse, but disabling the retired IE engine and mshta is needed to blunt the broader legacy-component attack surface.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Hunt for .url files containing 'mhtml:' and '!x-usc:', and alert on explorer.exe spawning iexplore.exe/mshta.exe followed by HTA execution.
detection.adequacy: The .url lure looks benign, so process-lineage and file-content detection outperform extension-based filtering.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, hunt for the Atlantida stealer / infostealer artifacts, rotate credentials that may have been harvested, and reimage confirmed hits.
response.adequacy: The payload is a credential-stealer, so mass credential rotation is required after any confirmed execution.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality that fully removes/disables the legacy Internet Explorer/MSHTML rendering path would have blunted the technique; leaving the retired engine reachable kept the attack surface alive.

attack_vector.description: An attacker who already holds Administrator credentials passes crafted input as the argument of an affected NX-OS configuration CLI command; insufficient argument validation lets the input execute as an arbitrary root command on the underlying Linux OS, escaping the restricted CLI.
attack_vector.privileges_required: high (authenticated Administrator credentials required)
attack_vector.complexity: low — CVSS AC:L once admin access is held; a crafted CLI argument suffices
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — discovered by Sygnia during a Velvet Ant investigation. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply Cisco's fixed NX-OS release and enforce phishing-resistant MFA plus strict least-privilege on switch admin accounts.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone is insufficient because the real leverage is stolen admin credentials; credential hardening and monitoring are co-equal controls.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Out-of-band monitoring for unexpected root/bash processes on Nexus devices and integrity checks that do not rely on NX-OS command accounting.
detection.adequacy: The exploit runs beneath the CLI-logging layer, so standard command-accounting detection is blind to it.

response.what_would_have_worked: Forensically image affected switches, hunt for Velvet Ant custom malware, rotate all admin credentials, rebuild from verified images.
response.adequacy: State-actor persistence on network gear survives reboots; assume the device is fully compromised.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: System monitoring is undermined because the exploit executes below the NX-OS command-accounting layer, so detection controls relying on CLI logs miss it.

attack_vector.description: An attacker emails a crafted text/xml attachment; when the victim previews it in a vulnerable Roundcube version, attacker JavaScript runs in the authenticated webmail session and can steal mail, contacts, and session tokens.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none for the attacker (unauthenticated email delivery); requires the victim to preview the attachment
attack_vector.complexity: low — send an email; exploitation triggers on preview with a public technique
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported through Roundcube coordinated disclosure. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to Roundcube 1.3.12 / 1.4.5+ which removes text/xml from inline-preview allowed types, and enforce a strict Content-Security-Policy on the webmail app.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the specific vector, but a hardening CSP is needed to blunt the broader class of webmail XSS.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mail-gateway rules flagging XML attachments and monitoring for anomalous mailbox forwarding-rule creation or session-token reuse.
detection.adequacy: Detection helps but the script runs client-side inside the session, so prevention via patch + CSP is primary.

response.what_would_have_worked: Invalidate sessions, reset affected mailbox credentials, and review forwarding rules and sent-mail for exfiltration.
response.adequacy: Because the target is mailbox data, response must assume mail and contacts were read.

UK-CAF-C1.gap: Security monitoring focused on network indicators misses client-side script execution within the webmail DOM, delaying detection of mailbox exfiltration.

attack_vector.description: A local user obtains CAP_NET_ADMIN (often via an unprivileged user namespace), creates an nft object referencing a set in another table, deletes that table to free the set, and reclaims the dangling object to corrupt kernel memory and escalate to root.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local user with CAP_NET_ADMIN in any user or network namespace)
attack_vector.complexity: low — reliable public PoC with high success rates
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by Team Orca of Sea Security with Trend Micro ZDI. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Patch/reboot to the fixed kernel and disable unprivileged user namespaces where not required
prevention.adequacy: Patching removes the UAF; disabling unprivileged userns is the key stopgap until the reboot window.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Auditd/eBPF monitoring for unprivileged-userns + nftables syscall bursts and unexpected uid-0 escalation
detection.adequacy: Kernel-UAF exploitation is fast and quiet; detection is best-effort.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, assume root compromise, rebuild rather than clean
response.adequacy: A root-level kernel compromise cannot be reliably remediated in place.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality controls rarely disable unprivileged user namespaces (kernel.unprivileged_userns_clone), which is what grants the CAP_NET_ADMIN needed to reach the nf_tables path.

CVE-2022-24816.name: OSGeo GeoServer JAI-EXT Code Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a WPS request to the GeoServer WMS/OWS endpoint invoking the ras:Jiffle process with a Jiffle script containing injected Java; the script is compiled by Janino and executed, giving remote code execution on the server.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated on a default GeoServer install)
attack_vector.complexity: low — publicly documented request structure and reproduction environments exist
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to the JAI-EXT/GeoServer projects. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade jt-jiffle/GeoServer to a fixed version, or as a mitigation remove janino-x.y.z.jar from the classpath to disable Jiffle script compilation.
prevention.adequacy: Removing janino stops compilation immediately for operators who cannot upgrade at once; the version bump is the durable fix.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on WPS ras:Jiffle requests to the WMS/OWS endpoint and on the GeoServer JVM spawning child shell processes.
detection.adequacy: The malicious request rides the legitimate WPS surface, so behavioral process-spawn telemetry is the stronger signal.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, hunt for web shells and outbound C2, and rebuild if execution is confirmed.
response.adequacy: Unauth RCE on an internet-facing map server warrants treating the host as compromised.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management must track transitive dependencies (jt-jiffle inside GeoServer); a top-level GeoServer inventory alone misses the vulnerable component.

CVE-2024-4358.name: Progress Telerik Report Server Authentication Bypass by Spoofing Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker reaches the Report Server registration endpoint on an already-configured instance and, via a spoofed request, creates a new administrator account, then commonly chains CVE-2024-1800 deserialization for remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network attacker)
attack_vector.complexity: low — invoking the still-reachable registration endpoint is straightforward.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Soroush Dalili and Sina Kheirkhah. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to Report Server 2024 Q2 (10.1.24.514) or later; restrict the management interface to trusted networks.
prevention.adequacy: Patching closes the registration bypass, but internet-exposed servers were exploited quickly, so network restriction was also needed pre-patch.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on post-setup access to the registration endpoint and on unexpected new administrator accounts.
detection.adequacy: Detection depends on auditing account-creation events that many deployments do not centralize.

response.what_would_have_worked: Remove rogue admin accounts, rotate secrets, and inspect for CVE-2024-1800 deserialization follow-on / web shells.
response.adequacy: Response must assume RCE occurred where the two CVEs were chained.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement failed at the application layer: the registration endpoint remained reachable and trusted a spoofed identity, so no downstream authorization control was consulted.

CVE-2024-26169.name: Microsoft Windows Error Reporting Service Improper Privilege Management Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local low-privileged user manipulates Windows Error Reporting registry values so that the WerFaultSecure service, which runs with a null security descriptor, launches an attacker-controlled process with SYSTEM privileges.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local authenticated user)
attack_vector.complexity: low — reliable registry-based technique proven in Black Basta ransomware intrusions
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — in-the-wild tool analyzed by Symantec; reported to Microsoft. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the March 2024 cumulative update and restrict/monitor writes to the WER registry keys.
prevention.adequacy: Patch removes the null-DACL path; since it was a zero-day for Black Basta, detection was the only pre-patch defense.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR rules for WerFault processes spawning SYSTEM shells and standard-user WER registry edits.
detection.adequacy: This behavior is a strong ransomware pre-encryption indicator.

response.what_would_have_worked: Treat as active ransomware staging: isolate the host, hunt for Black Basta tooling, and review for lateral movement.
response.adequacy: SYSTEM escalation in this chain precedes encryption.

SOC2-CC7-anomaly-detection.gap: Anomaly-detection controls that do not baseline WER registry manipulation and WerFault SYSTEM children miss the exact behavior ransomware operators used.

attack_vector.description: A locally present, crafted application exploits a logic error in the Pixel firmware/Android Framework to bypass a privilege boundary, escalating from an unprivileged context to system-level privileges without additional execution privileges.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (no additional execution privileges) — local access with user interaction
attack_vector.complexity: low — a purpose-built app triggers the logic error; the constraint is local presence on the device.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — fixed by Google/Android security. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install the June 2024 Pixel security update (patch level 2024-06-05) / Android 14 QPR3 fix and restrict sideloaded apps via MDM.
prevention.adequacy: Patching removes the flaw, but the zero-day window before OEM delivery means app-vetting and sideload restrictions are needed as compensating controls.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile threat defense flagging apps that acquire privileges inconsistent with their declared scope.
detection.adequacy: On-device firmware escalation is hard to observe without dedicated MTD tooling.

response.what_would_have_worked: Update affected Pixels, review for unauthorized apps, and re-provision devices suspected of targeted compromise.
response.adequacy: Targeted spyware use may warrant device replacement rather than in-place remediation.

AU-ISM-1546.gap: Mobile-device patching guidance cannot close a firmware logic flaw until the OEM update lands, so the control is insufficient against active zero-day use.

attack_vector.description: On Windows Apache + PHP-CGI with a Best-Fit code page, an unauthenticated attacker sends a request whose soft-hyphen (0xAD) is remapped to '-', injecting PHP-CGI options (auto_prepend_file=php://input, allow_url_include=1) so attacker-supplied PHP executes or script source is disclosed.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network)
attack_vector.complexity: low — trivial once the Best-Fit code-page precondition is met, with public exploit code widely available
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by DEVCORE (Orange Tsai). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade PHP to 8.1.29/8.2.20/8.3.8 and migrate off PHP-CGI to PHP-FPM; if unavoidable, add mod_rewrite rules blocking the encoded option pattern
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes it, but leaving the CGI SAPI in place keeps the fragile Best-Fit surface; FPM migration is the durable fix.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on %AD in query strings, PHP-CGI option strings in requests, and php-cgi.exe spawning shells
detection.adequacy: WAFs not decoding Best-Fit will miss the payload, so host-side process telemetry is important.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate host, hunt for web shells/dropped tooling, rotate credentials reachable from the web root
response.adequacy: Rapid mass exploitation means many hosts were hit before patching completed.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary/WAF rules keyed on literal '-' options miss the Best-Fit 0xAD encoding, so perimeter filtering fails to block the argument injection.

CVE-2024-4610.name: Arm Mali GPU Kernel Driver Use-After-Free Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local, non-privileged app performs improper GPU memory-processing operations against the Mali kernel driver to access already-freed memory, using the use-after-free to corrupt kernel memory and escalate privileges or escape the app sandbox.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local non-privileged app / user)
attack_vector.complexity: low — Arm confirmed real-world exploitation; a crafted app can trigger it locally
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by Arm in its Mali GPU driver bulletin. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update to Mali driver r41p0 via the device's OEM/firmware update; on managed fleets, enforce minimum patch-level / firmware policy and retire devices that no longer receive updates.
prevention.adequacy: The driver fix closes the UAF, but it depends on OEMs shipping it; many devices remain exposed, so device-lifecycle policy is the real control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile EDR/MTD watching for Mali driver crashes, kernel-memory corruption, and apps escalating beyond their sandbox.
detection.adequacy: Kernel-driver UAF exploitation on mobile is stealthy; detection is limited and usually only visible via crash telemetry.

response.what_would_have_worked: Treat a suspected-exploited device as kernel-compromised: wipe/reset, revoke device-bound credentials, and re-enroll.
response.adequacy: Kernel compromise on a mobile device undermines all on-device secrets, so full reset and credential rotation is warranted.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation depends on Arm's fix propagating through SoC vendors and Android OEM update chains, so device patch latency vastly exceeds the exploitation window for this KEV-listed mobile LPE.

CVE-2017-3506.name: Oracle WebLogic Server OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker POSTs a crafted SOAP/XML request to the WebLogic WLS-WSAT endpoint; the server deserializes the work-context XML and, via a ProcessBuilder gadget, executes attacker-supplied OS commands, giving remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network via HTTP)
attack_vector.complexity: high — NVD rates AC:H, but public exploit tooling makes practical exploitation reliable against exposed servers
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported via Oracle PSIRT by human researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Oracle April 2017 CPU; remove or restrict the WLS-WSAT subcomponent and block the /wls-wsat/ path at the perimeter.
prevention.adequacy: Patching fixes it, but leaving wls-wsat enabled reintroduces risk via related SOAP flaws; disabling the subcomponent is the durable control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on POSTs to wls-wsat with XMLDecoder/ProcessBuilder markers and on the WebLogic JVM spawning shell processes.
detection.adequacy: Detection is reactive; a single crafted request already runs commands.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the WebLogic host, remove dropped cryptominers/webshells, and rotate credentials reachable from the server.
response.adequacy: Given years of exploitation, assume prior compromise and hunt for established persistence.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality guidance rarely mandates disabling/removing the unused WLS-WSAT (SOAP) subcomponent, leaving the command-injection endpoint enabled by default.

attack_vector.description: An unprivileged local user (often via an unprivileged user namespace) crafts nf_tables rules that make nf_hook_slow() double-free on an NF_DROP verdict carrying a drop error resembling NF_ACCEPT, corrupting kernel memory to escalate to root.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local unprivileged user, user namespaces enabled)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a reliable public exploit ('Flipping Pages') bypasses KASLR/SMEP/SMAP across multiple kernels
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Mingi Cho (Theori); public exploit by Notselwyn. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade past commit f342de4e2f33 (or apply distro kernel updates/livepatch) and set kernel.unprivileged_userns_clone=0 to remove the reachable attack surface
prevention.adequacy: Patch removes the UAF, but reboot lag means disabling unprivileged user namespaces is the fast compensating control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unprivileged processes gaining UID 0 after nf_tables netlink activity and on kernel oops referencing nft_verdict_init/nf_hook_slow
detection.adequacy: In-kernel corruption offers limited userland telemetry, so namespace/netlink monitoring is the main signal.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, rotate credentials, and hunt for ransomware staging following root escalation
response.adequacy: CISA flags ransomware use, so root escalation should be treated as an active intrusion.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality controls rarely disable unprivileged user namespaces (user.max_user_namespaces), leaving the nf_tables attack surface reachable to any local user.

CVE-2024-24919.name: Check Point Quantum Security Gateways Information Disclosure Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a path-traversal request to the gateway's MyCRL endpoint and reads arbitrary appliance files, harvesting credentials and configuration that enable follow-on VPN/lateral access.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, internet-facing gateway with VPN blades enabled)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted POST with a traversal payload
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by Check Point after in-the-wild exploitation. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Check Point sk182336 hotfix, then rotate all local accounts, service accounts, and certificates on the gateway because credentials may already be stolen.
prevention.adequacy: Patching stops the read, but is insufficient alone — any credentials exposed before patching must be assumed compromised and rotated.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Inspect gateway logs for MyCRL traversal requests and monitor for anomalous VPN/lateral logins using gateway-resident credentials.
detection.adequacy: The file read leaves sparse logs; detection often depends on catching the downstream credential reuse.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate all secrets on affected gateways, review VPN session logs, and hunt for lateral movement from harvested accounts.
response.adequacy: Because ransomware actors used this for access, treat any pre-patch exposure as a credential breach.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection is undermined because the vulnerable component IS the boundary device; SC-7 assumes the gateway is trustworthy, but the traversal turns the VPN concentrator into an unauthenticated file-read oracle.

CVE-2024-4978.name: JAVS Viewer Installer Embedded Malicious Code Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Users downloaded a trojanized JAVS Viewer 8.3.7 installer from the vendor's supply chain; the installer dropped a malicious fffmpeg.exe that beaconed to a C2 server and executed unauthorized PowerShell, granting attacker control of the courtroom recording host.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none from the victim's side beyond running the vendor installer (the CVSS PR:H reflects installer/admin context)
attack_vector.complexity: low — running the signed vendor installer is sufficient; no additional exploitation needed
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — investigated and disclosed by Rapid7. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Reinstall from a verified-clean JAVS build, verify authenticode signer continuity before deploying vendor updates, and application-control on binary provenance.
prevention.adequacy: Updating alone is insufficient — affected hosts must be reimaged and credentials rotated because a backdoor already executed.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Hash-block fffmpeg.exe (SHA256 421a4ad2...), alert on unexpected authenticode signer for JAVS binaries, and EDR on installer-spawned PowerShell/C2.
detection.adequacy: Detection requires the published IOCs, which post-date initial distribution.

response.what_would_have_worked: Reimage affected endpoints, rotate all credentials used on those hosts, and hunt for C2 beacons from the JAVS fleet.
response.adequacy: Software update does not remove an already-installed backdoor; reimage is required.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: SI-3 malicious-code protection trusted a signed, vendor-distributed installer and failed to catch the embedded supply-chain backdoor.

CVE-2024-5274.name: Google Chromium V8 Type Confusion Vulnerability (CVE-2024-5274)

attack_vector.description: A user visits a crafted HTML page that triggers a type confusion in V8; the attacker gains code execution and, per the scope-changed CVSS, can affect resources beyond the renderer sandbox.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated), user interaction required to visit the page
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single malicious page reaches the flaw; exploitation is drive-by.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Google TAG and Chrome Security. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Force-update Chrome to 125.0.6422.112+ and enforce browser relaunch fleet-wide.
prevention.adequacy: The update is inert until the browser is relaunched; deployment must be paired with enforced restart.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for renderer-process crashes and abnormal browser child processes.
detection.adequacy: Crash telemetry is post-hoc and rarely catches a clean exploit.

response.what_would_have_worked: Investigate hosts that visited attacker-controlled pages and confirm post-update browser version.
response.adequacy: Response depends on browsing history that is often unavailable.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The gap between Google's emergency release and enterprise browser-relaunch rollout leaves a window that TAG-observed in-the-wild exploitation targets.

CVE-2020-17519.name: Apache Flink Improper Access Control Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a GET to the Flink JobManager REST logs endpoint with double-URL-encoded '../' sequences, causing the process to return the contents of any file it can read (config, credentials, /etc/passwd).
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, no auth on the Flink REST interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted GET request, widely automated
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to the Apache Flink project. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to Flink 1.11.3/1.12.0 and remove the JobManager REST/dashboard from public networks (reverse-proxy auth, network ACLs on port 8081).
prevention.adequacy: Patching fixes the traversal, but because Flink's REST interface is unauthenticated by design, network isolation remains necessary to prevent other exposure.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on '..%252f' sequences to /jobmanager/logs and on the Flink REST port being reachable from untrusted networks.
detection.adequacy: The double-encoding is a strong signature, but internet-exposed dashboards are scanned continuously, so exposure reduction matters more than detection.

response.what_would_have_worked: Assume any secrets/config readable by the JobManager were exfiltrated; rotate credentials and keys, and review access logs for traversal reads.
response.adequacy: File read is limited to the process's access, but that often includes datastore credentials and key material warranting rotation.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: The Flink JobManager REST/dashboard (default 8081) is frequently exposed to the internet; boundary protection is the primary missing control since Flink offers no built-in auth on this interface.

CVE-2024-4947.name: Google Chromium V8 Type Confusion Vulnerability (CVE-2024-4947)

attack_vector.description: A victim visits an attacker-controlled page (a fake blockchain/game site in the observed campaign) that runs crafted JavaScript triggering a V8 type confusion, corrupting the heap and executing code inside the renderer sandbox.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; requires the victim to load a malicious page — CVSS UI:R)
attack_vector.complexity: low — CVSS AC:L; a crafted HTML page triggers the type confusion
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Kaspersky researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update Chrome/Chromium browsers to 125.0.6422.60+ with auto-update enforced; block the campaign's fake-game delivery domains.
prevention.adequacy: Patching helps only after disclosure; the pre-patch zero-day window and reliance on user visits require layered browser hardening.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Web-proxy/DNS blocking of the malicious campaign infrastructure and EDR on renderer exploitation follow-on.
detection.adequacy: In-renderer exploitation is hard to observe directly; detection is most effective at the delivery-domain and post-exploitation stages.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force browser updates, hunt for follow-on payloads on hosts that visited the campaign sites, reset exposed credentials.
response.adequacy: Sandbox-contained execution may still stage further exploitation; scope follow-on activity.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Endpoint malicious-code protection does not reliably stop in-renderer memory-corruption execution delivered from a legitimate-looking site.

CVE-2023-43208.name: NextGen Healthcare Mirth Connect Deserialization of Untrusted Data Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted serialized payload to an internet-facing Mirth Connect endpoint; unsafe deserialization leads to OS command execution as the service account, bypassing the incomplete fix for CVE-2023-37679.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network attacker)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted request with a public Horizon3.ai PoC available
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — discovered by Horizon3.ai. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to Mirth Connect 4.4.1+ and restrict the interface listener to trusted HL7 peers via network segmentation.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is necessary but because the prior fix was incomplete, teams must verify the specific 4.4.1 build and not assume an earlier hotfix sufficed.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on the Mirth Connect Java process spawning shell/command interpreters and on deserialization-gadget payloads in HTTP bodies.
detection.adequacy: Given ransomware association, rapid detection of post-exploitation child processes is critical.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, rotate integration and database credentials, assess PHI exposure for breach-notification, and hunt for ransomware precursors.
response.adequacy: PHI exposure triggers regulatory breach-notification duties alongside eviction.

GDPR-Art32.gap: Article 32 'appropriate technical measures' for PHI were undermined by an unauthenticated RCE that grants full access to health-data flows passing through the integration engine.

CVE-2024-4761.name: Google Chromium V8 Out-of-Bounds Memory Write Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A victim visits an attacker-controlled or compromised web page whose crafted JavaScript triggers an out-of-bounds write in V8, corrupting renderer memory; chained with a sandbox escape this yields code execution on the host.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated); user interaction required (victim loads the page)
attack_vector.complexity: high — reliable V8 heap exploitation requires precise memory shaping, but was operationalized as a zero-day
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Google's Chrome security team. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Force Chrome/Chromium update to 124.0.6367.207+ and relaunch all browser sessions and embedded Chromium runtimes
prevention.adequacy: Auto-update helps but only takes effect on relaunch; embedded Chromium apps must be tracked separately.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on renderer crashes and unexpected child processes spawned from the browser
detection.adequacy: Sandbox-escape chains may leave little host-side signal before code execution.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate affected endpoints, hunt for post-exploitation persistence, and force browser relaunch fleet-wide
response.adequacy: Drive-by delivery means many endpoints may be hit simultaneously.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management often omits embedded Chromium runtimes (Electron apps, WebView) that inherit the same V8 flaw but do not auto-update with the browser.

CVE-2021-40655.name: D-Link DIR-605 Router Information Disclosure Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker forges a POST request to /getcfg.php requesting the device account service; the router returns the administrator credentials in cleartext, which are then reused to take over the device.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted request returns the credentials; a public PoC exists
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — public researcher disclosure with PoC. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Retire and replace the EoL DIR-605; until then, block WAN-side access to the management interface and never expose /getcfg.php to the internet.
prevention.adequacy: No patch exists, so remediation is device replacement; network-boundary controls only reduce exposure.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Deploy the FortiGuard getcfg.php information-disclosure IPS signature and alert on unauthenticated /getcfg.php requests.
detection.adequacy: Consumer routers lack native logging; upstream IPS/network monitoring is the only practical detection.

response.what_would_have_worked: Assume credentials are compromised, change them where the device is retained, and replace the hardware.
response.adequacy: Since credentials are disclosed directly, rotation on a still-vulnerable device is only temporary; replacement is required.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement fails at the endpoint: /getcfg.php returns sensitive account data without authenticating or authorizing the requester.

CVE-2014-100005.name: D-Link DIR-600 Router Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker lures an authenticated router administrator to a malicious page that auto-submits forged requests to hedwig.cgi/pigwidgeon.cgi/diagnostic.php, hijacking the admin session to create accounts, enable remote management, or change settings.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none for the attacker, but relies on hijacking an authenticated administrator's session (UI/browsing required)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a static crafted HTML page performs the forged requests.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — publicly disclosed by an independent researcher in 2014. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Retire and replace the EOL DIR-600; where still in use, disable remote management and avoid administering the router from a browser used for general web access.
prevention.adequacy: No patch exists for these EOL revisions, so only device replacement fully removes the risk.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for configuration changes and new admin accounts on the router and for cross-origin requests to its CGI endpoints.
detection.adequacy: Consumer/SMB routers rarely emit centralized logs, limiting detection.

response.what_would_have_worked: Factory-reset, disable remote management, and replace the device; audit for unauthorized firmware/config.
response.adequacy: Reset is temporary on EOL hardware that remains vulnerable to re-exploitation.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The affected DIR-600 revisions are end-of-life with no forthcoming fix, so flaw-remediation via patching is impossible and the only remedy is device retirement.

CVE-2024-30040.name: Microsoft Windows MSHTML Platform Security Feature Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker delivers a crafted document that, when opened, causes the MSHTML platform to bypass OLE/mitigation prompts and load malicious content, leading to code execution in the user's context.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (attacker-side), but requires the victim to open the file
attack_vector.complexity: low — social-engineering a document open; reliable once delivered
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Microsoft by vendor/anonymous researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the May 2024 cumulative update and block/disable OLE object loading in Office plus attachment filtering.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the bypass; app-hardening helps but the flaw specifically defeats the MSHTML feature protections.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Detect Office/host apps spawning script interpreters or making outbound requests after opening a document.
detection.adequacy: Process-lineage detection catches post-open execution even against a feature bypass.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate affected hosts, pull the lure document, and hunt for follow-on payloads.
response.adequacy: Bypass is typically the first stage of a multi-step intrusion.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection that relies on OLE/MSHTML mitigations is exactly what this security-feature bypass defeats, so signature/heuristic AV on the mitigation layer is insufficient.

CVE-2024-30051.name: Microsoft DWM Core Library Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Local code triggers a heap buffer overflow in the DWM Core Library caused by an integer-division size miscalculation in CCommandBuffer::Initialize, corrupting heap memory to gain SYSTEM privileges; QakBot chained it after initial access.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local authenticated user)
attack_vector.complexity: low — reliable public PoCs exist and the flaw was weaponized in commodity malware.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Kaspersky/DBAPPSecurity/Google TAG researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Deploy the May 2024 Windows cumulative update and pair it with EDR that blocks the QakBot delivery chain feeding the exploit.
prevention.adequacy: Patching removes the overflow, but hosts compromised before patching may already have SYSTEM-level implants requiring cleanup.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on dwmcore.dll heap corruption and on local processes acquiring SYSTEM tokens.
detection.adequacy: Memory-corruption escalation is only visible with endpoint exploit-protection telemetry, not network monitoring alone.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate infected hosts, eradicate QakBot, and rotate credentials exposed to SYSTEM-level access.
response.adequacy: Given ransomware precursor use, response must assume broader intrusion and check for lateral movement.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: This was an actively exploited zero-day at patch release; any remediation cadence slower than the May 2024 Patch Tuesday left endpoints escalatable to SYSTEM during live QakBot campaigns.

attack_vector.description: A crafted HTML page (typically after compromising the renderer) triggers a use-after-free in Chrome's Visuals component, corrupting heap state to attempt escape from the browser sandbox.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; requires the victim to load attacker-controlled content, and a renderer compromise for the full escape)
attack_vector.complexity: high — chained renderer-compromise plus a reliable UAF sandbox escape, consistent with targeted zero-day use
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by an anonymous researcher to Google. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Force Chrome/Chromium update to 124.0.6367.201+ across the fleet and restart browsers
prevention.adequacy: Auto-update helps but only after browser restart; unmanaged endpoints remain exposed during the active zero-day window.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for renderer crashes and anomalous browser child processes/network callbacks
detection.adequacy: Sandbox-escape exploits leave minimal disk artifacts, limiting detection.

response.what_would_have_worked: Confirm browser versions fleet-wide, isolate suspected-compromised hosts, hunt for post-escape implants
response.adequacy: Targeted zero-day usage may precede any public IoCs, so version assurance is the primary lever.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation depends on the Chrome auto-update reaching every endpoint; unmanaged or air-gapped browsers lag the fix while an active zero-day is in circulation.

CVE-2023-7028.name: GitLab Community and Enterprise Editions Improper Access Control Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker submits the GitLab password-reset form supplying multiple email addresses; GitLab delivers the reset link to the attacker-controlled unverified address, letting the attacker set a new password and take over the account when 2FA is absent.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to the GitLab web interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted reset request; public PoCs and mass scanning
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported through GitLab's bug-bounty program. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to the fixed 16.x patch releases; enforce 2FA on all accounts so a hijacked reset email alone cannot complete takeover.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the reset-redirect, but universally-enforced MFA is what neutralizes the impact even on unpatched instances.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on /users/password requests carrying multiple email parameters and on reset emails sent to addresses differing from the account owner's verified email.
detection.adequacy: The abuse is a valid-looking HTTP request; log-based detection of the array-email pattern is the most reliable signal.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force password resets, invalidate sessions and personal-access-tokens/SSH keys, and review repositories for unauthorized clones or CI/CD changes.
response.adequacy: Account takeover on a source platform is a supply-chain event, so response must assume code and secret exposure.

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: Password-only authentication is fully defeated once the reset email is redirected; without enforced MFA the identity control permits complete takeover, so it is inadequate against this flaw.

CVE-2024-29988.name: Microsoft SmartScreen Prompt Security Feature Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker delivers a file (often inside an archive) crafted so that Windows fails to apply or honor the Mark-of-the-Web; when the user extracts and runs it, SmartScreen does not prompt, and the payload executes. It is chained with WinRAR CVE-2023-38831 and CVE-2024-21412 for full delivery.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (requires user to open the delivered file — UI:R)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoCs and active tradecraft, though it depends on social-engineering the user to open the file
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found in-the-wild by Trend Micro ZDI. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the April 2024 Windows update; block risky archive/file types at the mail/web gateway and enforce application allowlisting so a MotW-bypassed payload still cannot execute.
prevention.adequacy: Patching restores the prompt, but because the file still relies on user execution, allowlisting/attachment controls are needed as defense in depth.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Detect internet-origin files executing without a SmartScreen prompt and archive-extraction-to-execution chains associated with Water Hydra/DarkMe.
detection.adequacy: Detection catches the delivery chain but the bypass removes the built-in user warning.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the endpoint, remove DarkMe/dropper artifacts, and hunt for the WinRAR/CVE-2024-21412 chain components across the fleet.
response.adequacy: Response addresses infected hosts but the initial user-execution vector remains a persistent risk without patch + hardening.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection that relies on SmartScreen/MotW mediation is defeated by a bypass that suppresses the very prompt operators expect to warn on internet-origin files.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a request with server-side template-injection syntax to the CrushFTP WebInterface; the server renders the expression, escaping the VFS sandbox to read arbitrary files (config/session data), bypass authentication to gain admin, and execute code.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, internet-facing)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoCs and a Metasploit module make exploitation turnkey
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by CrushFTP and analyzed by Airbus CERT/Rapid7. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to CrushFTP 10.7.1 / 11.1.0+ and place the WebInterface behind a DMZ/reverse proxy restricting access
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the SSTI, but exposed MFT appliances need network restriction because exploitation began before many could patch.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Detect template-expression syntax in WebInterface requests and out-of-sandbox file reads, plus anomalous new admin sessions
detection.adequacy: Preauth exploitation is fast, so request-content and session-anomaly detection are the practical signals.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate CrushFTP admin credentials and session keys, audit for rogue admin accounts, and hunt for exfiltrated files
response.adequacy: Admin bypass enables persistence, so credential rotation and account audit are required post-patch.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary controls leave the CrushFTP WebInterface publicly reachable, and the SSTI rides normal HTTPS so a firewall does not stop it.

CVE-2024-20359.name: Cisco ASA and FTD Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker holding administrator credentials copies a crafted file to the disk0: filesystem; on the next device reload the improperly-validated file executes as root and persists across reboots.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low relative to the outcome — administrator-level access on the device is required, but it escalates to persistent root
attack_vector.complexity: high — requires prior administrator compromise and a device reload, consistent with the targeted ArcaneDoor operation
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by Cisco Talos during the ArcaneDoor investigation. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the fixed ASA/FTD release, verify image integrity, and tightly control and monitor administrator access to prevent the prerequisite admin foothold.
prevention.adequacy: Patching blocks new exploitation, but a device compromised before patching keeps its persistent root implant; integrity verification and possible rebuild are required.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Run Cisco's forensic image-integrity checks (verify, show memory region) to spot Line Runner artifacts and unexpected disk0: files.
detection.adequacy: Root persistence can subvert on-box logging, so out-of-band integrity verification is the reliable detection.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the appliance, capture forensic images, rebuild from known-good software, and rotate all device and admin credentials.
response.adequacy: Given a state-sponsored persistent implant, assume full device compromise and rebuild rather than clean in place.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least privilege assumes administrator accounts are trustworthy, but this flaw lets an admin-level actor escalate to persistent root code execution — a privilege boundary AC-6 does not enforce within the appliance.

CVE-2024-20353.name: Cisco ASA and FTD Web Services Denial of Service Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker sends a crafted HTTP request to the ASA/FTD management or VPN web server; incomplete HTTP-header error checking triggers an infinite loop (CWE-835) that reloads the device, causing denial of service. In ArcaneDoor it was chained with CVE-2024-20359 for implant persistence.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, no user interaction)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted HTTP request to an exposed web service triggers the reload
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified by Cisco PSIRT/Talos during the ArcaneDoor investigation. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade ASA/FTD to the fixed releases in cisco-sa-asaftd-websrvs-dos-X8gNucD2; disable HTTP management and restrict WebVPN/management access to trusted sources.
prevention.adequacy: Patching is necessary but the management/VPN web services should also be restricted, since the appliance is the boundary control itself.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for unexpected device reloads/crash loops, review syslog for the web-server process faults, and hunt for Line Dancer/Line Runner implant indicators.
detection.adequacy: Reload alerts detect the DoS but the paired implant activity requires deeper forensic review.

response.what_would_have_worked: Follow Cisco's ArcaneDoor forensic guidance (memory capture, integrity verification), rebuild compromised devices, and rotate credentials/certs.
response.adequacy: A reload alone may mask implant persistence; forensic validation is required, not just a reboot.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: SC-7 treats the firewall as the boundary, but the vulnerable web service runs on that same edge appliance, so the control cannot protect the device that enforces it.

CVE-2022-38028.name: Microsoft Windows Print Spooler Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: After gaining an initial foothold, APT28's GooseEgg tool modifies a Print Spooler JavaScript constraints file and causes the Spooler service to execute it with SYSTEM permissions, spawning attacker-chosen applications, backdoors, and credential-theft tooling.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (authenticated local user / existing foothold)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the GooseEgg launcher automates the escalation with no user interaction.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Microsoft by the NSA and analyzed by Microsoft Threat Intelligence. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the October 2022 Print Spooler update and disable the Print Spooler service on systems that do not need to print.
prevention.adequacy: Patching alone is insufficient culturally because organizations deferred this 7.8 EoP for over a year; disabling the Spooler where unneeded is the durable control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on GooseEgg artifacts (execute.bat/doit.bat, wayzgoose*.dll in the spooler drivers path), Print Spooler spawning SYSTEM child processes, and new scheduled tasks tied to the escalation.
detection.adequacy: The Microsoft report provides concrete file and behavioral IOCs, but detection is post-escalation.

response.what_would_have_worked: Contain the host, remove GooseEgg persistence, rotate credentials exposed to a SYSTEM-level actor, and hunt laterally given APT28 attribution.
response.adequacy: SYSTEM access by a state actor implies broad compromise; assume-breach hunting is warranted.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Print Spooler should be disabled on servers that do not print; leaving the service enabled by default preserves the escalation surface this control is meant to remove.

CVE-2024-3400.name: Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends GlobalProtect requests with a crafted SESSID cookie whose value is written to disk as an attacker-controlled filename; a later process executes that content as an OS command with root privileges. Observed chains deploy the UPSTYLE Python backdoor that reads the device error log for further commands.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, internet-facing GlobalProtect portal/gateway)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a crafted HTTP request with a malicious SESSID cookie; public PoCs and a Metasploit module exist
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found in incident response by Volexity (UTA0218), analyzed by Palo Alto Unit 42. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the PAN-OS hotfix (10.2.9-h1/11.0.4-h1/11.1.2-h3 or later); before the fix, enable the vendor Threat Prevention IDs and disable device telemetry as interim mitigation.
prevention.adequacy: Patching is definitive, but because the flaw was a live zero-day, the interim threat-signature and telemetry-disable mitigations were needed before hotfixes were broadly available.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Hunt for anomalous files under GlobalProtect/device-telemetry temp paths, crafted SESSID cookie values, UPSTYLE artifacts, and outbound connections from the management plane.
detection.adequacy: The backdoor manipulates on-box logs, so external/network telemetry is more trustworthy than the appliance's own logs.

response.what_would_have_worked: Run Palo Alto's forensic guidance / customer support 'Tech Support File' analysis, assume full device compromise, rotate all secrets and certificates on the firewall, and rebuild from known-good firmware.
response.adequacy: Root RCE on the perimeter device means every credential and key it held must be considered exposed.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: The compromised device IS the network boundary; boundary protection cannot compensate when the firewall's own preauth GlobalProtect endpoint yields root RCE.

CVE-2024-3273.name: D-Link Multiple NAS Devices Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted HTTP GET to /cgi-bin/nas_sharing.cgi using the hardcoded 'messagebus' backdoor account (CVE-2024-3272) with a base64-encoded command in the 'system' parameter; the value is passed to a shell and executed as root, and botnets weaponized it to drop Mirai.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; hardcoded backdoor account requires no password)
attack_vector.complexity: low — CVSS AC:L; a single crafted GET request, publicly scripted
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — publicly disclosed by researcher 'netsecfish'. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Retire and replace the EoL D-Link NAS devices; if temporarily retained, remove them from any internet exposure and firewall the management interface.
prevention.adequacy: No patch is available, so only decommissioning or full network isolation removes the risk; blocking alone at the perimeter is fragile against misconfiguration.

detection.what_would_have_worked: IDS/WAF signatures for GET /cgi-bin/nas_sharing.cgi with user=messagebus and a base64 system= parameter; monitoring for Mirai C2 egress from NAS subnets.
detection.adequacy: Signatures are effective given the fixed URI/parameter pattern, but only if the device path is visible to inspection.

response.what_would_have_worked: Take exposed NAS offline, wipe/replace hardware, hunt for Mirai persistence and lateral movement from the storage subnet.
response.adequacy: Compromised EoL firmware cannot be trusted after infection; replacement is the durable response.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: No patch exists for these EoL NAS devices, so flaw remediation cannot apply; the only fix is retirement, which SI-2's patch framing does not compel.

CVE-2024-3272.name: D-Link Multiple NAS Devices Use of Hard-Coded Credentials Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker sends an HTTP GET to /cgi-bin/nas_sharing.cgi using the hardcoded 'messagebus' account (empty password); chained with the companion command-injection flaw this yields unauthenticated remote code execution on end-of-life D-Link NAS devices.
