attack_vector.privileges_required: none — the hardcoded credential removes any real authentication requirement
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted GET request with public exploit code
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by researcher netsecfish (VDB-259283). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Retire and replace the affected DNS-320L/325/327L/340L units and, until then, remove all internet exposure (disable UPnP port-forwarding, firewall the management interface).
prevention.adequacy: No patch exists, so prevention depends entirely on decommissioning or full network isolation — patch-centric programs have no valid action here.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on inbound requests to nas_sharing.cgi carrying user=messagebus or base64 system parameters, and monitor NAS devices for outbound botnet C2.
detection.adequacy: Detection is a stopgap; the device cannot be fixed, so the finding should drive removal, not just alerting.

response.what_would_have_worked: Take the device offline, assume full compromise of stored data, rebuild any accounts/keys stored on it, and replace the hardware.
response.adequacy: Because the credential is unpatchable, only physical retirement ends the exposure.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: No patch will ever ship — the devices are end-of-life — so flaw-remediation is unattainable; the only remediation is retirement, which asset-owners routinely fail to perform for cheap NAS boxes.

attack_vector.description: An attacker with local/physical access interrupts a factory reset initiated by a device-admin app, exploiting a logic error so the wipe does not complete, and gains local privilege escalation — defeating remote-wipe/data-protection assurances.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none additional (local access); user interaction required
attack_vector.complexity: low — a logic error triggered by interrupting the reset flow, no memory-corruption primitive
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by the GrapheneOS project. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the April 2024 Pixel security update (2024-04-01 patch level) fleet-wide
prevention.adequacy: Patching fixes the logic error; organizations relying on remote wipe should not assume a wipe succeeded on unpatched devices.

detection.what_would_have_worked: MDM alerting on failed/incomplete factory resets and out-of-date Pixel patch levels
detection.adequacy: Physical/forensic exploitation leaves little enterprise-visible signal.

response.what_would_have_worked: Treat any lost/seized unpatched Pixel as potentially unwiped; rotate credentials and revoke device access
response.adequacy: Data may already be extractable if the device was unpatched when it left custody.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch-application timeframes do not account for mobile-OS/firmware, where forensic-extraction exploitation of an unpatched wipe bypass is the real risk.

attack_vector.description: With physical possession of a Pixel in the After-First-Unlock state, an attacker reboots it into fastboot mode where USB access is enabled before memory is zeroed, then dumps uninitialized fastboot memory to recover data that the lock screen would otherwise protect.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none in software, but requires physical access to the device
attack_vector.complexity: high — requires physical possession and specialized forensic tooling
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Google with GrapheneOS analysis. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the April 2024 Pixel update (which zeroes memory before enabling USB in fastboot) and power devices fully off — not merely lock — when physical seizure is a risk.
prevention.adequacy: The firmware fix closes the memory leak, but until patched the only mitigation is a full power-off (Before-First-Unlock state), since a locked-but-booted device remains exposed.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Detection is impractical for physical forensic extraction; MDM policies can enforce power-off and short lock timeouts to shrink the window.
detection.adequacy: There is no device-side telemetry for offline fastboot memory dumps.

response.what_would_have_worked: Treat a seized/lost unpatched device as data-compromised; rotate any credentials or tokens it stored.
response.adequacy: Because extraction is offline and silent, response must assume disclosure occurred.

GDPR-Art32.gap: Security-of-processing controls assume lock-screen protection preserves data confidentiality; the fastboot memory leak undermines confidentiality of personal data on a seized device despite a locked screen.

CVE-2023-24955.name: Microsoft SharePoint Server Code Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker holding Site Owner privileges (often obtained by first exploiting CVE-2023-29357's auth bypass) injects code through a crafted SharePoint API/BDC path that SharePoint evaluates server-side, achieving remote code execution used in ransomware operations.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (Site Owner) — but effectively none when chained with CVE-2023-29357
attack_vector.complexity: low — the chained public technique is reliable once the auth bypass grants Site Owner context.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — demonstrated by STAR Labs (Jang) at Pwn2Own Vancouver 2023. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the SharePoint security updates for both CVE-2023-24955 and CVE-2023-29357; limit internet exposure and enforce least privilege on Site Owner roles.
prevention.adequacy: Patching both chained CVEs is required; patching only one leaves the composite unauthenticated-RCE path partially open.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on SharePoint worker-process (w3wp.exe) spawning command interpreters and on CVE-2023-29357-style auth-bypass API calls.
detection.adequacy: Requires process-lineage and SharePoint API telemetry that default logging does not provide.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the server, rotate machine keys/service credentials, and hunt for ransomware staging and web shells given the Known ransomware association.
response.adequacy: Because this CVE is tied to ransomware chains, response must assume attempted domain-wide impact.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Ransomware operators weaponized this SharePoint RCE, and the observed exploitation cadence outpaced the flaw-remediation SLAs many operators apply to on-prem SharePoint.

CVE-2019-7256.name: Nice Linear eMerge E3-Series OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker sends a crafted HTTP request to an eMerge E3 web endpoint that passes a parameter into a shell command without sanitization, injecting OS commands that execute on the panel and enable botnet enrollment or full takeover.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network-reachable)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public single-request exploit, heavily automated by botnets
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by an independent researcher (Applied Risk). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply vendor firmware updates and remove the eMerge E3 web interface from internet exposure behind a segmented OT network/VPN.
prevention.adequacy: Many panels never receive firmware fixes, so network isolation is the durable control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on shell metacharacters in requests to the panel and on outbound downloads from the physical-security VLAN.
detection.adequacy: Egress monitoring flags botnet enrollment even when firmware stays vulnerable.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate and re-image affected panels, block the endpoints at the perimeter, and audit the OT segment.
response.adequacy: Re-imaging is only durable with patched firmware plus segmentation.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: eMerge E3 access-control panels are frequently exposed directly to the internet; boundary controls that would block the CGI endpoints are commonly absent for physical-security appliances.

CVE-2021-44529.name: Ivanti Endpoint Manager Cloud Service Appliance (EPM CSA) Code Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted request to the CSA web interface (/client/index.php), which passes attacker input to code-execution routines via csrf-magic.php, running arbitrary code as the low-privileged 'nobody' user as an initial foothold.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a Metasploit module and Exploit-DB entry automate the unauthenticated RCE.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by researcher Jakub Kramarz. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade the CSA to 4.6.0-512 or later and remove the management interface from internet exposure.
prevention.adequacy: Patching closes the injection, but appliances exposed for years may already be compromised, so the CSA should be assumed breached and rebuilt.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor CSA web logs for unauthenticated POSTs to /client/index.php and for shell activity spawned under 'nobody'.
detection.adequacy: Appliance-native logging is limited, so exploitation may only be visible via network or downstream host telemetry.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate and rebuild the appliance, rotate managed-endpoint credentials, and hunt for privilege-escalation and persistence following the initial foothold.
response.adequacy: The 'nobody' foothold is routinely chained to escalation and lateral movement, so response must extend beyond the appliance itself.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection that exposes the CSA web interface to the internet leaves the unauthenticated injection endpoint directly reachable.

CVE-2023-48788.name: Fortinet FortiClient EMS SQL Injection Vulnerability (CVE-2023-48788)

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends specially crafted packets to the FortiClient EMS FmDatabaseServer (TCP/8013) with SQL injected into the FCTUID field, enabling and invoking xp_cmdshell to run arbitrary commands as SYSTEM on the EMS host.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network)
attack_vector.complexity: low — reliable public PoC/Metasploit coverage once TCP/8013 is reachable
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Fortinet by Microsoft researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to FortiClientEMS 7.2.3/7.0.11+ and restrict TCP/8013 (FmDatabaseServer) to trusted management networks
prevention.adequacy: Patch is essential; network segmentation of the EMS DB service is a needed compensating control given known ransomware exploitation.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on the EMS/MSSQL process spawning cmd/powershell and on xp_cmdshell being enabled
detection.adequacy: Requires process-lineage and SQL-audit telemetry not enabled by default on many EMS hosts.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate EMS host, disable xp_cmdshell, rotate credentials/managed-endpoint trust, hunt for web shells and lateral movement
response.adequacy: EMS controls many endpoints, so a compromise can pivot fleet-wide and demands broad containment.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection frequently leaves the EMS FmDatabaseServer TCP/8013 reachable; exposing that service to untrusted networks is the precondition for exploitation.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated request to a bogus path with a jsp= parameter pointing at an authenticated REST endpoint bypasses auth; the attacker then creates an admin account/token and uploads a malicious plugin to achieve RCE on the build server.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network-reachable web interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — single crafted HTTP request, reliable public exploit
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Rapid7 (Stephen Fewer). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to 2023.11.4 immediately and restrict the TeamCity management interface to VPN/allowlisted networks
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the bypass, but any server exposed pre-patch must be treated as compromised (rotate tokens, audit users/plugins) — patching alone does not evict an established foothold or clean poisoned build artifacts.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on new admin users/tokens, plugin uploads, and 200 responses to normally-authenticated REST paths from unauthenticated sessions
detection.adequacy: Detection catches post-exploitation admin actions but not the initial single-request bypass.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate all TeamCity tokens/credentials, remove unknown plugins and admin accounts, and review downstream build/deploy artifacts for tampering
response.adequacy: Because CI/CD compromise is a supply-chain event, response must extend to every artifact built after first exploitation.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: A 30-day flaw-remediation SLA is far slower than the observed window: internet-facing TeamCity servers were mass-exploited within days of disclosure, so periodic patch cycles cannot close this before compromise.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Coordinated vulnerability-management requirements assume time to triage; they do not mandate the emergency out-of-band patching that a preauth-to-admin CI/CD bypass with public exploit demands.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security expectations do not by themselves require removing CI/CD management interfaces from direct internet exposure, which is what actually blocks unauthenticated reach to this endpoint.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential-Eight patch maturity targets (48h for internet-facing) still trail the same-day exploitation seen here, and do not address the supply-chain blast (poisoned build artifacts) from an owned CI/CD server.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management as an annual/periodic control does not compel the network segmentation that would keep an unauthenticated attacker off the TeamCity admin surface.

CVE-2024-23225.name: Apple Multiple Products Memory Corruption Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: As a late stage of a targeted exploit chain, an attacker who already holds arbitrary kernel read/write uses this memory-corruption flaw to defeat kernel memory protections and gain durable kernel control.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local; presumes an earlier chain stage supplying kernel r/w)
attack_vector.complexity: high — requires a pre-existing kernel primitive and reliable mitigation-bypass, consistent with commercial spyware
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Apple via coordinated disclosure. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the iOS/iPadOS/macOS update and enable Lockdown Mode for high-risk users
prevention.adequacy: The patch removes the primitive, but by the time a mobile zero-day is patched, targeted users may already be compromised — Lockdown Mode reduces but does not eliminate exposure.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Enterprise mobile-threat-defense telemetry and iOS crash-log/sysdiagnose forensics
detection.adequacy: Kernel-chain exploitation leaves minimal on-device evidence; detection typically lags.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force OS update across the fleet, re-image or replace suspected-targeted devices, rotate credentials accessed from them
response.adequacy: Device compromise at kernel level warrants treating the device as untrusted rather than merely patched.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Mobile/OS flaw-remediation SLAs assume scheduled patch windows, but a zero-day kernel-protection bypass in an active spyware chain is exploited before any organizational patch cadence can react.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality/hardening guidance does not reach the kernel memory-protection primitives this bug defeats; MDM configuration cannot substitute for the OS fix.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Timely-patching obligations do not mandate the rapid mobile-fleet enforcement (forced OS update within hours) needed against a targeted mobile zero-day.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Device-security expectations assume mitigations like KTRR/PPL hold; this flaw is precisely a bypass of those mitigations, so the control's assumed baseline is invalid until patched.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential-Eight OS-patching maturity is scoped to workstations/servers and does not compel the same-day mobile-device update discipline this actively-exploited iOS/iPadOS flaw requires.

CVE-2024-23296.name: Apple Multiple Products Memory Corruption Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker already holding arbitrary kernel read/write uses this RTKit memory-corruption flaw to bypass kernel memory protections, a late stage of a targeted exploit chain.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local; presumes an earlier chain stage supplying kernel r/w)
attack_vector.complexity: high — requires a pre-existing kernel primitive and reliable RTKit mitigation-bypass
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Apple via coordinated disclosure. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the iOS/iPadOS/macOS update; enable Lockdown Mode for high-risk users
prevention.adequacy: The update removes the primitive, but targeted users may already be compromised before the patch is applied.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile-threat-defense telemetry and sysdiagnose/crash-log forensics
detection.adequacy: Kernel-chain exploitation is low-artifact; detection typically lags.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force fleet OS update, re-image suspected-targeted devices, rotate accessed credentials
response.adequacy: Kernel-level compromise warrants treating the device as untrusted.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Scheduled OS-patch SLAs cannot beat a zero-day RTKit memory-corruption bypass that is exploited in the wild at disclosure time.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Hardening/least-functionality guidance does not extend to the RTKit real-time coprocessor firmware where this flaw lives; MDM policy cannot mitigate it.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management obligations lack the rapid mobile-fleet enforcement mandate needed when the flaw is an actively-exploited kernel-protection bypass.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: The control assumes device mitigations hold; this bug defeats them, invalidating the assumed protection baseline until the update lands.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential-Eight patch maturity is workstation/server-scoped and does not compel same-day mobile OS updates for an active mobile zero-day.

attack_vector.description: A malicious app abuses a Framework UI flaw to hide the foreground-service notification Android normally forces, so background sensor/data access runs without the persistent status-bar indicator that would alert the user.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local; a malicious app already installed, no additional privileges)
attack_vector.complexity: low — no user interaction required once the app is installed
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported via Android's security bulletin process. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the June 2023 Pixel patch level and restrict app installs to vetted sources
prevention.adequacy: The patch restores the notification guarantee, but a device that ran a malicious app pre-patch may already have leaked data.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile-threat-defense checks for apps holding sensitive runtime permissions with suppressed foreground-service notifications
detection.adequacy: Most MDM/MTD tooling does not inspect notification integrity, so this is rarely caught.

response.what_would_have_worked: Remove the offending app, review sensor/data-access grants, rotate credentials entered on the device
response.adequacy: Because the abuse is covert, response depends on independent discovery of the malicious app.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: OS-patch SLAs for mobile fleets rarely enforce the specific Pixel/Android June-2023 patch level fast enough to close a flaw already exploited in the wild.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: Endpoint monitoring rarely inspects Android foreground-service/notification integrity, so the very signal that would reveal a hidden malicious service is not collected.

NIS2-Art21-incident-handling.gap: Incident-handling obligations do not account for suppressed on-device indicators; a hidden foreground service defeats the user-facing signal responders would rely on.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Device-security expectations assume the OS surfaces ongoing-activity notifications; this flaw removes that assurance, so the control's assumed transparency baseline fails.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application-hardening guidance is desktop-browser-centric and does not address Android app-permission/foreground-service abuse that this notification-hiding flaw enables.

CVE-2021-36380.name: Sunhillo SureLine OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker submits shell metacharacters in the ipAddr/dnsAddr fields of /cgi/networkDiag.cgi; SureLine passes them into a system command run as root, yielding arbitrary command execution and an interactive shell.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network-reachable web CGI)
attack_vector.complexity: low — single crafted HTTP request, publicly documented
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — discovered by NCC Group. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to 8.7.0.1.1 and remove the appliance's management interface from internet exposure
prevention.adequacy: Patching fixes input handling, but exposed appliances should be assumed compromised given years of public exploitability and root-level impact.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for requests to /cgi/networkDiag.cgi containing shell metacharacters and for the CGI process spawning shells or outbound connections
detection.adequacy: Embedded appliances rarely emit logs to a SIEM, limiting detection.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rebuild/reflash the appliance, rotate any credentials it stored, and segment it off the internet
response.adequacy: Root-level command execution means only a full rebuild reliably evicts an attacker.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The flaw was patched in 2021 yet remains actively exploited in 2024, showing that long-lived niche OT/network appliances routinely fall outside routine flaw-remediation cadences.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary-protection guidance does not force these surveillance/telemetry appliances off the public internet, which is the only thing that stops unauthenticated reach to the vulnerable CGI.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security obligations rarely enumerate embedded/OT appliances like SureLine, so the exposed management CGI goes unsegmented and unmonitored.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security expectations assume input validation on management interfaces; an unauthenticated root command-injection means the assumed control simply is not present on the device.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential-Eight patching is scoped to mainstream OS/applications and does not compel firmware updates for niche network appliances, leaving a 3-year-old fix unapplied.

CVE-2024-21338.name: Microsoft Windows Kernel Exposed IOCTL with Insufficient Access Control Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local process opens the appid.sys device and sends a crafted IOCTL whose weak access control allows an untrusted-pointer dereference, yielding kernel read/write; Lazarus used this to install the FudModule rootkit and disable EDR.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local; the exploit elevates an admin/medium-integrity process to kernel)
attack_vector.complexity: low — reliable public exploit once weaponized
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Avast (Jan Vojtesek). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the February 2024 update; enable HVCI/memory-integrity and Microsoft's vulnerable-driver blocklist
prevention.adequacy: The patch fixes the IOCTL, but until applied the exploit blinds EDR, so a patched host may still bear an installed rootkit — kernel-integrity controls are the durable mitigation.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on non-System handles to appid.sys and IOCTL 0x0022A018; hunt for FudModule/EDR-tampering artifacts
detection.adequacy: Because the goal is to disable EDR, detection must run before the rootkit loads.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate and re-image affected hosts, restore EDR integrity, and hunt laterally given Lazarus tradecraft
response.adequacy: A kernel rootkit warrants full rebuild rather than in-place cleaning.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Monthly patch cadence (Patch Tuesday) leaves a multi-day window during which a public admin-to-kernel exploit disables endpoint defenses; SLA-based remediation is slower than the observed weaponization.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege alone does not stop this: the exploit takes an already-admin process to kernel, so restricting standard users is insufficient — kernel-integrity controls (HVCI) are needed.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management requirements do not compel the driver-blocklist/HVCI hardening that would blunt a vulnerable-driver-style kernel escalation between patch cycles.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security expectations assume EDR remains authoritative, but this flaw is used specifically to blind EDR via a kernel rootkit, defeating the control's monitoring assumption.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application-hardening/driver-control guidance does not enumerate appid.sys, so the exposed-IOCTL path is not blocked by standard hardening baselines.

new_control_requirements.name: VULNERABLE-DRIVER-BLOCKLIST-ENFORCEMENT
new_control_requirements.description: Enforce a kernel vulnerable/malicious-driver blocklist (HVCI plus the vendor recommended-driver blocklist) so a signed-but-vulnerable driver cannot be loaded for bring-your-own-vulnerable-driver privilege escalation.

CVE-2023-29360.name: Microsoft Streaming Service Untrusted Pointer Dereference Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local process drives the mskssrv.sys streaming driver to dereference an attacker-controlled pointer, converting a user-mode foothold into a kernel write primitive and SYSTEM privileges.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none-to-low (local user; no admin required)
attack_vector.complexity: low — reliable public exploit code exists
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — coordinated disclosure to Microsoft. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the June 2023 update; restrict/disable the Microsoft Streaming Service where unused
prevention.adequacy: The patch fixes the dereference, but as a post-compromise LPE it only matters after initial access — defense-in-depth against initial footholds remains necessary.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on standard users opening mskssrv/KS device handles and on SYSTEM processes spawned from medium-integrity parents
detection.adequacy: Driver-level LPE telemetry is uncommon, so detection depends on generic escalation heuristics.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, determine the initial-access vector, and re-image if SYSTEM was obtained
response.adequacy: SYSTEM-level compromise justifies rebuild and credential rotation.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Monthly patch cadence leaves the window a public LPE needs; SLA-based remediation is slower than the exploit's post-compromise use to reach SYSTEM.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege limits initial access but does not prevent a low-priv process from abusing an exposed kernel driver to reach SYSTEM; driver-attack-surface reduction is required.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management obligations do not mandate reducing exposure of optional media/streaming kernel components that provide this escalation path.

UK-CAF-C1.gap: Security-monitoring expectations rarely include driver-level LPE telemetry, so the escalation to SYSTEM proceeds without an alerting baseline.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application-hardening baselines do not disable or restrict the Microsoft Streaming Service driver, leaving the vulnerable IOCTL/KS path reachable by any local user.

CVE-2020-3259.name: Cisco ASA and FTD Information Disclosure Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted GET request with an invalid URL to the ASA/FTD web services interface; a buffer-tracking flaw returns device memory contents, which can include cleartext VPN credentials later used for ransomware entry.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, internet-facing web services interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted HTTP request, though only specific AnyConnect/WebVPN configs are vulnerable
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported via Cisco PSIRT. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the fixed ASA/FTD release and rotate all VPN credentials that could have been resident in memory; enforce MFA on VPN
prevention.adequacy: Patching stops the leak, but any credentials exposed before patching remain usable unless rotated and protected by MFA — patch alone does not undo the disclosure.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for malformed GET requests to the WebVPN endpoint and correlate with new VPN logins from unusual sources
detection.adequacy: Memory-disclosure requests can look benign, so detection relies on downstream anomalous-login correlation.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force VPN credential rotation, enable MFA, and hunt for Akira precursors given the ransomware association
response.adequacy: Because leaked credentials seed later intrusion, response must assume credential compromise.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The fix shipped in 2020 yet the flaw was still enabling ransomware entry in 2024, showing edge-VPN appliances routinely miss flaw-remediation cadences long after patches exist.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary-protection controls treat the ASA/FTD as the boundary itself; they do not address the appliance leaking its own memory (and stored credentials) to unauthenticated requests.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Vulnerability-management obligations do not force credential rotation after a memory-disclosure exposure, so leaked VPN credentials remain valid and enable later ransomware access.

UK-CAF-B2.gap: Identity/access controls assume VPN credentials stay secret; a memory-disclosure that exposes them undermines the control unless MFA and post-exposure rotation are enforced.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential-Eight patching targets do not specifically drive perimeter-appliance firmware updates, leaving a years-old ASA/FTD fix unapplied on exposed devices.

CVE-2024-21410.name: Microsoft Exchange Server Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker coerces a privileged account to authenticate (e.g. PetitPotam), then relays the NTLM credential to the Exchange EWS endpoint; without Extended Protection the relay succeeds and the attacker gains the victim's Exchange privileges.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (network position to coerce/relay authentication; no valid Exchange credentials needed)
attack_vector.complexity: low — built from public relay/coercion tooling
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — addressed by Microsoft via Extended Protection by default. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install the Feb 2024 Exchange update AND enable Extended Protection; disable NTLM where possible and require MFA
prevention.adequacy: The patch enables Extended Protection but administrators must confirm it is actually on for every vdir — a server that was patched without EP verification remains exploitable.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Detect NTLM relays to EWS and authentication-coercion RPC (MS-EFSR/MS-RPRN) preceding Exchange access
detection.adequacy: Relay traffic can blend with legitimate NTLM; detection needs source/behavior correlation.

response.what_would_have_worked: Verify Extended Protection everywhere, rotate exposed credentials, and hunt for unauthorized Exchange/AD changes
response.adequacy: Because the escalation grants a victim identity, response must audit actions taken under relayed accounts.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Applying the Exchange update is not enough on its own — Extended Protection must be actively enabled — so a standard patch-and-reboot SLA leaves the relay path open even on 'patched' servers.

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: Identity/authentication controls that permit NTLM allow credential relay; without channel-binding/Extended Protection and MFA, the authentication assurance the control assumes does not hold.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security obligations do not by default require disabling/segmenting NTLM or blocking authentication-coercion RPC, which is what stops the relay chain.

UK-CAF-B2.gap: Identity-and-access-control expectations assume authentication cannot be replayed; NTLM relay defeats that, so the control fails absent Extended Protection.

ISO-27001-2022-A.5.16-Federated.gap: Identity-management control does not, by itself, mandate channel binding / EPA for legacy NTLM, leaving relayed authentication to Exchange viable.

CVE-2024-21412.name: Microsoft Windows Internet Shortcut Files Security Feature Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A victim opens a crafted .url internet shortcut that redirects to a second .url on a WebDAV/SMB share; the indirection strips Mark-of-the-Web, so SmartScreen does not warn and the referenced payload executes.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (attacker-side); requires user interaction to open the shortcut
attack_vector.complexity: low — public exploit/Metasploit module, but depends on luring the user to open the file
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Trend Micro ZDI (Water Hydra tracking). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the February 2024 SmartScreen update; block/limit outbound WebDAV and treat .url attachments as high-risk at the mail gateway
prevention.adequacy: The patch restores the MOTW check, but because delivery relies on user interaction, mail/web filtering and user awareness remain necessary layers.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on .url files referencing remote .url/network paths and on WebDAV (davclnt) fetches spawning executables
detection.adequacy: The bypass suppresses the usual SmartScreen signal, so detection must rely on the shortcut-chain and network-fetch behavior.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate hosts that opened the lure, hunt for DarkGate loaders, and reset credentials that could have been harvested
response.adequacy: Because the bypass delivers a loader, response must chase the follow-on payload rather than just the shortcut.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection leans on SmartScreen/Mark-of-the-Web reputation, which this flaw bypasses outright, so signature/reputation defenses do not fire on the delivered payload.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Monthly patch cadence lags the same-week weaponization by Water Hydra/DarkGate; SLA-based patching leaves the SmartScreen bypass open during active campaigns.

NIS2-Art21-incident-handling.gap: Incident-handling requirements assume defensive prompts surface risky files; a MOTW/SmartScreen bypass removes that signal, so the assumed detection point is absent.

UK-CAF-C1.gap: Security-monitoring expectations do not specifically cover WebDAV-fetched shortcut chains, so the bypass's network-share fetch is not routinely alerted.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application-hardening measures (block/limit WebDAV, disable script hosts, restrict .url handling) are not mandated, leaving the shortcut-chain delivery path open.

new_control_requirements.name: DOWNLOAD-PROVENANCE-ENFORCEMENT
new_control_requirements.description: Enforce Mark-of-the-Web / content-provenance on all internet-sourced files and block execution of provenance-stripped shortcuts, installers, and script hosts regardless of a reputation service verdict, which attackers bypass by design.

CVE-2024-21351.name: Microsoft Windows SmartScreen Security Feature Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A user is lured to open a crafted internet-shortcut file; the shortcut chains to a second remote shortcut that never receives the Mark-of-the-Web tag, so SmartScreen skips its reputation warning and the payload runs.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (attacker-supplied file; requires the victim to open it — UI:R)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the crafted-shortcut chain reliably suppresses the MotW/SmartScreen prompt
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by security researchers to Microsoft. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the February 2024 Windows cumulative update; block .url/.lnk and WebDAV/SMB egress at the mail/web gateway; strip incoming shortcut attachments.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the specific bypass, but shortcut-chain delivery and WebDAV abuse remain a class of technique that reputation controls alone will not stop.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on downloaded files lacking a Zone.Identifier stream and on .url/.lnk files resolving to remote WebDAV/SMB shares.
detection.adequacy: Effective post-delivery but presumes EDR visibility into MotW state and shortcut internals.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate hosts that executed shortcut-delivered payloads, hunt for follow-on loaders, rotate exposed credentials.
response.adequacy: Response is reactive; the bypass had already delivered code by the time host telemetry fired.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: SmartScreen IS the malicious-code-protection control being bypassed; SI-3's reputation/reputation-check expectation gives false assurance when Mark-of-the-Web is not applied to the delivered file.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Monthly patch cadence leaves a bypass window: this was a live zero-day when KEV-listed, so remediation had to outrun active exploitation, not the normal SI-2 SLA.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: A calendar-based patch program does not address a client-side defense-evasion bug already weaponized in phishing lures before the fix ships.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application-hardening guidance assumes SmartScreen/MotW warnings fire; this flaw silences them, so the hardening baseline over-credits the reputation prompt.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.7.gap: Anti-malware control effectiveness is undermined when the OS reputation gate can be skipped, so A.8.7 coverage is present-but-inadequate for MotW-bypass delivery.

new_control_requirements.name: DOWNLOAD-PROVENANCE-ENFORCEMENT
new_control_requirements.description: Enforce Mark-of-the-Web / content-provenance on all internet-sourced files and block execution of provenance-stripped shortcuts, installers, and script hosts regardless of a reputation service verdict, which attackers bypass by design.

CVE-2023-43770.name: Roundcube Webmail Persistent Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: The attacker emails a plain-text message containing a crafted link; Roundcube's link-replacer converts it into HTML with attacker-controlled attributes/script, so JavaScript runs in the victim's authenticated webmail session when the message is viewed.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated — attacker only needs to send an email; victim must open it)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted email triggers the stored XSS on view
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — fixed by the Roundcube project, exploitation reported by ESET. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to Roundcube 1.6.3 / 1.5.4 / 1.4.14; enforce a strict Content-Security-Policy on the webmail origin; sanitize/encode link rendering.
prevention.adequacy: Patch removes the specific sink, but webmail remains a high-value XSS target absent a defense-in-depth CSP.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for outbound requests from the webmail origin to unknown collectors and for messages whose plain-text links contain markup.
detection.adequacy: Detects exfiltration after script execution rather than preventing it.

response.what_would_have_worked: Invalidate webmail sessions, rotate credentials of targeted accounts, review sent-mail and forwarding rules for tampering.
response.adequacy: Reactive; credential theft may already have occurred by the time sessions are invalidated.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The fix shipped in September 2023 but the flaw was already an operational zero-day against targeted governments; a routine SI-2 timeline is slower than the observed espionage exploitation.

PCI-DSS-4.0-6.2.4.gap: Injection-flaw handling in 6.2.4 targets first-party code review; it does not force timely upgrade of a third-party webmail component whose parser is the injection sink.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management is present but under-weights an internet-facing webmail XSS as a targeted-intrusion vector rather than a low-severity nuisance.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Vulnerability-management obligations do not by themselves prioritize a CVSS-medium XSS that is, in practice, a nation-state credential-theft primitive.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security expectations assume webmail rendering is trustworthy; they do not mandate a content-security-policy / output-encoding control that would neutralize the stored-XSS payload.

CVE-2023-4762.name: Google Chromium V8 Type Confusion Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A victim visits an attacker-controlled web page; malicious JavaScript drives a V8 type confusion that lets the attacker treat a memory object as the wrong type, yielding code execution inside the renderer as a stage toward full compromise.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; requires the victim to load a crafted page — UI:R)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted HTML page triggers the type confusion, though a reliable full chain needs a sandbox escape
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported through Google's vulnerability process. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update Chrome/Chromium to 116.0.5845.179 or later and force-relaunch; enforce automatic browser updates fleet-wide.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is decisive, but only if relaunch is enforced; a running unpatched browser stays vulnerable until restart.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on renderer crashes and on child processes spawned by the browser after browsing untrusted sites.
detection.adequacy: Renderer exploitation is largely in-process and low-signal until a sandbox escape spawns activity.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate affected hosts, force browser update + relaunch, hunt for post-exploitation implants.
response.adequacy: Reactive; targeted-surveillance payloads may already have staged persistence.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Browser flaw-remediation must be same-day for a live zero-day; a standard SI-2 cadence leaves users exploitable during the window commercial-surveillance actors were already using.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential-8 patch timelines (48 hours for internet-facing / extreme-risk) can still trail a browser zero-day already weaponized before the CVE is public.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Endpoint-browser patch management is rarely enforced as tightly as server patching, yet this bug's exploitation vector is everyday web browsing.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management under-prioritizes client browsers relative to servers, leaving the actual exploited surface under-governed.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security assurance assumes the browser sandbox holds; a renderer type-confusion with sandbox escape defeats that assumption without additional exploit-mitigation controls.

CVE-2022-48618.name: Apple Multiple Products Memory Corruption Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Within an exploit chain, code that already holds arbitrary kernel read/write wins a time-of-check/time-of-use race to defeat Pointer Authentication, removing a key mitigation and enabling reliable kernel-level control on the device.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (requires a pre-existing arbitrary read/write primitive from an earlier chain stage)
attack_vector.complexity: high — winning the TOCTOU race and holding prior R/W is non-trivial, consistent with targeted implant use
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — credited to Apple. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Force iOS/iPadOS 16.2, macOS Ventura 13.1, watchOS 9.2, tvOS 16.2 (or later) via MDM; retire devices that cannot reach a patched OS.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the race, but the bug matters only in a chain; the earlier R/W bugs must also be patched to break the full path.

detection.what_would_have_worked: MDM posture checks flagging out-of-date OS builds; mobile threat defense detecting jailbreak/implant indicators.
detection.adequacy: Kernel-level compromise is low-signal; detection largely depends on device-integrity attestation.

response.what_would_have_worked: Re-image / factory-reset compromised devices, rotate any credentials handled on them, force OS update before re-enrollment.
response.adequacy: Reactive; a kernel implant may survive until a full wipe.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: OS flaw-remediation cadence must be immediate on managed mobile fleets; the exploited pre-15.7.1 window shows a lagging patch program is exploitable even for a chained secondary bug.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential-8 patching centers OS/application timelines but rarely enforces same-week mobile-OS updates, leaving the PAC-bypass stage available longer.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management obligations seldom mandate MDM-forced iOS/macOS updates fast enough to close a mitigation-bypass used in targeted implants.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management under-weights an exploit-mitigation-bypass because in isolation it needs a prior R/W primitive, understating its role in a full chain.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security assurance credits hardware mitigations like PAC; this flaw defeats that mitigation, so control coverage over-relies on the bypassed protection.

CVE-2024-21893.name: Ivanti Connect Secure, Policy Secure, and Neurons Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted request to the SAML component that forces the appliance to make a server-side request to its own loopback backend on 127.0.0.1:8090, then chains the reachable command-injection flaw (CVE-2024-21887) to run OS commands and drop webshells.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, internet-facing gateway)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public tooling automates the SSRF-plus-command-injection chain and bypasses the first mitigation
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by Ivanti, weaponized by public researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Feb 1 2024 patch set (not just the interim mitigation.release.20240107 XML, which was bypassed), then run the external integrity checker and factory-reset before re-patching if compromise is suspected.
prevention.adequacy: Patching alone is insufficient because appliances compromised before patching retain implants; CISA directed disconnect-and-rebuild.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Run Ivanti's Integrity Checker Tool, alert on loopback-targeting SAML requests and new webshell files, and monitor outbound C2 from the appliance.
detection.adequacy: The ICT missed some tampering; on-box detection from a compromised appliance is not fully trustworthy.

response.what_would_have_worked: Disconnect the appliance, factory-reset, rebuild on a patched image, and rotate all secrets/certificates handled by the gateway.
response.adequacy: Full rebuild plus credential rotation is required because in-place patching cannot evict a pre-existing implant.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection assumes the VPN gateway is a trust anchor; an SSRF that pivots to a loopback backend turns the boundary device itself into the attack surface, which SC-7 does not address.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The observed exploitation window (days from disclosure to mass scanning) is far shorter than typical flaw-remediation SLAs, and the first mitigation was itself bypassed.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security measures do not compensate for a preauth RCE on the perimeter appliance that terminates the network; the control that should protect the network is the vulnerable asset.

DORA-Art-9.gap: ICT protection requirements for financial entities do not force the rapid factory-reset-and-rebuild that a compromised, ransomware-targeted VPN appliance actually requires.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security assurance for a remote-access gateway is undermined when a mitigation ships and is then bypassed; point-in-time hardening does not survive an evolving exploit chain.

CVE-2023-22527.name: Atlassian Confluence Data Center and Server Template Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a POST to /template/aui/text-inline.vm containing an OGNL expression; Confluence evaluates the template server-side, executing arbitrary OS commands that are used to drop miners, C2 implants or webshells.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, internet-facing)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted POST triggers OGNL evaluation; public tooling automates it
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by Atlassian. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to a fixed Confluence release immediately; place a WAF rule blocking OGNL patterns to /template/aui/text-inline.vm as interim virtual-patching.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is decisive, but hosts exposed during the exploitation window likely need compromise assessment, not just an upgrade.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on text-inline.vm POSTs with OGNL syntax and on shell child-processes of the Confluence JVM.
detection.adequacy: Effective if request/process telemetry exists; encoded payload variants can evade naive signatures.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, hunt for webshells/Sliver beacons and miners, rebuild if implants are found, rotate service credentials.
response.adequacy: Reactive; persistence is common, so rebuild is frequently required.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The gap from disclosure to mass exploitation was days; a normal flaw-remediation SLA leaves an unauthenticated critical-RCE Confluence exposed during peak scanning.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection alone does not help when the exposed service is a business-critical collaboration server that must be internet-reachable for its users.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management cadence is slower than the observed exploitation window and does not require emergency isolation of an unpatched Confluence instance.

DORA-Art-9.gap: ICT protection requirements do not force the compromise-assessment-and-rebuild that a post-exploited Confluence host needs, given webshell/C2 persistence.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security assurance for the application does not mandate WAF/virtual-patching that would blunt the OGNL-injection request pattern before the vendor fix is applied.

CVE-2024-23222.name: Apple Multiple Products WebKit Type Confusion Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A victim opens attacker-controlled web content in Safari or any WebKit-based renderer; the content triggers a type confusion in WebKit, letting the attacker execute arbitrary code in the browser process as a foothold on the device.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; requires the victim to load crafted web content — UI:R)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a crafted page triggers the type confusion, consistent with observed zero-day use
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — credited to Apple. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Force Safari 17.3 / iOS 17.3 (or the 15.8.7 / 16.7.5 backports) and the corresponding macOS/tvOS/visionOS updates via MDM; enable Lockdown Mode for high-risk users.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is decisive; Lockdown Mode reduces WebKit attack surface for targeted individuals as defense-in-depth.

detection.what_would_have_worked: MDM posture checks for out-of-date OS/Safari builds; mobile threat defense for implant indicators.
detection.adequacy: Renderer exploitation is low-signal until post-exploitation activity appears.

response.what_would_have_worked: Update devices, review for implants, re-image and rotate credentials for targeted users.
response.adequacy: Reactive; targeted spyware payloads may already have staged persistence.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: OS/browser flaw-remediation must be same-day for a live WebKit zero-day; a standard SI-2 cadence leaves devices exploitable via ordinary web browsing.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential-8 patch timelines can trail an actively exploited WebKit zero-day, and MDM enforcement of iOS/macOS updates is often looser than server patching.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management obligations rarely force MDM-driven same-week mobile-OS updates that this browsing-borne zero-day demands.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management under-prioritizes client browser/WebKit surfaces relative to servers, leaving the exploited surface under-governed.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security assurance credits the WebKit/Safari sandbox; a renderer type-confusion undermines that assumption absent additional exploit-mitigation controls.

CVE-2023-34048.name: VMware vCenter Server Out-of-Bounds Write Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker with network access to vCenter Server sends crafted DCERPC packets that trigger an out-of-bounds write in the vmdird service, corrupting memory to crash the service or, with control of the byte-swapped base address, execute code — a foothold that UNC3886 used to reach ESXi hosts.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to vCenter)
attack_vector.complexity: high — reliable RCE requires defeating the byte-swapped base address; DoS is the easier outcome
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by VMware, exploitation attributed to UNC3886 by Mandiant. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply VMSA-2023-0023 patches (including the EOL back-ports VMware released); restrict DCERPC/management access to vCenter to a hardened management network.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the flaw, but a ~2-year dwell means retrospective compromise assessment of vCenter and ESXi is also required.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on vmdird crashes/core dumps and on unexpected DCERPC sources; monitor ESXi for unsigned VIBs and rogue vpxuser activity.
detection.adequacy: Crash telemetry is the earliest signal but is rarely collected; implant detection on ESXi is difficult.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the management network, hunt for VIRTUALPITA/VIRTUALPIE implants on ESXi, rebuild compromised hosts, rotate vCenter/ESXi credentials.
response.adequacy: Reactive and costly; hypervisor-level implants can persist across guest workloads.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection is bypassed because vCenter is an internal management plane that many networks leave broadly reachable; the DCERPC surface is exposed to any host that can reach the appliance.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The bug was exploited for ~2 years before disclosure, so no flaw-remediation SLA could have helped; SI-2 addresses known flaws, not a silent nation-state zero-day.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security controls that assume the virtualization management plane is trusted do not segment or monitor DCERPC to vCenter, leaving the exploited path open.

DORA-Art-9.gap: ICT protection requirements do not mandate the compromise assessment of the hypervisor management layer needed after a stealthy, long-dwell vCenter intrusion.

UK-CAF-C1.gap: Security-monitoring expectations rarely include vmdird crash telemetry, so the earliest reliable signal of this exploit went uncollected.

CVE-2023-35082.name: Ivanti Endpoint Manager Mobile (EPMM) and MobileIron Core Authentication Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted request whose URI prefix bypasses the authentication filter, reaching MDM management-API endpoints to read user PII and alter server configuration — a foothold on the control plane for the entire managed mobile fleet.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, internet-facing MDM server)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a crafted request path skips authentication; public tooling detects and exercises it
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — discovered by Rapid7, disclosed by Ivanti. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply Ivanti's RPM mitigation / EPMM 11.11 fix; restrict management-portal exposure and place the API behind an authenticating reverse proxy.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the bypass, but an MDM server exposed during the window may already have leaked fleet data and needs review.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unauthenticated API access returning data and on crafted URI-prefix patterns to /mifs endpoints.
detection.adequacy: Effective if request-level logging distinguishes authenticated from unauthenticated access.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the server, audit for unauthorized config changes and PII access, rotate device/enrollment secrets.
response.adequacy: Reactive; the MDM control plane touches every managed device, so blast radius is fleet-wide.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement is defeated at the source: a crafted URI prefix bypasses the authentication filter, so the access-control decision point never runs for the sensitive API.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The flaw was exploited shortly after Rapid7's public details, faster than a routine flaw-remediation SLA closes an internet-facing MDM server.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security controls do not compensate for an unauthenticated API bypass on a device that, by design, must be internet-reachable for mobile enrollment.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management does not by itself force emergency isolation of an MDM control plane whose compromise exposes the entire managed fleet.

UK-CAF-B2.gap: Identity-and-access-control assurance is undermined when authentication can be skipped entirely; B2 assumes the auth gate is reached for protected functions.

CVE-2024-0519.name: Google Chromium V8 Out-of-Bounds Memory Access Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A victim visits an attacker-controlled page; JavaScript drives an out-of-bounds memory access in V8, corrupting the heap to gain code execution in the renderer as a stage toward device compromise.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; requires the victim to load a crafted page — UI:R)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a crafted HTML page triggers the out-of-bounds access, though full compromise needs a sandbox escape
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported through Google's vulnerability process. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update Chrome/Chromium to 120.0.6099.224 or later and force-relaunch; enforce automatic browser updates fleet-wide.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is decisive only if relaunch is enforced; a running unpatched browser stays vulnerable until restart.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on renderer crashes and on browser child-processes after browsing untrusted sites.
detection.adequacy: Renderer exploitation is largely in-process and low-signal until a sandbox escape spawns activity.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate affected hosts, force update + relaunch, hunt for post-exploitation implants.
response.adequacy: Reactive; zero-day payloads may already have staged persistence.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Browser flaw-remediation must be same-day for a live zero-day; a standard SI-2 cadence leaves users exploitable during active exploitation via routine browsing.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential-8 patch timelines can trail a browser zero-day already exploited before the CVE is public, and force-relaunch is often not enforced.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Endpoint-browser patch management is rarely enforced as tightly as server patching, yet browsing is the exact exploited vector.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management under-prioritizes client browsers, leaving the actual exploited surface under-governed.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security assurance assumes the renderer sandbox holds; a V8 out-of-bounds primitive undermines that assumption without additional exploit-mitigation controls.

CVE-2023-6549.name: Citrix NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway Buffer Overflow Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends crafted requests to a NetScaler appliance configured as a Gateway or AAA virtual server, triggering an out-of-bounds memory operation that crashes the packet engine (denial of service) and can leak adjacent memory.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network-reachable Gateway/AAA vserver)
attack_vector.complexity: low — no authentication or user interaction; only a reachable Gateway/AAA vserver is required
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by Citrix/Cloud Software Group PSIRT after in-the-wild detection. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to the fixed 14.1-12.35 / 13.1-51.15 / 13.0-92.21 (or FIPS-equivalent) build and reboot; restrict the Gateway/AAA vserver exposure where feasible.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the flaw but requires a maintenance reboot; during the upgrade window the appliance stays exposed, so patch alone does not cover the mass-exploitation gap.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unexpected nsppe crashes / appliance restarts and on malformed requests to Gateway/AAA vservers.
detection.adequacy: Crash-based detection is reactive — the DoS has already landed by the time it fires.

response.what_would_have_worked: Emergency out-of-cycle firmware upgrade, session teardown, and monitoring for repeat DoS attempts.
response.adequacy: Restores availability but does not retroactively protect any memory disclosed during OOB reads.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw-remediation SLAs measured in weeks are far slower than the days-scale mass-exploitation window that followed the 16 Jan 2024 zero-day disclosure; patching alone leaves an internet-facing NetScaler exposed during the emergency upgrade-and-reboot gap.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Requires network-security measures but does not compel isolating the NetScaler Gateway/AAA data plane from untrusted networks, which is what actually bounds reachability of this pre-auth denial-of-service.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Calls for resilient networks and systems yet does not force appliance-firmware currency on an internet-facing VPN concentrator whose crash denies remote access to the entire workforce.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The internet-facing 48-hour patch objective was outrun by exploitation that began before most operators completed the firmware upgrade plus mandatory reboot.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management as written does not elevate a KEV-listed, actively-exploited appliance flaw above routine CVEs, so it under-prioritises the emergency reboot window.

CVE-2023-6548.name: Citrix NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway Code Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A low-privileged authenticated user who can reach the NetScaler management interface (NSIP/CLIP/SNIP) submits crafted input that injects and executes code on the appliance management plane.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (authenticated to the management interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single low-privileged management account plus network reachability of the mgmt interface
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by Citrix/Cloud Software Group PSIRT. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to the fixed build, reboot, and confine the management interface to an isolated administrative network away from untrusted access.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the code-injection path, but the flaw only became reachable because the management plane was network-exposed — segmentation is the durable control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor management-interface access sources and alert on unexpected appliance processes after mgmt requests.
detection.adequacy: Appliance shells offer limited native telemetry, so detection is partial.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate management credentials, rebuild/reimage the appliance if compromise is suspected, and restrict mgmt-plane exposure.
response.adequacy: Reimaging is the only reliable way to clear post-RCE persistence on an appliance.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: A merely low-privileged management account is sufficient for code execution, so least-privilege on the management plane does not contain the flaw once any management access exists.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security obligations do not explicitly require the NetScaler management interface (NSIP/CLIP/SNIP) to be off the public network, which is the precondition the entire exploit depends on.

UK-CAF-B2.gap: Identity and access-control expectations do not force administrative interfaces onto an isolated management network, leaving the exposed mgmt plane exploitable by any low-privileged account.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application-hardening guidance does not address restricting appliance management-interface exposure, so the code-injection sink stays reachable.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management does not prioritise the emergency upgrade of a KEV-listed appliance whose management plane yields RCE from a low-privileged account.

CVE-2018-15133.name: Laravel Deserialization of Untrusted Data Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: With a known application APP_KEY, an attacker encrypts a phpggc PHP object-injection gadget chain into an X-XSRF-TOKEN; Laravel decrypts and unserializes it, instantiating a gadget that executes arbitrary commands.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none over the network, but conditioned on prior knowledge of the APP_KEY secret
attack_vector.complexity: high — requires the APP_KEY, which is why NVD rates AC:H; trivially high-impact once the key leaks
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — PHP object-injection gadget-chain research (phpggc/community). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to Laravel 5.6.30+, rotate any APP_KEY that may have been exposed, and keep .env out of the web root and source control.
prevention.adequacy: The framework fix is necessary but insufficient if a leaked APP_KEY is not rotated — key exposure re-enables equivalent object-injection paths.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Flag X-XSRF-TOKEN values that decrypt to serialized objects and web processes spawning shells.
detection.adequacy: Encrypted tokens hide the payload until decryption, so detection needs application-layer inspection.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate APP_KEY and all downstream secrets, audit for webshells, and review .env exposure.
response.adequacy: Without APP_KEY rotation, remediation is incomplete.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Patching to Laravel 5.6.30+ closes the unserialize path, but the real trigger is a leaked APP_KEY; flaw-remediation alone does not force key rotation after the secret is exposed.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Vulnerability-management duties address the framework CVE but not the recurring root cause — application secrets (APP_KEY) exposed via .env files in web roots or source repos.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Secure-configuration expectations do not specifically require APP_KEY confidentiality and rotation, so the deserialization sink remains weaponizable wherever the key leaks.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch-application guidance does not cover the credential-hygiene half of this flaw; an up-to-date but key-leaked deployment is still exploitable if a vulnerable branch is pinned.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management does not chain to secrets management, leaving the APP_KEY exposure that makes this object-injection reachable unaddressed.

new_control_requirements.name: FRAMEWORK-DEFAULT-SECRET-DETECTION
new_control_requirements.description: Deployment gating that fails any release shipping or running an unrotated framework default cryptographic secret (APP_KEY / SECRET_KEY and equivalents), which turn a leaked constant into unauthenticated deserialization RCE.

CVE-2023-29357.name: Microsoft SharePoint Server Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker presents a spoofed JWT whose signature is not properly validated by SharePoint, is granted administrator context, and can then chain CVE-2023-24955 code injection to reach remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; forged token substitutes for credentials)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoCs automate token forgery/replay against an exposed SharePoint server
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Jang of STAR Labs at Pwn2Own Vancouver 2023. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the June 2023 SharePoint Server 2019 security update (build 16.0.10399.20005+), run PSConfig, and restrict internet exposure of on-prem SharePoint.
prevention.adequacy: Patch fixes the token-validation flaw, but internet-exposed SharePoint should also be fronted by authenticated reverse proxy — the ransomware use showed patch-lag estates were hit.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on JWT bearer tokens with invalid signatures being accepted and on anonymous-to-admin transitions.
detection.adequacy: Requires application/ULS-log instrumentation many operators do not centralize.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the server, hunt for the CVE-2023-24955 RCE stage and webshells, and rotate service/machine keys.
response.adequacy: Post-admin compromise can seed persistence that survives patching alone.

NIST-800-63B-rev4.gap: Authentication assurance guidance assumes token signatures are validated; it does not defend against a server-side flaw that accepts a spoofed/forged JWT, so strong credential controls are bypassed entirely.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management timelines are outpaced by public PoC availability plus ransomware use; the June 2023 fix predates KEV listing, yet exposed 2019 servers remained unpatched into active exploitation.

UK-CAF-B2.gap: Identity and access-control expectations presume the auth layer is sound; a token-spoofing bypass grants admin without any valid credential, defeating access policy at the application tier.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The patch-applications objective did not close the window between the June 2023 update, the September 2023 PoC release, and the January 2024 KEV/ransomware escalation for laggard SharePoint estates.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management did not prioritise a critical, PoC-available SharePoint auth bypass ahead of its ransomware weaponization.

CVE-2023-46805.name: Ivanti Connect Secure and Policy Secure Authentication Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A remote unauthenticated attacker sends a directory-traversal request to the ICS/IPS web component that slips past the authentication/control checks, exposing restricted endpoints and priming the paired CVE-2024-21887 command injection for full RCE.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, internet-facing web component)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted traversal request; public tooling automates the chain
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found in-the-wild by Volexity, coordinated with Ivanti/Mandiant. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Ivanti mitigation.release XML immediately then the firmware fix and reboot; run the external Integrity Checker Tool; minimise internet exposure of the gateway.
prevention.framework_requiring_it: CISA BOD 22-01 (KEV remediation, added 2024-01-10); CISA ED 24-01
prevention.adequacy: A patch did not exist at disclosure, so the interim mitigation and factory-reset guidance were load-bearing; patch alone did not cover the zero-day window.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Run the external ICT, hunt for ../ traversal on the web component and webshells under dana-na, and watch for ICT tampering.
detection.adequacy: Attackers were observed tampering with the built-in ICT, so only the external tool plus network telemetry is reliable.

response.what_would_have_worked: Factory reset before upgrading, rotate all secrets/certs exposed to the appliance, and treat the device as compromised.
response.adequacy: Persistence survived naive patching; a full reset plus credential rotation was required.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection does not prevent the appliance itself, which is the boundary, from being bypassed via path traversal; the flaw lives in the very device meant to enforce the perimeter.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security obligations treat the VPN concentrator as a trusted control, but this pre-auth bypass turns the enforcement point into the entry point, which the control does not anticipate.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Resilient-network expectations assume the remote-access gateway is trustworthy; they do not require compensating segmentation for a gateway that is itself the vulnerable, internet-facing asset.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The patch objective was overtaken by zero-day mass exploitation before a patch existed — initial guidance was a mitigation.release XML, not a fix, leaving a control gap the SLA cannot cover.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management provides no play for a KEV appliance zero-day with only a temporary mitigation, so exposed devices stayed reachable during active exploitation.

CVE-2024-21887.name: Ivanti Connect Secure and Policy Secure Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An administrator-context request (reachable unauthenticated when paired with CVE-2023-46805) injects OS commands into an ICS/IPS web-component endpoint, executing arbitrary code and enabling webshell deployment on the appliance.
attack_vector.privileges_required: high (authenticated administrator) alone, but effectively none when chained with the CVE-2023-46805 auth bypass
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted request; public tooling automates the full unauthenticated chain
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found in-the-wild by Volexity, coordinated with Ivanti/Mandiant. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the interim mitigation then the firmware fix and reboot, factory reset before upgrade, and reduce internet exposure of the gateway.
prevention.framework_requiring_it: CISA BOD 22-01 (KEV remediation, added 2024-01-10); CISA ED 24-01
prevention.adequacy: No patch existed at disclosure; interim mitigation and reset guidance carried the load, and patch alone did not clear implanted webshells.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Run the external Integrity Checker Tool, hunt for webshells under dana-na, and monitor for command metacharacters in web-component API calls.
detection.adequacy: The built-in ICT was subverted, so only the external tool plus network telemetry is trustworthy.

response.what_would_have_worked: Treat the appliance as compromised: factory reset, rotate every secret/cert it held, and rebuild before returning to service.
response.adequacy: Post-RCE persistence required a full reset and credential rotation, not just an upgrade.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: System monitoring does not natively cover a closed appliance whose own Integrity Checker Tool was subverted by attackers, so command-injection and webshell activity evaded the expected detection surface.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security duties assume the gateway enforces trust, but command injection on the appliance itself runs inside the trust boundary the control relies on.

UK-CAF-C1.gap: Security-monitoring expectations were defeated because operators lacked telemetry from the appliance and the built-in integrity check was tampered with, hiding the command-execution stage.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The patch objective could not apply during a zero-day with no fix; interim mitigation plus factory reset were required beyond the normal patch SLA.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management offers no route for a KEV appliance command-injection zero-day where only a temporary mitigation existed before a patch.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker appends ?public=true to Joomla 4.x REST config/user endpoints; the improper access check returns the application configuration, including MySQL username, password, and host, which is reused for deeper access.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated REST request)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single GET request; the exact URL is public and templated in scanners
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to the Joomla project and fixed in 4.2.8. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to Joomla 4.2.8, restrict the /api endpoints, and rotate database credentials that may have been disclosed.
prevention.adequacy: The update fixes the access check, but any credentials leaked while exposed remain valid until rotated — patch alone leaves the secret compromised.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on requests to /api/index.php/v1/config/* and ?public=true patterns from untrusted sources.
detection.adequacy: Single-request disclosure is fast; detection is largely retrospective.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate the database password and any secrets in the config, and review DB access logs for use of leaked credentials.
response.adequacy: Without credential rotation, remediation is incomplete.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement is exactly what failed at the application tier; the compensating layer (a network device) is not required to authenticate REST endpoints, so the improper access check leaks config regardless.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Vulnerability-management duties address the CVE but not the downstream harm — leaked database credentials must be rotated, which patch-tracking alone does not trigger.

UK-CAF-B2.gap: Identity and access-control expectations assume endpoint authorization is enforced; a broken access check exposes secrets that then enable credentialed follow-on access.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The patch objective closes the endpoint but does not mandate rotating the database password that was disclosed while the site was vulnerable.
