ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management does not chain to secrets rotation, leaving leaked DB credentials valid after the Joomla update.

CVE-2016-20017.name: D-Link DSL-2750B Devices Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A remote unauthenticated attacker sends a request to login.cgi with shell metacharacters in the cli parameter; the CGI passes it to a shell, executing arbitrary commands typically used to install botnet malware.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, pre-login CGI)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted HTTP request; automated by IoT botnet loaders
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — public exploit since 2016, adopted by Mirai-class botnets. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Replace the end-of-life DSL-2750B (no fixed firmware exists), and where still deployed disable remote/WAN management and block login.cgi from untrusted networks.
prevention.adequacy: There is no patch, so KEV remediation can only be met by decommissioning or isolating the device — patching is impossible.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for login.cgi requests carrying cli= command payloads and for CPE fetching remote binaries.
detection.adequacy: Residential/edge CPE rarely has centralized logging, so detection coverage is thin.

response.what_would_have_worked: Remove the device from service and replace it; assume any exposed unit is or was botnet-infected.
response.adequacy: Reboot/reflash is only temporary on an unpatchable device that will be reinfected.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality guidance would disable remote management on the CPE, but the device exposes login.cgi by default and, being end-of-life, offers no patch — the durable control is decommissioning, which flaw-remediation processes do not schedule.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security duties do not compel replacing or isolating an unpatchable, internet-facing end-of-life router that cannot be remediated in place.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Resilient-network expectations do not force retirement of legacy CPE whose vendor has issued no fix, leaving a permanently exploitable node on the estate.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The patch objective is unachievable because no patch exists; the control provides no fallback of mandated replacement for end-of-life hardware.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management assumes a fix path; for an EOL device with an unauthenticated RCE it has no remediation to apply short of removal.

new_control_requirements.name: EOL-ASSET-DECOMMISSION
new_control_requirements.description: Network-reachable end-of-life devices with no vendor patch path must be isolated and decommissioned within a bounded window; mandatory compensating segmentation applies until removal since no remediation exists.

CVE-2023-41990.name: Apple Multiple Products Code Execution Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A malicious iMessage attachment is processed without any user interaction; parsing a crafted font via the undocumented Apple-only ADJUST TrueType instruction corrupts memory and executes code, seeding the multi-stage Triangulation implant.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (zero-click; delivered via iMessage, requires no victim action)
attack_vector.complexity: high — bespoke exploit chain built by a capable actor, though victim-side it is fully automatic
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — discovered by Kaspersky researchers (Operation Triangulation). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Apple updates (iOS 15.7.8/16.3 and companions) promptly and enable Lockdown Mode for high-risk users to disable auto-processing of risky attachments.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the font flaw, but as a zero-click 0-day it was exploited before a fix existed; Lockdown Mode is the only pre-patch compensating control for targeted users.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Run Kaspersky's triangle_check against device backups and monitor for the anomalous post-exploit network/data-usage patterns of TriangleDB.
detection.adequacy: Mobile forensics is specialized and after-the-fact; real-time detection on the device is minimal.

response.what_would_have_worked: Update and reboot affected devices, and for confirmed targets consider device replacement plus credential/session rotation.
response.adequacy: Advanced implants may persist across the delivery chain; replacement is the conservative response for high-value targets.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation depends on Apple's patch, but a zero-click 0-day is weaponized before any patch exists; the SLA cannot cover the pre-disclosure exploitation window against high-value mobile targets.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management obligations do not address a client-side, zero-interaction mobile exploit delivered via iMessage, where there is no user action or perimeter control to interrupt the chain.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Resilient-system expectations assume a controllable OS update cadence, but mobile OS 0-days require rapid forced updates and lockdown postures the control does not mandate.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The operating-system patch objective is defeated by a 0-day with no fix at time of exploitation; the Essential Eight has no mobile zero-click compensating control.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management provides no play for a targeted mobile 0-day; only enrolling high-risk users in Apple Lockdown Mode meaningfully reduces exposure, which the control does not require.

new_control_requirements.name: MOBILE-ZERO-CLICK-HARDENING
new_control_requirements.description: High-risk mobile users must operate in a reduced-attack-surface mode (e.g. Lockdown Mode) that disables automatic processing of untrusted message attachments, fonts, and link previews, closing the zero-click delivery path patch SLAs cannot outrun.

CVE-2023-27524.name: Apache Superset Insecure Default Initialization of Resource Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker takes one of Superset's well-known default SECRET_KEY values, signs a session cookie carrying an admin identity (e.g. with flask-unsign), and is authenticated as administrator, then abuses admin database/SQL features toward code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; the forged cookie substitutes for a login)
attack_vector.complexity: low — default keys are public and cookie forging is a one-liner with off-the-shelf tools
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Horizon3.ai. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Set a unique, high-entropy SECRET_KEY (superset_config.py or SUPERSET_SECRET_KEY) and upgrade to 2.1.0+, which refuses the default; rotate the key and invalidate existing sessions.
prevention.adequacy: Upgrading helps, but the true fix is changing the secret and rotating it; a version bump without a strong SECRET_KEY leaves prior forged sessions/data exposure risk.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Detect session cookies that validate against known default keys and anonymous-to-admin transitions in Superset logs.
detection.adequacy: Requires application-log visibility that many deployments do not centralize.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate SECRET_KEY and all database credentials reachable from Superset, invalidate sessions, and review for unauthorized queries/exports.
response.adequacy: Without key and credential rotation, an attacker who already forged admin access may retain footholds.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Secure-configuration/least-functionality controls do not, by default, verify that a shipped default cryptographic secret was changed at deployment; Superset started fine with the insecure default, so the control gap is unenforced configuration.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Vulnerability-management duties cover the CVE but not the systemic root cause — inventorying deployments still running a known default SECRET_KEY, which needs config auditing rather than patch tracking.

UK-CAF-B2.gap: Identity and access-control expectations assume session tokens are unforgeable; a predictable signing key lets an attacker mint admin sessions, defeating access policy at the framework layer.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application-hardening guidance does not specifically require changing framework default secrets, so the forgeable session key persists in hardened-looking deployments.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.9.gap: Configuration-management as written does not mandate detecting and remediating unchanged vendor-default secrets, which is precisely what makes this session-forgery reachable.

new_control_requirements.name: FRAMEWORK-DEFAULT-SECRET-DETECTION
new_control_requirements.description: Deployment gating that fails any release shipping or running an unrotated framework default cryptographic secret (APP_KEY / SECRET_KEY and equivalents), which turn a leaked constant into session forgery and RCE.

CVE-2023-29300.name: Adobe ColdFusion Deserialization of Untrusted Data Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted serialized-object payload to a network-reachable ColdFusion endpoint; a gadget class (e.g. JdbcRowSetImpl) drives deserialization into arbitrary code execution, typically dropping a web shell.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network)
attack_vector.complexity: low — no user interaction and public gadget/Nuclei tooling exists
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Adobe / analyzed by ProjectDiscovery. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply ColdFusion 2018u17 / 2021u7 / 2023u1 (or later); restrict admin/AJAX endpoints and run ColdFusion under a low-privilege service account.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is decisive, but the exploitation window opened before many operators patched; segmentation alone does not block a request to the legitimate endpoint.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on new web shell files under CFIDE/webroot and on java-runtime processes spawning shells; inspect for deserialization gadget strings.
detection.adequacy: Detection catches post-exploitation but not the single deserialization POST that grants code execution.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, hunt for web shells and persistence, rotate secrets accessible to the ColdFusion account, and rebuild if compromised.
response.adequacy: Effective only after the fact; ransomware association raises the cost of delayed response.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: A standard 30-day flaw-remediation SLA is far longer than the observed window between disclosure and mass web-shell deployment; an emergency out-of-band ColdFusion patch was required well inside any routine cycle.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management catalogs the CVE but does not compel the emergency patch cadence a top-EPSS, ransomware-linked preauth RCE demands.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Baseline patch-management cadence does not force the rapid remediation an actively-exploited internet-facing app-server RCE requires; the KEV due date (2024-01-29) is tighter than typical SLAs.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security/patch controls do not on their own detect the initial deserialization POST, which reaches a legitimate application endpoint and blends with normal ColdFusion traffic.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The 48-hour internet-facing patch target is frequently missed on legacy ColdFusion estates, leaving a preauth RCE exposed after public gadget chains dropped.

CVE-2023-38203.name: Adobe ColdFusion Deserialization of Untrusted Data Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker submits a serialized payload using a gadget class (JdbcRowSetImpl) that Adobe's deserialization denylist did not block, achieving RCE — a bypass of the fix for the related CVE-2023-29300.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network)
attack_vector.complexity: low — no user interaction; public gadget analysis and Nuclei tooling exist
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — ProjectDiscovery research. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply ColdFusion 2018u18 / 2021u8 / 2023u2 (or later); move deserialization to an allowlist and least-privilege the service account.
prevention.adequacy: The re-patch is decisive, but the denylist bypass shows blocklist-based mitigation is structurally fragile.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Watch for JNDI/RMI egress from the ColdFusion process and new web shells; flag gadget-class strings in requests.
detection.adequacy: Post-exploitation only; the triggering request looks like normal endpoint traffic.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate, hunt for web shells and persistence, rotate credentials, and rebuild on confirmed compromise.
response.adequacy: Reactive; ransomware linkage increases the cost of any delay.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The out-of-band Adobe fix for this denylist bypass landed days after disclosure; a routine flaw-remediation SLA cannot keep pace with a chained, actively-exploited preauth RCE.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality/deny-by-default at the object layer is exactly what Adobe's denylist attempted and failed — an allowlist-based deserialization policy, not a blocklist, is required, which this control does not by itself mandate.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management does not compel the emergency re-patch that a fix-bypass zero-day demands within days.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-handling.gap: Vulnerability-handling processes assume a single patch closes the issue; a denylist bypass requires re-remediation, which routine handling under-covers.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Network/patch controls do not detect a deserialization POST to a legitimate endpoint using a not-yet-blocked gadget class.

attack_vector.description: An attacker crafts an Excel file whose Number-format string contains Perl code; when a service embedding Spreadsheet::ParseExcel parses the file, the module passes the format string into a string eval and executes the attacker's code.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (attacker supplies a file; delivery is via email/upload to the parsing service)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoC exists; exploitation is triggered simply by the target parsing the file
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — Barracuda/Mandiant incident response. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade the embedded module to Spreadsheet::ParseExcel 0.66; inventory appliances for the bundled copy and disable/patch the eval path.
prevention.adequacy: Patch fixes the eval, but operators must first discover the module inside an appliance they cannot directly patch.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Sandbox inbound attachments and alert on the mail-parsing process spawning perl/shells or making outbound connections.
detection.adequacy: Behavioral detection is the main hope where the vulnerable library is embedded and unpatchable by the operator.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate affected appliances, hunt for SEASPY/SALTWATER-style implants, and follow Barracuda's guidance (replace rather than patch compromised ESG units).
response.adequacy: Barracuda advised device replacement for compromised ESG appliances, underscoring the limits of remediation.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation targets first-party software; a vulnerability in a transitive Perl dependency embedded in an appliance is invisible to patch cycles that track the appliance version, not its bundled modules.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management rarely enumerates deep third-party library dependencies inside an appliance, so the vulnerable Spreadsheet::ParseExcel copy goes untracked.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Perimeter/network controls do not stop a malicious document delivered as a legitimate email attachment to a mail-security appliance whose job is to parse it.

DORA-Art-9.gap: ICT protection expectations do not by themselves compel SBOM-level visibility into embedded parsing libraries that a supply-chain-style document exploit abuses.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application-hardening guidance focuses on macro/Office hardening on endpoints, not on server-side spreadsheet parsers that eval format strings.

CVE-2023-7024.name: Google Chromium WebRTC Heap Buffer Overflow Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A remote attacker serves a crafted HTML page that drives WebRTC into a heap buffer overflow, corrupting the heap in the renderer to gain code execution in the browser process context.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (remote; requires the victim to visit a page)
attack_vector.complexity: low for the victim (a single page visit), though exploit development is sophisticated and was used in targeted attacks
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — Google Threat Analysis Group. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Enforce Chrome auto-update to 120.0.6099.129+ and require browser relaunch across the fleet; deploy site-isolation and reduced attack surface for high-risk users.
prevention.adequacy: The update fixes the flaw only once every Chrome process is restarted; unrestarted browsers stay vulnerable.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Endpoint telemetry on renderer crashes and anomalous child processes from the browser; block/monitor known malicious infrastructure.
detection.adequacy: Client-side memory-corruption exploitation is hard to detect at the network layer; the delivering page looks like normal web traffic.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force-update and relaunch browsers, investigate targeted users for follow-on implants, rotate credentials entered in-browser.
response.adequacy: Reactive; targeted-operation use means high-value users may already be compromised.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: A monthly or slower browser-update SLA leaves a client-side zero-day exploitable via any web page; auto-update must be enforced, which flaw-remediation policy often does not treat as urgent for endpoints.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management tends to prioritize server/appliance patches; a browser heap-overflow zero-day needs endpoint-fleet-wide forced restart to take effect.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Baseline patch cadence does not force the near-immediate browser relaunch across a fleet that a drive-by client zero-day requires.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Endpoint/patch controls do not detect exploitation delivered as ordinary web content to a legitimately running browser.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The application-patch target for browsers is frequently unmet on managed fleets, and 'patched' Chrome is not protected until every process is restarted.

CVE-2023-49897.name: FXC AE1021, AE1021PE OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker who can authenticate to the router (often via default/weak credentials) submits a crafted value to a management parameter that is passed to an OS shell without sanitization, executing arbitrary commands and typically installing a Mirai-variant bot.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (authenticated to the device management interface)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted authenticated request; weaponized by an automated botnet
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — Akamai SIRT. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply FXC fixed firmware, change default credentials, and remove the management interface from untrusted networks.
prevention.adequacy: Firmware fixes the injection, but unmanaged edge devices are seldom updated, so credential hygiene and exposure reduction are essential compensations.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor router egress for ELF payload retrieval and DDoS beaconing; alert on management-plane logins from unexpected sources.
detection.adequacy: Most operators lack telemetry on CPE routers, so conscription is noticed only via outbound abuse.

response.what_would_have_worked: Factory-reset and re-flash, rotate credentials, and block C2 infrastructure.
response.adequacy: Effective per-device but hard to scale across a dispersed CPE fleet.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: SOHO/edge routers like the AE1021 rarely receive centrally managed patches, so a firmware fix does not reach the exposed fleet within the KEV window.

NIST-800-63B-rev4.gap: Because exploitation requires a login, weak/default/reused router credentials directly enable the injection; authenticator strength for embedded-device admin accounts is routinely unaddressed.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security baselines seldom cover edge CPE devices whose management plane is reachable and whose command injection turns a valid login into RCE.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Proactive security-event discovery does not extend to consumer-grade routers, so botnet conscription goes unnoticed until the device emits DDoS traffic.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch targets assume managed IT assets; unmanaged FXC edge routers fall outside the patch program entirely.

CVE-2023-47565.name: QNAP VioStor NVR OS Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker authenticated to a VioStor NVR submits a crafted value to a web-interface field that reaches an OS shell unsanitized, executing arbitrary commands and typically installing a Mirai-variant bot for DDoS.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (authenticated to the NVR web interface, often via default/weak credentials)
attack_vector.complexity: low — one crafted authenticated request; automated botnet weaponization
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — Akamai SIRT. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to QVR 5.0.0+ (or retire the EoL device), change default credentials, and remove the NVR from internet exposure.
prevention.adequacy: Because the affected models are legacy, replacement/segmentation is often the only real fix; patch alone may be unavailable for the oldest units.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor NVR egress for payload retrieval and DDoS beaconing; alert on management logins from unexpected sources.
detection.adequacy: Standalone appliances usually lack telemetry; abuse is seen only downstream.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate and re-image or decommission the NVR, rotate credentials, and block C2.
response.adequacy: Reactive; EoL status makes long-term remediation a replacement decision.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The fix requires migrating off end-of-life QVR 4.x to QVR 5.0.0+, which is a device-replacement/upgrade effort a flaw-remediation SLA cannot satisfy within the KEV window.

NIST-800-63B-rev4.gap: Exploitation requires a login, so weak/default/reused NVR admin credentials are the enabling condition; authenticator strength on legacy appliances is unmanaged.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security baselines rarely inventory internet-exposed NVRs, leaving their reachable management plane open to command injection.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Vulnerability management tends to overlook end-of-life video-surveillance appliances that no longer receive routine updates.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Security-event discovery does not extend to standalone NVRs, so botnet conscription surfaces only as outbound DDoS abuse.

CVE-2023-6448.name: Unitronics Vision PLC and HMI Insecure Default Password Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker with network reachability to an exposed Unitronics PLC/HMI authenticates using the publicly known factory default password and gains administrative control, enabling defacement, logic changes, or process disruption.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access; the default credential is public)
attack_vector.complexity: low — no exploit code needed, just the known default password and a reachable management port
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — coordinated disclosure / CISA-EPA incident response. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Change the default password (upgrade VisiLogic to 9.9.00+), remove the PLC/HMI from internet exposure, and place it behind a segmented OT boundary/VPN.
prevention.adequacy: Changing the credential closes the primary path, but network exposure of the OT management protocol must also be eliminated.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on external logins to PCOM/TCP 20256 and on unexpected PLC state changes; monitor HMI defacement.
detection.adequacy: Many OT networks lack monitoring on the management protocol, so intrusion is noticed only after disruption.

response.what_would_have_worked: Restore known-good PLC logic, rotate all device credentials, and remove internet exposure; coordinate with sector CISA/EPA guidance for water utilities.
response.adequacy: Effective but safety-critical processes may be disrupted before response begins.

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: Identification/authentication controls that permit a shared factory default password to remain active provide no real assurance; the device ships pre-authenticated to anyone who knows the default.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege is meaningless when the sole administrative account uses a universally known default credential reachable from the network.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Exposing an OT management protocol (PCOM/20256) to the internet is the enabling condition; network-security baselines frequently omit OT/ICS segmentation for small PLC estates.

UK-CAF-B2.gap: Identity and access control for OT devices seldom enforces mandatory default-credential change on commissioning, leaving critical-service PLCs trivially accessible.

AU-ISM-1546.gap: Privileged/default-account controls are inconsistently applied to embedded OT devices, so the factory password persists on safety-relevant equipment.

new_control_requirements.name: OT-DEFAULT-CREDENTIAL-ELIMINATION
new_control_requirements.description: No internet-reachable OT device (PLC/HMI/RTU) may ship or run with a vendor-default or blank administrative credential; enforce mandatory credential rotation at commissioning and segment the OT management plane from untrusted networks.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a path-traversal HTTP request to Qlik Sense that yields an anonymous session, allowing further requests to unauthorized backend endpoints; combined with CVE-2023-41265 this reaches remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public Nuclei template and ZeroQlik chain exist; used at scale by Cactus ransomware
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — Praetorian research. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Qlik Sense patch for the relevant train (Aug 2023 IR / May 2023 Patch 4 / Feb 2023 Patch 8 / Nov 2022 Patch 11 / Aug 2022 Patch 13) and restrict the Qlik proxy from untrusted networks.
prevention.adequacy: Patching is decisive, but the medium base score led many to deprioritize it despite its role as the chain's entry point.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on anonymous-session creation and traversal-style request paths against the Qlik proxy; monitor Qlik services spawning shells.
detection.adequacy: The auth-bypass request looks like ordinary HTTP; without app-aware logging the initial access is invisible.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the Qlik host, hunt for the CVE-2023-41265 follow-on and Cactus tooling, rotate the Qlik service-account credentials, and rebuild if compromised.
response.adequacy: Reactive; ransomware chaining means fast containment is critical.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The path-traversal fix shipped as scattered per-release patches across many Qlik Sense trains; a single flaw-remediation SLA struggled to cover an estate on mixed patch levels before ransomware weaponization.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: A boundary/WAF permitting the request to the legitimate Qlik Sense endpoint does not stop a traversal that generates an anonymous session; the request is valid at the network layer.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management under-prioritized a CVSS-6.5 path traversal that becomes critical only when chained, so patching lagged the exploited chain.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Severity-driven vulnerability programs deprioritize a medium-rated auth-bypass primitive that is the first link of an RCE ransomware chain.

UK-CAF-C1.gap: Security monitoring often lacks visibility into anonymous-session creation inside the BI application, so the initial-access step goes undetected.

attack_vector.description: A remote attacker tunnels an HTTP request inside a raw HTTP request so the Qlik backend repository app executes it under the service account, escalating privileges; combined with the CVE-2023-41266 anonymous session it yields unauthenticated remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low as scored, but effectively none when chained with CVE-2023-41266's anonymous session
attack_vector.complexity: low — public ZeroQlik chain; scope-change (S:C) escalation to the service account
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — Praetorian research. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the fixed Qlik Sense train (and the follow-up for CVE-2023-48365), least-privilege the Qlik service account, and restrict the proxy from untrusted networks.
prevention.adequacy: The first patch was incomplete, so operators had to re-remediate; least-privilege limits blast radius of the service-account RCE.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Deploy request-smuggling-aware inspection and alert on Qlik services spawning shells or tunneled backend requests.
detection.adequacy: Standard WAFs may not normalize the ambiguous raw request that carries the tunneled payload.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, hunt for Cactus tooling and persistence, rotate the service-account credentials, and rebuild on compromise.
response.adequacy: Reactive; the RCE runs with service-account rights, so credential rotation and host rebuild are essential.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Qlik's first fix was incomplete (superseded by CVE-2023-48365), so a single flaw-remediation cycle left the RCE chain reachable; re-remediation was required.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary/WAF inspection does not reliably normalize the ambiguous raw HTTP request that drives the tunneling, so the smuggled request passes through to the backend.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege gaps let the Qlik service account (under which tunneled requests execute) run with broad rights, amplifying the RCE into full host compromise.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Vulnerability programs treat the fix as complete after one patch, but the incomplete-fix follow-up (CVE-2023-48365) required a second remediation the process did not anticipate.

UK-CAF-C1.gap: CAF Objective C security monitoring does not mandate HTTP-request-smuggling-aware detection, so the tunneled request that reaches the backend and yields service-account RCE is not surfaced.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential Eight patch-application maturity treats the first Qlik advisory as complete, but the incomplete-fix follow-up (CVE-2023-48365) required a second remediation cycle the model does not anticipate.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical vulnerability management closed the first advisory but not the request-smuggling primitive the incomplete fix left reachable until the follow-up patch.

CVE-2023-33107.name: Qualcomm Multiple Chipsets Integer Overflow Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local, already-present unprivileged application issues a crafted IOCTL to the Adreno GPU (kgsl) driver; an integer overflow during shared-virtual-memory assignment corrupts kernel memory and escalates privileges, typically as one stage of a mobile exploit chain.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local; requires code execution as an unprivileged app on the device)
attack_vector.complexity: high — reliable kernel memory-corruption exploitation is sophisticated and was used in targeted attacks
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — Qualcomm/Google TAG/Project Zero indications of in-the-wild use. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the OEM security update carrying the December 2023 Qualcomm patch level and reboot; restrict sideloading so a malicious app cannot reach the driver.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is decisive but gated on the OEM/carrier update chain; devices without an available build stay exposed.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile EDR/attestation flagging anomalous kgsl IOCTL usage or unexpected privilege transitions.
detection.adequacy: On-device detection of a kernel LPE is limited, especially within a targeted spyware chain.

response.what_would_have_worked: Update firmware, re-image high-risk devices, and investigate for follow-on implants on targeted users.
response.adequacy: Reactive; targeted use means compromise may precede any available OEM patch.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The fix must traverse Qualcomm to Android OEM to carrier before reaching devices; this multi-hop patch chain routinely exceeds any flaw-remediation SLA, leaving the LPE exploitable for months.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality does not constrain a legitimate GPU IOCTL surface (kgsl) that any installed app can reach; the privileged driver interface is exposed by design.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Vulnerability management for mobile fleets depends on OEM update availability the organization does not control, so a known-exploited kernel LPE stays open on unpatched handsets.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management cadence cannot force firmware/driver updates onto devices whose vendor has not yet shipped the OEM build, undermining timely remediation of a KEV kernel flaw.

AU-ISM-1546.gap: Mobile-device hardening controls rarely account for a GPU-driver LPE that undoes app-sandbox isolation once a malicious app is already present.

CVE-2023-33106.name: Qualcomm Multiple Chipsets Use of Out-of-Range Pointer Offset Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local, low-privileged Android app submits an oversized sync-point list in an IOCTL_KGSL_GPU_AUX_COMMAND to the Adreno GPU (KGSL) driver, causing an out-of-range pointer write that is corralled into a privilege-escalation primitive.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local code execution as an unprivileged app on the device)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted IOCTL triggers the corruption
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — exploitation reported by Google Threat Analysis Group and Google Project Zero. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install the OEM firmware/OS update carrying Qualcomm's December 2023 driver fix; restrict sideloading and enforce app vetting to limit local-attacker code on the device.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone is often unreachable in time because the fix depends on OEM/carrier firmware delivery that lags disclosure by months.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile EDR flagging anomalous IOCTL usage against KGSL, unexpected privilege transitions, or spyware implant behavior.
detection.adequacy: Kernel-driver LPE leaves little userspace telemetry; detection on-device is limited.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate affected devices, force firmware update, and rotate credentials/tokens held on the device if implant is suspected.
response.adequacy: Response is constrained by OEM patch availability and BYOD ownership boundaries.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation assumes a deployable vendor patch, but the Qualcomm Adreno-driver fix must traverse the OEM/carrier firmware chain, so the exploitation window vastly exceeds any realistic org patch SLA.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Timely-patching obligations cannot compel handset OEMs/carriers to ship the chipset driver fix; an org's MDM cannot patch a GPU kernel driver the OEM has not released.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The 48-hour patch target for actively exploited flaws is unachievable when remediation depends on an OEM firmware image that may not exist yet for a given device model.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Secure configuration and maintenance presumes patchable software; a GPU/DSP kernel-driver local privilege escalation on BYOD/unmanaged mobile is largely outside the organisation's patch control.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical vulnerability management presumes an available remediation path, but here it is gated behind OEM firmware rebuilds, leaving a long unremediable exposure.

CVE-2023-33063.name: Qualcomm Multiple Chipsets Use-After-Free Vulnerability (DSP Services)

attack_vector.description: An unprivileged local process issues a remote call from HLOS to the DSP (via FastRPC) that frees and then reuses DSP Services memory, giving a use-after-free primitive escalated to elevated privileges.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local code execution with FastRPC/DSP access)
attack_vector.complexity: low — reachable through the standard HLOS-to-DSP remote-call path
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — exploitation reported by Google Threat Analysis Group and Google Project Zero. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Deploy the OEM firmware update carrying Qualcomm's December 2023 DSP fix; restrict which apps can reach FastRPC/DSP interfaces.
prevention.adequacy: Patch reachability depends on OEM firmware delivery that lags disclosure, leaving a prolonged exposure.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile EDR monitoring anomalous FastRPC/DSP invocations and unexpected privilege transitions.
detection.adequacy: DSP-driver corruption is nearly invisible to userspace telemetry.

response.what_would_have_worked: Quarantine device, force firmware update, and rotate on-device secrets if compromise suspected.
response.adequacy: Constrained by OEM patch availability and device ownership.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation presumes a patch the org can deploy; the DSP-driver fix must arrive via OEM firmware, so real-world remediation trails the active-exploitation window by months.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management duties cannot force OEMs/carriers to ship the chipset DSP fix; enterprise MDM cannot remediate a DSP kernel driver directly.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The exploited-flaw patch SLA is not meetable when the fix is bound to an OEM firmware image that may lag or never ship for older device models.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Maintenance/secure-configuration objectives assume patchable software; a DSP kernel-driver use-after-free on BYOD mobile sits outside the org's patch reach.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical vulnerability management assumes a remediation path exists, yet it is OEM-gated for chipset drivers.

CVE-2022-22071.name: Qualcomm Multiple Chipsets Use-After-Free Vulnerability (process shell memory)

attack_vector.description: A local app frees process shell memory through an IOCTL munmap call during process initialization, winning a race that leaves a dangling reference reused for privilege escalation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local unprivileged app execution)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a race that is winnable with repeated attempts
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed via Qualcomm's bulletin; exploitation flagged by Google TAG/Project Zero. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the OEM firmware update carrying Qualcomm's May 2022 fix; restrict untrusted local app execution.
prevention.adequacy: OEM firmware delivery lag and legacy-model support gaps leave many devices unpatched.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile EDR watching for anomalous munmap-race patterns and privilege transitions.
detection.adequacy: Race-condition LPE in a driver is hard to observe from userspace.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate and update affected devices; rotate on-device secrets if implant suspected.
response.adequacy: Bounded by OEM patch availability.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation assumes a patch the org can push, but the chipset fix travels via OEM firmware, so devices remain exposed long after the bulletin.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patching duties cannot compel OEMs/carriers to deliver the chipset driver update; enterprise tooling cannot patch the affected driver directly.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The exploited-flaw patch window is not achievable when older device models may never receive the OEM firmware carrying the fix.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Maintenance objectives assume patchable software; a chipset-driver use-after-free on unmanaged mobile is outside the org's patch reach.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical vulnerability management presumes an available remediation path, but chipset-driver fixes are OEM-gated and frequently unavailable for legacy models.

CVE-2023-42917.name: Apple Multiple Products WebKit Memory Corruption Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker serves crafted web content that triggers an out-of-bounds write in WebKit's WebContent process, yielding arbitrary code execution when the victim merely views the page.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated — victim visits attacker-controlled web content)
attack_vector.complexity: low — CVSS AC:L; a single malicious page, though a reliable memory-corruption primitive is required to weaponize
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Apple and Google TAG (Clement Lecigne). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update to iOS/iPadOS 17.1.2 / macOS 14.1.2 / Safari 17.1.2 (or 16.7.2 backport); enable Lockdown Mode for high-risk users to shrink the WebKit attack surface.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is necessary but did not cover the pre-disclosure zero-day window; Lockdown Mode is the only pre-patch mitigation.

detection.what_would_have_worked: MDM/EDR review of WebContent crash reports and iOS sysdiagnose for exploitation artifacts.
detection.adequacy: Mobile telemetry is limited; targeted implants are stealthy.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force OS update, and for suspected targets rotate credentials and consider device replacement/forensics.
response.adequacy: On-device forensics on iOS is constrained without specialist tooling.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation with a defined patch SLA is meaningless for a zero-day already exploited before disclosure; the exposure existed before any patch was available.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Timely patching cannot close a window that opened pre-disclosure; it addresses only the residual fleet after Apple shipped 17.1.2/16.7.2.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The application/OS patch target starts the clock at disclosure, but targeted exploitation preceded it, so the control never covered the active window.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Secure-maintenance controls presume auto-update coverage; jailbroken, MDM-deferred, or end-of-support Apple devices remain exposed to malicious web content.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical vulnerability management reacts to known advisories, but this was weaponized as an unknown zero-day, defeating advisory-driven prioritization.

CVE-2023-42916.name: Apple Multiple Products WebKit Out-of-Bounds Read Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Crafted web content triggers an out-of-bounds read in WebKit that leaks process memory, typically used to defeat ASLR and stabilize a companion code-execution exploit.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated — victim visits attacker-controlled web content)
attack_vector.complexity: low — CVSS AC:L; a single malicious page triggers the read
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Clement Lecigne of Google TAG. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update to the patched Apple OS/Safari builds; enable Lockdown Mode for high-risk users.
prevention.adequacy: Patch did not cover the pre-disclosure window; Lockdown Mode was the only pre-patch mitigation.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Review of WebContent crash artifacts and iOS sysdiagnose for exploitation traces.
detection.adequacy: An info-leak leaves minimal on-device evidence.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force OS update and, for suspected targets, rotate secrets and pursue device forensics.
response.adequacy: Constrained by limited iOS forensic access.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: A patch SLA cannot cover a zero-day info-leak exploited before disclosure; the leak fed a chained exploit while no fix existed.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management timelines only address the residual fleet after Apple shipped the fix, not the pre-disclosure exploitation window.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The patch clock starts at disclosure, but targeted exploitation preceded it, so the control did not cover the active window.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Maintenance controls assume auto-update coverage; deferred-update or end-of-support Apple devices stay exposed to memory-disclosure via web content.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Advisory-driven vulnerability management cannot prioritize an unknown zero-day used to leak memory and defeat mitigations like ASLR.

attack_vector.description: From an already-compromised renderer, an attacker feeds a malicious graphics primitive that overflows an integer in Skia's size calculation, producing an undersized buffer whose subsequent writes corrupt adjacent memory to escape the sandbox.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (attacker must already control the renderer process)
attack_vector.complexity: high — requires a prior renderer compromise to reach the Skia sink and chain the escape
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Google TAG (Benoit Sevens, Clement Lecigne); RCA by Google Project Zero. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update Chrome/Chromium to 119.0.6045.199+; ensure embedded WebViews and Chromium-derived products rebuild against the fixed Skia; keep site isolation enabled.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is necessary but does not retroactively cover the pre-fix zero-day window; downstream Skia consumers patch on their own slower schedules.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on renderer crashes referencing Skia and on unexpected child processes spawned by the browser.
detection.adequacy: Sandbox-escape stages are brief and easily lost in normal browser crash noise.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force browser update fleet-wide and investigate hosts with anomalous browser child-process activity.
response.adequacy: Attribution to this specific stage requires memory forensics rarely available.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: A patch SLA cannot cover a browser zero-day exploited before the 119.0.6045.199 fix shipped; the sandbox escape widened the impact of any prior renderer compromise while unpatched.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Timely patching addresses only post-fix rollout; managed-browser update lag and pinned versions leave a real exposure to a chained escape.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Browser hardening reduces renderer attack surface but does not stop an integer-overflow sandbox escape once the renderer is compromised, which is the precise gap this flaw exploits.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Secure-configuration/maintenance controls assume auto-updating browsers; kiosks, embedded WebViews, and Flutter/Chromium-derived products often lag the Chrome fix.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Advisory-driven vulnerability management cannot pre-empt an unknown browser zero-day used specifically to break out of the renderer sandbox.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker requests the graphapi-bundled GetPhpInfo.php endpoint, which returns full phpinfo() output; in containerized deployments this leaks environment variables holding the ownCloud admin password, mail credentials, and license key.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network request)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single GET (plus a trivial /.css suffix to bypass Docker rewrite rules)
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by ownCloud PSIRT. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update graphapi to 0.2.1/0.3.1, delete the GetPhpInfo.php file, disable phpinfo in containers, and rotate every credential that could have been exposed.
prevention.adequacy: Patching alone is insufficient — the file persists after disabling the app, and leaked secrets remain valid until rotated.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Web/WAF logs for GET requests to GetPhpInfo.php (with or without a /.css suffix) and 200 responses containing phpinfo tables.
detection.adequacy: Detection is straightforward but only confirms exposure after the fact; assume credentials are compromised.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate ownCloud admin, mail, database, and license credentials; audit for use of the leaked secrets.
response.adequacy: Credential rotation is the decisive step given automated mass harvesting.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Even after patching graphapi to 0.2.1/0.3.1, disabling the app does not remove the vulnerable GetPhpInfo.php file, and any secrets already leaked stay valid until rotated — flaw remediation alone is insufficient.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: A network boundary does not help when ownCloud is intentionally internet-facing and the disclosure is an unauthenticated GET on the normal service port.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-handling.gap: Vulnerability handling that stops at applying the update misses the mandatory follow-on: delete the residual file and rotate every credential exposed via phpinfo.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch timelines cannot undo credential exposure that occurred during the mass-scanning window before the fix landed; harvested secrets require rotation, not patching.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.9.gap: Configuration management failed to strip a debug library from production, and mitigation requires re-securing configuration (remove file, disable phpinfo, rotate secrets), not just a version bump.

CVE-2023-4911.name: GNU C Library Buffer Overflow Vulnerability (Looney Tunables)

attack_vector.description: A local user invokes a SUID-root binary with a maliciously crafted GLIBC_TUNABLES environment variable; ld.so overflows a buffer while parsing the tunables, giving code execution with root privileges.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (any local user account)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoCs reliably produce a root shell with a single command
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — discovered by the Qualys Threat Research Unit. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install the patched glibc package and reboot/restart affected services; as an interim mitigation reduce reliance on setuid binaries or apply the vendor's tunables mitigation.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is decisive but operationally heavy; until it and a reboot land, the SUID attack path stays open.

detection.what_would_have_worked: auditd/EDR rules on execve events carrying oversized GLIBC_TUNABLES values, especially on setuid binaries, and alerts on unexpected root shells from unprivileged users.
detection.adequacy: Environment-variable-based triggers are detectable but easily missed without explicit rules.

response.what_would_have_worked: Patch and reboot, hunt for miner/implant persistence (e.g. Kinsing), and review privileged actions performed after any suspected escalation.
response.adequacy: Post-root activity can be extensive; assume broad access if exploited.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Patching glibc across a large Linux fleet is slow and reboot/service-restart gated, while a reliable public PoC turns any local foothold into instant root — the exploitation window dwarfs typical glibc patch cadence.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least privilege does not contain this flaw: it escalates any unprivileged local user to root through ordinary SUID binaries, defeating the privilege boundary the control assumes.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Timely-patching duties are hard to meet for a core system library on servers requiring change windows/reboots, leaving broad exposure to a trivially reliable LPE.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The exploited-flaw patch target is difficult to hit for glibc, which underpins every dynamically linked binary and often needs a reboot to fully take effect.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical vulnerability management must prioritize a ubiquitous library flaw with a public root-shell PoC, yet fleet-wide glibc remediation is operationally heavy and easily deprioritized.

CVE-2023-36584.name: Microsoft Windows Mark of the Web (MOTW) Security Feature Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker crafts a delivered file so Windows fails to apply the Mark-of-the-Web tag; when the victim opens it, SmartScreen and Office Protected View do not trigger, smoothing execution of the next stage in a phishing chain.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (requires the user to open a delivered file; no authentication)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a crafted file that evades MOTW tagging
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Microsoft; associated with RomCom/Storm-0978 activity. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the October 2023 Windows cumulative update; enforce attachment/download policies and application-control so unmarked files still face execution constraints.
prevention.adequacy: Patch removes the bypass, but layered controls are needed because MOTW is only one of several delivery-stage defenses.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Endpoint rules flagging internet-origin files lacking a Zone.Identifier stream and Office documents opening without Protected View from external sources.
detection.adequacy: Detection must not rely on the very prompts the bug suppresses.

response.what_would_have_worked: Investigate the full phishing chain, patch endpoints, and hunt for RomCom-style follow-on payloads.
response.adequacy: The bypass is a delivery enabler; response should target the chained payload.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Patching the MOTW bypass closes one evasion, but the flaw's value is defeating a defensive prompt during delivery — the exposure lasts until every endpoint receives the OS cumulative update.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: Detection controls that lean on SmartScreen/Protected View prompts are blinded precisely because this bug suppresses those security features, so anomaly detection must compensate elsewhere.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Timely OS patching is required, but the bypass is weaponized in phishing chains that reach users before the cumulative update is fleet-wide, so patch cadence alone leaves a delivery-stage gap.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application-hardening and macro/Protected-View controls are undermined when MOTW is not applied, which is the exact protection this flaw circumvents.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.7.gap: Protection-against-malware controls that depend on the OS trust prompt are insufficient when the file arrives without the MOTW marking that would trigger scanning/warnings.

CVE-2023-1671.name: Sophos Web Appliance Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted request to the warn-proceed handler of the Sophos Web Appliance, injecting shell metacharacters into a parameter that the appliance passes to an OS command, achieving remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (pre-authentication)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted HTTP request; public PoC/scanner available
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Sophos. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update to Sophos Web Appliance 4.3.10.4+; because the product is end-of-life, migrate to a supported web-gateway and remove the appliance from internet exposure.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is available but the EOL status means many instances stay vulnerable; decommissioning is the durable fix.

detection.what_would_have_worked: WAF/proxy logs for shell metacharacters in requests to the appliance UI and host telemetry for unexpected shell/child processes under the web service.
detection.adequacy: Command-injection strings are detectable, but a compromised gateway can tamper with its own logs.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the appliance, rebuild/replace it, rotate any credentials it stored or proxied, and hunt for lateral movement from the gateway.
response.adequacy: A compromised inline web gateway is a high-trust pivot; assume broad exposure.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation is undercut because Sophos Web Appliance is end-of-life; many exposed instances will never receive the 4.3.10.4 fix, so patching cannot close the population of vulnerable devices.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection is ineffective when the appliance is itself the internet-facing security gateway; the pre-auth injection is reachable on the very interface the device presents to the network.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Vulnerability management that assumes a supported product cannot remediate an EOL appliance; the required action is decommissioning/replacement, not patching.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The exploited-flaw patch SLA is unmeetable for an end-of-life appliance with no forthcoming update path.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: Secure-maintenance objectives assume a maintainable product; an unsupported internet-facing appliance with a pre-auth RCE cannot be brought into compliance by patching.

CVE-2020-2551.name: Oracle Fusion Middleware WebLogic IIOP Deserialization RCE

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted IIOP request to the WebLogic listen port carrying a Java deserialization gadget chain, achieving remote code execution and full server takeover without credentials.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access via IIOP)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public gadget-chain exploits reduce it to a single crafted request against an exposed listener
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed through Oracle's Critical Patch Update process. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Jan 2020 Oracle CPU and disable/segment the IIOP subprotocol; front WebLogic with a boundary that blocks T3/IIOP from untrusted networks.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone is insufficient while the IIOP listener remains internet-reachable; protocol hardening and segmentation are needed to survive the disclosure-to-patch gap.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on inbound IIOP/GIOP to 7001 from untrusted sources and on the WebLogic JVM spawning shells or making outbound LDAP/RMI callbacks.
detection.adequacy: Serialized-object detection at the boundary catches many gadget deliveries but can be evaded with novel chains.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, rotate WebLogic/domain credentials and keystores, and hunt for web shells and persistence dropped by the process user.
response.adequacy: Post-RCE the attacker runs as the server process; assume full domain-config compromise.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation on a quarterly Oracle CPU cadence leaves the IIOP listener exploitable for months; the observed exploitation window (mass-scanned within days of the CPU) far outruns typical WebLogic patch SLAs.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection that permits the T3/IIOP listener (7001) to reach the internet or flat internal networks is the precondition for unauthenticated RCE; simply patching does not close the exposed management protocol.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management that treats a 'critical' CVSS as a scheduled patch item misses that this flaw is preauth, network-reachable, and has public gadget-chain exploits — it demands emergency out-of-band remediation, not the standard cycle.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Essential/important entities running WebLogic must handle actively-exploited RCE inside the incident window, but the article does not force IIOP-protocol hardening or listener segmentation that would blunt exploitation between disclosure and patch.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The Essential Eight 'patch applications within 48 hours for exploited flaws' target is only met if the org tracks Oracle CPUs as security-critical; the control is silent on disabling/segmenting the IIOP subprotocol as a compensating measure.

CVE-2023-36033.name: Windows DWM Core Library Elevation of Privilege

attack_vector.description: After gaining a low-privilege foothold, an attacker abuses a memory-corruption / untrusted-pointer flaw in the DWM Core Library to escalate from the Window Manager\DWM context to SYSTEM.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local, authenticated low-privilege user)
attack_vector.complexity: low — reliable in-the-wild exploitation was demonstrated, though the technique was novel
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported after in-the-wild detection by security researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install the Nov 2023 cumulative update; minimize local foothold opportunities (phishing/exec controls) so the LPE has nothing to chain from.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is necessary but cannot cover the zero-day window; behavioral detection of privilege escalation is required alongside.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR rules for integrity-level jumps and SYSTEM token theft originating from graphics/DWM interactions.
detection.adequacy: Default Windows logging rarely records the token manipulation; specialized EDR telemetry is needed.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, assume SYSTEM compromise, hunt for follow-on persistence and credential theft.
response.adequacy: Once SYSTEM is obtained the host must be treated as fully compromised.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Monthly Patch-Tuesday remediation is the only fix, but this was a zero-day exploited before the patch existed — SI-2 cannot cover the pre-disclosure window and must be paired with exploit-mitigation and EDR.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least privilege limits what an initial foothold can do, but a DWM-to-SYSTEM LPE by design defeats the privilege boundary; AC-6 alone does not stop kernel/graphics-subsystem token theft.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: System monitoring is the realistic control for a post-foothold LPE, yet default logging rarely captures the integrity-level jump; without EDR tuned for token manipulation the escalation is invisible.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management scheduled on patch cadence cannot address an actively-exploited zero-day LPE; it needs behavioral detection of privilege escalation as a compensating layer.

AU-ISM-1546.gap: Applying vendor patches within the ISM timeframe still leaves the zero-day window uncovered; the control does not mandate the EDR/exploit-guard telemetry that would catch DWM token abuse.

attack_vector.description: An attacker delivers a crafted .url Internet Shortcut that points to a WebDAV/SMB-hosted payload; opening it bypasses Defender SmartScreen's warning and Mark-of-the-Web checks, silently executing malware.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (requires user to open the crafted shortcut)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoC shortcuts exist and require only a single user click
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported after in-the-wild detection by security researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Nov 2023 update; block outbound WebDAV/SMB to untrusted hosts and restrict Internet-Shortcut auto-execution via app-control.
prevention.adequacy: Patch removes the specific bypass, but egress control and app-hardening are needed because the reputation layer itself is the thing being defeated.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on .url files targeting external WebDAV/SMB and on script/LNK execution without a SmartScreen prompt.
detection.adequacy: Detection is feasible but many environments do not monitor WebDAV egress or shortcut-target resolution.

response.what_would_have_worked: Contain the endpoint, pull the loader chain, and hunt for Phemedrone/DarkGate/Mispadu stealer artifacts.
response.adequacy: Info-stealer payloads exfiltrate credentials quickly, so rapid credential rotation is required.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection is precisely the control being bypassed — the flaw suppresses SmartScreen/Defender prompts, so relying on the built-in reputation check is exactly what fails; SI-3 needs augmenting with app-control and content-execution restrictions.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Patching closes the .url bypass, but the flaw was already weaponized by multiple malware families before broad deployment; SI-2 cadence does not stop the delivery technique in the interim.

NIS2-Art21-incident-handling.gap: The control mandates handling incidents but does not require blocking outbound WebDAV/SMB or enforcing Mark-of-the-Web integrity, which is what actually stops this defense-evasion vector.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.7.gap: Protection-against-malware guidance assumes the reputation/warning layer works; this CVE defeats that layer, so the control is insufficient without hardened attachment/shortcut handling and egress control.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application hardening (block WebDAV, restrict script hosts, disable Internet-Shortcut auto-launch) would blunt this, but the Essential Eight control is not tied to the specific SmartScreen-bypass behavior and is easily left at defaults.

new_control_requirements.name: DOWNLOAD-PROVENANCE-ENFORCEMENT
new_control_requirements.description: Enforce Mark-of-the-Web / content-provenance on all internet-sourced files and block execution of provenance-stripped shortcuts, installers, and script hosts regardless of a reputation service verdict, which attackers bypass by design.

CVE-2023-36036.name: Windows Cloud Files Mini Filter Driver Elevation of Privilege

attack_vector.description: From a low-privilege foothold, an attacker triggers a heap-based buffer overflow in the Cloud Files Mini Filter Driver via crafted placeholder/reparse-point operations, escalating to SYSTEM.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local, authenticated low-privilege user)
attack_vector.complexity: low — demonstrated reliable in-the-wild exploitation
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported after in-the-wild detection by security researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install the Nov 2023 cumulative update; reduce foothold opportunities so the LPE has nothing to chain from.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is necessary but cannot cover the zero-day window; kernel-aware detection is needed alongside.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR rules for cldflt.sys IOCTL abuse and for integrity-level jumps following cloud-file operations.
detection.adequacy: Default logging rarely records driver-level abuse; kernel telemetry is required.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, assume SYSTEM compromise, and hunt for follow-on persistence.
response.adequacy: Once SYSTEM is obtained the host is fully compromised.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Monthly patch remediation is the only fix, but this was a zero-day used before the patch existed; SI-2 cannot cover the pre-disclosure window for a kernel-driver LPE.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least privilege confines an initial foothold, yet a driver LPE to SYSTEM is designed to break that boundary; AC-6 alone does not prevent kernel-mode token theft.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: System monitoring is the realistic control for a post-foothold LPE, but default telemetry rarely captures cldflt.sys IOCTL abuse or the integrity-level jump without kernel-aware EDR.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management on patch cadence cannot address an actively-exploited kernel zero-day; it needs behavioral escalation detection as a compensating layer.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: The 48-hour patch-exploited-flaw target still leaves the zero-day window open, and the control does not mandate the kernel/EDR telemetry that would surface driver-level escalation.

CVE-2023-47246.name: SysAid Server Path Traversal to Code Execution

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker uses a path-traversal request to write a file (a WAR-packaged web shell) into the SysAid Tomcat webroot, then executes it to run a PowerShell loader for GraceWire and Cl0p ransomware tooling.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, preauth path traversal)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted request writes the web shell; exploited at scale by a ransomware affiliate
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by SysAid during incident response; attributed to Lace Tempest. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to 23.3.36 immediately, remove SysAid from direct internet exposure, and enforce webroot write restrictions.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the traversal, but active ransomware exploitation means hosts must be assumed compromised and hunted for web shells even after patching.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on new files in the Tomcat webroot and on the SysAid java process spawning PowerShell / writing user.exe.
detection.adequacy: Detection is straightforward if webroot integrity and process-lineage monitoring exist, which many deployments lack.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the server, remove the web shell, rotate secrets, and hunt for Cl0p pre-ransomware staging and lateral movement.
response.adequacy: Given ransomware-actor involvement, response must assume data theft and imminent encryption.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Emergency patching to 23.3.36 is required, but the flaw was already exploited as a zero-day by a ransomware affiliate; SI-2 cadence does not cover the active-exploitation window before the fix landed.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection that exposes the SysAid web interface to the internet is the precondition for the preauth file write; patching does not remove the exposed attack surface or the webroot write primitive.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: Detection is the practical backstop, but default configs do not alarm on new files appearing in the Tomcat webroot or on java spawning PowerShell — the exact web-shell-deploy behavior seen here.

NIS2-Art21-incident-handling.gap: The 24-hour NIS2 notification clock starts once ransomware pre-staging is detected, but the article does not require the webroot-integrity monitoring or egress control that would catch the web shell before ransomware deployment.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management treats this as a scheduled patch, missing that it is a preauth, ransomware-actor-exploited RCE demanding out-of-band emergency remediation and web-shell hunting.

CVE-2023-36844.name: Juniper Junos OS EX Series J-Web PHP External Variable Modification

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends crafted J-Web requests that modify PHP environment variables, causing partial integrity loss that is chained with other J-Web flaws toward pre-auth remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to J-Web)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public watchTowr PoC / EDB-51776 automates the chain
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by Juniper SIRT and weaponized by watchTowr Labs. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Junos fixed releases and disable or restrict J-Web to trusted management networks.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the individual flaw, but because it is a chain link, disabling/segmenting J-Web is the more robust mitigation.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unauthenticated J-Web PHP requests and on the request patterns of the public chain.
detection.adequacy: Appliance logging of J-Web request internals is often limited, reducing detection fidelity.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the device, verify firmware integrity, and check for uploaded files and config tampering.
response.adequacy: A compromised management plane can imply full device control; treat as such.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The medium base score invites deprioritized patching, but this flaw is a preauth link in a public RCE chain against internet-facing appliances — SI-2 SLAs keyed to CVSS-alone underrate it.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection that exposes J-Web to untrusted networks is the exploitation precondition; the durable mitigation is disabling/restricting J-Web, which patch-only remediation ignores.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least functionality would disable the J-Web management interface where feasible, blunting the whole chain, but CM-7 is rarely applied to appliance management planes left internet-reachable.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security obligations do not specifically require isolating device management planes; leaving J-Web exposed keeps this chain viable despite general compliance.

AU-ISM-1546.gap: Patching within the ISM window helps, but the control does not mandate restricting the J-Web management interface that is the actual attack surface.

CVE-2023-36845.name: Juniper Junos OS EX/SRX J-Web PHPRC Modification RCE

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sets the PHPRC environment variable via a crafted J-Web request to load a planted PHP config (auto_prepend_file) that executes a previously uploaded payload, achieving remote code execution on SRX/EX devices.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to J-Web)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public watchTowr PoC / EDB-51776 and a later single-request variant automate it
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by Juniper SIRT and weaponized by watchTowr Labs / VulnCheck. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Junos fixed releases and disable or restrict J-Web to trusted management networks.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is essential, but removing J-Web from untrusted exposure is the more durable defense given rapid weaponization.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unauthenticated J-Web requests setting PHPRC and on the PHP process loading non-default .ini files.
detection.adequacy: Appliance visibility into PHP CGI internals is limited, weakening detection.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the device, verify firmware/config integrity, and hunt for uploaded payloads and persistence.
response.adequacy: Post-RCE the management plane is fully controlled; treat the device as compromised.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Emergency patching is required, but exploitation began within a week of disclosure while many appliances lagged; SI-2 SLAs are slower than the observed weaponization timeline for this preauth RCE.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection that leaves J-Web internet-reachable is the exploitation precondition; the durable fix is removing J-Web from untrusted exposure, which patching alone does not do.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least functionality would disable J-Web / the PHP CGI surface where unused, eliminating the PHPRC vector, but CM-7 is seldom enforced on appliance management planes.

DORA-Art-9.gap: DORA's ICT protection requirements for financial entities do not specifically mandate isolating network-device management planes, leaving this preauth RCE exploitable despite general compliance.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security obligations do not force management-plane segmentation of firewalls/switches, so an exposed J-Web keeps this critical chain viable.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: CAF B4 system-security expectations do not force removing J-Web from untrusted exposure, so the PHP external-variable vector stays reachable on internet-facing appliances.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application-hardening guidance does not extend to disabling the J-Web / PHP-CGI management surface on network appliances, leaving the PHPRC vector present.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical vulnerability management patches the CVE but does not mandate removing the exposed management plane the exploit chain depends on.

CVE-2023-36846.name: Juniper Junos OS SRX J-Web Missing Authentication File Upload

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a request to user.php that lacks an authentication check, uploading arbitrary files into a restricted J-Web directory; combined with the PHPRC flaw this yields pre-auth RCE.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; user.php requires no auth)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public watchTowr PoC / EDB-51776 automates the upload and chain
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by Juniper SIRT and weaponized by watchTowr Labs. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Junos fixed releases and disable or restrict J-Web to trusted management networks.
prevention.adequacy: Patch restores the missing auth check, but disabling/segmenting J-Web is the more robust mitigation for a chain link.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unauthenticated user.php uploads and on new PHP/.ini files in J-Web directories.
detection.adequacy: Appliance upload logging is often limited, reducing detection fidelity.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the device, remove uploaded payloads, and verify firmware/config integrity.
response.adequacy: Uploaded web shells imply management-plane compromise; treat as such.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The medium base score understates a preauth file-upload primitive that is a documented link in a public RCE chain; CVSS-keyed patch SLAs deprioritize it wrongly.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement is exactly what is missing — user.php requires no authentication for a critical upload function; the fix restores the check, but until patched no access-control policy compensates.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection that exposes J-Web is the precondition; segmenting/disabling the management interface is the durable mitigation patch-only remediation ignores.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security duties do not mandate management-plane isolation, so an exposed J-Web keeps the unauthenticated upload reachable.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Hardening/removing unused management functionality would drop the user.php surface, but the Essential Eight control is not tied to this appliance interface and is easily left enabled.

CVE-2023-36847.name: Juniper Junos OS EX J-Web Missing Authentication File Upload

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a request to installAppPackage.php that lacks an authentication check, uploading arbitrary files into a restricted J-Web directory; combined with the PHPRC flaw this yields pre-auth RCE.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; installAppPackage.php requires no auth)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public watchTowr PoC / EDB-51776 automates the upload and chain
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by Juniper SIRT and weaponized by watchTowr Labs. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Junos fixed releases and disable or restrict J-Web to trusted management networks.
prevention.adequacy: Patch restores the missing auth check, but disabling/segmenting J-Web is the more robust mitigation for a chain link.
