detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unauthenticated installAppPackage.php uploads and on new PHP/.ini files in J-Web directories.
detection.adequacy: Appliance upload logging is often limited, reducing detection fidelity.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the device, remove uploaded payloads, and verify firmware/config integrity.
response.adequacy: Uploaded web shells imply management-plane compromise; treat as such.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The medium base score understates a preauth file-upload primitive that is a documented link in a public RCE chain; CVSS-keyed patch SLAs deprioritize it wrongly.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement is exactly what is missing — installAppPackage.php requires no authentication for a critical upload function; patching restores the check, and until then no access policy compensates.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection that exposes J-Web is the precondition; segmenting/disabling the management interface is the durable mitigation patch-only remediation ignores.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security duties do not mandate management-plane isolation, so an exposed J-Web keeps the unauthenticated upload reachable.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Hardening/removing unused management functionality would drop the installAppPackage.php surface, but the Essential Eight control is not tied to this appliance interface and is easily left enabled.

CVE-2023-36851.name: Juniper Junos OS SRX J-Web Missing Authentication File Upload/Download

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a request to webauth_operation.php that lacks an authentication check, uploading and downloading arbitrary files via J-Web; the write primitive chains with the PHPRC flaw for pre-auth RCE while the read primitive exposes sensitive files.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated; webauth_operation.php requires no auth)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public watchTowr PoC / EDB-51776 automates the chain
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by Juniper SIRT and weaponized by watchTowr Labs. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Junos fixed releases and disable or restrict J-Web to trusted management networks.
prevention.adequacy: Patch restores the missing auth check, but disabling/segmenting J-Web is the more robust mitigation for a chain link with a file-read primitive.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unauthenticated webauth_operation.php upload/download requests and on new PHP/.ini files or sensitive-file reads via J-Web.
detection.adequacy: Appliance logging of J-Web file operations is often limited, reducing detection fidelity.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the device, remove uploaded payloads, rotate any secrets exposed via arbitrary read, and verify firmware/config integrity.
response.adequacy: Arbitrary file read plus write implies both credential exposure and management-plane compromise.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement is exactly what is missing — webauth_operation.php requires no authentication for both upload and download of files; patching restores the check, and until then no access policy compensates.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The medium base score understates a preauth upload/download primitive documented as part of a public RCE chain; CVSS-keyed patch SLAs deprioritize it wrongly.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection that exposes J-Web is the precondition; segmenting/disabling the management interface is the durable mitigation patch-only remediation ignores.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security duties do not mandate management-plane isolation, so an exposed J-Web keeps the unauthenticated upload/download reachable.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security outcomes assume management interfaces enforce authentication; an unauthenticated file upload/download endpoint defeats that assumption without dedicated management-plane isolation.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker registers arbitrary services with an exposed SLP directory agent, then sends small spoofed-source SrvReq packets so the oversized responses are reflected at a victim, producing a very high amplification factor.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, UDP/427 reachable)
attack_vector.complexity: low — no auth, single-packet registration, well-documented amplification technique
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by BitSight/Curesec researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Disable SLP where unused; block/deny UDP 427 at the network boundary; restrict SLP to trusted management segments
prevention.adequacy: Vendor patches alone do not help if the service stays enabled and reachable — the compensating control (disable/firewall 427) is the load-bearing fix.

detection.what_would_have_worked: NetFlow monitoring for UDP/427 amplification egress; alerting on SrvReg from external sources
detection.adequacy: Detection catches abuse-in-progress but does not remove the reflector; must be paired with the boundary deny.

response.what_would_have_worked: Immediately firewall UDP/427 inbound, disable SLP, and notify upstream provider if the host was used as a reflector
response.adequacy: Reactive only; the amplification harms third parties, so proactive exposure removal is the real control.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection is treated as perimeter firewalling, but SLP/UDP-427 is routinely left exposed on management and appliance networks; without an explicit egress/ingress deny on 427 the host remains a usable amplifier even when 'patched'.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security measures rarely enumerate legacy discovery protocols (SLP) as attack surface, so the amplification vector persists on essential-service infrastructure that is otherwise considered hardened.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security guidance focuses on the defended asset's own availability, not on the asset being weaponized as a reflector against third parties, leaving this abuse pattern unmanaged.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application/service hardening baselines seldom mandate disabling SLP where unused, so the default-enabled service keeps the amplification primitive live.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.9.gap: Configuration management does not flag internet-reachable SLP as a non-compliant baseline, so exposed directory agents pass audit while remaining exploitable.

CVE-2023-22518.name: Atlassian Confluence Data Center and Server Improper Authorization Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker reaches the setup-restore endpoints, which fail to enforce authorization, resetting the Confluence instance and letting the attacker create an instance-administrator account and take full control.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network access to the web app)
attack_vector.complexity: low — single unauthenticated HTTP request path, publicly documented
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Atlassian PSIRT. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the fixed release immediately; block external access to /json/setup-restore* and management endpoints; place Confluence behind a VPN/allowlist
prevention.adequacy: Patch is decisive, but exposed instances were compromised before patching — reachability restriction is the necessary interim control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on hits to setup-restore endpoints and on new admin-account creation
detection.adequacy: Detects takeover but often fires after the admin account already exists; must pair with response.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate instance, audit for rogue admins/web shells, restore from known-good backup, rotate secrets
response.adequacy: Full-loss-of-integrity flaw means assume-compromise on any exposed unpatched instance.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement is assumed at the app layer, but the flaw lets an unauthenticated request reset authorization state and mint an admin — the control cannot mediate a request that bypasses the authorization check itself.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Standard 30-day flaw-remediation SLAs are far slower than the sub-week mass-exploitation window observed here; the patch cadence assumed by the control is inadequate for an internet-facing critical.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-handling.gap: Vulnerability-handling processes that wait for a maintenance window leave a preauth admin-takeover exposed during the exact interval attackers weaponized it.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential-8 'patch applications within 48 hours for internet-facing critical' is the right target but is commonly under-implemented for on-prem Confluence, leaving the window open.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability-management evidence (a CVE ticket) satisfies the audit without proving the reachable setup-restore endpoints were actually blocked or the instance patched.

CVE-2023-46604.name: Apache ActiveMQ Deserialization of Untrusted Data Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A remote attacker sends a crafted OpenWire packet manipulating serialized class types so the broker instantiates an arbitrary class (typically a Spring ClassPathXmlApplicationContext pointing at a remote XML), which loads and executes attacker-defined commands.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network access to the broker)
attack_vector.complexity: low — single crafted packet, public Metasploit module
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — disclosed by the Apache ActiveMQ project. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to 5.15.16/5.16.7/5.17.6/5.18.3; restrict OpenWire 61616 to trusted networks; disable unused broker transports
prevention.adequacy: Patch removes the class-instantiation primitive; network restriction is the necessary interim guard for exposed brokers.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on broker processes spawning shells and on outbound fetches of remote Spring XML
detection.adequacy: Strong post-exploit signal but fires after code execution; pair with prevention.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate broker, hunt for ransomware/miner payloads and persistence, rebuild if compromised
response.adequacy: Given ransomware use, assume-compromise for exposed unpatched brokers.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw-remediation timelines assume scheduled patching, but exposed brokers were exploited within days of PoC release — the SLA is slower than the observed weaponization window.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection frequently overlooks message-broker ports; leaving OpenWire 61616 internet-reachable turns a preauth deserialization bug into RCE regardless of app-layer controls.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management obligations rarely track middleware/message-broker inventory, so ActiveMQ instances slip out of the essential-service patch scope that would have forced timely upgrade.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security expectations don't specifically require restricting broker protocols to trusted segments, so the exposed OpenWire endpoint stays reachable.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability-management inventories often miss embedded/bundled ActiveMQ, so the affected component is never assessed against this CVE.

CVE-2023-46748.name: F5 BIG-IP Configuration Utility SQL Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker with access to the BIG-IP Configuration utility injects SQL that the utility passes to its backing database in a way that results in arbitrary system-command execution; in practice paired with CVE-2023-46747 to gain the needed unauthenticated reach.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (authenticated to the Configuration utility) — or none when chained with CVE-2023-46747
attack_vector.complexity: low — documented request; trivial once management-plane reach is obtained
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Praetorian researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the fixed BIG-IP release; block Configuration-utility access from untrusted networks; restrict the management port and self-IPs
prevention.adequacy: Patch fixes the injection, but management-plane exposure is what enabled real-world chained exploitation — restrict it regardless.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor TMUI/mgmt logs for SQL syntax in parameters and for tmsh execution from the web context
detection.adequacy: Useful but appliance logging is often not centralized; pair with exposure reduction.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the appliance, audit for rogue admin users and config changes, rotate credentials/keys, rebuild if command execution confirmed
response.adequacy: System-command execution on a BIG-IP warrants assume-compromise handling.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: The load-bearing mitigation is restricting the management port / self-IP to trusted admin networks; where boundary protection permits broad reach to TMUI, an authenticated SQLi becomes command execution on a critical appliance.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege on the Configuration utility is undermined because the SQLi escalates any low-privileged authenticated context to system-command execution, breaking the privilege boundary the control assumes.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management SLAs for network appliances typically lag the F5 hotfix cadence, and this flaw is most dangerous chained with a preauth bypass — a slow patch leaves both halves exploitable.

AU-ISM-1546.gap: ISM management-interface segregation guidance is frequently unmet for BIG-IP, leaving the Configuration utility reachable where the SQLi can be exercised.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability-management closes on the CVE ticket without verifying the management-plane exposure that makes exploitation trivial was actually removed.

CVE-2023-46747.name: F5 BIG-IP Configuration Utility Authentication Bypass Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker smuggles a request to the internal AJP connector, bypassing Configuration-utility authentication and reaching endpoints that execute arbitrary system commands — typically chained with CVE-2023-46748 for the SQLi command primitive.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network access to the management port/self-IP)
attack_vector.complexity: low — publicly detailed request-smuggling technique with Metasploit coverage
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Praetorian researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the fixed BIG-IP release; restrict the management port and self-IPs to a trusted admin network; never expose TMUI to the internet
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the bypass; management-plane isolation would have prevented exploitation even before patching.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on internal-only mgmt endpoints reached from external requests and on command execution from the web context
detection.adequacy: Effective if appliance logs are centralized; many deployments lack this.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate, hunt for rogue admins/web shells/ransomware staging, rotate all secrets, rebuild on confirmed RCE
response.adequacy: Preauth RCE with ransomware use warrants assume-compromise for exposed unpatched devices.

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: Identification-and-authentication controls are defeated at the transport layer — the request smuggling reaches an internal channel that never enforces auth — so account-level IA cannot stop the bypass.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection is the decisive control: leaving the management port / self-IP reachable is what converts this bypass into preauth RCE, yet appliances are frequently deployed with the management plane exposed.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Appliance patch SLAs lag the F5 hotfix, and mass exploitation began within days of PoC — the assumed remediation window is too slow for an internet-facing preauth RCE.

UK-CAF-B2.gap: Identity-and-access-control expectations don't account for an alternate-channel bypass of the auth layer, so compliant IAM still leaves the appliance exploitable.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential-8 rapid-patch targets for internet-facing services are the right control but are commonly unmet for BIG-IP, leaving the preauth path open.

CVE-2023-5631.name: Roundcube Webmail Persistent Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker sends an HTML email whose crafted SVG document bypasses Roundcube's HTML sanitizer; when the victim views the message, attacker-controlled JavaScript executes in the webmail session and can read/exfiltrate mail.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (attacker only needs to email the victim; victim views the message)
attack_vector.complexity: low — single crafted email, no attachment interaction required
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — in-the-wild use reported by ESET (Winter Vivern). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to 1.6.4/1.5.5/1.4.15; strip/rewrite SVG in inbound HTML mail; enforce a strict Content-Security-Policy on the webmail app
prevention.adequacy: Patch fixes the sanitizer; CSP is a valuable defense-in-depth that would have blunted script execution.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for outbound requests from the webmail origin to unknown domains and for SVG-bearing HTML mail
detection.adequacy: Feasible but requires webmail-aware monitoring most orgs lack.

response.what_would_have_worked: Notify affected users, force credential/session reset, review mailbox rules and exfiltration logs
response.adequacy: Exfiltration is silent, so proactive session invalidation matters after any confirmed exposure.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection (mail AV/anti-spam) does not inspect for sanitizer-bypassing SVG/HTML that yields client-side script execution, so the weaponized email passes content filtering.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw-remediation timelines for webmail are often relaxed given the medium CVSS, yet a targeted APT exploited it promptly — the severity-driven patch prioritization under-weights active exploitation.

NIS2-Art21-incident-handling.gap: Incident-handling processes centered on server compromise miss session-level mail exfiltration triggered purely by viewing an email, so the breach is detected late or not at all.

UK-CAF-C1.gap: Security-monitoring expectations focus on host/network telemetry, not on anomalous webmail DOM/script behavior, leaving this XSS-driven exfiltration unobserved.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability-management deprioritizes a 'medium' XSS, so the KEV-listed, actively-exploited flaw lingers unpatched on internet-facing webmail.

CVE-2023-20273.name: Cisco IOS XE Web UI Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Using the local account created via CVE-2023-20198, the attacker sends crafted input to the IOS XE web UI, which insufficiently validates it, injecting OS commands that run as root and are used to write a persistent implant.
attack_vector.privileges_required: high (requires an authenticated account) — obtained for free by chaining CVE-2023-20198
attack_vector.complexity: low — publicly documented chained exploit
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified by Cisco during active-exploitation investigation. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Disable the HTTP/HTTPS Server (no ip http server / no ip http secure-server) or restrict it to trusted mgmt networks; apply fixed IOS XE releases
prevention.adequacy: Disabling the web UI removes the surface for the whole chain; patch is required but the exposure removal is the stronger control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Run the implant check (logon_hash POST), monitor syslog for webui-process config changes and unexpected users
detection.adequacy: Detects post-exploit implant; must be combined with disabling the interface.

response.what_would_have_worked: Remove the implant, delete rogue accounts, reload, upgrade, and rotate credentials
response.adequacy: Mass campaign means assume-compromise for any exposed device that had the web UI on.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality would have disabled the HTTP/HTTPS Server (web UI) that both this injection and CVE-2023-20198 abuse; leaving the management web UI enabled by default keeps the command-injection surface live.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection should keep the IOS XE management web UI off untrusted networks; internet-exposed web UIs are exactly what enabled the mass campaign.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security obligations for essential-service routers rarely mandate disabling/segmenting the device management plane, so the web UI remains reachable and injectable.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application-hardening baselines for network devices seldom require turning off the web UI, leaving the root-command-injection path exploitable.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.9.gap: Configuration-management baselines don't flag an internet-facing IOS XE web UI as non-compliant, so the exposed injectable interface passes audit.

CVE-2023-4966.name: Citrix NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway Buffer Overflow Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends an oversized request to a Gateway/AAA endpoint, triggering an out-of-bounds read that returns leaked memory containing valid session tokens, which are then replayed to hijack authenticated sessions and bypass MFA.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network access to the gateway)
attack_vector.complexity: low — single crafted request, public PoC ('Citrix Bleed')
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — root cause published by Assetnote; disclosed by Citrix. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to fixed builds AND terminate all active/persistent sessions (kill icaconnection/pcoipconnection/aaa/rdp sessions) per Citrix guidance
prevention.adequacy: Patch is necessary but insufficient — without invalidating existing sessions the leaked tokens stay usable, which is how post-patch intrusions occurred.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on session-token reuse from new IPs and on oversized OpenID-config requests
detection.adequacy: Effective but requires correlating session source IPs, which many deployments don't.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force full session termination, hunt for lateral movement from hijacked sessions, reset credentials, review AD activity
response.adequacy: Given ransomware use and token persistence, assume-compromise for any exposed unpatched gateway.

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: MFA at the gateway is bypassed because the attacker replays a leaked, already-authenticated session token — the control validates the initial login, not the ongoing session material the flaw exposes.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Patching alone is insufficient: Citrix explicitly required terminating all active sessions after upgrade, because leaked tokens remain valid; a patch-only remediation leaves hijackable sessions live.

NIS2-Art21-incident-handling.gap: Incident handling that assumes a patched appliance is remediated misses that stolen session tokens survive the patch, so intrusions continued post-update.

UK-CAF-B2.gap: Identity-and-access-control assumes session tokens are confidential; a memory-disclosure that leaks them defeats the model without any credential theft.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability-management that closes on 'patched' fails to include the mandatory session-invalidation step, so the residual token-replay risk persists.

CVE-2023-20198.name: Cisco IOS XE Web UI Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker abuses the IOS XE web UI to issue a privilege-15 command that creates a local user/password, gaining normal-user access that is then escalated (via CVE-2023-20273) to root to install an implant.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network access to the web UI)
attack_vector.complexity: low — CVSS 10.0, public exploit, single request to an exposed web UI
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — identified by Cisco during active-exploitation investigation. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Disable the HTTP/HTTPS Server (no ip http server / no ip http secure-server) or restrict it to trusted mgmt networks; apply fixed IOS XE releases
prevention.adequacy: Disabling the web UI removes the entire attack surface; patching is required but the exposure removal is the decisive control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Run the implant check, audit for unexpected privilege-15 users, monitor syslog for programmatic config by the webui process
detection.adequacy: Detects the created account/implant post-fact; must be paired with disabling the interface.

response.what_would_have_worked: Remove rogue accounts and implant, reload, upgrade, rotate all device credentials
response.adequacy: Tens of thousands of devices were implanted — assume-compromise for any exposed device that had the web UI enabled.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least-functionality would have disabled the HTTP/HTTPS Server web UI that is the entire attack surface; it ships enabled and reachable, so the preauth account-creation path is exposed by default.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection should keep the device management web UI off the internet; the mass compromise was driven precisely by internet-exposed web UIs.

NIST-800-53-AC-2.gap: Account-management controls assume accounts are provisioned through governed processes, but the flaw lets an unauthenticated attacker mint a privilege-15 account outside any IAM workflow.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Essential-service network-security obligations rarely require disabling/segmenting router management planes, leaving the web UI reachable for exploitation.

AU-ISM-1546.gap: ISM management-traffic-segregation guidance is widely unmet for edge routers, so the web UI remains internet-facing and abusable.

CVE-2023-21608.name: Adobe Acrobat and Reader Use-After-Free Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A victim opens a malicious PDF that triggers a use-after-free in Acrobat/Reader, corrupting memory to achieve arbitrary code execution in the current user's context; typically delivered via phishing.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none on the app, but requires user interaction (victim opens the file)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoC; reliable once the victim opens the document
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Adobe by credited external researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Adobe security update; enable Protected View / Enhanced Security (sandbox); block/limit untrusted PDFs at the mail gateway
prevention.adequacy: Patch removes the UAF; sandboxing/Protected View is valuable defense-in-depth against the next unknown client bug.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on Acrobat/Reader spawning shells or crashing on document render
detection.adequacy: Strong signal but reactive; pair with patching and sandboxing.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the endpoint, hunt for dropped payloads/persistence, reimage on confirmed execution, review phishing delivery
response.adequacy: Client RCE in user context warrants endpoint containment and credential review.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Endpoint-software patch cadence for client apps like Acrobat is often slow relative to how quickly a KEV-listed, PoC-available UAF is weaponized in phishing, leaving the client exposed.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection frequently fails to detect a novel crafted-PDF UAF exploit, so signature-based mail/endpoint AV lets the weaponized document through.

NIS2-Art21-incident-handling.gap: Incident processes oriented to server compromise under-cover client-side code execution from opening a document, delaying detection of endpoint intrusion.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security expectations don't reliably enforce Protected View / sandboxing and prompt client-app patching, so the exploit runs in the user's context.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Essential-8 patch-applications targets cover this class, but Acrobat/Reader is commonly under-patched on endpoints, leaving the UAF exploitable.

CVE-2023-20109.name: Cisco IOS and IOS XE Group Encrypted Transport VPN Out-of-Bounds Write Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker who compromises a key server, or reconfigures a group member to register against an attacker-controlled key server, sends malformed GDOI/G-IKEv2 group-policy attributes that trigger an out-of-bounds write, yielding code execution or a device reload (DoS).
attack_vector.privileges_required: high (administrative control of a group member or key server required)
attack_vector.complexity: high — requires prior administrative foothold on a GET VPN participant and crafted protocol attributes
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by Cisco PSIRT during code review after observing exploitation attempts. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to fixed IOS/IOS XE releases and restrict which key servers group members will register with; segment control-plane management access.
prevention.adequacy: Patching closes the OOB write, but because the trust model assumes authorized KS/GM peers, hardening peer-authentication and management-plane access is needed alongside the patch.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on unexpected device reloads, GDOI registration to unknown key servers, and configuration changes repointing group members.
detection.adequacy: Crash-only telemetry catches the DoS path but not silent code execution.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate GET VPN group keys, re-validate KS/GM configuration integrity, and audit admin credentials on affected routers.
response.adequacy: Effective only if the initial administrative compromise is also remediated.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw-remediation SLAs for embedded network-OS images lag: GET VPN routers are change-controlled infrastructure that operators reboot infrequently, so the patch window far exceeds the observed exploitation window.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege on the control plane does not help — the attack presupposes an attacker who already holds legitimate administrative control of a key server or group member, so a compromised-but-authorized KS/GM still weaponizes the OOB write.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security baseline controls treat the GET VPN trust domain as internal; they do not validate the integrity of GDOI/G-IKEv2 group-policy attributes exchanged between KS and GM, which is exactly the trust this flaw abuses.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management that scans hosts and web apps rarely enumerates IOS/IOS XE feature-level CVEs on GET VPN routers, leaving this class of appliance-firmware flaw untracked.

AU-ISM-1546.gap: Patch-application timeframes for network appliances are routinely exceeded because of change-freeze and maintenance-window constraints on core routing gear.

CVE-2023-41763.name: Microsoft Skype for Business Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted request to an internet-facing Skype for Business Server that forces the server to make an outbound HTTP request, disclosing internal IP/port information and enabling internal-network reconnaissance from the perimeter.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network-reachable)
attack_vector.complexity: low — publicly documented request; automated by Nuclei template
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — independent/vendor security research. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the October 2023 Microsoft update and restrict the Skype server's outbound network egress to required destinations only.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the coercible request path; egress filtering limits the blast radius of any residual SSRF.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for server-originated HTTP connections to unexpected internal/external hosts and for unauthenticated hits on autodiscover endpoints.
detection.adequacy: Egress anomaly detection is the strongest signal for SSRF abuse.

response.what_would_have_worked: Review what internal hosts the server was coerced to reach and treat any disclosed internal topology as known to the attacker.
response.adequacy: Information disclosure cannot be undone; assume internal recon succeeded.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection assumes the Skype for Business front-end is a trusted egress point; the SSRF turns the server itself into an internal-network proxy, bypassing perimeter segmentation that treats server-originated traffic as benign.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The patch shipped in a Microsoft security update whose deployment on on-prem Skype servers is frequently deferred, leaving an exploitation window that the high EPSS shows is actively scanned.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Vulnerability-management programs prioritize by CVSS; at base 5.3 this SSRF is under-triaged despite being KEV-listed and heavily exploited, so it slips SLA prioritization.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management that focuses on high/critical CVSS misses this medium-scored but actively-exploited internal-reconnaissance primitive.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System-security controls do not constrain outbound requests the collaboration server is allowed to make, so the SSRF-driven internal scanning is not prevented by the existing boundary posture.

attack_vector.description: A victim opens an attacker-crafted document in WordPad; the file coerces the client into an outbound authentication attempt that leaks NTLM hash material to an attacker-controlled host, which can then be relayed or cracked.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (attacker unauthenticated; requires victim to open a file — UI:R)
attack_vector.complexity: low — social-engineering delivery of a lure document; NTLM-leak primitive is well understood
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — coordinated vendor disclosure. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the October 2023 Windows update, block outbound SMB (TCP 445) at the perimeter, and remove/disable WordPad.
prevention.adequacy: Patching plus egress SMB blocking neutralizes the NTLM-leak path even before every endpoint is updated.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on wordpad.exe making outbound SMB/WebDAV connections and on NTLM authentication to external hosts.
detection.adequacy: Process-to-network correlation is the clearest signal of exploitation.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force password/credential resets for users whose NTLM material may have leaked and hunt for relay activity.
response.adequacy: Leaked hashes must be treated as compromised credentials.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The fix ships in a Windows monthly update; endpoints that defer OS patching remain exposed, and WordPad is a default-installed component that operators rarely track as attack surface.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access-enforcement controls do not stop credential leakage — the disclosed NTLM material lets an attacker relay or crack the hash to gain access that access-control lists assume is protected.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management timelines for endpoint OS components exceed the window in which a phishing lure can harvest NTLM hashes at scale.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management under-prioritizes a medium-CVSS information-disclosure flaw even though it feeds directly into NTLM relay / lateral movement.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application-hardening guidance rarely disables or removes WordPad, leaving a legacy document handler that can be coerced into outbound authentication.

CVE-2023-22515.name: Atlassian Confluence Data Center and Server Broken Access Control Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated, internet-based attacker reaches Confluence setup/onboarding endpoints to move the instance into a configurable state and create a new Confluence administrator account, then logs in with full administrative control.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network-reachable)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a short sequence of HTTP requests; publicly weaponized within days
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — surfaced via customer reports of active exploitation and Atlassian investigation. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to a fixed Confluence release; before patching, block external access to /setup/* endpoints or take the instance off the public internet.
prevention.adequacy: Because it was a zero-day, endpoint restriction was the only prevention during the exposure window; patching is definitive afterward.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on requests to /setup/setupadministrator.action and on creation of new administrator accounts.
detection.adequacy: Admin-account-creation monitoring is the highest-fidelity detection.

response.what_would_have_worked: Enumerate and remove attacker-created administrator accounts, rotate secrets, and assume data access; follow Atlassian's remediation guidance.
response.adequacy: A created admin persists across a naive patch, so account hunting is mandatory.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: This was a zero-day exploited before a patch existed; flaw-remediation controls cannot close a gap for which no fix is yet published, so compensating perimeter controls were the only defense during the exposure window.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection often left Confluence Data Center/Server directly internet-facing; without restricting access to the setup/onboarding endpoints, the perimeter did not contain the pre-auth admin-creation path.

NIS2-Art21-incident-handling.gap: Incident-handling obligations assume detection precedes admin-account creation; the exploit mints a legitimate-looking admin account, so post-hoc handling must include hunting for attacker-created admins, which baseline procedures do not mandate.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management could not enumerate an unknown flaw; the control provides no coverage for a zero-day in a broken onboarding state machine.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch-application timelines are irrelevant to a zero-day; the control offers no protection until Atlassian's fix is released, underscoring the need for network restriction of admin/setup endpoints.

CVE-2023-40044.name: Progress WS_FTP Server Deserialization of Untrusted Data Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A pre-authenticated attacker sends a crafted serialized .NET object to the WS_FTP Server Ad Hoc Transfer module; unsafe deserialization executes an attacker gadget chain, running arbitrary OS commands as the service account.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (pre-authenticated against the Ad Hoc Transfer module)
attack_vector.complexity: low — publicly documented gadget chain, Metasploit module available
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Assetnote researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to WS_FTP Server 8.7.4 / 8.8.2+ and disable the Ad Hoc Transfer module if unused.
prevention.adequacy: Patching removes the sink; disabling the Ad Hoc Transfer module removes the attack surface entirely where the feature is not needed.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on child processes spawned by the WS_FTP service and on serialized payloads to Ad Hoc Transfer endpoints.
detection.adequacy: Service-process child-spawn is the strongest post-exploitation signal.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the server, hunt for web shells / persistence, rotate service and stored transfer credentials.
response.adequacy: Given ransomware association, assume follow-on lateral movement from a compromised MFT host.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Managed-file-transfer appliances are patched on change-controlled cycles; the observed exploitation window after disclosure was far shorter than typical MFT patch SLAs.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: WS_FTP Server is deliberately internet-facing to serve external transfers, so boundary protection cannot simply block it; the Ad Hoc Transfer module remained reachable pre-authentication.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management timelines for MFT software repeatedly lag the exploitation of pre-auth RCE, as the parallel MOVEit/GoAnywhere incidents demonstrated.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management must specifically track MFT/edge software as a high-value target class; generic host scanning under-weights this deserialization sink.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Even a 48-hour patch target for internet-facing services was outpaced by public PoC availability and ransomware tooling for this flaw.

CVE-2023-42824.name: Apple iOS and iPadOS Kernel Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local attacker (typically a sandboxed app or an earlier stage of an exploit chain) leverages a kernel flaw fixed by improved checks to elevate to kernel privileges on iOS/iPadOS before 16.6, enabling implant installation on targeted devices.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local code execution on the device required first)
attack_vector.complexity: high — kernel exploitation used in targeted attacks, chained with other primitives
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — patched by Apple as an actively-exploited zero-day. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install iOS/iPadOS 16.7.1 promptly and enforce minimum-OS compliance via MDM; enable Lockdown Mode for high-risk users.
prevention.adequacy: Patching is definitive; Lockdown Mode reduces exposure for targeted individuals during the window.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile threat-defense / MDM attestation and anomaly detection on managed devices.
detection.adequacy: Kernel zero-day exploitation is largely invisible to standard mobile telemetry.

response.what_would_have_worked: For suspected targeting, back up, wipe, and re-provision the device; rotate credentials accessed from it.
response.adequacy: Assume full device compromise for targeted victims.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Mobile-OS flaw remediation depends on user-driven or MDM-pushed updates; devices on iOS < 16.6 remain exploitable until the 16.7.1 update is installed, and fleet update compliance is typically slower than targeted-exploitation timelines.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege at the app layer is bypassed by a kernel privilege escalation; once kernel is reached the app-sandbox boundary the control relies on no longer holds.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: System-monitoring controls have little visibility into iOS internals; endpoint telemetry on managed mobile devices rarely detects kernel-level exploitation of a zero-day.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management cannot enumerate an unknown kernel zero-day; coverage begins only after Apple's disclosure.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch-application controls provide no protection during the zero-day window and depend on mobile-fleet update enforcement that is weaker than for managed workstations.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker abuses a REST-endpoint authentication bypass to mint an administrator access token, then uses privileged API access to alter build configuration and execute arbitrary code on the TeamCity server, pivoting into the software supply chain.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, network-reachable)
attack_vector.complexity: low — single-request token generation, widely weaponized
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Sonar research. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to TeamCity 2023.05.4 (or apply JetBrains' security patch plugin) and restrict the server to trusted networks / behind VPN.
prevention.adequacy: Patching closes the bypass; network restriction limits exposure of a high-value CI target.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on creation of admin access tokens via REST, new admin accounts, and build-config changes; monitor for TeamCity process child-spawns.
detection.adequacy: Token-creation and build-step tampering are the clearest exploitation signals.

response.what_would_have_worked: Revoke all access tokens, rotate CI secrets and signing keys, audit build artifacts for tampering, and rebuild from a known-good state.
response.adequacy: Because CI compromise threatens downstream artifacts, secret rotation and artifact review are mandatory.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The fix required upgrading TeamCity or applying the security patch plugin; CI servers are patched cautiously to avoid breaking pipelines, so remediation lagged the near-immediate nation-state and ransomware exploitation.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: TeamCity is frequently exposed to the internet for remote developer/agent access, so boundary protection did not contain the pre-auth token-generation endpoint.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-handling.gap: Vulnerability-handling processes did not treat CI/CD tooling as a supply-chain crown jewel, so this authentication-bypass was not prioritized commensurate with its blast radius into build artifacts.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management scanning of developer infrastructure is often lighter than for production, leaving CI servers under-monitored for KEV-listed pre-auth RCE.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Hardening guidance rarely covers CI/CD platforms; TeamCity admin APIs remained broadly reachable rather than restricted to trusted networks.

CVE-2023-28229.name: Microsoft Windows CNG Key Isolation Service Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A low-privileged local process makes racing concurrent RPC calls to the CNG Key Isolation service in lsass.exe; flawed critical-section handling of reference counters produces a use-after-free the attacker leverages to gain limited SYSTEM privileges.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (authenticated local user)
attack_vector.complexity: high — a timing-sensitive race condition (reflected in the AC:H vector), though a public PoC lowers the practical bar
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by @k0shl of Cyber Kunlun. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Windows security update fixing CVE-2023-28229 and minimize interactive access on sensitive hosts.
prevention.adequacy: Patching is definitive; without it, least-privilege alone cannot stop a SYSTEM escalation.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR monitoring for lsass.exe crashes/handle anomalies and unusual KeyIso RPC patterns.
detection.adequacy: Race-condition exploitation gives faint signals; lsass anomaly monitoring is the best option.

response.what_would_have_worked: Investigate the initial-access vector that provided local execution, and treat the host as fully compromised once SYSTEM is reachable.
response.adequacy: LPE is a chain step; the initial foothold must also be remediated.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The fix ships in a Windows monthly update; hosts that defer OS patching remain exploitable, and a local privilege-escalation is often deprioritized behind remote CVEs despite being a reliable post-initial-access step.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege / low-rights user accounts are the assumed starting point; this LPE defeats the boundary by escalating to SYSTEM, so restricting user privileges alone does not contain it.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: System-monitoring rarely flags anomalous RPC to the CNG Key Isolation interface, so exploitation inside lsass.exe proceeds without a clear detection signal.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management under-prioritizes local EoP flaws even when KEV-listed, delaying remediation of a proven privilege-escalation primitive.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch-application timeframes for endpoint OS updates commonly exceed the window in which a public PoC enables reliable local escalation.

CVE-2023-4211.name: Arm Mali GPU Kernel Driver Use-After-Free Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local, non-privileged app performs improper GPU memory-processing operations against the Mali kernel driver to access already-freed memory, achieving kernel privilege escalation as a stage in a targeted surveillance exploit chain.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local non-privileged app execution)
attack_vector.complexity: high — kernel use-after-free exploitation, observed in targeted attacks by a surveillance vendor
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Google TAG (Maddie Stone) and Project Zero (Jann Horn). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install OEM firmware/security updates carrying the fixed Mali driver; prefer devices with timely vendor patch delivery.
prevention.adequacy: Patching is definitive but bottlenecked on the OEM update chain, leaving a long real-world exposure tail.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile threat-defense / attestation on managed devices; app-vetting to reduce untrusted local code.
detection.adequacy: Kernel-driver exploitation is largely invisible to standard mobile telemetry.

response.what_would_have_worked: For suspected targeting, wipe and re-provision the device and rotate credentials it accessed.
response.adequacy: Assume full device compromise where surveillance implants are involved.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Mali GPU driver fixes must flow through SoC vendors and OEMs into device firmware/OTA updates; this multi-party patch chain leaves many Android devices exposed long after Arm's fix, far beyond the targeted-exploitation window.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least-privilege at the app layer is bypassed by a kernel use-after-free; a non-privileged app reaching kernel memory defeats the sandbox boundary the control assumes.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: Mobile-device monitoring has little visibility into GPU-driver-level kernel exploitation, so this LPE proceeds without a practical detection signal.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management struggles to track third-party SoC/GPU-driver CVEs across a heterogeneous device fleet, leaving firmware-level flaws under-managed.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch-application controls depend on OEM update delivery for mobile firmware, which is slower and less complete than for managed workstations.

CVE-2023-5217.name: Google Chromium libvpx Heap Buffer Overflow Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A remote attacker serves crafted media that triggers VP8 encoding in libvpx, overflowing a heap buffer to corrupt memory and potentially execute code in the browser/application process; used in the wild to deliver commercial spyware.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (remote; victim visits a crafted page — UI:R)
attack_vector.complexity: low from the victim's side (visit a page); high on the attacker's exploit-development side
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Google TAG's Clément Lecigne. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update Chrome to 117.0.5938.132+, update Firefox and every application bundling libpvx to a build with libvpx >= 1.13.1.
prevention.adequacy: Patching the browser is necessary but insufficient — every embedding application must also be updated, which requires SBOM-driven inventory.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for renderer-process crashes on media processing and post-exploitation implant behavior.
detection.adequacy: The exploit is stealthy; crash telemetry and implant hunting are the practical signals.

response.what_would_have_worked: For suspected targeting, treat the endpoint as compromised, wipe/reimage, and rotate credentials accessed from it.
response.adequacy: Spyware delivery implies deep compromise for targeted victims.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Because libvpx is a bundled library, the vulnerable code lives inside every application that ships it; flaw remediation must reach not just Chrome/Firefox but every embedding app, so SI-2 tracking of a single browser CVE under-scopes the true exposure.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection cannot reliably detect a memory-corruption exploit delivered through a legitimate media codec; signature/heuristic AV does not see the heap overflow in VP8 encoding.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Vulnerability-management inventories rarely enumerate transitive/bundled libraries like libvpx, so many affected applications are not flagged as needing the fix.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management focused on named products misses the software-composition dimension (SBOM) needed to find every libvpx-embedding artifact.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Browser/application hardening does not prevent exploitation of a zero-day codec bug; only patching the underlying libvpx closes it.

CVE-2018-14667.name: Red Hat JBoss RichFaces Framework Expression Language Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A remote, unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted UserResource$UriData request whose serialized-object chain is evaluated as an EL expression, executing arbitrary Java in the servlet container.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network attacker)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted HTTP request to a reachable resource endpoint triggers it.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported through Red Hat Product Security. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Red Hat errata / remove or upgrade off RichFaces 3.x; block external access to UserResource endpoints as a stopgap.
prevention.adequacy: Patching is insufficient where RichFaces 3.x is embedded transitively in end-of-life apps with no upstream fix; component inventory/replacement is required.

detection.what_would_have_worked: WAF/IDS signatures for serialized-Java magic bytes and UserResource$UriData paths; alert on JVM process spawns.
detection.adequacy: Detection catches known payload shapes but gadget chains can be re-encoded.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the app server, rotate secrets reachable from the JVM, and rebuild from a known-good image.
response.adequacy: Post-RCE the whole application context must be treated as compromised.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: RichFaces 3.x is end-of-life; flaw remediation assumes a vendor patch, but affected apps embed the vulnerable JAR transitively, so a patch cycle keyed to the product misses the bundled framework.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management that inventories only first-party components will not surface an embedded RichFaces 3.x dependency inside a deployed WAR, leaving the EL-injection sink unremediated.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Requires timely handling but does not mandate the SBOM-level component discovery needed to find a legacy JSF library shipped inside third-party Java apps.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection does not stop the exploit because the resource path is a legitimate application endpoint reachable over the normal HTTPS listener.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch-application maturity targets the OS/app vendor; an unmaintained embedded framework has no upstream patch, so the control cannot be satisfied by patching alone.

CVE-2023-41991.name: Apple Multiple Products Improper Certificate Validation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A malicious app exploits improper certificate validation in the Security framework to bypass code-signature checks, allowing unsigned/spyware code to run as part of a chained exploit.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (malicious app, no prior elevated rights) with limited user interaction
attack_vector.complexity: high — developed and operated by a commercial spyware vendor as part of a multi-CVE chain.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Citizen Lab and Google TAG. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install iOS 16.7 / iPadOS 16.7 / macOS Ventura 13.6; enable rapid MDM-forced updates and Lockdown Mode for high-risk users.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone is insufficient across unmanaged/BYOD fleets where updates lag; targeted users need Lockdown Mode.

detection.what_would_have_worked: MDM compliance reporting on OS build; mobile threat-defense telemetry for anomalous app signing/behavior.
detection.adequacy: Commercial spyware is engineered to minimize on-device indicators.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force-update affected devices, engage Apple Threat Notification follow-up, and re-image/replace targeted high-risk devices.
response.adequacy: A confirmed spyware implant warrants device replacement, not just an update.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation depends on the Apple OS update reaching every device; MDM patch SLAs of days-to-weeks far exceed the same-day weaponization observed for the Predator chain.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management for a mobile fleet cannot force an OS patch onto personal/BYOD devices, leaving the signature-validation bypass exploitable until the user updates.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Mandates handling but provides no mechanism to close a zero-day signature-check bypass before the vendor patch is installed across the estate.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch-application maturity assumes patchable, managed endpoints; the bypass is reachable on any device still on the vulnerable iOS/macOS build.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System security expects a trustworthy code-integrity anchor, but this flaw defeats the code-signature trust anchor itself, so the control's assumption is invalid until patched.

CVE-2023-41992.name: Apple Multiple Products Kernel Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local process (delivered via the chained WebKit stage) abuses a kernel improper-check flaw to elevate to kernel privileges, giving the spyware full device control.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local code execution from a sandboxed context)
attack_vector.complexity: high — engineered as the kernel stage of a commercial-spyware chain.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Citizen Lab and Google TAG. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install iOS 16.7 / macOS Monterey 12.7 / Ventura 13.6; enforce rapid updates via MDM and Lockdown Mode for at-risk users.
prevention.adequacy: Patch alone is insufficient where update deployment lags; the LPE only closes once the OS build ships.

detection.what_would_have_worked: MDM OS-build compliance and mobile threat-defense telemetry for privilege anomalies.
detection.adequacy: Kernel-level spyware minimizes observable indicators.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force-update, re-image, or replace confirmed-targeted devices; review Apple Threat Notifications.
response.adequacy: Kernel compromise warrants device replacement for high-risk targets.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation depends on the Apple OS update reaching every device; the kernel LPE stays exploitable on any endpoint still on the vulnerable build.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Mobile-fleet vulnerability management cannot force an OS patch onto BYOD/personal Apple devices, so the LPE persists until the user updates.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Provides no mechanism to neutralize a kernel privilege-escalation zero-day before the vendor patch is deployed across the estate.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch-application maturity assumes patchable endpoints; unpatched iOS/macOS devices keep the kernel escalation reachable.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System security relies on kernel integrity that this improper-check flaw directly violates, invalidating the control until patched.

CVE-2023-41993.name: Apple Multiple Products WebKit Code Execution Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A victim opens a targeted link/page and WebKit processes crafted web content, giving the attacker arbitrary code execution in the renderer as the opening stage of the spyware chain.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated) with user interaction (visiting a link)
attack_vector.complexity: high — a one-click browser exploit operated by a commercial spyware vendor.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Citizen Lab and Google TAG. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install iOS 16.7 / macOS Sonoma 14; enable Lockdown Mode for high-risk users and update all WebKit-embedding apps.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is necessary but downstream WebKit embedders and BYOD devices update on their own schedule; Lockdown Mode narrows the window for targets.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Renderer-crash telemetry and mobile threat-defense heuristics; scrutiny of unsolicited one-time links.
detection.adequacy: Zero-click/one-click chains leave minimal user-visible indicators.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force-update, re-image/replace targeted devices, and review Apple Threat Notifications.
response.adequacy: A browser-stage compromise that chains to kernel warrants treating the device as fully compromised.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation depends on the Apple/WebKit update reaching every device and every third-party WebKit embedder; the RCE stays live on any unpatched build.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Vulnerability management cannot force an OS/browser patch onto BYOD Apple devices or downstream WebKit-embedding apps, leaving the web-content RCE open.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Mandates handling but offers no mechanism to close a browser-engine zero-day before the vendor patch is deployed.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Browser/app hardening (disabling Java, ads, extensions) does not stop a memory-safety WebKit exploit that fires from ordinary web content; only the engine patch closes it.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System security assumes a sound browser sandbox, but the WebKit flaw is the initial code-execution primitive that breaks that assumption.

CVE-2023-41179.name: Trend Micro Apex One and Worry-Free Business Security Remote Code Execution Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker who has already obtained administrative-console access manipulates the 3rd-party AV uninstaller module to inject and execute arbitrary OS commands on the management host.
attack_vector.privileges_required: high (administrative console access is a precondition)
attack_vector.complexity: low — once console access is held, the uninstaller-module abuse is straightforward.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — handled by Trend Micro PSIRT after in-the-wild exploitation. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the Apex One / Worry-Free hotfix and restrict administrative-console access to trusted management networks.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the sink, but because a security product's console is high-value, network isolation and MFA are needed alongside the patch.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on uninstaller-module invocation and child-process spawns from the console service; monitor console logins from unexpected sources.
detection.adequacy: Detection depends on logging the management server, which many deployments under-instrument.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the management server, rotate console/admin credentials, and audit for prior command execution.
response.adequacy: Compromise of a security-management server implies broad downstream trust must be re-evaluated.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement on the admin console is the only barrier; once console access is obtained (the exploit's precondition) AC-3 no longer constrains the command-execution path through the uninstaller module.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection is the practical mitigation (restrict the console to trusted networks), but SC-7 as commonly implemented still exposes the console to a broad internal segment a compromised admin can reach.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Vulnerability management assumes a patch is applied promptly, yet this was a zero-day exploited before the hotfix, so the control lagged the exploitation window.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management timelines for the security product's own console did not pre-empt the zero-day; the control is insufficient without console network isolation.

AU-ISM-1546.gap: Restricting privileged access reduces who can reach the console but does not remove the code-injection sink in the uninstaller module for those who legitimately have access.

attack_vector.description: An attacker holding over-privileged (arn:aws:s3:::*) credentials with Console API access sends a crafted PostPolicyBucket request that bypasses metadata bucket-name checking, writing objects into arbitrary buckets to escalate privilege.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (valid but over-scoped credentials required)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted request against an exposed Console API, often chained with CVE-2023-28432.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported and fixed by MinIO maintainers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to RELEASE.2023-03-20T20-16-18Z, scope IAM policies away from arn:aws:s3:::* wildcards, and disable/limit the Console API (MINIO_BROWSER=off) from untrusted networks.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the bypass, but over-broad wildcard policies remain a standing risk without least-privilege IAM.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Audit-log alerts on cross-bucket writes and object writes into config/system buckets by non-admin principals.
detection.adequacy: Detection requires audit logging that many self-hosted deployments do not centralize.

response.what_would_have_worked: Rotate all MinIO credentials, review objects written to sensitive buckets, and reset any exposed configuration.
response.adequacy: Because the flaw enables config/credential tampering, full key rotation is required.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least privilege is undermined because the wildcard arn:aws:s3:::* grant the exploit relies on is a common default; AC-6 as written does not force the fine-grained bucket scoping that would deny the cross-bucket write.

NIST-800-53-AC-3.gap: Access enforcement is exactly what the metadata-bucket-name bypass defeats; the object write reaches a bucket the policy should have blocked.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management assumes prompt upgrade to RELEASE.2023-03-20T20-16-18Z; unpatched, internet-exposed MinIO instances remain exploitable.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security measures do not help when the Console API is deliberately exposed and reached with valid (over-privileged) credentials.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch-application maturity is the real fix; the control is unmet on the many self-hosted MinIO deployments that lag releases.

attack_vector.description: A local app abuses improper exception handling in the Exynos NPU driver to trigger a use-after-free, gaining arbitrary kernel memory write and code execution for privilege escalation.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (local code execution on the device)
attack_vector.complexity: high — requires driver-specific memory-corruption exploitation tied to particular Exynos builds.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — fixed by Samsung Mobile Security. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install the January 2022 SMR (Release 1) or later on affected Exynos devices; enforce update compliance via MDM.
prevention.adequacy: Patch is the fix, but carrier/OEM update lag on Exynos devices leaves a long exposure window that the control cannot shorten.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile threat-defense telemetry for driver crashes / privilege anomalies; MDM patch-level reporting.
detection.adequacy: On-device kernel exploitation offers limited observable indicators.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force the SMR update or replace the device; treat a kernel-compromised handset as fully untrusted.
response.adequacy: Kernel-level compromise warrants device re-provisioning for sensitive users.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Firmware/driver flaw remediation depends on the Samsung SMR reaching the specific carrier/device build; SMR rollout lag leaves many Exynos devices exploitable for months.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Mobile-fleet vulnerability management cannot force an OEM SMR onto carrier-gated or BYOD Samsung devices.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Provides no mechanism to close a kernel-driver LPE before the monthly SMR is delivered to the device.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch-application maturity assumes patchable endpoints; unpatched Exynos devices keep the use-after-free reachable.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System security relies on kernel/driver integrity that this NPU-driver use-after-free directly violates.

CVE-2014-8361.name: Realtek SDK Improper Input Validation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A remote, unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted UPnP SOAP NewInternalClient request to the Realtek miniigd service; unsanitized input is passed to a shell, executing arbitrary commands and typically enrolling the device in a botnet.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated network attacker)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted SOAP request, widely automated by IoT botnets.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — publicly disclosed by security researchers. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply OEM firmware that fixes the Realtek SDK where available; block WAN-side UPnP/SOAP; replace end-of-life devices.
prevention.adequacy: Patching is impossible on the many EOL devices; network isolation and replacement are the only durable fixes.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Monitor for inbound UPnP/SOAP with shell metacharacters and for CPE reaching out to download payloads.
detection.adequacy: SOHO/edge devices are rarely instrumented, so detection is limited to the network boundary.

response.what_would_have_worked: Factory-reset and re-flash, or replace the device; block the C2 destinations observed.
response.adequacy: Botnet persistence on embedded firmware often survives soft reboots; replacement is safest.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Many affected consumer/embedded devices are end-of-life with no vendor firmware; flaw remediation has no patch to apply, so the control is structurally unmet.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Vulnerability management cannot inventory or patch the Realtek SDK buried in third-party OEM firmware across heterogeneous SOHO hardware.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security controls are the only realistic defense (block WAN-side UPnP/SOAP), yet many devices expose the service to the WAN by default.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection must block WAN-side access to the UPnP/SOAP port; default configs and ISP-provided CPE frequently leave it reachable.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch maturity is unattainable for unmaintained embedded firmware; device replacement is the only remediation.

CVE-2017-6884.name: Zyxel EMG2926 Routers Command Injection Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker reaches the router's diagnostic nslookup function and injects shell metacharacters through the ping_ip parameter, executing arbitrary OS commands on the CPE.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (authenticated to the router web UI, often via default/weak credentials)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted diagnostic request with public exploit code available.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — publicly disclosed by an independent researcher. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Replace the end-of-life EMG2926; change default credentials; block WAN-side access to the management/diagnostic interface.
prevention.adequacy: No sustained firmware stream exists for the EOL device; replacement plus network isolation is the durable fix.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on diagnostic-endpoint requests carrying shell metacharacters and on CPE payload downloads.
detection.adequacy: Consumer CPE is rarely logged, limiting detection to the network boundary.

response.what_would_have_worked: Factory-reset/replace the device and rotate any credentials it stored or proxied.
response.adequacy: Compromised SOHO routers should be replaced rather than trusted after cleanup.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: The EMG2926 is end-of-life; flaw remediation has no supported firmware to apply, so it cannot be satisfied — device replacement is the only fix.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Vulnerability management on unmanaged SOHO/edge CPE rarely tracks router firmware, leaving the injection sink open.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection must deny WAN-side access to the admin/diagnostic interface; exposed management on the WAN defeats the assumption.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security measures are the practical mitigation but do not remove the injection sink for anyone who reaches the authenticated diagnostic page.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch maturity is unmet on EOL consumer hardware; there is no ongoing vendor patch stream.

attack_vector.description: An unauthenticated attacker posts to /_ignition/execute-solution on a Laravel app in debug mode, abusing insecure file read/write to poison a log or PHAR and trigger phar:// deserialization of a gadget chain for remote code execution.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated) — requires the app to be running with APP_DEBUG=true
attack_vector.complexity: low — public exploit tooling automates the log-poison + phar chain.
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Charles Fol (Ambionics Security). No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade Ignition to >= 2.5.2 and set APP_DEBUG=false in production so /_ignition/execute-solution is not exposed.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the sink, but the dominant real-world exposure is the debug misconfiguration, which requires secure-config enforcement, not just a package bump.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on requests to /_ignition/execute-solution, on writes to laravel.log followed by phar:// access, and on PHP shelling out.
detection.adequacy: Detection is effective if web/app logging captures the Ignition endpoint, which debug-exposed apps often under-monitor.

response.what_would_have_worked: Disable debug mode, rotate APP_KEY and any secrets in .env, and rebuild the app host from a known-good image.
response.adequacy: Post-RCE the entire app environment (including leaked .env secrets) must be treated as compromised.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least functionality is the real defense — Ignition's debug mode must not run in production; CM-7 baselines often omit the framework-level APP_DEBUG hardening that closes the endpoint.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation requires upgrading Ignition to >= 2.5.2, but the far more common exposure is a misconfigured debug flag that a package patch alone does not address.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Vulnerability management keyed to Composer dependencies may miss that the exploit precondition is a deployment misconfiguration (debug mode), not only an outdated package.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management SLAs did not pre-empt mass exploitation; the control is insufficient without secure-configuration enforcement.

PCI-DSS-4.0-2.2.3.gap: Secure configuration standards must prohibit debug/verbose error pages in production; where 2.2.3 is not enforced for the app framework, the Ignition endpoint stays exposed.

CVE-2023-26369.name: Adobe Acrobat and Reader Out-of-Bounds Write Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: An attacker delivers a crafted PDF; opening it in a vulnerable Acrobat/Reader build triggers an out-of-bounds write and arbitrary code execution in the current user's context.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (attacker needs no account; victim must open the file)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted document reliably reaches the vulnerable parser on open
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to Adobe and fixed in APSB23-34. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the APSB23-34 update; enable Protected View / disable embedded JavaScript and sandbox untrusted PDFs at the mail gateway.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the flaw but the zero-day was exploited before the fix, so application hardening was needed in the interim.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on Acrobat spawning shells/anomalous children and on OOB-write crash telemetry.
detection.adequacy: Detection catches post-exploitation but not the initial memory write.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, rotate the user's credentials, hunt for persistence dropped by the payload.
response.adequacy: Response is reactive to a code-execution event that already occurred.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation on a zero-day exploited before patch availability leaves no clean patch window; the KEV due date (2023-10-05) trailed active exploitation, so SI-2 SLAs alone do not close the exposure.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Hardening endpoint applications (disabling Acrobat's embedded JavaScript / opening untrusted PDFs in Protected View) is the compensating control the KEV entry does not mandate; patch cadence alone left users exposed at open-time.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-handling.gap: Requires timely handling but not the user-interaction-blocking controls (attachment sandboxing, mail filtering) needed to stop a document-delivered client RCE between disclosure and patch.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System security expects patched software but does not force the endpoint isolation/EDR detection required for a memory-corruption RCE that fires on document open.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management is periodic; a client-side zero-day exploited on open needs continuous endpoint monitoring beyond A.8.8's assessment cadence.

attack_vector.description: A malicious app already on the device exploits a WindowState logic error to launch a background activity and escalate to higher privilege with no additional execution privilege and no user interaction.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (a locally installed, low-privileged app)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the logic error is reachable from an ordinary app without special conditions
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — fixed by Google in the Android Framework. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the 2023-09-01 patch level; MDM-enforce a minimum patch level and restrict app sideloading.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the flaw but OEM delivery lag leaves many devices exposed; MDM gating is the practical control.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile threat defense flagging apps abusing background-activity launches or privilege gain.
detection.adequacy: MTD coverage is uneven across Android OEMs.

response.what_would_have_worked: Remove the offending app, wipe/re-enroll the device, and block the app hash fleet-wide.
response.adequacy: Reactive once privilege has been escalated.
