NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Mobile-OS flaw remediation depends on OEM/carrier patch propagation; the KEV due date (2023-10-04) is unreachable for the long tail of devices that never receive the 2023-09-01 patch level.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch-application maturity assumes an update is deliverable; fragmented Android OEM update chains mean many fleet devices stay vulnerable well past the KEV window.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management policy cannot force OEM firmware timelines, so a framework privilege-escalation flaw persists on managed mobile endpoints the policy nominally covers.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System security expects current software but provides no lever over OEM/carrier delivery for an OS-level local privilege escalation.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management periodicity does not match a mobile local-privilege-escalation flaw needing MDM-enforced minimum-patch-level gating.

CVE-2023-20269.name: Cisco Adaptive Security Appliance and Firepower Threat Defense Unauthorized Access Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: Because AAA is not properly separated between RA VPN and the HTTPS-management/site-to-site features, an unauthenticated attacker specifies a default connection profile to brute-force valid credentials, then logs in over RA VPN; on ASA 9.16 and earlier a clientless SSL VPN session can be established with an unauthorized user. Akira ransomware used this for initial access.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated for the brute force; valid credentials still required to open the tunnel)
attack_vector.complexity: low — internet-facing appliance, default group reachable, no MFA on targeted gateways
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — Cisco PSIRT advisory following incident-response observations. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply Cisco's fixed release; enforce MFA on all RA VPN profiles and remove/lock the default connection profile and DfltGrpPolicy.
prevention.adequacy: Patch fixes AAA separation, but MFA and default-group hardening were the controls that actually blunted the ransomware initial access.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alerting on RA VPN authentication brute-force volume and on logins to default tunnel groups.
detection.adequacy: Brute-force noise is detectable but blends with normal VPN traffic without tuning.

response.what_would_have_worked: Force password resets, enable MFA, review VPN session logs and hunt for Akira staging.
response.adequacy: Reactive once credentials are compromised.

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: Identification and authentication is defeated when RA VPN lacks MFA; without phishing-resistant MFA the AAA-separation flaw turns password guessing into valid-account access.

NIST-800-63B-rev4.gap: Single-factor VPN authentication is below the assurance this flaw demands; the brute-force path is only meaningful because a second factor was not enforced on the default tunnel group.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security measures do not by themselves mandate MFA on the RA VPN or lockout on the default group, so the AAA-separation weakness stays exploitable on a compliant deployment.

AU-ISM-1546.gap: Requires MFA for remote access but does not force removal/lockdown of the default connection profile that the brute force targets.

UK-CAF-B2.gap: Identity and access control expects strong authentication yet does not compel the tunnel-group hardening (no-MFA default group disabled, rate-limiting) needed to stop credential brute forcing on the appliance.

CVE-2023-4863.name: Google Chromium WebP Heap-Based Buffer Overflow Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A crafted WebP image with malformed lossless (VP8L) Huffman coding tables overflows libwebp's BuildHuffmanTable, writing out of bounds and enabling code execution in the decoding process — reachable via a web page (crafted HTML) or any app that renders WebP.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (attacker needs no account; victim renders the image, often without explicit interaction)
attack_vector.complexity: low — decoding the image is enough; the same primitive works across many products that bundle libwebp
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported by Apple SEAR and Citizen Lab. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update libwebp to >= 1.3.2 everywhere (Chrome 116.0.5845.187+ and every bundled copy); maintain an SBOM to find embedded copies.
prevention.adequacy: Patching the browser is insufficient because the codec is vendored across many independent applications.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR/crash telemetry on image-decoder processes and SBOM-driven detection of vulnerable libwebp builds.
detection.adequacy: Crash telemetry is post-hoc; SBOM coverage was uneven at disclosure.

response.what_would_have_worked: Enumerate all software bundling libwebp, force updates, and block WebP from untrusted sources where feasible.
response.adequacy: Reactive and constrained by third-party update timelines.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation must reach every application that statically or dynamically links libwebp, not just the browser; SI-2 patch tracking keyed to Chrome misses the sprawl of Electron/native apps that ship their own libwebp.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management scoped to OS/browser inventories does not enumerate embedded libwebp in third-party software, so a supply-chain-wide image-decoder flaw stays unpatched in bundled copies.

NIS2-Art21-vulnerability-management.gap: Vulnerability management does not by itself require an SBOM/component inventory to find every product bundling the vulnerable codec, leaving unmanaged copies exposed to a crafted image.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch maturity assumes a single vendor update path; a library embedded across many independent applications defeats a browser-centric patch model.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System security expects patched software but gives no mechanism to inventory and update a codec vendored into dozens of applications.

attack_vector.description: A crafted Word/Office document contains an external reference that forces the client to authenticate outbound (SMB/WebDAV) to an attacker-controlled server on open or Preview-pane render, disclosing the victim's NetNTLM hash for relay or offline cracking.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (attacker needs no account; the victim opens or previews the document)
attack_vector.complexity: low — the Preview Pane alone triggers it; no macro or extra interaction required
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to and fixed by Microsoft. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the September 2023 update; block outbound SMB/WebDAV at the perimeter, disable the Preview Pane, and enforce SMB signing / NTLM-relay hardening.
prevention.adequacy: Patch fixes the parser, but egress filtering and NTLM hardening are what make a leaked hash unusable.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on Office processes making outbound SMB to non-corporate hosts.
detection.adequacy: Detects the leak attempt but the hash may already have egressed.

response.what_would_have_worked: Reset exposed account credentials, hunt for NTLM relay against internal services, deploy SMB signing.
response.adequacy: Reactive once the hash is captured.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection that does not block outbound SMB/WebDAV (ports 445/139) to the internet leaves the forced-authentication leak path open, so a document-triggered NTLM egress reaches the attacker despite a compliant perimeter.

NIST-800-53-IA-2.gap: Continued reliance on NTLM as an authentication material lets a leaked NetNTLM hash be relayed or cracked; IA-2 does not by itself force NTLM deprecation or channel binding.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security measures do not inherently mandate egress filtering of SMB or SMB signing/NTLM-relay hardening needed to neutralize the credential leak.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application hardening (disabling the Preview Pane, blocking outbound SMB, enforcing SMB signing) is the mitigating control the KEV entry does not require; patching alone left the Preview-pane vector live until applied.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management periodicity does not cover the NTLM-relay hardening (SMB signing, Extended Protection for Authentication) that turns a leaked hash from a breach into a non-event.

CVE-2023-36802.name: Microsoft Streaming Service Proxy Privilege Escalation Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A local process opens the mskssrv device and issues IOCTL_FRAMESERVER_INIT_CONTEXT to initialize FsContext2 to an attacker-chosen object type, then triggers FSStreamReg::PublishRx which uses that field as an FsStreamReg object, yielding a kernel write and elevation to SYSTEM.
attack_vector.privileges_required: low (any local code execution)
attack_vector.complexity: low — public PoCs reliably escalate on unpatched Windows 10/11
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — credited to named researchers and vendor/PSIRT reporting. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Apply the September 2023 cumulative update; reduce local attack surface and constrain untrusted code execution.
prevention.adequacy: Patch removes the driver flaw, but with public PoCs any pre-patch foothold reached SYSTEM.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR detection of anomalous handle opens to mskssrv/KsProxy and unexpected SYSTEM token acquisition.
detection.adequacy: Detects exploitation attempts but the primitive is fast and kernel-level.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, assume SYSTEM compromise, and rebuild if escalation is confirmed.
response.adequacy: Reactive once SYSTEM is obtained.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation via monthly cumulative updates trails a kernel LPE already exploited in the wild with public PoCs; the patch cadence leaves a window an attacker with any code execution can use to reach SYSTEM.

NIST-800-53-AC-6.gap: Least privilege is bypassed at the kernel boundary — a driver type confusion elevates any low-privileged foothold to SYSTEM regardless of user-level restrictions, so AC-6 alone does not contain post-compromise escalation.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management policy does not force attack-surface reduction of the mskssrv driver, so the kernel escalation primitive stays reachable between disclosure and the monthly update.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch maturity focused on internet-facing software under-weights a local kernel LPE that turns a phishing foothold into full SYSTEM control.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System security expects timely patching but provides no compensating control for a publicly-weaponized kernel driver flaw in the interim.

CVE-2023-41064.name: Apple iOS, iPadOS, and macOS ImageIO Buffer Overflow Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A maliciously crafted image sent as a PassKit attachment over iMessage is processed by ImageIO with no user interaction, overflowing a buffer and executing code; combined with CVE-2023-41061 (BLASTPASS) to install Pegasus.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (zero-click; victim need not open anything)
attack_vector.complexity: low for the operator (weaponized by a commercial spyware vendor); the underlying chain is sophisticated
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by The Citizen Lab. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install iOS 16.6.1 / the corresponding macOS updates; enable Lockdown Mode for high-risk users.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the flaw, but it was a zero-day at delivery — Lockdown Mode was the only pre-patch mitigation.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile forensic acquisition (MVT), Lockdown Mode notifications, and Apple threat notifications for targeted users.
detection.adequacy: Zero-click implants leave minimal telemetry; detection needs specialized forensics.

response.what_would_have_worked: Update immediately, enable Lockdown Mode, rotate credentials/tokens, and seek expert forensic analysis for targeted individuals.
response.adequacy: Reactive against an implant that may have already exfiltrated data.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation cannot pre-empt a zero-click zero-day used by a commercial spyware vendor; the patch shipped only after Pegasus delivery, so SI-2 SLAs give no protection during the exploitation window.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: System monitoring on mobile endpoints is thin — a zero-click ImageIO exploit leaves little host telemetry, so SI-4 detection does not reliably surface the compromise.

NIS2-Art21-incident-handling.gap: Incident handling assumes detectable events; a zero-click spyware implant produces minimal signals, so the control cannot trigger without specialized forensic acquisition (e.g. Lockdown Mode, MVT).

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch maturity cannot address a flaw exploited before a fix exists; the mitigating control was Apple's Lockdown Mode, which the framework does not require.

UK-CAF-D1.gap: Response and recovery planning is not scoped for targeted commercial-spyware zero-click intrusions that leave little evidence and demand vendor-assisted forensics.

CVE-2023-41061.name: Apple iOS, iPadOS, and watchOS Wallet Code Execution Vulnerability

attack_vector.description: A maliciously crafted attachment processed by Wallet exploits a validation flaw for code execution with no user interaction; chained with CVE-2023-41064 (BLASTPASS) to install Pegasus.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (zero-click)
attack_vector.complexity: low for the operator (weaponized by a commercial spyware vendor); the full chain is sophisticated
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by The Citizen Lab. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Install iOS/iPadOS 16.6.1 and watchOS 9.6.2; enable Lockdown Mode for high-risk users.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the flaw, but it was a zero-day at delivery — Lockdown Mode was the only pre-patch mitigation.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Mobile forensic acquisition (MVT), Lockdown Mode notifications, and Apple threat notifications.
detection.adequacy: Zero-click implants leave minimal telemetry.

response.what_would_have_worked: Update immediately, enable Lockdown Mode, rotate credentials, and seek expert forensic analysis for targeted individuals.
response.adequacy: Reactive against an implant that may have already exfiltrated data.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation cannot pre-empt a zero-click zero-day used to deliver Pegasus; the Wallet validation fix shipped only after in-the-wild use, so SI-2 SLAs offer no protection during exploitation.

NIST-800-53-SI-4.gap: System monitoring on mobile endpoints yields little signal from a zero-click Wallet exploit, so SI-4 detection cannot reliably surface the intrusion.

NIS2-Art21-incident-handling.gap: Incident handling assumes detectable telemetry; a zero-click spyware chain leaves minimal evidence, requiring specialized forensic acquisition to trigger the process.

AU-Essential-8-Patch.gap: Patch maturity cannot cover a flaw exploited before a fix exists; Apple's Lockdown Mode was the effective pre-patch mitigation and is not framework-mandated.

UK-CAF-D1.gap: Response and recovery planning is not scoped for targeted commercial-spyware zero-click intrusions that demand vendor-assisted forensics.

attack_vector.description: An attacker reaches an internet-exposed RocketMQ component that lacks permission verification and calls updateBrokerConfig (or forges the remoting protocol) to set rocketmqHome/filterServerNums to a shell payload, so the broker executes commands as its service user. DreamBus botnet weaponized it.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (unauthenticated, remote)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a public Metasploit module and PoCs automate the config-injection path
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — reported to and fixed by the Apache RocketMQ project. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Upgrade to RocketMQ 5.1.1+/4.9.6+ and never expose NameServer/Broker/Controller to untrusted networks; require authentication on the remoting interface.
prevention.adequacy: Patch adds hardening but network isolation is the decisive control — an exposed broker is exploitable if the config path is reachable.

detection.what_would_have_worked: Alert on updateBrokerConfig requests and on the RocketMQ java process spawning shell children.
detection.adequacy: Detects exploitation but the RCE fires on the first request.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the broker, remove DreamBus/cryptominer payloads, rotate service credentials, and rebuild if persistence is found.
response.adequacy: Reactive once command execution has occurred.

NIST-800-53-SC-7.gap: Boundary protection is the crux — RocketMQ's NameServer/Broker/Controller were never meant to face the extranet, so exposing them (no permission verification) turns a config API into preauth RCE that SC-7 segmentation should have prevented.

NIST-800-53-CM-7.gap: Least functionality is violated when the update-configuration remoting function is reachable unauthenticated; CM-7 does not by default disable/lock down the broker management interface.

NIS2-Art21-network-security.gap: Network-security measures do not inherently mandate isolating message-broker management ports from untrusted networks, leaving the unauthenticated config path exploitable on a compliant deployment.

AU-ISM-1546.gap: Access-control guidance for services does not force authentication on the RocketMQ remoting interface, so the missing permission check remains the exploited gap.

ISO-27001-2022-A.8.8.gap: Technical-vulnerability management cadence trails an internet-facing preauth RCE with a public Metasploit module and active botnet use.

attack_vector.description: A ZIP/RAR archive includes a benign file (e.g. an ordinary .jpg) alongside a folder with the same name; when the victim opens the benign file in WinRAR before 6.23, the contents of the same-named folder — which include executable script content — are processed and run.
attack_vector.privileges_required: none (attacker needs no account; the victim opens the decoy file)
attack_vector.complexity: low — a single crafted archive plus a lure; a public Metasploit module builds it
attack_vector.ai_factor: Not AI-discovered — found by Group-IB investigating in-the-wild attacks. No AI involvement documented.

prevention.what_would_have_worked: Update to WinRAR 6.23; apply application control to block scripts spawned from archive temp directories and filter archive attachments at the gateway.
prevention.adequacy: Patch closes the extension-spoofing flaw, but un-updated installs are common — application control and mail filtering were needed in the interim.

detection.what_would_have_worked: EDR alerting on WinRAR spawning cmd.exe/scripts and on archives with same-named file+folder pairs.
detection.adequacy: Detects post-open execution but the payload runs on first open.

response.what_would_have_worked: Isolate the host, remove dropped payloads (traders were targeted with stealers), and rotate credentials handled on the endpoint.
response.adequacy: Reactive once the script has executed.

AU-Essential-8-App-Hardening.gap: Application hardening / attack-surface reduction (blocking script execution from archive temp paths, application control) is the mitigating control; patching WinRAR alone left users exposed while the decoy-open primitive was actively weaponized.

NIST-800-53-SI-2.gap: Flaw remediation depends on users updating a bundled desktop archiver that auto-update poorly; the KEV due date (2023-09-14) is unreachable for the long tail of un-updated WinRAR installs.

NIST-800-53-SI-3.gap: Malicious-code protection keyed to file signatures under-detects a benign-looking archive whose payload is a script hidden in a same-named folder, so AV alone misses the extension-spoofing decoy.

NIS2-Art21-patch-management.gap: Patch-management policy cannot force updates of a widely-installed third-party desktop tool, so vulnerable WinRAR persists on managed endpoints past the KEV window.

UK-CAF-B4.gap: System security expects current software but provides no lever to inventory/update a ubiquitous non-managed archiver, nor the application control needed to block the archive-spawned script.

new_control_requirements.name: ARCHIVE-CONTENT-TYPE-PROVENANCE
new_control_requirements.description: Archive and document handlers must dispatch rendering/execution on the content-verified file type rather than the displayed name or extension, and must propagate Mark-of-the-Web to every extracted file.
